body:
I'll think more about this, but I suppose it'd be best to track external and internal resources differently 

So the counter idea would be scrapped, being replaced by an icon instead.

If either the resource is external or the _current scene_ has multiple instances of the same resource, it will show up the `make_unique` option beside it. 

Because basically the issue with my current approach stems from trying to track down External Resources and them being in every nook and cranny of the engine (Everything is resources loool). Technically it'd be possible of marking every place they appear, but they wouldn't be properly tracked if you drag and dropped them from a previous project, or used a previous version of the engine.

author: SanNull

body:
Hey everyone, small update: Reinforced Support for Subresources, so they're taken into account when making external ones unique, and also when they're deleted with the parent. Arrays/Dictionaries are also using the counter now.
The PR is in a functional state, besides some quirks, so It's 'okay' for use (at least for my projects). This PR def needs some refactoring, perhaps some optimization ,reducing recursion calls, but I'll force myself to take a break from it until Godot 4.6 starts development, around the time someone from the Godot team might actually take a look at this.  

Note (mostly to self): Subresources in this PR are treated as inseparable from their parents, as it's impossible for a Resource and its nested child not have the same Nodes referencing them. So I took advantage of this when needed to keep better track of Subresources' counter. Also, dont use Vscode for git, it suckssss

body: Ah, I misunderstood the changes. Right now, when you create a new (unique) resource, you see this: (The tooltip doesn't match the actions that are available. And the right-click recursive action is new, right? In that case, it should probably also be added to the dropdown menu.) I know people are used to the way Blender handles this, but just showing a number with no immediate context feels like it goes against Godot's beginner-friendly style. Blender can be very dense and cryptic. As a beginner, I might read that as "this shape has one circle". Most of the complaints about Make Unique I'm seeing in discussions seem to be from users who are just looking for godotengine/godot-proposals#904, an indicator that appears to warn them when a resource is shared, and editing it could be destructive. https://www.reddit.com/r/godot/comments/1naylu5/why_is_make_unique_the_most_painful_feature_in/ I think displaying the number of references is extraneous, and might be information overload for people who are already struggling and annoyed with the whole concept of references. The specific number seems more like an "FYI", so personally I'd rather see it in the tooltip, like in this example (not a screenshot of the PR): Maybe just solving the first proposal isn't what this PR is about, but I think there's a lot of appetite for it, and the simpler version seems like an easy win.

body:
Refactored so Disabling and Enabling duplication works more seemingly, also added better descriptions as to why an option couldn't be used.
Furthermore I decided to implement https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/109458#issuecomment-3272488648, as:
- The counter is more of a 'secondary' information and should thus be accessed by hovering the button.
- The icon should be descriptive of the feature, and (for external resources at least), a `make_unique` icon wasn't cutting it (it would also be weird imo to have a counter for internal Resources and use a chain icon🔗  exclusively to external ones)

Feel free to express your agreement or disagreement towards this change! 

![showtime](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b7bcf932-af63-4540-898d-5a5be884be91)


One problem with the button accepting input however, is that it gets disabled as soon as make it unique. This is not a problem for Resources with no nesting, but for nested Resources, you either `make_unique_recursive` right off the bat or it disappears with `make_unique.` This might be a non issue but I decided to mention that


![showtime2](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/53dd72c3-7bb3-4190-a446-925595206546)


Also, to fully implement https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/10319 I'd have to tinker with the `Make_Unique_Recursive `popup, by disabling uniqueness if it's already been made unique, but I'll leave that to a next PR

body:
I did a code review. I didn't delve very deep into the implementation, at glance it looks ok. It's limited only to editor code, which is good.

The only concern is scanning all properties whenever object/array/dictionary is changed. I did test some cases that I think could be heavily affected and didn't notice any big slowdowns, so it's not as bad as it looks.

Also the code duplication. OBJECT/ARRAY/DICTIONARY is a famous trio in inspector-related code. I've seen the same methods many times already, but since they always do something a bit different, it's difficult to really unify it :/
author: KoBeWi

body:
Thanks for you guys Input! > I gave it a bit testing and it seems to work fine overall. One issue I found is the note about sub-resources This is most likely due to how External Resources are checked for recursive uniqueness, I'll take a look on that. But the main idea is that `make_unique_recursive` is only enabled if there's at least one subresource `A` that's either external or has multiple copies.
As for the MultiNodeEdit: > So for multinode, it still makes sense if the Resource is used in other nodes. This is tricky. Maybe check if usage count is equal to number of nodes? But I guess that's unreliable.
In that case, it's pretty difficult to determine how to proceed. My initial idea would be to have https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/13163 implemented so `Make Unique` actually duplicates for each Node instead of making only of copy that is shared between selected Nodes. Then, I guess we could enable/disable MultiNodeEdit using these: - To enable duplicating property `A`, we must find at least a Node from selection which shares Resource `A` with another Node in the Tree. Then, we duplicate this Resource the amount necessary, while leaving the others Nodes whose `A` is already unique alone. - If `A` is already unique for all Nodes in a selection, disable it.
> It takes space. It's similar to the quick load button, which users weren't very happy about. Though it only shows in specific cases, so probably it's fine? That was one of my worries, but currently there's no way to indicate that if not by an icon. Reduz's puts a counter on here, which is a really weird design IMO and not rly descriptive.

> The only concern is scanning all properties whenever object/array/dictionary is changed. I did test some cases that I think could be heavily affected and didn't notice any big slowdowns, so it's not as bad as it looks. Yeah it's wasteful currently, removing Resources only to re-add them again, with the exception of the one that changed, but covering all possible cases an Array/Dictionary can change would be pretty painful 😅
--- > Noticing that there are places where TTR is applied to variables like TTR(tooltip), that won't work AFAIK as the string won't be extractable, TTR should be around raw strings so the content can be extracted for translation data @AThousandShips is there a way to circumvent this? My previous approach was changing the tooltip text directly after a set of conditions were met, and there was a lot of overlap (e.g A external Resource with Subresources vs an Internal Resources with Subresouces share more of less the same message).
author: SanNull

The previous ones involved either increasing the minimum inspector size to accommodate all buttons or removing the preview altogether. Although, it's worth pointing out that the preview is usually not helpful for bigger sprites. I didn't remove the preview however bc 3D meshes seem to be more helpful even when the inspector's size is pretty small: I also solved an edge case where saved to disk resources would be included in the hash map if it was previously built in. body:
While this is arguably better than not letting the user know at all, I suspect this change will instead lead to content creators seeing a warning every time they go to change the font on something like a `RichTextLabel`, because they will likely always check the override checkbox before going to select the font. This strikes me as a somewhat unfortunate user experience, and I would be surprised if not more people start complaining if/when we merge this, given that the theme override is the de facto way you typically change fonts for something like `RichTextLabel`.

The messages themselves could probably be phrased in some better way as well. I don't think people will necessarily know/understand that manually checking the override checkbox for resource-backed properties ends up duplicating the entire resource, so a warning message mentioning some attempt to copy an embedded resource and asking them to save it somewhere else, when all you did was check the override checkbox, might not get through.

I can't help but feel like the better short-term change is to disallow the manual checking of the override checkbox for resource-backed properties altogether. If somebody absolutely wants a copy of the resource embedded/sub-resourced then they could presumably use Make Unique on the default/fallback resource. That would however mean that there's a somewhat weird discrepancy between non-resource properties and resource-backed properties, but that still feels like the "lesser evil" here.

body:
While the use of `RBSet` is discouraged in new code, we have no active goal of getting rid of `RBSet`. We have ~160 results for it in the codebase, so actively removing it would be risking regressions in quite a few parts of the codebase for no practical benefit.
While I agree getting rid of `RBSet` would be nice, since it has no real purpose, I'd rather see it organically disappear as we refactor code than risking regressions over changes right now.

author: deralmas

body:
> > some directions on this one would be appreciated, I could not get dynload-wrapper working
> 
> I think I can give a hand :D
> 
> What issues are you having exactly?

I tried to generate sowrap from `pipewire.h` with the following command:
```
../dynload-wrapper/generate-wrapper.py --include ./thirdparty/linuxbsd_headers/pipewire/src/pipewire/pipewire.h --sys-include ./thirdparty/linuxbsd_headers/pipewire/src/pipewire/pipewire.h --include-dir ./thirdparty/linuxbsd_headers/pipewire/src --include-dir ./thirdparty/linuxbsd_headers/pipewire/spa/include --soname libpipewire-0.3.so.0 --init-name pipewire --output-header ./platform/linuxbsd/pipewre-so_wrap.h --output-implementation ./platform/linuxbsd/pipewire-so_wrap.c
```

But there are lots of parse errors, mostly coming from SPA headers like:
```
pycparser.plyparser.ParseError: ./thirdparty/linuxbsd_headers/pipewire/spa/include/spa/utils/hook.h:469:32: before: )
```
~~Since SPA is header-only~~, is there any ways to prevent dynload-wrapper from parsing SPA headers while PipeWire is using them? I tried both `--omit-prefix` and `--ignore-headers` options but that didn't work.
author: j20001970

body:
Hi, I investigated the issue and I think I found a solution :D

We parse C code through [pycparser](https://github.com/eliben/pycparser), which by design does not support compiler extensions like `__typeof__`. Luckily, as [recommended by the author](https://github.com/eliben/pycparser/issues/554#issuecomment-2353002123), there's an addon called [pycparserext](https://github.com/inducer/pycparserext). You'll have to install it with `pip install pycparserext`

In addition to that, the fake libc is missing the definition of `locale_t`.

I addressed those issues in a very rough patch to dynload-wrapper which you can try below. I haven't tried building the files but at a glance they seem correct.

Note that, according to `--help`, `--sys-include` is meant to be specified as if you were including something in the code, not a path (i.e. `<pipewire/pipewire.h>`).

I did this invocation:

```sh
../dynload-wrapper/generate-wrapper.py \
	--include-dir ./thirdparty/linuxbsd_headers/pipewire/src/ \
	--include-dir ./thirdparty/linuxbsd_headers/pipewire/spa/include/ \
	--include ./thirdparty/linuxbsd_headers/pipewire/src/pipewire/pipewire.h  \
	--soname libpipewire-0.3.so.0 \
	--init-name pipewire \
	--sys-include '<pipewire/pipewire.h>' \
	--output-header ./platform/linuxbsd/pipewire-so_wrap.h \
	--output-implementation ./platform/linuxbsd/pipewire-so_wrap.c
```

With this dynload-wrapper patch:
```diff
diff --git a/fake_libc_include/_fake_typedefs.h b/fake_libc_include/_fake_typedefs.h
index 3442dc1..031f33f 100644
--- a/fake_libc_include/_fake_typedefs.h
+++ b/fake_libc_include/_fake_typedefs.h
@@ -176,4 +176,6 @@ typedef struct xcb_connection_t xcb_connection_t;
 typedef uint32_t xcb_window_t;
 typedef uint32_t xcb_visualid_t;
 
+typedef struct locale_t locale_t;
+
 #endif
diff --git a/generate-wrapper.py b/generate-wrapper.py
index 62b6294..a122823 100755
--- a/generate-wrapper.py
+++ b/generate-wrapper.py
@@ -29,6 +29,7 @@ import textwrap
 from datetime import datetime
 
 try:
+    import pycparser
     from pycparser import c_parser, c_ast, parse_file, c_generator
     from pycparser.c_ast import Decl, FuncDecl, PtrDecl
 except:
@@ -36,6 +37,14 @@ except:
     print("Try installing it with pip install pycparser or using your distributions package manager.")
     sys.exit(1)
 
+try:
+    import pycparserext
+    from pycparserext import ext_c_parser, ext_c_generator
+    from pycparserext.ext_c_parser import FuncDeclExt
+except:
+    print("pycparserext not found.")
+    sys.exit(1)
+
 VERSION="0.7"
 URL="https://github.com/hpvb/dynload-wrapper"
 NOW=datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
@@ -43,7 +52,8 @@ PROGNAME=os.path.basename(sys.argv[0])
 FLAGS=""
 
 def stringify_declaration(t):
-    generator = c_generator.CGenerator()
+    #generator = c_generator.CGenerator()
+    generator = ext_c_generator.GnuCGenerator()
     return generator.visit(t)
 
 def get_name(t):
@@ -73,14 +83,17 @@ def parse_header(filename, omit_prefix, initname, ignore_headers = [], ignore_al
             cpp_args.append(include_dir)
 
     print(f"cpp_args: {cpp_args}")
-    ast = parse_file(filename, use_cpp=True, cpp_path='gcc', cpp_args=cpp_args)
+    text = pycparser.preprocess_file(filename, cpp_path='gcc', cpp_args=cpp_args)
+    #parser = c_parser.CParser()
+    parser = ext_c_parser.GnuCParser()
+    ast = parser.parse(text)
 
     functions = []
     sym_definitions = []
 
     for ext in ast.ext:
         if isinstance(ext, Decl):
-            if not isinstance(ext.type, FuncDecl):
+            if not isinstance(ext.type, FuncDeclExt):
                 continue
 
             skip = False
```

Again please excuse the crudity of this model. I didn't have time to ~build it to scale or paint~ polish it. ;)

Lastly, for some reason I get some debug `lextab.py` and `yacctab.py` files. I don't have time to figure out why so please remember to delete them before committing.

HTH :D

CC @hpvb (I'll make a PR soon™! Cleaned up of course)
author: deralmas

Since [godot-camerafeed-demo](https://github.com/shiena/godot-camerafeed-demo) only needs to receive the `camera_feeds_updated` signal once, I added the `CONNECT_ONE_SHOT` flag (https://github.com/shiena/godot-camerafeed-demo/commit/fc802d1e65a8faa8e72c7678463463c92c0e1a19). This prevents redundant emits. `CameraLinux` updates `CameraFeed` every second when `set_monitoring_feeds` is enabled, but this behavior feels somewhat overly intrusive.

Unlike on Windows, changing the format in https://github.com/shiena/godot-camerafeed-demo doesn't appear to change the resolution even if I click **Stop** then **Start**. I've tried 160×90 but it looked just as sharp as the default, while it was very blurry on Windows. Also unlike on Windows, formats don't list a framerate, only a resolution: It's possible that this can be chalked up to hardware support (I noticed 1080p is only listed on Windows, not Linux), still.

body:
The missing framerate in format list was fixed in https://github.com/shiena/godot-camerafeed-demo/commit/99c7a9f68cb30f20cbab02732b28835f6fc9fdd5.

0da6848110a97e1e56fa22dc060077c0da1d5d48 fixed missing camera feed formats where there is only one framerate for the resolution. This might fix 1080p camera feed format if only one framerate is available.

> Unlike on Windows, changing the format in https://github.com/shiena/godot-camerafeed-demo doesn't appear to change the resolution even if I click **Stop** then **Start**. I've tried 160×90 but it looked just as sharp as the default, while it was very blurry on Windows.

Can confirm that if camera feed format was selected first while the camera feed is still active, then clicking **Stop** -> **Start** will not change camera feed format as expected. 0da6848110a97e1e56fa22dc060077c0da1d5d48 also removed the restriction on requiring camera feeds to be deactivated to set format, so it should have be fixed now.

Some camera indicators can get stuck on "Creating" in KDE's applet if you exit the app while switching formats: Also, running in `--verbose` mode shows the camera being connected, disconnecting then reconnecting immediately after: ``` CameraServer: Registered camera Elgato Facecam 4K (V4L2) with ID 1 and position 0 at index 0 CameraServer: Removed camera Elgato Facecam 4K (V4L2) with ID 1 and position 0 CameraServer: Registered camera Elgato Facecam 4K (V4L2) with ID 1 and position 0 at index 0 ``` The camera indicator doesn't show up in `master`, which means PipeWire is being used even if the camera name ends with a `(V4L2)` suffix. The PR currently has a bug that the camera feeds will fail to start streaming on systems using PipeWire 1.6.1 (maybe 1.6.0) onward. The stream from PipeWire side will fail with error message like `error use input buffers: -22 (invalid argument)`. Tested on Arch running KDE (desktop) and GNOME (laptop) desktop environment.

Strangely, PipeWire [tutorial 5](https://docs.pipewire.org/page_tutorial5.html) from official documentation also seems stopped working (the program does not report captured frame byte size), while some webcam capture applications using PipeWire like OBS still work as expected. So it is likely that there are still something the PR does not handle well. Please do not merge this until the issue is solved.
author: j20001970

body:
> The PR currently has a bug that the camera feeds will fail to start streaming on systems using PipeWire 1.6.1 (maybe 1.6.0)...

d7c64a8717c02ee7c3ead6e0d1d38106076725cc have fixed aforementioned issue by [specifying buffer datatype](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/d7c64a8717c02ee7c3ead6e0d1d38106076725cc/modules/camera/camera_feed_pipewire.cpp#L359) when connecting the stream. The PR should now work on newer PipeWire version. [on_stream_state_changed](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/d7c64a8717c02ee7c3ead6e0d1d38106076725cc/modules/camera/camera_feed_pipewire.cpp#L134) callback is also added to notify stream error.

Now to respond the review feedback from @Calinou (thanks for the review!):

> Also, running in `--verbose` mode shows the camera being connected, disconnecting then reconnecting immediately after:

When [CameraPipeWire::set_monitoring_feeds](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/d7c64a8717c02ee7c3ead6e0d1d38106076725cc/modules/camera/camera_pipewire.cpp#L255) is set to false, all feeds created by `CameraPipeWire` will be removed from the server. And in godot-camerafeed-demo, [reloading camera list](https://github.com/shiena/godot-camerafeed-demo/blob/4fe062ac062fa2b85f134b71b960c3cab85c20bb/camerafeed.gd#L107) will first stop monitoring feeds then start monitoring again. So the behaviour is kind of expected with the following order:

1. Registering feeds by calling `CameraServer.get_singleton()->set_monitoring_feeds(true)` from [`CameraTexture` constructor](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/d7c64a8717c02ee7c3ead6e0d1d38106076725cc/scene/resources/camera_texture.cpp#L152).
2. Removing feeds by setting `CameraServer.monitoring_feeds` to false in [_reload_camera_list](https://github.com/shiena/godot-camerafeed-demo/blob/4fe062ac062fa2b85f134b71b960c3cab85c20bb/camerafeed.gd#L113)
3. Registering feeds again by setting `CameraServer.monitoring_feeds` to true in [_reload_camera_list](https://github.com/shiena/godot-camerafeed-demo/blob/4fe062ac062fa2b85f134b71b960c3cab85c20bb/camerafeed.gd#L120)

> Some camera indicators can get stuck on "Creating" in KDE's applet if you exit the app while switching formats:

I can't seem to reproduce the problem, does that mean exiting app need to happen after a new format is selected, as well as before the feed start providing frames with the new format?

> Framerate is pretty smooth, although 4K60 doesn't seem to be available

I'm not sure if Windows could provide webcam resolution higher than the specs. I took a look at the Elgato [website](https://www.elgato.com/us/en/p/facecam-4k), it states the video resolution up to `2160p60 (MJPG only)` when connected by USB3.0. So both the `master` branch and this PR should support formats listed in the specs by the look of the screenshots.
author: j20001970

body:
I did check it and I indeed get double print. Without `await` the editor will crash from what looks like stack overflow.

Although this behavior is expected. `edit_node()` does call `_handles()` in the plugin, and then `edit(null)`, because another plugin is going to edit the object.
author: KoBeWi

body:
> Although this behavior is expected. `edit_node()` does call `_handles()` in the plugin, and then `edit(null)`, because another plugin is going to edit the object.

Sounds like a bug or unexpected behavior, we can discuss it later since the docs here says that `_edit()` will be called later with `null` Object to allow cleaning the plugin, which makes `edit_node()` tells the same plugin that just handled the `Object` and returned `true` to clean, it's confusing.
author: WhalesState

body: Well, only one object can be edited by any plugin at the time (it's actually one per "context", but in this case the SVG file and scene root share the context). Hence if you try to edit another object, the current plugin, which does not handle it, has to cleanup.
author: KoBeWi

body:
Yes but we have an exception here when `_handles(Object)` returns `true` it already calls `_edit(Object)` and `_edit(null)` at same frame which is indeed a bug. We should exclude the Plugins that can handle the `Object` from the cleanup.

Edit: This also can be fixed if the clean up happens before the handling which should be the expected behavior.
author: WhalesState

body:
`edit_node()` requests editor to edit the node. The editor goes through all relevant plugins, calls `_handles()` passing the object and if a plugin returns `true` for the object, the editor will call `_edit()`.

What happens in your code:
- You click SVG file
- Editor gets edit request from FileSystem dock
- Your plugin receives `_handles()` call and returns true
- Your plugin receives `_edit()` call
- In the `_edit()` callback, you request to edit _another_ object
- The editor gets request and goes over plugins
- Your plugin gets a `_handles()` call with the new object that you just requested and returns `false`
- _Another_ plugin returns true for `_handles()`
- Your plugin receives `_edit(null)`, because it no longer edits (doesn't matter if it was editing a different object)
- Another plugin receives `_edit(the new object)` call
author: KoBeWi

body:
Just for a historical note, the adding everything to the origin in the BVH, then moving paradigm is something we noticed as problematic a while ago (circa 2020?) around when I was writing the BVH used for physics and rendering in 3.x (this was since replaced for rendering in 4.x by Juan's "dynamic BVH"). PouleyKetchoup did a fair bit of research into this if I remember right when he was maintaining physics but some of these issues / PRs may have been deleted (can't find them now by searching, but will post if I can find).

The issue was not just adding to the BVH itself, it was that you would get pairings of every combination at the origin (which scales with `O(N squared)` or something like that). Pairings would occur and affect lighting, shadows, collision detection, etc etc.

As far as processing the better solution would be add the object, move to initial location, _then_ activate in the BVH. But this had compat breaking implications. Pouley left the project before we fixed up any of this.

body: The return from `_disconnect` is `true` if and only if disconnection happened
author: AThousandShips

body:
> I'm not sure if this is the best fix for the issue. It seems like `get_persistent_signal_connection_count` is used to determine if a signal was actually disconnected.
> 
> A different solution might be to return a boolean from `Object::disconnect` which informs whether a disconnect happened. It seems like `Object::disconnect` is calling `_disconnect`, which _does_ return a bool. While it is not documented what the bool does, it seems like it might fit the bill. If `disconnect` was made to return that bool, and further uresd for the update check instead of calling `get_persistent_signal_connection_count`, it may be possible to eliminate unnecessary lookups, reducing the lookup speed further without introducing new optimization logic.

Using `_disconnect` directly in `Node::disconnect` or updating `Object::disconnect` to return a `bool` both work just as well, if there's appetite to either 1) have `Node` use an internal `Object` function, or 2) change the definition of `disconnect`. 

Happy to do either, which is the preference?
author: precup

body: Changing `disconnect` would be a major compatibility breakage so I think we should avoid that
author: AThousandShips

body:
I started making the code change, and then realized why I stopped looking into that in the first place:

`Object::_disconnect` returns `true` iff a signal connection was removed. However, we actually only care if a signal connection with the `CONNECT_PERSIST` flag was removed, and if I use `Object::_disconnect`, we don't know, and end up emitting the change signal spuriously when disconnecting non persistent signals. I wasn't able to find any way to cleanly check that.
author: precup

body:
> I started making the code change, and then realized why I stopped looking into that in the first place:
> 
> `Object::_disconnect` returns `true` iff a signal connection was removed. However, we actually only care if a signal connection with the `CONNECT_PERSIST` flag was removed, and if I use `Object::_disconnect`, we don't know, and end up emitting the change signal spuriously when disconnecting non persistent signals. I wasn't able to find any way to cleanly check that.

That's a good point.
One solution would be for `disconnect` to look up the entry in `signal_map` itself, and check whether it is a persistent entry. Only if `_disconnect` returns true, AND it was a persistent entry, the change signal should be fired.
The other solution would be to return `Pair<bool, int>` of `{ was_removed, flags }` from `_disconnect`, where `flags` is the `flags` of the removed `SignalData` (if removed).

The first solution would be a more local solution. The latter would be faster.
I'd probably go for the first since it's simpler and probably 'fast enough'.
author: Ivorforce
precup

body:
Thanks for the thorough review.  I indeed didn't consider that `signal_map` is private. 

I'd suggest in this case to add a function `uint32_t Object::get_signal_connection_flags(const StringName &p_name, const Callable &p_callable)` which can be used to retrieve `flags` without changing access modifiers on `signal_map`. (edit: I first suggested to return `const Connection &`, but it doesn't contain any other useful information). `Object::connect` accepts `uint32_t p_flags` which implies the usefulness of this function.

I'd still use the boolean from `_disconnect` to determine whether it was changed.

author: bruvzg

body:
> I've been wondering why `is_action_just_pressed()` is not available in `InputEvent`, but only the `Input` singleton. In case there isn't a technical reason for this, it might be better to implement there.

When you receive an `InputEvent` (for example within the `_unhandled_input` function), then the associated action has been triggered in this frame, so I don't see any reason for implementing it there, since there is already [`InputEvent.is_action_pressed()`](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/classes/class_inputevent.html#class-inputevent-method-is-action-pressed), which already achieves that.

You use `Input.is_action_just_pressed()` within `_process` to check in each frame the Input singleton, if any actions have been triggered.

One thing, that has always bothered me, is that `is_action_just_pressed()` needs to be checked in every frame, while you can achieve the same effect, listening to the actual `InputEvents` in `_unhandled_input`.

body:
Blocking the signal is intentional, as otherwise that method would get called again from the signal emitted from that method, potentially causing an infinite loop.
The EditorSceneTabs tab_changed signal is only intended to tell the EditorNode that a tab has been clicked via the tab bar, as the EditorNode is what handles the changing the scene. So this shouldn't use the signal, and instead be triggered from `EditorNode::_set_current_scene_nocheck` or somewhere.

body:
I'm realizing now that there's one other potential problem with this change.

If you're calling methods on `PhysicsServer*D` from a thread other than the main thread during physics processing, you will now just queue up those operations endlessly until the physics command queue has resumed its processing. This could maybe lead to out-of-memory scenarios.

We also never shrink the allocated size of that queue, so if that other thread was doing a ton of physics operations one frame then you'll be stuck with that allocated memory for the rest of the program's lifetime.
author: mihe

body:
> If you're calling methods on PhysicsServer*D from a thread other than the main thread during physics processing, you will now just queue up those operations endlessly until the physics command queue has resumed its processing. This could maybe lead to out-of-memory scenarios.

This is surely just a cost of using the queues to deal with multithreading. I don't think it's unreasonable for any queue to contain a frame / ticks worth of commands. The emphasis should be on keeping the commands compact for this reason.

Maybe there's some specific situation you were thinking of?

Essentially imo the need to prevent race conditions is more important than the need to conserve memory, unless there is some specific that precludes this.
author: lawnjelly

body:
> This is surely just a cost of using the queues to deal with multithreading. I don't think it's unreasonable for any queue to contain a frame / ticks worth of commands. The emphasis should be on keeping the commands compact for this reason.

Yeah, actually, thinking about this some more, you likely run into this exact problem with non-main threads when `physics/*d/run_on_separate_thread` is left at its default of `false` anyway, since those commands would only ever get flushed at `PhysicsServer*DWrapMT::sync`, so nevermind I guess.
author: mihe

body:
I should point out one potential "optimization" to this PR, which is that the macros in `server_wrap_mt_common.h` that call `CommandQueueMT::push` don't need the same `ASYNC_COMMAND_COND` that the synchronizing macros do, meaning the ones that call `CommandQueueMT::push_and_ret` and `CommandQueueMT::push_and_sync`.

Changing the `CommandQueueMT::push` macros to instead queue the commands like normal would mean that you could theoretically still get the benefit of deferring those costs to the physics thread (i.e. after `PhysicsServer*DWrapMT::end_sync` is called).

The "problem" with this optimization is that the synchronizing macros then become a serious performance footgun, since we're still forced to flush the queue (on the main thread) when any of those methods are called, in order to keep the order of operations. So just adding a single `CharacterBody3D` with its default template script could mean that its `move_and_slide` call ends up being very expensive indeed (because of its call to `PhysicsServer3D::body_get_collision_layer`) depending on how many commands were deferred until that call. So this would essentially be another manifestation of #94205, albeit potentially much worse, since we wouldn't have the benefit of a physics thread that can process some of the commands before the flush/synchronization happens.

So I figured it's better to just execute everything on the main thread immediately during physics processing, to have more predictable performance.
author: mihe

body:
> So this would essentially be another manifestation of #94205, albeit potentially much worse, since we wouldn't have the benefit of a physics thread that can process some of the commands before the flush/synchronization happens.

After thinking about this some more, this is a weak argument against this optimization, since the usage of `PhysicsServer3D::body_get_collision_layer` in `move_and_slide` shouldn't be happening in the first place.

I've gone ahead and pushed this optimization after all, which should hopefully make the performance impact of this PR less noticeable. I've also pushed a separate PR (#109607) to fix #94205 properly, which means `move_and_slide` shouldn't cause any more problems with `physics/*d/run_on_separate_thread`.
author: mihe

body:
> this can only be a stopgap solution as this is closer to a hack than a fix

Just to be clear, since I didn't add this to the PR description since my last push, we are still offloading non-read/non-sync tasks to the physics thread with this approach, but now they're instead deferred until the end of the physics processing. So me saying that we are now "effectively disabling `physics/*d/run_on_separate_thread` during the physics processing part of the main loop" is not entirely true.

Seeing as how much of the physics cost comes from the state synchronization (i.e. `flush_queries`) and the physics step, neither of which are affected by this change, I wouldn't expect *that* much of a performance impact from this. It's only if you're doing expensive (or many) physics server calls, like changing the shape data of complex collision shapes, that I would expect to see an impact, since we're now taking away a slice of the frame for the physics thread to do its thing.

So while this PR is still a bit heavy-handed in its approach, I wouldn't necessarily consider it an urgent code smell to get rid off, but perhaps I'll be proven wrong by some future issue reports.

body:
> I think we would need to expose the stencil buffer as a post-process texture to get ideal results

I did some research when making a stencil demo for 4.5 release page and that's how stencils are usually used in production anyway, there are very few practical things that can be achieved by reading stencils in surface shaders, even the current outline preset that's gonna ship in 4.5 is very limiting because it doesn't really work with hard normals

Depending on whether or not somebody familiar with rendering wants to work on this for 4.6 it might be best to wait for that to be merged first to make sure editor outlines use the best possible implementation that scales well to large selections
author: passivestar

body:
>Depending on whether or not somebody familiar with rendering wants to work on this for 4.6 it might be best to wait for that to be merged first to make sure editor outlines use the best possible implementation that scales well to large selections

If it does get sufficiently implemented I wouldn't mind reworking this to use a stencil solution, but otherwise this current solution is pretty contained, and any kind of rework should be relatively painless assuming we have whatever relevant rendering API exposed. 

I can support whatever the decision is.
author: ryevdokimov

body:
> > I think we would need to expose the stencil buffer as a post-process texture to get ideal results
> 
> I did some research when making a stencil demo for 4.5 release page and that's how stencils are usually used in production anyway, there are very few practical things that can be achieved by reading stencils in surface shaders, even the current outline preset that's gonna ship in 4.5 is very limiting because it doesn't really work with hard normals

What you are describing is a technique called "software stencils" which is basically just using a regular texture to store arbitrary data. What we expose in Godot 4.5 is "hardware stencils" which are very inflexible, but a lot faster than software stencils. 

What this PR does is implement software stencils using a Viewport and then it does the post processing in a Canvasitem shader. So its actually already doing the technique that you suggest!

We have no plans to expose a readable stencil buffer. But we will eventually support adding custom buffers to the opaque pass which will allow the exact same effects as software stencils (I.e. write to a stencil mask texture during rendering, read from that texture in later passes)

> Depending on whether or not somebody familiar with rendering wants to work on this for 4.6 it might be best to wait for that to be merged first to make sure editor outlines use the best possible implementation that scales well to large selections

This PR should target 4.6 in any case. But (as you can see above) I think the technique is totally fine and doesn't need to wait for any further rendering changes

Ideally, I would use <kbd>Space</kbd>/<kbd>Shift</kbd> for global up/down (matching Minecraft creative behavior), but we already use <kbd>Shift</kbd> as a speed modifier. Maybe <kbd>Space</kbd>/<kbd>Ctrl</kbd>, but <kbd>Ctrl</kbd> is a key you often press (e.g. to toggle snapping).

body: @Calinou I'm against adding R/F for global up/down because it defeats the purpose of unifying these controls across most 3D software. We could add Space/Ctrl to do this, though, that's a good idea. I don't think people use snapping while moving the camera, right? EDIT: PR updated.
author: aaronfranke

body: @passivestar @lander-vr I'd also appreciate your input on this PR in terms of UX :slightly_smiling_face:
author: Calinou

body:
I think this is fine, although you could have just 4 actions instead of 6:

- `freelook_up` <kbd>E</kbd>
- `freelook_down` <kbd>Q</kbd>
- `freelook_up_alt_axis` <kbd>Space</kbd>
- `freelook_down_alt_axis` <kbd>Ctrl</kbd>

The 2 new actions would also depend on `freelook_navigation_scheme` but move on the opposite axis between local/global. This approach would embrace the existence of `freelook_navigation_scheme` instead working around it with new local/global shortcuts. Another alternative would be to deprecate `freelook_navigation_scheme` and leave *only* explicit local/global shortcuts, but that would be a more intrusive change, not to mention that it also affects the `forward` vector and not just `up`, so that is probably not a good option

If you want to add `R/F` bindings by default to better match the expectations of users coming from other software you don't need extra actions for those either, you could just add them to the existing `freelook_up` and `freelook_down` actions alongside the already bound `Q/E`
author: passivestar

body:
@Calinou I updated the PR to fix Shift+F conflicting with the controls. Now, right-mouse-activated freelook allows users to press Shift and/or F without accidentally disabling freelook, and Shift+F without right mouse still works.

@passivestar The idea is to provide R/F and Space/Ctrl as dedicated keys that always perform these functions, while E/Q are configurable. This way users can configure what they want E/Q to do, without affecting R/F. If R/F were bound to the same actions as E/Q, this ability would not exist.
author: aaronfranke

body:
> This way users can configure what they want E/Q to do, without affecting R/F.

Do you expect the user to need both dedicated and scheme-dependent keys at the same time while using the editor and to use those 6 different keys? The `navigation_scheme` setting will probably at most be only ever changed once when the user first downloads godot
author: passivestar

body:
@passivestar Yes, because the point is to give users the freedom regardless of whatever navigation scheme they have selected. It is perfectly valid to want to do a different style of up/down movement independent of the editor setting, so that both up/down behaviors are available regardless of how the users configure the WASDQE keys to behave

Also, more importantly, I want to have consistency between applications. Having these exist on separate keys (R/F and Space/Ctrl) allows users to always press those keys to always get that behavior regardless of application *and* configuration.

> It's worth noting that compilers can be pretty opaque, and the reduction may come as a result of context rather than it not inlining these specific if clauses. 3kb is certainly small enough of a difference that I wouldn't be confident it's a reliable save.

body: The instance is not always guaranteed to be a `CollisionObject*D`, e.g. with tile maps, grid maps, CSG, or manual use of the physics server. I agree we should get rid of the sync somehow though.
author: rburing

body:
> The instance is not always guaranteed to be a `CollisionObject*D`, e.g. with tile maps, grid maps, CSG, or manual use of the physics server. I agree we should get rid of the sync somehow though.

Right, good point. I guess there's technically `SoftBody3D` as well, but I'm not sure if those are relevant for floor/wall collisions, or even hit by `body_test_motion` at all.

Anyway, that's very annoying, and a bit of a showstopper.

There's gotta be something else we can do to avoid this, other than tacking on more arbitrary data in `MotionResult` or `PhysicsDirectBodyState*D`.
author: mihe

body:
The manual use of the physics server use-case really kills pretty much anything other than `MotionResult` or `PhysicsDirectBodyState*D` though.

Adding another getter to `PhysicsDirectBodyState*D` is arguably the lesser evil of the two, but I'm not super happy about that, as that class has until now largely just been about `_integrate_forces` stuff, and collision layer/mask doesn't feel very force integration-y.

It's not the end of the world though, I guess, and while it sets a questionable precedent, I don't think it's worth keeping this synchronization around to avoid it, so I'll go ahead and just do it.

The concern you (@rburing) had [here](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/94205#issuecomment-2227314490) about the `!Thread::is_main_thread()` check is presumably moot anyway, since we've been calling `body_get_direct_state` from `move_and_slide` for years now. The only place I see that being a real problem is if you use the thread groups feature, but we have plenty of other nodes that break with that (e.g. `RayCast*D`, `ShapeCast*D`).
author: mihe

body:
There, the collision layer and mask are now exposed through `PhysicsDirectBodyState*D` and `move_and_slide` makes use of those getters instead of going through `PhysicsServer*D`.

I'd appreciate a review, if/when you can find the time, @rburing.

Keep in mind that using `PhysicsDirectBodyState*D` in `move_and_slide` is not strictly thread-safe as-is, and should ideally be merged along with #109591, but the call to `get_velocity_at_local_position` in `move_and_slide` is already suffering from this same problem, so we're not making things any worse really.
author: mihe

body: Since current GDExtension physics servers don't implement this (yet) on their direct body state, can we expose the property and then add GDExtension compatibility methods that fall back to syncing with `body_get_collision_layer`?
author: rburing

body:
> can we expose the property and then add GDExtension compatibility methods that fall back to syncing with `body_get_collision_layer`?

I was just about to say that none of the other methods are properties, but I had of course completely forgot that most of them are in fact bound as properties through `_bind_methods`, so that needed to be done either way.

That would presumably mean that we also need to invoke the getter as a property, meaning we'd be calling `Object::get`? That seems a little bit off to me.

There is another way of achieving the same thing, which is to not use the `EXBIND*` macros in `PhysicsDirectBodyState*DExtension` and just do the `GDVIRTUAL` declarations manually, along with a `GDVIRTUAL_IS_OVERRIDDEN` check that falls back on `PhysicsServer*D::body_get_collision_layer`. That could then be compiled out with `DISABLE_DEPRECATED` in favor of the normal `EXBIND*` stuff.

Both would be a bit odd-looking, but maybe the latter is preferable?
author: mihe

body:
+1 for the latter, sounds fine to me. I'll approve afterward.

You could add me as coauthor if you want, but no big deal either way.
author: rburing

body:
I pushed the method/property bindings.

However, it seems there's a bigger problem with this GDExtension compatibility stuff, which is that we don't have the `RID` for the body whose `PhysicsDirectBodyState*D` we're dealing with, in something like `PhysicsDirectBodyState*DExtension`, which we need to call `PhysicsServer*D::body_get_collision_layer`. So whatever "dynamic dispatch" we'd want to do would need to happen inside of `CharacterBody*D::_set_platform_data`, where we actually have the `RID`.

I'm not sure if it's actually possible from outside the class to check whether that particular instance of `PhysicsDirectBodyState*D` has overridden a particular method.

EDIT: Added you as co-author.
author: mihe

body: Ah, right. I think (if not `DISABLE_DEPRECATED`) we could cast to `PhysicsDirectBodyState3DExtension` and use the `GDVIRTUAL_IS_OVERRIDDEN_PTR` macro to check for an overridden method from outside. It becomes a bit cursed-looking though.

There's also `FixedVector`?

But I forgot to say yesterday, it may still be better to use `alloca` for small sizes as you may be over-allocating a lot of bloat for these fixed arrays (although it depends whether the stack blowing is due to the number of calls to the routine or the number of elements).

Essentially, I'm not altogether convinced of the argument for fixed array here over `alloca` (it may not matter of course), maybe you can make this clear the argument.
author: lawnjelly

body:
> it depends whether the stack blowing is due to the number of calls to the routine or the number of elements

In the case of the bug that brought this to the forefront, I believe it's the number of elements, not the number of calls.

> Essentially, I'm not altogether convinced of the argument for fixed array here over `alloca` (it may not matter of course), maybe you can make this clear the argument.

I'm happy to go either way! I took current route because it was suggested at the core team meeting. But I'm not personally aware of any additional implications of `alloca()` that make it preferable to avoid entirely, over just using it carefully.
author: dsnopek

body:
> In the case of the bug that brought this to the forefront, I believe it's the number of elements, not the number of calls.

In this particular case then it's probably largely academic, and not a hill to die on either way. :+1: 

BTW I did have a little look at the relative costs.

Costs processing wise are pretty similar between `alloca` and a fixed array afaik .. `alloca` can do a small calculation for how much to increment the stack, but nothing worth worrying about in the context of such a function, and will use the precise memory needed on the stack rather than wasting space (more chance of overflows, possibility of being less cache friendly).

author: Repiteo

body:
As mentioned on [the contributors chat](https://chat.godotengine.org/channel/buildsystem?msg=bWmkvbai5aPrc9fuB), that CI run of `Test Godot project` with ThreadSanitizer enabled resulted in around 40 reports of various race conditions, but for some reason the test is still considered to have run successfully. ThreadSanitizer is supposed to set the exit code to 66 when something has been reported.

It could be that `TSAN_OPTIONS=halt_on_error=1` is needed. It seems like a good thing to set either way. If you look at the timestamps in the logs you'll see that it spent roughly 13 minutes just reporting those ThreadSanitizer warnings, which I suspect is due to the time spent symbolicating all the stack traces.
author: mihe

body: Looks like `halt_on_error` is an option multiple sanitizers can use; will try applying that and seeing what comes up
author: Repiteo

body:
It looks like that did the trick, and apparently surfaced some UBSan errors in one of the other tests as well.

Looking at past logs for that `Linux / Editor with doubles and GCC sanitizers` job, it looks like we have quite a few UBSan errors that have gone unnoticed.
author: mihe

body: That certainly fixed the issue of not getting an error, but I'm not 100% sure if that's the ideal solution. I mean, it's obviously good that a non-zero exit code is now properly returned, but I wonder if that can be accomplished *without* the early exit
author: Repiteo

body:
Would we actually want it to continue though, especially if it's as slow as it seems to be with printing these reports?

Note that you can also pass a suppression file using `TSAN_OPTIONS=suppressions=some_file.txt`. For whatever reason I can't find up-to-date docs for the TSan suppression format, but as best I can tell it hasn't changed all that much from [this](https://github.com/google/sanitizers/wiki/ThreadSanitizerSuppressions) old documentation. You'll likely want `print_suppressions=1` then as well, which will make it print all the matched suppressions at the end.

UBSan does [actually document](https://clang.llvm.org/docs/UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer.html#issue-suppression) their suppression format though. Although that documentation makes it sound like these runtime suppressions don't always work for some reason.
author: mihe

body:
We actually do have some suppression files on the repo, but they don't seem to have been updated in a while

More pertinently, I've realized why error codes are inconsistent. It comes down to:
1. The CI checking action ignoring Godot's exit code status (???)
2. Running tests will override whatever exit code a sanitizer would've output
author: Repiteo

body: Looks like the tests are now back to being reported as having been successful, despite reporting UBSan/TSan errors.

author: Lielay9

body:
I've battle tested the feature long enough to be certain it works as expected and enables sorting meshes in a way that is better for sprites in 2.5D games. This is the list of changes since the draft:

Change names from `alpha_sort_heuristic`  to `transparency_sort_mode`. There are functions already named "alpha sort" that are short for "alphabetic sorting", and there exists a precedence for calling it "transparency sorting" e.g. SpriteBase3D documentation:
>It can display translucent areas, but **transparency sorting** issues may be visible when multiple transparent materials are overlapping.

The custom axis sorting now uses the `AABB` support instead of center similar to orthogonal. Even more accurate heuristics exist, like sorting based on how many other AABBs the AABB is shadowed by, but at large they're rather slow (slower than doing OIT).

author: Swarkin

body:
> - Non-mouse events (like keypresses)

This is something I noticed if we limit the number of processed events per frame to 1. We should make it so only mouse motion events are considered in the limit, but I'm not sure how.
author: Calinou

body:
> Wouldn't limiting the number of processed events cause some inputs to get dropped?

It should not drop anything, just delay it to the next frames.
author: bruvzg

body:
> It should not drop anything, just delay it to the next frames.

That would cause more problems. The input queue would just keep getting bigger and bigger and more backed up until the user stops moving the mouse. I've seen this sort of thing happen before with the brush tool in graphics programs even with just a 250hz mouse polling rate.

For a typical "gamer" setup imagine the application is running its event loop V-Synced on a 240hz monitor, with an 8000hz mouse polling rate. Every frame we have about 33 input events coming in. If we process 5 of those and leave the rest, that's 28 left in the queue. Then we add another 33 input events on top of that. So every frame the queue grows by 28 events, and over one second at 240 FPS that will balloon to 6720 events. That's nearly a second's worth of delayed input and rapidly increasing.

Since most people buy high polling rate mice for smoother and more responsive movement (even if it's somewhat placebo on the high end), we need to be careful about how we skip incoming input events. Drops in FPS are bad, but incorrect input handling is arguably much worse.
author: KeyboardDanni

body:
> The input queue would just keep getting bigger and bigger and more backed up until the user stops moving the mouse.

According to https://ph3at.github.io/posts/Windows-Input/ which is where this idea comes from, it won't, but this is something that should be carefully tested. Raw input events are filtered out and removed from queue by next buffered input call all at once, so it's limit only for non input events.
author: bruvzg

body:
> That would cause more problems. The input queue would just keep getting bigger and bigger and more backed up until the user stops moving the mouse. I've seen this sort of thing happen before with the brush tool in graphics programs even with just a 250hz mouse polling rate. I didn't encounter this issue in my testing (see the videos where you can see the precise number of InputEventMouseMotions that Godot "sees" in real time). I've also tested this at lower FPS caps like 30 and 60 and couldn't notice an issue either. > Since most people buy high polling rate mice for smoother and more responsive movement (even if it's somewhat placebo on the high end), we need to be careful about how we skip incoming input events. Drops in FPS are bad, but incorrect input handling is arguably much worse. To clarify, most of the benefit from a high polling rate mouse is in jitter reduction, rather than latency reduction or smoothness (when the polling rate exceeds the display refresh rate). The potential of latency reduction is limited by the law of diminishing returns; the time between polls goes from 1 ms in 1,000 Hz to 0.125 ms in 8,000 Hz, so you only save 0.875 ms of latency *at most* (half of that on average). As this image shows:
You can reproduce the scenario here by moving your mouse at a speed that's as constant as possible, recording a slow motion video (or relying on your eye persistence), then looking at the gaps between each cursor. On my display in 480 Hz mode, there's a clear improvement in jitter reduction between 1,000 Hz and 2,000 Hz polling. There's a small difference between 2,000 Hz and 4,000 Hz polling, and a tiny (almost unnoticeable) difference between 4,000 and 8,000 Hz.

Rebased and tested again, it works as expected. I've updated the first MRP to implement @firebelley's suggested peak mspf counter.

However, there's an issue I recently noticed that I'm not sure how to fix. When you move the mouse quickly, some unrelated events like keyboard presses will act strangely, repeating themselves on certain occasions. This is noticeable in the `InputRepro2` testing project if you press <kbd>X</kbd> to toggle input accumulation while moving the mouse. The issue will occur more often if you lower `MAX_EVENTS_PER_FRAME` in `display_server_windows.cpp`. Setting it to `1` and running the project with **Max FPS** set to `10` in the Project Settings is a surefire way to make it happen.

In this case, holding down <kbd>X</kbd> will toggle input accumulation every frame, and if you moved the mouse after this, it will keep moving on its own as you press and release a key. I'm not sure how we can distinguish keyboard from mouse input in the function that calls `TranslateMessage()` and `DispatchMessageW()`.

@bruvzg Any ideas? I've tried to create a separate `peekKeysMouseButtons()` lambda that is limited by a separate `MAX_EVENTS_PER_FRAME` counter (see the second commit), but it didn't resolve the issue. Also, I have yet to find a solution for the captured mouse cursor position appearing to move for 1 frame after clicking or pressing a key, although the issue may be benign in practice as you normally don't see the mouse cursor position when captured.
author: Calinou
vorvek

body:
@vorvek Thanks for testing and investigating the captured cursor issue! I've integrated the fix in the PR, and it seems to work better overall (tested on 1,000 Hz and 8,000 Hz polling rates). That said, the cursor will still sporadically move when clicking or using the mouse wheel in captured mouse mode (but at least the hardware cursor isn't visible anymore).

Freelook now works in the 3D editor, but only when holding the right mouse button. Pressing <kbd>Shift + F</kbd> to toggle freelook will instantly exit freelook due to the key combination being triggered twice in a row.
author: Calinou
vorvek

body:
I re-checked and noticed some other issues that weren't covered by the PR:

1) **The buffered read doesn't drain the queue.** With a null buffer, `GetRawInputBuffer()` returns the byte size of the *first* pending message in `pcbSize`, not a message count ([docs](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/winuser/nf-winuser-getrawinputbuffer)). So `dw_size = n_buffer * sizeof(RAWINPUT)` holds roughly `sizeof(RAWINPUT)` messages (about 48 mouse events on x64), and `process_raw_input()` only reads once per frame. An 8,000 Hz mouse at 60 FPS queues ~133 events per frame, so there's a leftover every frame during fast movement. In this PR the leftovers survive to the next frame because the `PeekMessage()` filters skip `WM_INPUT`, but they pile up as a backlog that adds latency exactly when the mouse moves fast. This is more or less the backlog @KeyboardDanni was worried about, just caused by the buffer sizing rather than the pump cap. The docs say to call it "several times ... until all raw input messages have been read", so I wrapped the read in a loop that runs until the queue reports empty.

On my fork it was worse than latency: I pump the queue unfiltered, so the leftover `WM_INPUT` messages got dispatched into the now-empty `WM_INPUT` case and freed unread. That dropped most of the captured-mode motion in the 8k @ 60 FPS scenario. Easy to miss at 1,000 Hz because everything fits in a single read.

2) **The no-op `WM_INPUT` case can still eat events.** Anything that arrives between the `GetRawInputBuffer()` call and the end of the message pump gets dispatched to the empty `WM_INPUT` handler, and `DefWindowProc` frees the data unread. In this PR that only happens through the unfiltered `PeekMessage()` used when `app_focused` is false, so it's rarer, but it's the same kind of loss. I restored the old `GetRawInputData()` handling there as a fallback. It only sees the handful of stragglers the batch read missed, so the per-message flood this PR fixes can't come back through it.

3) **WOW64.** The same docs page has a note that a 32-bit process on 64-bit Windows has to use `*pcbSize * 8` and align the `RAWINPUT` structures by 8 bytes, otherwise the buffer walk reads garbage. Doesn't affect me since my fork only ships x86_64, but the official x86_32 Windows templates would hit it, so it probably needs handling before this can merge.

4) **`process_raw_input()` hardcodes `MAIN_WINDOW_ID`**, while `_set_mouse_mode_impl()` captures the mouse for `_get_focused_window_or_popup()`. If a secondary window captures the mouse, the recentering and the relative math run against the wrong window. The old `WM_INPUT` path used the actual receiving window, so this is a small regression. I switched the buffered path to `_get_focused_window_or_popup()` with a main-window fallback.

I've opened Calinou/godot#20 against this PR's branch with the fixes for 1, 2 and 4.
author: vorvek

body:
Web seems to be extremely buggy:
- all controllers register as "Standard controller"
- d-pad doesn't work
- mapping for face buttons are wrong
- triggers register only binary states and not as full axis
- vibration doesn't work
- there's high input lag, and some presses aren't registered at all

body:
When creating this PR, I was thinking that it could be used at runtime so that projects could implement a texture quality setting in a settings menu so that developers can target a wider range of hardware. I know as a quick hack, you could set the mipmap bias. The problem is that the full texture is still loaded into memory.

Maybe a better solution would be to have the importer write multiple ctex files at different resolutions and then pick the appropriate one at runtime?
author: LizzyTB

body:
> When creating this PR, I was thinking that it could be used at runtime so that projects could implement a texture quality setting in a settings menu so that developers can target a wider range of hardware. I know as a quick hack, you could set the mipmap bias. The problem is that the full texture is still loaded into memory.
> 
> Maybe a better solution would be to have the importer write multiple ctex files at different resolutions and then pick the appropriate one at runtime?

The aforementioned feature doesn't just set the mipmap bias, it forces the file reader to skip to later parts of the data in the file, then treats the following mipmap as the base image (with a lower resolution than the actual image).

For proper runtime texture quality changes, a more robust solution such as texture streaming is necessary.

Having multiple .ctex files will take up a lot of space in exported projects. Having separate resolution settings per platform/export preset seems like an alternative solution: https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/10034

body:
This PR doesn't have quite the right approach I'd prefer on the long run.

- The final solution shouldn't involve the DEBUG_ENABLED macro. Debug editor builds should be able to generate release templates perfectly fine, and this solution completely breaks the debug editor being able to use the shader baker with a release template. Even if the command is not passed, the hash itself will always be different from a DEBUG_ENABLED build or not.
- We don't want to avoid smolv compression under DEBUG_ENABLED, we only need to disable it if we're generating debug information explicitly with the command. A lot of us always work only under debug builds and avoiding such a core functionality for it to only be possible in release is not good.
- Embedding the debug information in the hash is completely fine, but it shouldn't be based conditionally on the DEBUG ENABLED macro. It should always be there and be based on whether the command line option was passed.
- The one caveat to this solution I propose is that shader baking should explicitly fail if the command line option was passed or we should make this an argument to the shader hash instead as well as the shader container so the shader baker can work regardless.

If this seems like out of scope of what you originally wanted to do, let me know and I can tackle it on a PR instead. But as it is, the solution breaks more than I'd like and I don't think it can be merged as is, especially with DEBUG_ENABLED basically breaking the shader baker.

Running this code 10 times in dev_build has proved that the single value constructor was faster for all cases.

```c++
int time = Time::get_singleton()->get_ticks_msec();
for (int i = 0; i < 100000000; i++) {
	const real_t radius2 = Math::sqrt(double(1685.58)) + 0.0001;
	Vector2 vec2 = Vector2(radius2, radius2);
}
print_line("Temporary Variable Elimination Time Before: " + itos(Time::get_singleton()->get_ticks_msec() - time));

time = Time::get_singleton()->get_ticks_msec();
for (int i = 0; i < 100000000; i++) {
	Vector2 vec2 = Vector2(Math::sqrt(double(1685.58)) + 0.0001);
}
print_line("Temporary Variable Elimination Time After: " + itos(Time::get_singleton()->get_ticks_msec() - time));

time = Time::get_singleton()->get_ticks_msec();
for (int i = 0; i < 100000000; i++) {
	Size2i vec2 = Size2i(Math::SQRT13, Math::SQRT13) * 4;
}
print_line("Redundant Operation Elimination Time Before: " + itos(Time::get_singleton()->get_ticks_msec() - time));

time = Time::get_singleton()->get_ticks_msec();
for (int i = 0; i < 100000000; i++) {
	Size2i vec2 = Size2i(Math::SQRT13 * 4);
}
print_line("Redundant Operation Elimination Time After: " + itos(Time::get_singleton()->get_ticks_msec() - time));

time = Time::get_singleton()->get_ticks_msec();
for (int i = 0; i < 100000000; i++) {
	Vector2 vec2 = Vector2(1, 1) * 45.5 * 48.89;
}
print_line("Dummy Vector Scaling Elimination Time Before: " + itos(Time::get_singleton()->get_ticks_msec() - time));

time = Time::get_singleton()->get_ticks_msec();

	Vector2 vec2 = Vector2(45.5 * 48.89);
}
print_line("Dummy Vector Scaling Elimination Time After: " + itos(Time::get_singleton()->get_ticks_msec() - time));

body: The title is definitely misleading: this isn't about contrusting a Vector2 with ints or a Vector2i with floats—this is about adding a contructor which accepts a *single scalar argument*
author: Repiteo

body:
I believe if this functionality is added, it would be better expressed as a named constructor:
```gdscript
var v = Vector3.splat(4)  # (4, 4, 4)
```
This is more explicit and can be immediately recognized.
It also prevents bugs:
```gdscript
# Used to be 3D, but game logic can be done in 2D -> now migrated
var a: Vector2 = ...
var b: Vector2 = ...

# Forgot to update that 2D cross is float, not vector
var v = a.cross(b)

var tile_coords = Vector2i(v / TILE_SIZE)
# currently: error because float cannot be converted to Vector2i
# with this PR: silently works but yields garbage
```
Note that the above code isn't even dynamically typed -- in those cases it's even more important to highlight "unusual" operations by making them more explicit.
author: Bromeon

body: To me `Vector2(0.0)` is more intuitive, coming from shader programming for example. Aside from performance this constructor is also nice for brevity (more notable with Vector3, which I hope will be added in a follow up PR) and `splat()` would remove that benefit.
author: F3der1co

body:
> I believe if this functionality is added, it would be better expressed as a named constructor:

Unfortunately, the named constructor ended up being slower in some cases. I had hoped it would work since it’s easier to implement and maintains compatibility. See: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/109692#issuecomment-3194481649.
author: WhalesState

body:
> Unfortunately, the named constructor ended up being slower in some cases

You ran the benchmarks in `dev_build` (i.e. [for debugging, not optimized](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/contributing/development/compiling/introduction_to_the_buildsystem.html#introduction-to-the-buildsystem)), and the results are immediately thrown away. A compiler is allowed to completely optimize out those statements, as the observable behavior would be the same. On a C++ level, a constructor and static method call shouldn't have different performance (in optimized builds), especially not if they're inline.

I'm not sure about the performance of the two in the GDScript VM; it's possible that there are differences. But if that's the case, then that's a perf problem that should be addressed independently of this addition, as there are many static methods that would benefit. In other words, I don't think that alone is a good reason to go for the shorter syntax.
author: Bromeon

body: The slowdown came from using `Math::sqrt` while it's not a `constexpr`, replacing it with `std::sqrt` or making `Math::sqrt` a `constexpr` has resulted in making the same check running 5x faster. That's why the compiler failed to optimize it.

body:
* Closing in favor of https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/109687, which already has an associated proposal: https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/3884.

* We can revisit this if a new proposal is made. For now, I think it’s better to stick with constructors since they’re faster in `GDScript` and avoid extra static methods that mostly make the code longer. The static version only showed benefits when eliminating temporary variables.

* If someone can show that it performs just as well as using the constructor directly, we can reconsider after running performance comparisons in both C++ and `GDScript`, with and without optimized builds.

author: mooflu

body:
Ideally, we'd be rendering GUI items with the premultiplied alpha blend mode instead, but changing the default blend mode for CanvasItemMaterial used in Control is probably too big of a change by now. We did manage to do something similar for font LCD subpixel optimizations, but you probably aren't applying shaders to them *directly*.

Can't we get ThorVG to return an image that's good right out of the gate, i.e. have it perform its own premultiplied alpha?
author: Calinou

body:
> Ideally, we'd be rendering GUI items with the premultiplied alpha blend mode instead, but changing the default blend mode for CanvasItemMaterial used in Control is probably too big of a change by now. We did manage to do something similar for font LCD subpixel optimizations, but you probably aren't applying shaders to them _directly_.
> 
> Can't we get ThorVG to return an image that's good right out of the gate, i.e. have it perform its own premultiplied alpha?

Yes, thorvg can output premult alpha.

Proposal:

1. add parameter premult_alpha=false to ImageLoaderSVG::create_image_from_utf8_buffer and friends to tell thorvg to output premult-alpha or not 
2. add process/premult_alpha to svg importer (default to false) - if enabled user will need to use BLEND_MODE_PREMULT_ALPHA
3. add process/fix_alpha_border_radius to svg importer (default 0 i.e. off); also need to add radius param to fix_alpha_edges OR add process/fix_alpha_border (default to false) - to be consistent with import as Texture2D which does not expose radius
5. Add settings for premult_alpha (2) and fix_alpha_border_radius (3) to SVGTexture
6. Change SVGTexture::create_from_string in order to enable fix_alpha_edges to be used for default theme icons (these are generally small, so performance impact of fix_alpha_edges should not be an issue)

Thoughts?
author: mooflu

body:
Removing this flag introduces an issue with `Input.warp_mouse`.

This script warps the mouse upon LMB release, and continuously prints mouse position (according to `Viewport.get_mouse_position`).

```gdscript
extends Node3D

var pressed: bool

func _process(_dt: float) -> void:
	if Input.is_mouse_button_pressed(MOUSE_BUTTON_LEFT):
		pressed = true
	else:
		if pressed:
			pressed = false
			Input.warp_mouse(Vector2(100, 100))
			print('RELEASED')
			
	print(get_viewport().get_mouse_position())
```

This PR:
```bash
(459.0, 93.5)
RELEASED
(437.0, 94.5) # should be (100, 100)
(437.0, 94.5)
# ... now after I move the mouse:
(99.0, 98.5)
```

master - with the flag a similar issue exists, but it "fixes itself" after a couple of frames:
```bash
(612.0, 284.0)
RELEASED
(612.0, 284.0)
(612.0, 284.0)
(612.0, 284.0)
(96.0, 94.0) # (no mouse move, it fixed itself)
```

(Note that in the `master` example I manually transformed the position to make it easier to compare. The position is returned in global screen coordinates, and with negative Y, which is what this PR fixes)

If you play it slower you can see that the rectangle stays in the old spot for a couple of frames.

I'm not against named constants, but things like `Math::sqrt(2.0)` will already be optimized out by compiler (but not in `DEV_ENABLED`, which can be a consideration sometimes).

https://godbolt.org/z/d7f73M4qh
author: lawnjelly

body:
If you have a look at the assembly, I don't think you'll see any difference before / after for non DEV builds.

Generally for this kind of optimization, we'd want to see assembly, and profiling / benchmarking.
author: lawnjelly

body:
> If you have a look at the assembly, I don't think you'll see any difference before / after for non DEV builds.
> 
> Generally for this kind of optimization, we'd want to see assembly, and profiling / benchmarking.

Since `Math::sqrt` is not `constexpr`, calls to it cannot be evaluated at compile time. Using `std::sqrt` directly may produce slightly different results. If there were truly no differences, then the existence of predefined constants like `Math::SQRT2` would be unnecessary, as all values would always be computed at runtime.

Either way, the now removed error-checking is clearly redundant, and it's possible that the `EditorHelp::_wait_for_thread` call in `EditorHelp::update_doc` does in fact resolve this somehow, but this definitely needs to be tested by someone who can reproduce this error more reliably than I can.

I've produced a set of Android APKs for performance testing: https://0x0.st/Kwkb.zip

Included within:

- `master` Mobile
- `master` Compatibility
- This PR Mobile
- This PR Compatibility

They have separate package names, so you can install all 4 APKs alongside on the same device.

Project source: [test_lightmap_directional_specular_lobe.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24042414/test_lightmap_directional_specular_lobe.zip) (same as before, but with a FPS counter added and camera angle modified to ensure as many pixels as possible are showing lightmapped materials)

On my Galaxy S25 Ultra, I get a locked 120 FPS with all four APKs, so testing with older or lower-end devices is appreciated.

I've done some benchmarking on desktop as well:

<details open>

- **CPU:** AMD Ryzen 9 9950X3D
- **GPU:** NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090
- **RAM:** 64 GB (2×32 GB DDR5-6000 CL30)
- **SSD:** Solidigm P44 Pro 2 TB
- **OS:** Linux (Fedora 43)
</details>

Frametime impact on the GPU in 3840×2160 compared to `master`:

- **Forward+:** +0.02 mspf
- **Mobile:** +0.03 mspf
- **Compatibility:** +0.02 mspf

body:
See Lawnjelly's comment on the previous PR https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/108358#issuecomment-3043496689

I agree with lawnjelly. 

We shouldn't be making code less clear and adding git noise when there is no measurable benefit. The only reason to use squared distance is when the algorithm requires squared distance _or_ the performance benefit justifies it. 

Changing from linear distance to squared distance is the opposite of good code hygiene, it is literally making the code harder to read and maintain.

body:
I modified the MRP from https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/99639 to use the name and it works fine. It is basically almost what i had in mind when i wrote that comment about a more convenient API. However i think the `EditorInterface::get_editor_plugin` method should just return null if it fails instead of creating an error so that it can be used to check the existence of a plugin in addition to providing its object - this can allow plugins to use other plugins as *optional* dependencies without errors (an alternative could be to add a `has_editor_plugin` method or something to that extent but i think the single method is better - though it could be renamed to `find_editor_plugin` instead so that its behavior is a bit more obvious and consistent with, e.g., `Node::find_child` which also returns null if a child is not found).

As a hypothetical example, a script that generates decal-like meshes from splines for roads, stripes, etc to be placed against the environment could also have optional dependencies on one or more terrain plugins that expose functionality to modify the terrain so that it can also provide UI to adjust the terrain to "match" the generated mesh but without having a hard dependency on any of them (e.g. one could use it just fine for urban environments, environments with a custom terrain system or environments whose terrain was made externally).

In any case, the following ZIP contains the MRP with the gridmap replacer script code modified to use `get_editor_plugin` and expose a name for itself and a second editor plugin that uses `get_editor_plugin` to access a function in the gridmap replacer script.

[gridmap-editor-scripting-test-with-editor-name.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22228020/gridmap-editor-scripting-test-with-editor-name.zip)

> Yeah, it's more equivalent of `NOTIFICATION_SCENE_INSTANTIATED` 🤔 I think it's irrelevant when creating new resources, because their exported variables are always "set".

On the contrary, it would be preferable if he were also notified for a created resource. I would use this method to connect signals that are emitted from exported property setters.

For example I am using the notification to set the flag to false
```gdscript
@export_custom(PROPERTY_HINT_ARRAY_TYPE, "ThingModule", PROPERTY_USAGE_DEFAULT) var modules: Array[ThingModule] = []:
	set(value):
		modules = value
		if not _is_deserialized:
			return
		module_changed.emit()
		for module in modules:
			if not is_instance_valid(module):
				continue
			if not module.property_list_changed.is_connected(module_changed.emit):
				module.property_list_changed.connect(module_changed.emit)
```

I could set the flag in the _init for resource creation but how can I differentiate a loaded resource vs a created resource ? Because at the point in time during the _init function the resource_path is not yet set.

But maybe it's out of scope of this PR and should be more like a NOTIFICATION_RESOURCE_READY notification ?

body: I looked how feasible it would be to make the notification received when resource is created and it's not really possible. When a resource is being loaded, a new instance is created before properties are assigned, and it's not possible to differentiate it from regular creation. Thus it would need to be a separate notification, so out of scope for this PR.
