body:
I tested this PR with my GDExtension use case, and it works great!

I have a custom resource created in GDExtension that needs to perform background loading and setup based on its stored properties right after it loads. This setup is required for the resource's other functions to work correctly.

Without this PR, the workaround required explicit initialization or lazy-loading, like this:
```
@export var test: CustomResource

func _enter_tree() -> void:
    test.setup()
    test.do_work()
```
Alternatively, I had to handle setup inside do_work() by tracking the initialization state under the hood.

I tried moving the setup to `NOTIFICATION_POSTINITIALIZE`, but the resource's properties aren't deserialized yet at that point. My workaround was to emit a signal from the setter of the last declared property in the Resource, connect to it in `NOTIFICATION_POSTINITIALIZE` and do the setup when the connected function was called.
This PR completely removes the need for that workaround. Now, I can cleanly handle the setup directly within `NOTIFICATION_RESOURCE_DESERIALIZED`.

body:
Imo you are still trying to do too much in one PR. The objective should be to make each PR the smallest unit it can be. These are unrelated changes.

* Changing double literals (`0.0`) to float literals (`0.0f`) is correct to do as it prevents promotion to 64 bit for 32 bit calculations, and will work fine with 64 bit, so is pretty easy to approve.
* Changing local variables to `const` is more dubious, in some cases this could just be needless code churn.

As an aside (being the one who brought up the whole problem of double literal promotion problem originally, but wasn't involved in the discussion to standardize this):

Imo using integers is more clear when using whole numbers (e.g. `x >= 0` rather than `x ->= 0.0f`), as the former works just as well and is less easy to get wrong, and easier to read. The `f` literal is only really necessary for fractions, `0.5f`. I think half the reason contributors _continue_ to make this mistake is because they aren't automatically using integer literals unless otherwise required.

_(Obviously with some exceptions such as where you need to promote integer calculation to float.)_
author: lawnjelly

body:
> Imo using integers is more clear when using whole numbers

I find it clearer to use float literals because when I see integers it's not clear if we're working with integers or floats. As a contrived example, if I see `if ((a - b) < 0)` in my own code, I expect both a and b to be integers, but if I see `if ((a - b) < 0.0f)` then I know at least one of those is a float (because if both are integers it makes no sense to write it that way). Another example, if I see `x / 2` my assumption is that this is integer division, and I would instead use `x / 2.0f` or `x * 0.5f` for floats.

body:
I was very surprised certain tests were failing and this is the explanation I finally figured out (PR updated to account for it):

Certain classes (e.g., `BaseMaterial3D`) call `notify_property_list_changed()` within their constructor, which emits a signal. In case the object is purely `memnew`-ed, to be later assigned to a `Ref`, the mechanism to preserve such object until the end of signal emission would be the first to wrap it in a `Ref`. That was problematic, because by the time the constructor ended and the main code tried to assign to `Ref`, the reference count would already be zero with the init ref flag being unset as well.

Therefore, I've updated the PR to account for that situation. I don't know why this sort of pathological case wasn't apparent before. It may be that the GLTF tests (the ones first triggering the issue) were written when the thread safe signals PR had already been merged (that's the PR inhibiting the logic of object preservation during signal). In any case, it all makes sense now and this PR does both restoring the functionality of the mechanism as well as preventing emissions from constructors to cause crashes.

P. S.: @dsnopek, in the end I've had to delete the safety line because there's actual code there now. Thanks anyway for noticing!

body:
> Is `GL_KHR_texture_compression_astc` related WEBGL with ASTC, like we have webgl with BPTC and ETC2?

`WEBGL_compressed_texture_astc` is the one used for WEBGL. 
According to https://opengles.gpuinfo.org/listreports.php?extension=GL_KHR_texture_compression_astc  `GL_KHR_texture_compression_astc` is only supported by 2 reported devices, so it's probably the vendors misunderstanding the extension's purpose.

Any PR opened here has to be processed, so opening a PR here should be done when wanting to get *our* attention on it, or getting feedback from us, but at minimum it should be communicated that the PR is just for feedback

If you need to do feedback on someone else's code I'd suggest having a PR in your own repos, or just looking at the commits directly

But generally please don't use this repo for internal review or feedback

body: Until I can get my hands on a macOS environment, I'll say the other clang-homebrew changes are out-of-scope for 4.5. Though I'd still like to get this merged, as it does address the erroneous change from gnu++ to c++, and it *technically* addresses the original issue (like a bandage on a broken leg)
author: Repiteo

body:
If you want to make it fully buildable add following changes as well:

```diff
diff --git a/drivers/metal/SCsub b/drivers/metal/SCsub
index b8347c7d7a..f0490ba7bf 100644
--- a/drivers/metal/SCsub
+++ b/drivers/metal/SCsub
@@ -1,5 +1,6 @@
 #!/usr/bin/env python
 from misc.utility.scons_hints import *
+from methods import is_apple_clang

 Import("env")

@@ -39,7 +40,8 @@ if "-std=gnu++17" in env_metal["CXXFLAGS"]:
 env_metal.Append(CXXFLAGS=["-std=gnu++20"])

 # Enable module support
-env_metal.Append(CCFLAGS=["-fmodules", "-fcxx-modules"])
+if env["platform"] != "macos" or is_apple_clang(env):
+    env_metal.Append(CCFLAGS=["-fmodules", "-fcxx-modules"])

 # Driver source files

diff --git a/drivers/metal/metal_objects.mm b/drivers/metal/metal_objects.mm
index 96ade55c1b..8928c87fe6 100644
--- a/drivers/metal/metal_objects.mm
+++ b/drivers/metal/metal_objects.mm
@@ -2031,11 +2031,11 @@ fragment ClearColorsOut fragClear(VaryingsPos varyings [[stage_in]], constant Cl
 }

 static const char *SHADER_STAGE_NAMES[] = {
-       [RD::SHADER_STAGE_VERTEX] = "vert",
-       [RD::SHADER_STAGE_FRAGMENT] = "frag",
-       [RD::SHADER_STAGE_TESSELATION_CONTROL] = "tess_ctrl",
-       [RD::SHADER_STAGE_TESSELATION_EVALUATION] = "tess_eval",
-       [RD::SHADER_STAGE_COMPUTE] = "comp",
+       "vert",
+       "frag",
+       "tess_ctrl",
+       "tess_eval",
+       "comp",
 };

 void ShaderCacheEntry::notify_free() const {
diff --git a/drivers/metal/rendering_device_driver_metal.mm b/drivers/metal/rendering_device_driver_metal.mm
index 9cf85db051..3e2bb67145 100644
--- a/drivers/metal/rendering_device_driver_metal.mm
+++ b/drivers/metal/rendering_device_driver_metal.mm
@@ -1819,14 +1819,14 @@ bool isArrayTexture(MTLTextureType p_type) {
        }

        static const MTLLoadAction LOAD_ACTIONS[] = {
-               [ATTACHMENT_LOAD_OP_LOAD] = MTLLoadActionLoad,
-               [ATTACHMENT_LOAD_OP_CLEAR] = MTLLoadActionClear,
-               [ATTACHMENT_LOAD_OP_DONT_CARE] = MTLLoadActionDontCare,
+               MTLLoadActionLoad,
+               MTLLoadActionClear,
+               MTLLoadActionDontCare,
        };

        static const MTLStoreAction STORE_ACTIONS[] = {
-               [ATTACHMENT_STORE_OP_STORE] = MTLStoreActionStore,
-               [ATTACHMENT_STORE_OP_DONT_CARE] = MTLStoreActionDontCare,
+               MTLStoreActionStore,
+               MTLStoreActionDontCare,
        };

        Vector<MDAttachment> attachments;
```
Tested to build and run with Xcode clang and homebrew LLVM 20 clang, not tested with OSXCross.

But I'm not sure if we should bother with supporting non-Apple clang at all.

body:
> This PR includes the changing of double literals to float literals, and using real_t consistently to avoid needless casting.

Isn't the casting done at compile time? Why using float literals even matter?
author: KoBeWi

body: @KoBeWi If you have `something.length() > 0.0` then it will cast `something.length()` to a double at runtime if Godot is compiled with `real_t` defined as `float`. However, `something.length() > 0.0f` has no runtime casts, because it's either float on both sides, or if Godot is compiled with doubles, `0.0f` is compile-time casted to double.

body: The original purpose of it is to allow multiple UI scales in multiple windows to support automatic scaling on different DPI displays (this feature will be implemented in 4.6), so it's pretty limited. And the `SVGTexture` name might create false expectations of a fully customisable vector graphics support, so we decided to rename it for now to bring less attention to the class itself, which might change a lot in the future.

author: JekSun97

body:
Is the SVG image format the only possible DPI-aware format that exists in the universe? DPITexture sounds like a broad term that may cover a bunch of use cases.
To me, the DPI Texture should be an abstract type, and implemented by the SVG Texture type, so that the team or the user can at least extend the type if needed.

If we ship the DPITexture as the current SVG implementation, I'm afraid we may never extend it to cover more vectorized use-cases in the future until 5.
author: Delsin-Yu

body:
> If we ship the DPITexture as the current SVG implementation, I'm afraid we may never extend it to cover more vectorized use-cases in the future until 5.

Your statement highlights why we changed the name. This was never meant to be a general purpose SVG rendering resource. It was only ever meant to be a way to have DPI aware icons and other GUI elements. 

We want to have a simple way of rendering vector graphics, but this is not the right way to do it. In the long run having an SVG texture that isn't intended to be used to render SVGs would be more confusing than making this change
author: clayjohn

body:
> Is the SVG image format the only possible DPI-aware format that exists in the universe? DPITexture sounds like a broad term that may cover a bunch of use cases.

There are other vector image formats out there (EPS, PDF, Adobe Illustrator, ...) but these are generally not used in games. However, it's likely technically possible to create an add-on to import those formats as DPITexture.

@clayjohn, It gives me the expectation that this Type can accept multiple different sources to achieve DPI-Awareness, such as selecting from an array of varying resolution Texture2Ds, e.g. [Windows App Icon](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/apps/design/style/iconography/app-icon-construction#app-icon), or I can extend this type to create procedurally generated texture such as DPI aware background with gradient and rounded corners (where in the reality it only works with SVGs)

author: lord-muad-dib

body:
It's part of EditorPlugin script template:
```GDScript
@tool
extends EditorPlugin


func _enable_plugin() -> void:
	# Add autoloads here.
	pass


func _disable_plugin() -> void:
	# Remove autoloads here.
	pass


func _enter_tree() -> void:
	# Initialization of the plugin goes here.
	pass


func _exit_tree() -> void:
	# Clean-up of the plugin goes here.
	pass
```
I'm not sure if there is a reasonable way to add a warning for this case. Adding singletons in `_enter_tree()` is also technically not wrong (i.e. it correctly adds the singleton), it's more a bad practice, because the singleton is registered in ProjectSettings and persists between editor sessions.

#define BIND_PROPERTY(property_name, class, variantType)                                                 \
	ClassDB::bind_method(D_METHOD("get_" #property_name), &class ::get_##property_name);                 \
	ClassDB::bind_method(D_METHOD("set_" #property_name, #property_name), &class ::set_##property_name); \

	ClassDB::bind_method(D_METHOD("set_" #property_name, #property_name), &class ::set_##property_name); \

author: AThousandShips

body:
> I've done that, but I wish I understood why the maintainers of this project forbid "squash and merge".

For several reasons, one is the workload on our production team (dozens of PRs is a *lot* to squash and make sure it was done right, considering squashing a single one can be annoying, imagine a dozen), another is that we want a clean history where we have a clear merge commit which means the PR is as is, without other changes, so it can be easily reverted, or cherry picked

We could use "squash and use merge commit" but GitHub doesn't allow that, we also have several maintainers and contributors who sign their commits because downstream consumers need that security, if we *only* used one type of merge method we'd lose that (squashed commits, or plain merged commits lose their sign information as it is recommitted), and having multiple means of merging things doesn't work with merge queues, and adds a burden to the production team
author: AThousandShips

author: Calinou

body:
> Can we enable texture repeat only if the region is also enabled?

Enabling repeat automatically with Regions would also affect it when using an Big Atlas and the region to limit it to a smaller one. If it's only enabled when the region is larger than the texture it's probably fine. But at that point that's a lot of convoluted logic to determine if repeat should be used or not.

> It's probably OK to add as an option, but since there are quite a lot of properties in SpriteBase3D already, it's likely better to figure out a sensible default automatically if we have a way to do it.

Given that SpriteBase3D is based on SpriteBase2D, where both of those settings exist in tandem (be it as a common setting of all CanvasItems), I don't see why only a single one would be included without the other. SpriteBase3D having too many properties is more a problem of organization i would say, as it doesn't use any categories or groups like 3d Materials displaying the same and even more settings.
author: HatAndTie

author: SanderVanhove

body: Just looking to bump this one as this is a fairly bad issue and has lay dormant for a while!
author: EMBYRDEV

body:
Can confirm that this fixes the issue when tested locally using the MRP.

The fix seems good:
- The peer's `recv_sync_ids` will contain the object if and only if the object was spawned by a `MultiplayerSpawner` owned by that peer.
- Similarly, the TrackedNode's `remote_peer` is only populated when spawned by a remote spawner.
- Therefore, if a TrackedNode has a `remote_peer`, then that peer's `recv_sync_ids` must contain the object, and vice versa.

It's worth mentioning that this doesn't impact synchronizers which aren't related to a spawned object, as those synchronizers use path-based lookup instead of `recv_sync_ids`. (I'm not sure why all synchronizers don't just use path-based lookup, it seems like it would simplify things?) Also this fix works with synchronizers that change authority.

As an aside: Another possible fix is to check against `E.key == sync->get_multiplayer_authority()` instead of `uint32_t(E.key) == tobj.remote_peer`. Currently, these two conditions are equivalent, but I've got a local branch that allows spawners to transfer authority, in which case the TrackedNode's `remote_peer` won't match the peer that spawned it. But as the code is currently, either condition works and fixes the issue.

body:
So, what is the intended method to choose where the window appears? Wayland isn't exactly super great for window positioning.

You can technically move the window position after it's open, but that's janky.

I suppose you could also maybe use a fullscreen window with passthrough then position within that window with a passthrough rect.

Maybe I'm just missing how the anchors for the layer are set, though...

I also can't figure out how to move the root window at runtime or before it appears... only subwindows...
This would probably be essential for a proper application launcher or other overlay tool that is started via a keybind since that root window will always exist afaik without more modifications to godot's source
author: Mervius

Hey! First, thanks for reminding me I need to work on my half-assed PR. I genuinely forgot it existed.

Currently, since Wayland uses an anchor+offsets method to position windows, I think I'm just translating window cartesian coordinates to anchor-offset. It's worked quite well to move windows, but also I don't think I actually have tried to move the main window.

I'll add that to my list of things to test and make better. I'm sure my addiction to Pacific Drive is temporary and will eventually subside, after which I'll work on this implementation again.

body:
I tested the PR as requested by @smix8, I tried the following:
- select tiles, press X, then move, then press ESC
- select tiles, press X, click to confirm, then CTRL+Z
- select tiles, press C, then move, then press ESC
- select tiles, press C, click to confirm, then CTRL+Z
I also checked that https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/108743 was still effective while performing these operations.

I can confirm that all the operations above are now working as expected; this PR fix https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/109860 and the broken Undo of cutting tiles.

Looking the code, the changes are minimal and to the point. There is a small redundancy between the undo code in `_do_paste()` and the cancel code in `_cancel_pending_move()`, but I am not sure how we could avoid it.

body: I think this is a good addition, I was actually thinking of doing something like this a few days ago. Currently if you want to have a body spring to a new resting transform you need to: detach body if it's already attached to joint, store original body transform, set body to desired resting transform, attach body to joint, move body back to original transform. PinJoint3D already has a method of doing this via the physics server with ```pin_joint_set_local_a```/```b```, although I assume it probably clears when you change the bodies attached to the joint unlike this pr.

body:
I agree it's awkward to have both, but (as the test project / regression / the fix in this PR shows) making bodies kinematic is not enough to make the collision properly animatable, so it would be a poor name for the property.

I think it would make sense to remove `use_kinematic_bodies` in favor of `collision_animatable` (with compatibility code).

(Theoretically someone could be depending on the current behavior, but it's doubtful.)

I really don't want to add another bool here, `use_kinematic_bodies` is supposed to work for moving platforms. If it does not, it's a bug.

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/61adafae2f9dace1f094b89ab2aceebd114e1c01/scene/2d/tile_map.cpp#L1525-L1593

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/master/scene/2d/physics/animatable_body_2d.cpp

body:
This is going to need this line:

```
Error: Validate extension JSON: Error: Field 'classes/Object/enums/ConnectFlags': is_bitfield changed value in new API, from false to true.
```

... added to a file in `misc/extension_api_validation/4.6-stable` to fix the CI error

But otherwise, I think this is OK from the perspective of GDExtension. Bindings frequently represent normal constants as `int64_t` and bitfields as `uint64_t`, so this change could potentially cause source compatibility issues with some bindings, but it won't cause any binary compatibility issues. Personally, I think that's acceptable.

I’ve searched through the codebase for usages of `push_back` with `Array` types, and there seem to be many opportunities to optimize them by switching to `resize` + `set` when the final size is already known. However, it’s not easy to benchmark and verify the performance impact of each individual change. I think it would be better to leave these broader optimizations to the core team, who can test and validate them properly.

* I have another example here https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/109898 using (preallocation + indexed assignment).

body:
I was able to optimize it even more using only pointers. (Note: for typed arrays and Variant it's still slow)

(Each test was run with an array size of **10,000,000** elements.)

### Benchmark Results

| TYPE              | AFTER   | BEFORE  |
|-------------------|---------|---------|
| PackedColorArray  | 1941 ms | 15567 ms |
| PackedVector4Array| 2004 ms | 15301 ms |
| PackedVector3Array| 1519 ms | 11715 ms |
| PackedVector2Array| 1116 ms | 7914 ms  |
| Array[Vector2i]   | 62238 ms| 62099 ms |
| PackedInt32Array  | 410 ms  | 3980 ms  |
| PackedInt64Array  | 410 ms  | 4146 ms  |
| PackedFloat32Array| 516 ms  | 4032 ms  |
| PackedFloat64Array| 516 ms  | 4076 ms  |
| PackedStringArray | 1120 ms | 6930 ms  |
| PackedByteArray   | 531 ms  | 4140 ms  |
| Dictionary        | 34861 ms| 46733 ms |

<details>
<summary>Script used for Benchmark</summary>

```gdscript
extends Control

const SIZE: int = 10000000

func _ready():
	await get_tree().process_frame
	test_color_arr()
	await get_tree().process_frame
	test_v4_arr()
	await get_tree().process_frame
	test_v3_arr()
	await get_tree().process_frame
	test_v2_arr()
	await get_tree().process_frame
	test_v2i_arr()
	await get_tree().process_frame
	test_int32_arr()
	await get_tree().process_frame
	test_int64_arr()
	await get_tree().process_frame
	test_float32_arr()
	await get_tree().process_frame
	test_float64_arr()
	await get_tree().process_frame
	test_string_arr()
	await get_tree().process_frame
	test_byte_arr()
	await get_tree().process_frame
	test_dict_arr()
	print("FINISHED")


func test_color_arr():
	var _res: Variant
	var arr: PackedColorArray

	arr.resize(SIZE)
	arr.fill(Color.AQUAMARINE)

	var time = Time.get_ticks_msec()
	_res = JSON.from_native(arr)
	prints("1m Color:", Time.get_ticks_msec() - time, "ms")


func test_v4_arr():
	var _res: Variant
	var arr: PackedVector4Array

	arr.resize(SIZE)
	arr.fill(Vector4(1, 2, 3, 4))
	
	var time = Time.get_ticks_msec()
	_res = JSON.from_native(arr)
	prints("1m Vector4:", Time.get_ticks_msec() - time, "ms")


func test_v3_arr():
	var _res: Variant
	var arr: PackedVector3Array

	arr.resize(SIZE)
	arr.fill(Vector3(1, 2, 3))
	
	var time = Time.get_ticks_msec()
	_res = JSON.from_native(arr)
	prints("1m Vector3:", Time.get_ticks_msec() - time, "ms")


func test_v2_arr():
	var _res: Variant
	var arr: PackedVector2Array

	arr.resize(SIZE)
	arr.fill(Vector2(1, 2))
	
	var time = Time.get_ticks_msec()
	_res = JSON.from_native(arr)
	prints("1m Vector2:", Time.get_ticks_msec() - time, "ms")


func test_v2i_arr():
	var _res: Variant
	var arr: Array[Vector2i]

	arr.resize(SIZE)
	arr.fill(Vector2i(1, 2))
	
	var time = Time.get_ticks_msec()
	_res = JSON.from_native(arr)
	prints("1m Vector2i:", Time.get_ticks_msec() - time, "ms")


func test_int32_arr():
	var _res: Variant
	var arr: PackedInt32Array

	arr.resize(SIZE)
	arr.fill(1)
	
	var time = Time.get_ticks_msec()
	_res = JSON.from_native(arr)
	prints("1m Int32:", Time.get_ticks_msec() - time, "ms")


func test_int64_arr():
	var _res: Variant
	var arr: PackedInt64Array

	arr.resize(SIZE)
	arr.fill(1)
	
	var time = Time.get_ticks_msec()
	_res = JSON.from_native(arr)
	prints("1m Int64:", Time.get_ticks_msec() - time, "ms")


func test_float32_arr():
	var _res: Variant
	var arr: PackedFloat32Array

	arr.resize(SIZE)
	arr.fill(1.0)
	
	var time = Time.get_ticks_msec()
	_res = JSON.from_native(arr)
	prints("1m Float32:", Time.get_ticks_msec() - time, "ms")


func test_float64_arr():
	var _res: Variant
	var arr: PackedFloat64Array

	arr.resize(SIZE)
	arr.fill(1.0)
	
	var time = Time.get_ticks_msec()
	_res = JSON.from_native(arr)
	prints("1m Float64:", Time.get_ticks_msec() - time, "ms")


func test_string_arr():
	var _res: Variant
	var arr: PackedStringArray

	arr.resize(SIZE)
	arr.fill("Hello World!")
	
	var time = Time.get_ticks_msec()
	_res = JSON.from_native(arr)
	prints("1m String:", Time.get_ticks_msec() - time, "ms")


func test_byte_arr():
	var _res: Variant
	var arr: PackedByteArray

	arr.resize(SIZE)
	arr.fill(true)
	
	var time = Time.get_ticks_msec()
	_res = JSON.from_native(arr)
	prints("1m Byte:", Time.get_ticks_msec() - time, "ms")


func test_dict_arr():
	var _res: Variant
	var dict: Dictionary[int, int]

	for i: int in SIZE:
		dict[i] = i
	
	var time = Time.get_ticks_msec()
	_res = JSON.from_native(dict)
	prints("1m Dictionary:", Time.get_ticks_msec() - time, "ms")

```
author: WhalesState

body: Is there any known issues this solves? Please explain why this is needed, this incurs an overhead so it's not free, so needs to be justified
author: AThousandShips

body:
> Is there any known issues this solves? Please explain why this is needed, this incurs an overhead so it's not free, so needs to be justified

If you create nodes from multiple threads (e.g., in my game I instantiate scenes on a background thread to avoid freezes and stutters on the main thread), the orphan node count becomes incorrect over time due to race conditions and differs from the actual `print_orphan_nodes()` output.

Even though I didn't see any overhead in my profiler, I improved it so that the overhead is only when creating/destroying nodes, instead of when they are added/removed from the `SceneTree`.
author: Ryan-000

body:
I just thought of a secret third option: It would be possible to remove `orphan_node_count`, and instead infer it from `ObjectDB` when orphan node counts should be counted.
I don't know how slow that would be compared to the current implementation, but it would have the major advantage of not needing the slow atomics (or risking data mangling) to keep track of the counts.

I'll schedule to discuss this at an upcoming core meeting.

We just discussed this in the Core meeting, and agreed to go ahead with this PR.

However, there was one concern raised, which is the additional construction and destruction cost of atomics that would be added to `Node`. To mitigate this, please wrap the `orphan_node_count` (the variable, as well as the related logic) with `#ifdef DEBUG_ENABLED`. The `Performance::_get_orphan_node_count` function should simply return 0 on non-debug builds.

body:
Interesting, after using this new build with Lua GDExtension I get the following error message:

> ERROR: Required virtual method LuaScript::_instance_create must be overridden before calling.

I guess const vs non-const affect virtual method resolution? So it seems this change could break existing `ScriptExtension`s. Not sure what's the best approach in this case.
author: gilzoide

body:
Const-ness is part of method hashes (which are compared during virtual call dispatch since Godot 4.4). Modifying the signature in a way that changes the hash constitutes a binary-breaking change in GDExtension.

Since Godot 4.4 (https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/100674), there is a compatibility system for virtual methods, which would allow you to provide a migration path that doesn't break existing extensions. See also https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/92175 for an example.

Regarding the change itself, I'm probably not the best to judge if it's necessary and worth the compat, hopefully others can jump in here :slightly_smiling_face:
author: Bromeon

body:
> Since Godot 4.4, there is a compatibility system for virtual methods, which would allow you to provide a migration path that doesn't break existing extensions.

Oh, I didn't know of that, awesome! I'll rewrite it using this compatibility system when I have the time. Thanks for the tip!
author: gilzoide

body:
> These virtual methods should not be declared const to allow subclasses to cache these instances / placeholders and update them in _update_exports or ScriptLanguageExtension::_reload_all_scripts

Reloading scripts is indeed a bit of a headache due to these methods being `const` and therefore not being able to store any metadata for new script instances. 
If this can indeed be achieved in a backwards-compatible way, then I think it would be a worthwhile change.
author: TitanNano

body:
Ok, I added the compatibility methods and it seems to work pretty well, tested with Lua GDExtension!

Existing GDExtensions should not be affected. Projects should still break when they update the extension_api.json to a version where the method is non-const, although I haven't tested doing that.

Notice that I had to call the compatibility methods before the new ones, since they are required virtual methods. When I first tried the other way around, I would receive the "Required virtual method ... must be overridden before calling" error even though the compatibility method would run correctly afterwards.

body:
I did look at fixing it further up the call stack but it seemed to be in generic importer land and not in anything blender related, and I didn't want to accidentally cause explosions for other well behaved importers.

> Should headless mode perform autodetection? I believe it currently will return true without having set a path, which is inconsistent with the non-headless behavior which disables blend import and returns false.

I don't know what "autodetection" is in this context? Disabling blend import seems to require saving and overwriting the user's editor settings which I am hesitant to do in a headless run. So I am avoiding that but returning `true` the same way that the [gui version does](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/master/modules/gltf/editor/editor_scene_importer_blend.cpp#L611) after it disables blend import. I must admit though, I don't know what the return value is supposed to signify, it doesn't appear to be documented. Testing it with both true and false, I get this behaviour:

`true`: fails to import then segfaults.
`false`: fails to import then the process exists with success (0 return code).

I guess we probably want the second one, and people can inspect the output to determine success or failure? A non-zero return code would sure be ideal, but I guess that is a whole separate issue...

body: To be honest, I don't fully understand what you're asking for in relation to your code, but the goal is to support sharing buffers between GDShaders and compute shaders, provided the buffer is created on the main RenderingDevice. My test project right now has to read back the values from the compute shader and then set the buffer that is read in both the vertex and fragment shader to those values, but once this goal is completed, the buffer would be bound to both in the same way a TextureRD can be bound to both, so no read back would be needed
author: Fluxx7

body:
Thanks that pretty much is what I expected, what you have is cool but that next step elevates what Godot is capable of so excited to see this one progress.

To clarify for anyone following on. With the new code as-is per frame the process is GPU (compute)->CPU(data copy)->GPU (spatial). With it being a future target to do, per frame GPU (compute)->CPU memory barrier on render thread->GPU (spatial) with no data copy/pass through on the CPU for the spatial shader to see updates from the compute step.

The future API would need to handle something like this (vkCmdBindDescriptorSets style):
myshadermat.set_shader_buffer("myBuffer",  rid_from_compute_uniform)

Based on prior art with instanced meshes, we may need to get the bufferRID from the material and feed it into the compute shader like we already can for the Godot MultiMesh node. I believe the trick is to expose the buffer RID on the material and then when defining the compute uniform use "add_id" to reference it so the GPU connects them together:
var buffer_rid := RenderingServer.spatialshader_get_buffer_rd_rid(myshadermat)
assert(buffer_rid.is_valid())
var spatial_shader_out_uniform := RDUniform.new()
spatial_shader_out_uniform.uniform_type = RenderingDevice.UNIFORM_TYPE_STORAGE_BUFFER
spatial_shader_out_uniform.binding = 4
spatial_shader_out_uniform.add_id(buffer_rid)
uniforms.append(multimesh_out_uniform)

I haven't delved into if that alone would work out of the box or if more wiring would be required. May have a dig later.
author: EnlightenedOne

body: I could pretty easily expose API to directly get or set the buffer RID for the GDShaders, but I'm trying to make the API as user-friendly as possible, and juggling RIDs in scripts always sucks imo. I could add those functions as an option I guess, but setting the buffer RID could make some of the other API stuff not work properly or more difficult to implement properly, so I'm hesitant to add that. Hopefully direct RID access won't be needed once I finish this refactor and get the API to work properly
author: Fluxx7

body: Nobody loves uniforms but we do already have to manage them to work on compute shaders so an abstraction might obfuscate that layer worst case, keen to see what you come up with. I am curious to know if with the RID exposed the mount would actually work as-is, minimalist and functional trumps no capability in my book.
author: EnlightenedOne

body:
Just stumbled on this, can't say how happy I am this seems to be actively worked on.

On the matter of juggling RIDs, I personally find it pretty user friendly to manage them and would prefer that over a more simple API that potentially would be less flexible in exchange for being more user friendly.
author: Deltt

body:
Part of the problem with letting the user access the RIDs is that the RID is currently being handled in the ShaderMaterial, and a problem could occur if the buffer RID gets replaced or if the user frees the buffer RID for some reason. These should be able to be planned around, it just requires more thought. 

Ultimately, I'd like to have the user friendly API for handling things like converting Variant values to bytes, ensuring proper alignment for the given buffer format (std140 vs 430), and having runtime warnings when the user tries to either assign to a buffer field that doesn't exist or doesn't assign a buffer field when assigning the entire buffer at once, and then either have this API cover all of the standard RID use cases kinda seamlessly or also expose more raw values like the RID without compromising the functionality of the API. 

It's kinda important imo that the buffers can safely be changed while editing the scene, since (for instance) altering the structure of a buffer (or any other bound resource) in a compute shader will break the shader completely until the code is updated to properly bind the resources in the expected size, while altering textures or uniforms in a GDShader never causes any errors afaik. 

I think this should all be able to done with a BufferRD object, but I'll continue to investigate. Eventually I intend to add support for writing compute shaders as GDShaders to allow for things like multiple kernels in the same file, easily shared uniforms, and all of the convenience of reflection-based features like setting uniforms, textures, and buffers by name, as well as handling some of the allocation and compilation process automatically. I'm trying to design the API with this in mind so that it will mesh well with that goal.

body:
> Is the breaking change that `OpenXRInterface::get_color_texture()` will now return a rendering server texture RID, rather than a rendering device texture RID?
> 
> If so, I actually think that makes more sense overall - it's always seemed a little weird to me that for OpenGL we return a rendering server texture RID, but not for Vulkan.

Yes, in hindsight it should have always worked this way. But changing it now, especially as we have some extensions doing things with swapchains, could cause issues.

It's possible the way I've done things hides the changes well enough that it will keep on working just fine (though there may be a few places I've missed).

The other part I'm not sure of is that this creates extra texture views and I don't know if that has any costs.
author: BastiaanOlij

body:
> I think we _may_ be able to prevent breaking binary compatibility for `XRInterfaceExtension` by having a virtual compatibility method that takes the rendering device texture and wraps it up in a rendering server texture? That would need some experimentation to see if it would work. (Not that there's many `XRInterfaceExtension`s in the wild - the main one being the OpenVR extension.)

Things is, it's always just been RIDs we're pushing around so most of the extensions are none the wiser. Yes we now need to sent through `texture` RIDs instead of `texture_rd` RIDs, but as we now expose the `texture` RIDs through our swapchain wrapper class, that part should be transparent.

This is why it wasn't a problem that compatibility already used `texture` RIDs without causing any problems.

However, if extensions perform additional actions here that assume we have a `texture_rd` RID that things fall a part. Or if I've forgotten some place that is still expecting `texture_rd` RIDs.

body: If so, this may cause a performance regression in 3D projects with HDR2D enabled, which could be unexpected (the term "HDR 2D" is not accurate anymore, docs need changes).
author: beicause

body:
> If so, this may cause a performance regression in 3D projects with HDR2D enabled, which could be unexpected (the term "HDR 2D" is not accurate anymore).

That's right. We discussed the performance tradeoff at length in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/86098 and in subsequent rendering meetings and decided that it was worth the change. If 2D is using full HDR, then the entire pipeline should use full HDR. 

That being said, its not necessarily going to be a performance regression because:
1. Not all games are bandwidth limited. This change only hurts bandwidth limited games
2. It opens up more opportunities to share resources between 2D and 3D which can have a positive impact on performance
3. Most modern phones support framebuffer compression for RGBA16 buffers now. So the original reason to limit to RGB10A2 is gone now for many devices
4. Once we support HDR output, this change is 100% necessary and will improve performance since we won't have to do a needless copy to promote the RGB10A2 buffer to full RGBA16. 
5. If users were already using HDR2D, they are paying the cost of having a RGBA16 buffer already, so they have already decided to accept reduced performance for the benefits it provides. 

Overall, we felt that making this change was worthwhile.

body: I'm actually not immediately sure why from glancing at the code changes, but this PR breaks debanding in the following configuration: - Mobile renderer - SMAA antialiasing - HDR 2D disabled View these screenshots in a dark viewing environment at 100% scaling: | Godot 4.5 | This PR |--|-- || MRP: [mobile-hdr-2d-debanding.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22452076/mobile-hdr-2d-debanding.zip) SMAA causes the first tonemap pass to match the scene rendering buffer format, which is 10-bit when HDR 2D is disabled, so 10-bit debanding in the tonemap shader is required for this configuration. The code that detects what buffer format should be used for debanding makes a lot of assumptions about data formats because the exact format of a given buffer isn't really saved in an accessible way for easy access. It's possible that the debanding code now thinks that this configuration is using a 16 bit buffer for even though it is actually using a 10 bit buffer? Edit: Sorry, some of what I wrote in the first pass of this comment wasn't quite right... I think I've corrected it(?) To be honest, I need to dig deep into this every time I try and think about it because it's a little complicated to keep track of the different buffer formats used through the different passes with each renderer and HDR 2D configuration 😵‍💫

body:
Thanks for the ping, @BastiaanOlij!

## HDR Output
@rsanchezsaez HDR output in Godot will be using Apple's Extended Dynamic Range (EDR) paradigm and adapted to work well across all platforms. Main development is happening in #94496 and will likely be merged soon, as it's a priority for 4.6. @stuartcarnie put together a rough draft prototype of how this might work on Mac OS and I have done a rebase or two that has resulted in the draft PR getting even more "hacky": #106814 Once we've merged the Windows HDR output, we will shift to making sure Mac OS is implemented correctly.

We will be having a meeting on Sunday to firm up the public-facing part of the HDR output API. We're coordinating in RocketChat in the rendering chat channel and you're welcome to join if you'd like, but the focus will be specifically on the user-facing HDR API and ensuring it will be reasonable for all platforms.

Does VisionOS use the same EDR and colour space paradigms as Mac OS and iOS with the same APIs?
author: allenwp

body: Hi @rsanchezsaez. I tried the code and it works great! I haven't look into the implementation, but I do have some suggestions from a user's perspective. # Need some documentation/warning on transparent objects and background When in mixed immersion mode, CompositorService requires **zero alpha channel if depth is zero**. otherwise blocky artifact occurs. This poses some issue when setting up background and transparent objects, as they don't write depth, but provide a non zero alpha value. This is what it looks like when open a existing scene directly in mixed immersion mode before adjustment. Notice the blocky grey artifacts around the character, and the transparent halo object is no displayed. That's because the background has non-zero alpha value, and the halo object does not writes depth. The following image shows how the character should look like in normal window. Recently I got some experience on CompositorService by implementing a custom AR renderer with metal and objc. So I'm no suprised by the artifact. The solution for me it quite simple. Adjust the background alpha to zero, force the halo object as opaque, done. But this needs more clarification for normal users. Here is how my scene looks like after fix. I suspect correctly handling transparent object rendering for visionOS might worth another PR. I only suggest some notes in documentation after this PR got merged. # Warning for near plane Warning for near plane does not correctly update in editor after param adjustment. This is important. In current implementation, NOTHING is shown in view if near plane is less than 0.1m. That's quite awkward. Because the default value is 0.05m. Also is it possible to clamp the near plane to 0.1m automatically? That might be more intuitive than showing an empty space and spaming the log with warning.

I'm not as familiar with the inner workings of CompositorService, but if something like this does make it into the documentation direct instructions akin to the following could be helpful:

author: CCranney

body:
> This PR provides a solid foundation for visionOS XR rendering support. While it does introduce some new design challenges, these problems are inherent to the device. Therefore, I suggest we merge this PR first and address other problems in subsequent work.

I don't think we should merge this as-is. Speaking from the XR side, this PR adds public APIs to `XRInterface` that we don't think should be there, and it's going to be something that shows up for folks working on XR on all platforms. (I don't know what the rendering team folks think about this with regard to the rendering changes.)

If we're going to merge something and then improve it later, then I think we should remove VRS support entirely, and have a subsequent PR that adds VRS from the zero.
author: dsnopek

body:
@dsnopek You are right. I've focused too much on the handling of rendering.

I think the biggest problem is the RenderingDevice. VisionOS support can not be implemented elegantly until metal rendering device is aware of MTLRasterizationRateMap. I concluded the workaround in this PR is fine given the current state of RenderingDevice.

For the interface part, I feel the implementation can be adjusted to avoid modify the XRInterface entirely. As I pointed out in related comment, the p_view argument for get_viewport is actually not required on visionOS. We should be able to use `get_region` instead of the newly introduced hacky method `get_viewport_for_view`, as long as the RenderingDevice is happy with potentially larger render region than render target size.

`get_viewports_are_hdr` can also be added after HDR implementation on other platforms are stablized.

To be honest I don't think visionOS XR support can be added without VRS support. When foveated rendering is disabled, the rendering result looks too jaggy for a $3499 device, as the CompositorService will only give you a texture with relatively low resolution :(
author: huisedenanhai

body:
I'll leave it up to @rsanchezsaez to give feedback on what is, and isn't possible on Metal, I am not an expert on Metal.

But we can't introduce an invasive API change just for one platform. It will create a massive burden for maintainers of the rendering engine, many of which don't even have access to a visionPro to test if changes don't break things.

We already have things in the engine that are causing us issues here, but which we can justify because they target architecture decisions prevalent within the industry.

We must find a way to hide implementation details such as these either on the platform side or find a really good way to abstract this. Metal in this case is the odd duck. If that introduces limitations in say, post processes, I can live with that.

Longer term thinking there has been a plan to abstract out `RendererSceneRenderRD` or maybe even `RendererSceneRender` so we can create a 3D render implementation specific for a platform or specific for a use case. But that isn't in the cards just yet.

body:
> There is another question, which is about HDR. I haven't look at HDR implementation in Godot. But in my experience, HDR is not a feature that should always be enabled. This PR forcefully enables HDR on visionOS.
> 
> 
> 
> @allenwp Will there be a feature toggle for HDR? If that's true, visionOS should also response to user's HDR setting.

That's correct: HDR should be optional. It can be difficult for a game developer to author correctly balanced bright scenes with unbounded HDR output enabled, so I believe there is still significant value to SDR scenes, even in VR. In the future, we may add an option to `Environment` that will allow the user to limit the max output to their choice of a value, which could be as low as `1.0` (equivalent to SDR with the HDR 2D setting enabled).

Additionally, I'm not sure, but will it be possible to use visionOS with HDR 2D disabled? If so, that's another reason that SDR is needed; HDR requires HDR 2D to be enabled, so if we want to support HDR 2D disabled, we need to also support SDR mode.

BUT, I could imagine that after the new `Environment` parameter for limiting the output range is merged, it's possible that SDR would no longer be absolutely necessary, as the user could use this to get something that is effectually SDR within HDR. Not sure about this idea, due to the paradigm being inconsistent with how HDR/SDR works on other platforms.

(Also, three weeks ago we firmed up the public API for the HDR output PR, which can be found in #94496; viewing the docs changes of the commit is a nice way to see the public API.)

body:
@dsnopek Thanks for your review on the `XRInterface` changes.

@BastiaanOlij Thank you as well for taking the time to do an exhaustive review.

I just pushed a new revision with
- Thread-safety fixes for the `Separate` threading model.
- App backgrounding and restoration support. On visionOS app background, the existing layer renderer is invalidated, and when you foreground the app again, the system provides a brand new layer renderer object. The solution is to provide the new one to the `VisionXRInterface` module after an app foreground.
- A small fix in `get_projection_for_view` where the projection matrix didn't respect the `world_scale`, which caused re-projection to be wrong.

I have kept the commits separate for now to facilitate reviewing. When it's time to merge, I'll rebase everything into a single commit on top of master.

Let us know if there's anything else we can do.

body:
@Clancey Thanks for rebasing against `master`. I rebased independently on my end, because the latest `master` introduced new conflicts. And also, your experiments with this branch look really cool!

It's worth noting that https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/115799/ which was recently merged, changed the `XRInterface` threading contract and introduced an issue with this PR. `get_render_target_size()` was previously called exclusively from the render thread but after that change it's called earlier from the game thread. I have addressed the issue by providing a thread-safe way to access the render target size that is computed the when `pre_render()` is called. The first frame it'll return a size of `{0,0}`, but subsequent calls will be correct.

author: Clancey

body:
> It's worth noting that #115799 which was recently merged, changed the `XRInterface` threading contract and introduced an issue with this PR.

`OpenXRInterface` has always had code to account for `get_render_target_size()` being called on either the main thread or render thread. So, I think this may actually be a lack documentation, rather than a change in contract. Although, since `XRInterface`s can be accessed by GDScript, I think you kind of have to assume that any bound method could get called at any time from any thread

body:
Built a physics demo on top of this branch to get real performance data before the merge: **Falling Cascade** — 25 emissive \`RigidBody3D\` cubes dropping onto a tilted catch plate in full-immersive mode on Apple Vision Pro M2 (RealityDevice14,1, visionOS 26.5, Xcode 26).

Results over a 95-second run (19 × 5-second sampling windows):
- **90 FPS locked, zero variance** — every window showed exactly 450 frames
- ~475 physics collisions with \`body_entered\` signals firing per hit, \`AudioStreamPlayer\` chime per collision
- No frame drops observed during sustained rigid-body stacking

One community gotcha worth calling out explicitly, since it burned time: **Forward+ silently renders nothing on this path.** The Mobile renderer requirement is documented in the PR description, but it's easy to miss if you're porting an existing project. Might be worth a prominent warning in the export dialog or docs once this lands — @huisedenanhai's zero-alpha background note from above falls in the same "silent failure" category.

Demo source is public if useful for testing or CI: https://github.com/ibrews/godot-avp-cascade

Happy to share the raw frame-delta logs or run additional workloads if the team wants more data points.

— Alex Coulombe, Agile Lens

body:
Now that I have hardware, I was finally able to test this!

For the most part it worked, but there was a little bit of weirdness:

- Nothing would render at all until I set the `XRCamera3D`'s  `near` property to `0.1` (rather than the default of `0.05`). The only way I found out that I needed to do this was from log messages in Console.app:
  ```
  error	06:38:20.379195-0500	export	modules/visionos_xr/visionos_xr_interface.mm:375:get_projection_for_view(): Your XRCamera3D Near value is lower than the minimum value supported by the visionOS platform. Make sure that Near divided by XROrigin's World Scale is higher than or equal to the value returned by LayerRender.Capabilities.supportedMinimumNearPlaneDistance. This value is 0.1 for Apple Vision Pro. Condition "scaled_z_near < minimum_supported_near_plane" is true. Returning: eye_projection
  ```
  It would be nicer if `VisionOSXRInterface` could automatically set the near value to the minimum, if the user provided a lower value
- My test scene has a sky and a single cube and my intention was for it to be fully immersive (ie no passthrough), however, it seems to automatically cut out my cube, and I only see the sky in a small border around the the cube. I tried setting "Immersion Style" to both "Full" and "Mixed", but the only difference was that with "Full" everything else is covered in black - I still don't see my sky around me. Did I miss a step somewhere? My script is only this (based on the code in the issue description):

body:
@dsnopek glad the `near = 0.1` fix sorted it — I hit that exact wall building the Falling Cascade demo on this branch. A few other gotchas I ran into that'd be worth capturing if a docs PR gets going (happy to write any of these up):

- **`XROrigin3D.current = true`** is mandatory — miss it and you get a locked 90 FPS rendering *nothing*. Silent and baffling.
- **Mobile renderer only** — Forward+ silently renders nothing on this path.
- **`WorldEnvironment` background alpha must be 0** in mixed immersion, or CompositorServices halos appear (huisedenanhai's depth shader is the cleaner fix).
- **`XRCamera3D.near ≥ 0.1`** (AVP minimum) — the one you just hit.
- **Hand tracking isn't in this branch yet** — it's in Clancey's `visionos_master_pr` fork. I got pinch-grab + throw working by building from there.

All of these are written up with repro details on my end — happy to contribute to a docs PR or hand the notes to whoever opens one.
author: ibrews
ibrews

body: > * **`XROrigin3D.current = true`** is mandatory — miss it and you get a locked 90 FPS rendering _nothing_. Silent and baffling. This one is weird because the first XROrigin3D node (and in 99% of the projects the only XROrigin3D node) should become current automatically. The `current` property was added in a scenario where multiple XROrigin3D nodes are in the current scene tree (we can have this situation in more complex projects in transition situations when loading in new levels). If you only have 1 XROrigin3D node and it's not working, someone broke something. Though I wonder what because that would have effected almost everyone, most people don't even know the `current` property was introduced. > * **Mobile renderer only** — Forward+ silently renders nothing on this path. This one is difficult and applies to all standalone devices. Though not rendering anything is suspect, Forward+ is a renderer that does not work well, and likely never will, on TBDR GPUs. That said, by default even when you select the Forward+ renderer in the creation dialog (and thus seeing that selected in the IDE), the project settings should be setup like this: Unless you change the `.mobile` setting to Forward+ to override the default behavior, visionOS should be treated like a mobile platform and thus select the mobile renderer. @rsanchezsaez, @stuartcarnie it is possible that we've missed this when we introduce the visionOS platform, Godot should treat visionOS like a mobile target. > * **`WorldEnvironment` background alpha must be 0** in mixed immersion, or CompositorServices halos appear (huisedenanhai's depth shader is the cleaner fix). It would be nice if we could do something about this but there are a lot of things standing in the way of making this automatic as there are many use cases in the Godot renderer that make this a problem. So for now this will have to remain a documentation thing. > * **`XRCamera3D.near ≥ 0.1`** (AVP minimum) — the one you just hit. When @rsanchezsaez mentioned this limitation (and originally had a warning added in) my jaw fell to the ground. We can't have this as a limitation int he engine, end of story. Sadly the far/near logic is central to the rendering engine and simply overriding it for a single platform will require some really troublesome changes. Personally I would really push for Apple to fix this restriction. Having a near plane at 0.1 means that you can't render anything closer than 10cm to the eye. That breaks things like looking through a scope (popular FPS games), or looking into a virtual microscope (science application, either a virtual classroom or medical training app). I'd really like to know the motivation for this seemingly arbitrary restriction. > * **Hand tracking isn't in this branch yet** — it's in Clancey's `visionos_master_pr` fork. I got pinch-grab + throw working by building from there. I've been told this is on the way, can't wait to see it! Without hand tracking and controller tracking we can't make full applications. > All of these are written up with repro details on my end — happy to contribute to a docs PR or hand the notes to whoever opens one. Many thanks for the feedback! Absolutely stellar! Documentation is definitely something we need to have in place before we have an official release, so that is something high on the priorities list.
author: BastiaanOlij

body:
@ibrews Thanks for the extensive testing and the documentation suggestions. Great experiments and performance benchmarking!

@stuartcarnia @dsnopek Thanks as well for the followups.

@BastiaanOlij Thanks for the additional comments.

> Unless you change the .mobile setting to Forward+ to override the default behavior, visionOS should be treated like a mobile platform and thus select the mobile renderer.

Last time we tested, the Forward+ -> Mobile fallback worked properly. I'm unsure if it stops working in some conditions.

> It would be nice if we could do something about this but there are a lot of things standing in the way of making this automatic as there are many use cases in the Godot renderer that make this a problem. So for now this will have to remain a documentation thing.

Agreed.

> We can't have this as a limitation int he engine, end of story. Sadly the far/near logic is central to the rendering engine and simply overriding it for a single platform will require some really troublesome changes.
>
> Personally I would really push for Apple to fix this restriction. Having a near plane at 0.1 means that you can't render anything closer than 10cm to the eye. That breaks things like looking through a scope (popular FPS games), or looking into a virtual microscope (science application, either a virtual classroom or medical training app).

I agree the use cases you mentioned are valid. We are tracking a feedback report with your comments to see if we can relax that restriction without other side effects in the future. However, we believe this PR should support visionOS 26, and the API is strict in this regard on that version.

If you agree we want to support visionOS 26, given we cannot change this retroactively, what should we do about it on this PR (if anything different than the error message we're already printing).

> Documentation is definitely something we need to have in place before we have an official release, so that is something high on the priorities list

We can prepare a documentation PR addressing some of the issues mentioned here.
author: rsanchezsaez

body:
Hey Ricardo,

> Last time we tested, the Forward+ -> Mobile fallback worked properly. I'm unsure if it stops working in some conditions.

It would be really good to verify, some things did change in 4.7 around the renderer selection as it has all become more hidden. But it should still work as described which is why I'm surprised @ibrews ran into issues.

 
> I agree the use cases you mentioned are valid. We are tracking a feedback report with your comments to see if we can relax that restriction without other side effects in the future. However, we believe this PR should support visionOS 26, and the API is strict in this regard on that version.
> 
> If you agree we want to support visionOS 26, given we cannot change this retroactively, what should we do about it on this PR (if anything different than the error message we're already printing).

Personally I feel the the error is all we can do. As I did mention privately, we could experiment with ignoring the zNear when it's too low, printing a warning (once) and just using 0.1 for the near value when putting the projection matrix together.

Most of the rendering engine will retrieve the near value from the projection matrix and ignore the setting on `(XR)Camera3D`, it's really only used when the UI performs a ray cast (which doesn't apply to us) and when generating the projection matrix.
There might be some more places that use it directly and would trip up, but I feel those are likely only applicable to the Forward+ renderer than the mobile renderer.

It's a bit of a hack if it works, and I can't guarantee that it will work (which is why we should definitely inform users they should adjust the near value), but it may be a better solution than just erroring out and having rendering just completely fail on a user.

> We can prepare a documentation PR addressing some of the issues mentioned here.

👍

author: Repiteo

body:
Hi there, hello! Someone interested in partial 3D texture updates here. 

It might not be the best place for questions, but still. I have a question about the implementation of the feature and its future:

As far as I can see, partial update is performed using data from a `PackedByteArray`, which is required to be the exact size of the patched texture region. Would it be possible to have a version that works on a region of the source data as well? The use case is simple. Let there be some voxel data that is edited on CPU and rendered using a 3D texture. Copying the edited regions into separate `PackedByeArray` after every editing becomes expensive very fast due to memory allocation and fragmentation, and data copying. If I understand correctly, the data is copied again into some rendering device buffers. Allowing to pass the full dataset and only copying data from the given region eliminates the unnecessary memory allocations and data copying. Exposing this version to GDExtension with `Span<uint8_t>` would be especially interesting, because it would allow using external data storage, which might not be `PackedByteArray`.

body:
> - "Why not just turn scaling to bilinear when going below 64x64 and be done with it?" 
>   - This is what I did at some point but FSR2 still has a much sharper look even at these low render sizes, which may be desirable. So I decided it wasn't good enough because there is some Legitimate Use Case (tm) for small viewports using FSR2 instead of bilinear

Upscaling needs a somewhat large viewport resolution to work well, it'll never be useful at sizes below 64x64. It's fine to fall back to bilinear in this case.
author: Calinou

body: > Upscaling needs a somewhat large viewport resolution to work well, it'll never be useful at sizes below 64x64. It's fine to fall back to bilinear in this case. Perhaps. I just felt that the less the fix intrudes upon the user's choices, the better. Falling back to Bilinear is permanent unless the user sets it to FSR2 again. The fix already will cause surprise and problems for some people and by looking at the way people use `SubViewport`, I can't say I feel confident in predicting what people might want to do that might be creative but unexpected. A 64x64 FSR2 viewport may be useful to some people for generating a 3d portrait for instance (though I wouldn't really recommend that use). That's just how I see it though, I'm fine with whatever is decided. I will however show an example of the difference so it's easier to decide if it's worth preserving FSR2 at these low resolutions or not. | | FSR2 scaling 0.67 [^1] | FSR2 scaling 1.0 | Bilinear scaling 0.67 | Bilinear scaling 1.0 [^2] | |---------|-------------------|------------------|---------------|--------------| | 64x64 | | | | | | 256x256 | | | | | [^1]: Due to the changes in this PR, this is effectively 1.0 scaling, not 0.67 (for the 64x64 case), I'm just using the setting in the inspector as the title. [^2]: Prior to this PR if this was a fallback to Bilinear, it would have jitter. Now it won't, so this is static while the same sized viewport with FSR2 has jitter. All screenshots are from the artifact of this PR. You can see that, for example, the icon's edges are better antialiased with FSR2, whereas with Bilinear it eats the right, bottom and top edge but keeps the left edge sharp. The background is a bit sharper with FSR2 as well. Whether that's significant in practice or useful, I don't know. I guess when I was testing this, I wrongly compared 0.67 bilinear with effectively 1.0 FSR2, so they looked much more different than they really are. Edit - also, if the fix remains as I wrote it, I feel uncomfortable with it being backported to 4.4 because it may break projects. It's not severe enough to not be fixed in 4.5 in my opinion but I also don't know what's considered truly worth backporting or fixing early. But I felt like I had to clarify my position on that. I am cleaning up a spreadsheet I made back when I was writing the code to see how the size change code behaves with different combinations of dimensions, which should make clearer whether this fix is risky or not and how much, when it comes to potential compatibility with existing projects. Edit 2 - added spreadsheet to main comment
author: mechalynx

body:
I have re-implemented this fix to not do any resizing and just fall back to Bilinear if any of the two dimensions of a viewport, after 3d scaling, fall below 64px. It's here: https://github.com/mechalynx/godot/tree/viewport_limit_fix_no_resize. If it's decided that resizing is not needed, then that version supercedes this one. I'm neutral on this: resizing is more minimal in taking control from the user but just falling back to bilinear is much simpler, much less code and will work for virtually everyone.

I'm not sure if I should push the changes into this pull request or make a new one that supercedes it, so I'll wait for advice on that so that I don't trigger build actions needlessly.

Hi, I'm not sure how to test if dbus works correctly 😅 But if udev doesn't work then some controllers (DualSense Edge, for example) will have incorrect mappings if they're hotplugged: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/106218#issuecomment-2931840733

body:
> How to correctly handle udev and dbus on Linux? SOWARP_ENABLED is not present on 3.x but there is a libudev-so_wrap. Is a libdbus-so_wrap required to make it work if dbus can't be found on the system? Do we need a thirdparty/linuxbsd_headers as well? I'm a bit out of my depth with understanding how and when which libraries is used from where.

Oh, sorry, I didn't notice the question here 😅 If SOWRAP_ENABLED is not present in 3.x and theres libudev-so_wrap, so I assume that Godot uses sowrap here and it's not optional, so I think it's fair to only use the code parts for SOWRAP_ENABLED. SDL also uses dynamic loading of dbus and udev functions (for udev the file has to be specified in the SDL build config file, otherwise it will try to get the functions from the built game/Godot binary, but still), basically as if they had SOWRAP_ENABLED defined too.

It's just that errors like this are spammed when the editor opens:

```
ERROR: Condition "err != OK" is true.
   at: _initialize_cache (servers/rendering/renderer_rd/shader_rd.cpp:823)
ERROR: Condition "f.is_null()" is true.
   at: _save_to_cache (servers/rendering/renderer_rd/shader_rd.cpp:512)
```

However, it *will* sometimes fail to save files, specifically on short WSL paths like ``\\wsl.localhost\FedoraLinux-42\home\hugo\project\project.godot``:

```
ERROR: Couldn't save project.godot - //wsl.localhost/FedoraLinux-42/home/hugo/project/project.godot.
   at: (core/config/project_settings.cpp:980)
ERROR: Cannot create file 'res://.godot/editor/filesystem_cache10'. Check user write permissions.
   at: (editor/editor_file_system.cpp:542)
```

However, with this PR, it'll just open the project manager as if the project path specified was invalid.
This also applies to short paths, not just paths > 260 characters.

The command-line option doesn't seem to be excluded from release builds or non-editor builds, and there's a lot of ifdefs around them with DEBUG_ENABLED, so it seems pretty explicit that it's included. It sounds like a documentation error.

No, shader baker is definitely affected by this setting because the shader creation runs on the editor itself, which is being run with this setting. We need to block the shader baker from being used if the commandline option is being used.

body:
Added the warning.

I'm unable to detect a way to fix @clayjohn's error. The extension doesn't make any API changes, and the extension is also requested as required if the command line option is enabled. This seems like a driver error. The opcode in question that is mentioned in the error is very standard and the extension shouldn't be affecting its usage, as the Phi operator is essential for any code that involves branching.

Regardless of what we might explore on how to solve this, this is a direct regression fix for something currently present in `master` so I don't think we should have much concern for merging this. What we do want to explore is some alternate way to offer the ability to not strip the debug information when smolv is used without affecting the shader cache hashes if possible. But I don't think we should block this PR and we can evaluate that later.

author: zeux

body:
Thanks for testing! This is actually an unintended consequence -- this mesh can simplify cleanly to 1024 triangles, but the next step can only simplify to 514 triangles due to locked borders - just shy of the goal (512). When pruning is enabled, simplifier is determined to get to the specified number which includes creating a 0-triangle LOD, which makes it so that we terminate the generation process. You can observe that difference by looking at the logs in --verbose mode now.

So your code previously was displaying a 514-triangle LOD, and with this change as is, the last LOD was the 1024-triangle LOD that still had a clean tessellation.

I'm going to take a look at improving this in meshoptimizer in the future because this part of behavior is not super helpful, but for now I adjusted the code to try to get as low as we can with pruning and, if this situation happens, to retry without it. This will probably be unnecessary in the future, but it prevents a possibility of a final low quality LOD to be skipped as a side-effect of this change. That adjustment doesn't normally change the behavior, e.g. the safe mesh from my original description simplifies the same as it did without this tweak.

Also I think the code here will also need a similar fix (noticed similar jittering with colliders), but `Node3D::get_global_transform` is const and `Node3D::get_global_transform_interpolated` isn't (which the compiler is upset about), and I'm not C++ savvy enough to know what the preferred way of addressing it is :P 

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/4ebf67c12dcdffcb69242569c118a371a654b6ae/scene/3d/spring_bone_collision_3d.cpp#L152-L160
author: dog-on-moon

body:
> Node3D::get_global_transform is const and Node3D::get_global_transform_interpolated isn't (which the compiler is upset about), and I'm not C++ savvy enough to know what the preferred way of addressing it is :P

You may be able to simply make `get_global_transform_interpolated()` const, this is likely historical from before `SceneTreeFTI` (might be used by the ifdeffed backup path, but that's just for testing).

This is very probably fine but I haven't looked at all at this class (it may not be present in 3.x), so it could do with checking by someone more familiar with `SpringBone3D`.

Often things like this are processed on physics tick in which case the non-interpolated is fine, but if this is causing a problem presumably it is being called on the frame. `get_transform_interpolated()` should work either way and fallback to the normal method if called on a physics tick.

BTW I would also double check this function is not being called on invisible nodes, as `get_global_transform_interpolated()` can get rather expensive when called on invisible nodes (as the xform has to be calculated through the chain on demand).

If I'm understanding the issue correctly, we need to rename the `.pdb` file to contain the same number of characters as the original, and presently it's making a longer name?

I just tested with `master` using the godot-cpp test project, and I end up with these files:

- `~libgdexample.windows.template_debug.dev.x86_64.dll`
- `~libgdexample.windows.template_debug.dev.x86_64_0.pdb`

And, assuming I've got this right, it should be something like `~libgdexample.windows.template_debug.dev.x86_0.pdb`, right?

When I try with this PR, however, I still end up with the same `.pdb` file name as with `master`.

Am I understanding what this issue and this PR is supposed to be doing correctly?
author: dsnopek

body:
> If I'm understanding the issue correctly, we need to rename the `.pdb` file to contain same number of characters as the original, and presently it's making a longer name.
> 
> I just tested with `master` using the godot-cpp test project, and I end up with these files:
> 
> * `~libgdexample.windows.template_debug.dev.x86_64.dll`
> * `~libgdexample.windows.template_debug.dev.x86_64_0.pdb`
> 
> And, assuming I've got this right, it should be something like `~libgdexample.windows.template_debug.dev.x86_0.pdb`, right?
> 
> When I try with this PR, however, I still end up with the same `.pdb` file name as with `master`.
> 
> Am I understanding what this issue and this PR is supposed to be doing correctly?

My apologies, a previous revision of the description had more information on repro.

This branch is only taken if the path buffer in the DLL is too small and it needs to shorten the filename. This will only occur if your build output directory is at `C:/` or if your compiler outputs PDB paths as relative to the DLL.

The easiest repro is to use a godot-rust sample project, as cargo always outputs relative paths. If successfully repro'd you'll get the warning mentioned in the description. 

Previously symbols would not load, as the written path did not match the filename on disk.

The reason you don't commonly run into this issue is because this code always writes relative paths and places a copy of the PDB in the same directory. This means that the new path is (almost?) always shorter than the full paths typically written by MSVC.
author: SK83RJOSH

body:
> This branch is only taken if the path buffer in the DLL is too small and it needs to shorten the filename. This will only occur if your build output directory is at `C:/` or if your compiler outputs PDB paths as relative to the DLL.
>
> The easiest repro is to use a godot-rust sample project, as cargo always outputs relative paths. If successfully repro'd you'll get the warning mentioned in the description.

Do you have such a project that can just be used with `cargo build`? Note that Godot isn't a Rust-based project, so not every maintainer may have Rust toolchains installed.

Or if you could specify more precisely how people could get the DLL behavior you described on godot-cpp, that would also be great! :slightly_smiling_face:
author: Bromeon

body:
> Do you have such a project that can just be used with `cargo build`? Note that Godot isn't a Rust-based project, so not every maintainer may have Rust toolchains installed.

Aye, and I'm not expecting that they do, it was just the simplest test case I could think of. No, nothing I can provide at the moment. 🙂

> Or if you could more precisely how people could get the DLL behavior you described on godot-cpp, that would also be great! 🙂

I suppose you could try passing [/PDBALTPATH](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/build/reference/pdbaltpath-use-alternate-pdb-path) to the linker with the name of the PDB only (`my_lib.pdb`).

As mentioned this should also be reproducible by setting your output directory to the root of any drive (you can map a directory to a drive letter to make this easier).

Beyond those two options you would need to modify the code to trigger this consistently.

The core issue, ultimately though, is fairly self evident from the linked commit. The find replace caused the append function to use the wrong overload. The substring length is interpreted as a bool.

If it's simpler I can just fix that single bug rather than fix the overall functionality.

Once confirmed, I'll create a separate branch for the general improvements, and include only the necessary fix in this one to make things easier on y'all.

Hopefully that means we can get it in before the next release. 🙏
author: SK83RJOSH

body:
Alright, confirmed that change is enough to fix symbol resolution. The rest of the changes were nice for clarity of the logic overall, but I likely shouldn't have bundled it into this PR.

For clarity on what's been fixed, it was choosing this overload:
`inline Error String::append_utf8(const Span<char> &p_range, bool p_skip_cr = false)`

It is now using this overload:
`Error String::append_utf8(const char *p_utf8, int p_len = -1, bool p_skip_cr = false)`

body:
**Testing project:** [benchmark-resource-set-path.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/23140543/benchmark-resource-set-path.zip)

## Benchmark

### Specifications

Linux Mint 22.2 (Zara) on X11 - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - integrated AMD Radeon Graphics (RADV PHOENIX) - AMD Ryzen 7 8745H w/ Radeon 780M Graphics (16 threads) - 25.22 GiB memory

The following results were measured using an editor built with `scons`.

Use a Resource array and set the resource paths of its elements to simulate the cached Resource.
Path format: `res://{idx}.res`.

Given that the number of cached items is limited in practice, the number of loop calls is increased to get a more accurate measurement.

### Case 0: Change the path to be empty

Sets the resource paths of all elements in the array to empty, and then resets the resource paths of all its elements again.

| times=5 | cache count | loop count | min cost | max cost | average cost |
