body:
**Testing project:** [benchmark-resource-set-path-03.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/23443761/benchmark-resource-set-path-03.zip)

## Benchmark

### Specifications

Linux Mint 22.2 (Zara) on X11 - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - integrated AMD Radeon Graphics (RADV PHOENIX) - AMD Ryzen 7 8745H w/ Radeon 780M Graphics (16 threads) - 25.22 GiB memory

The following results were measured using an editor built with `scons`.

Use a Resource array and set the resource paths of its elements to simulate the cached Resource.
Path format: `res://{idx}.res`.

Given that the number of cached items is limited in practice, the number of loop calls is increased to get a more accurate measurement.

### Case 0: Change the path to be empty

Sets the resource paths of all elements in the array to empty, and then resets the resource paths of all its elements again.

| times=5 | cache count | loop count | min cost | max cost | average cost |
| :----: | :-----: | :-----: | :----: | :----: | :----: |
| Before | 100 | 10000 | 448ms | 459ms | 451ms (100%) |
| After | 100 | 10000 | 419ms | 422ms | 420ms (93.1%) | 
| Before | 200 | 10000 | 894ms | 934ms | 905ms (100%) |
| After | 200 | 10000 | 833ms | 847ms | 838ms (92.6%) | 
| Before | 500 | 10000 | 2275ms | 2292ms | 2285ms (100%) |
| After | 500 | 10000 | 2097ms | 2148ms | 2120ms (92.8%) | 
| Before | 1000 | 10000 | 4576ms | 4671ms | 4630ms (100%) |
| After | 1000 | 10000 | 4297ms | 4353ms | 4328ms (93.5%) | 


### Case 1-2: Modify the path (not empty)

Change the resource paths of all elements, and then resets the resource paths of all its elements again.
Path format: `old_path` + `"test"` 

| times=5 | cache count | loop count | min cost | max cost | average cost |
| :----: | :-----: | :-----: | :----: | :----: | :----: |
| Before | 100 | 10000 | 590ms | 599ms | 593ms (100%) |
| After | 100 | 10000 | 563ms | 573ms | 567ms (95.6%) | 
| Before | 200 | 10000 | 1205ms | 1211ms | 1207ms (100%) |
| After | 200 | 10000 | 1157ms | 1169ms | 1160ms (96.1%) |

| Before | 1000 | 10000 | 6903ms | 6950ms | 6928ms (100%) |
| After | 1000 | 10000 | 6612ms | 6791ms | 6661ms (96.1%) |

author: Repiteo

body:
Looks fine, but I wonder if it wouldn't be better to pass the Vector2/Rect2 as reference and modify it inside the function, instead of returning new one 🤔
In many cases you create a new rect when there wasn't one. Then again, it's super cheap, so maybe it's not a concern, idk.
author: KoBeWi

body:
> Looks fine, but I wonder if it wouldn't be better to pass the Vector2/Rect2 as reference and modify it inside the function, instead of returning new one 🤔 In many cases you create a new rect when there wasn't one. Then again, it's super cheap, so maybe it's not a concern, idk.

It would make code even simpler imo, I can make that change
author: dagarsar

body:
> It would make code even simpler imo, I can make that change

Actually, I don't think this should be done. There are a few instances of both methods being used where the output is assigned to another variable and the input is used later, so these methods should not modify the input.
author: dagarsar

body: Thanks!

body: Can you upload a testing project which can be used to benchmark this?
author: Calinou

body:
Did you profile this? This code should be trivially vectorized by the compiler already.

I haven't worked on `blit_rect` at all. But I did extensively test `Image::_convert` and couldn't beat the performance of the automatic vectorization that the compiler does already. It is similar in that it copies over one pixel at a time.
author: clayjohn

body:
In a specific use case on Nintendo Switch (for which I can't really share) we did see an improvement of roughly 22 seconds (we generate a lot of npcs via atlases that get combined at runtime via a couple of blits). The whole process took 1 minute and 32 seconds with previous code whilst it takes 1 minute and 11 seconds with the per-line copy. 

I can see to create a small reproducible sample project for benching it also on other devices.
author: Rinnegatamante

body:
Here's a small project to bench this and here's the result for my specific setup. (The code has been ran on Godot 4.3.1 since that's the one we use but the same applies for current upstream).

[blit.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22030349/blit.zip)

With original blit_rect code:
```
Godot Engine v4.3.1.stable.custom_build.370864999 (2025-08-20 17:11:15 UTC) - https://godotengine.org
Vulkan 1.4.312 - Forward Mobile - Using Device #0: NVIDIA - NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 Ti

```

With per-line code:
```
Godot Engine v4.3.1.stable.custom_build.fae954344 (2025-08-28 15:03:58 UTC) - https://godotengine.org
Vulkan 1.4.312 - Forward Mobile - Using Device #0: NVIDIA - NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 Ti

This code also doesn't actually hit the full rect copy which would realistically also give an even higher benefit.
author: Rinnegatamante

body:
Ooooooh, those are very nice results!

To confirm, you have full optimizations enabled in both builds right?
author: clayjohn

body: The results are from an editor regular build (so speed_trace one, which is O2). The Switch gain was from a release build instead. I can re-run the test on release build as well.
author: Rinnegatamante

body:
> The results are from an editor regular build (so speed_trace one, which is O2). The Switch gain was from a release build instead. I can re-run the test on release build as well.

Thanks for confirming. I just wanted to make sure that optimizations were on, no need to test again. 

I guess auto-vectorization can only do so much here and simply can't compete with the case where you can just memcpy the whole thing at once
author: clayjohn

body:
> I guess auto-vectorization can only do so much here and simply can't compete with the case where you can just memcpy the whole thing at once

To add some context: Vectorization can at most use the latest SIMD we have enabled (SSE4.2). `memcpy` uses the best SIMD Operation available in the CPU.

This makes sense to me, key based sorting is much simpler (and apparently faster!) than custom comparator based sorting. Looking at your benchmarks, this could be worth it.
However, judging by the proposal, this is not a highly demanded feature. Perhaps it would be better to offer this function with a GDExtension for now? We could add it to Godot if more demand surfaces.

author: TCROC

body:
That call stack was in your original crash report, and surprised me, as this is happening when the app first opens. This is the first set of draw commands issued by Godot, to display the splash screen. This _might_ be a red herring. The function `RenderingDeviceDriverMetal::command_bind_push_constants` is only two lines:

```cpp
MDCommandBuffer *cb = (MDCommandBuffer *)(p_cmd_buffer.id);
cb->encode_push_constant_data(p_shader, p_data);
```

The crash is a NULL pointer access at offset `0x24` (`36`). Examining the registers:

```
x0: 0x000000010d206590   x1: 0x000000016f2edd20   x2: 0x000000016f2edd18   x3: 0x0000000000000000
    x4: 0x000000010d66c238
```

That align with the arguments of the function:

```
void RenderingDeviceDriverMetal::command_bind_push_constants(CommandBufferID p_cmd_buffer, ShaderID p_shader, uint32_t p_dst_first_index, VectorView<uint32_t> p_data)
```

| symbol | register | value |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| `x0` | `this` | 0x000000010d206590
| `x1` | `p_cmd_buffer` | 0x000000016f2edd20
| `x2` | `p_shader` | 0x000000016f2edd18
| `x3` | `p_dst_first_index` | 0x0000000000000000
| `x4` | `p_data` | 0x000000010d66c238
| `sp` | stack pointer | 0x000000016f2edc40

> [!NOTE]
>
> `p_cmd_buffer` and `p_shader` refer to the stack, so they must dereference the value on the stack to obtain the underlying pointer to a heap-allocated object.

If I look at the first few lines of `encode_push_constant_data`, given it is likely the function was inlined, we see:

```cpp
void MDCommandBuffer::encode_push_constant_data(RDD::ShaderID p_shader, VectorView<uint32_t> p_data) {
	switch (type) {
		case MDCommandBufferStateType::Render: {
			MDRenderShader *shader = (MDRenderShader *)(p_shader.id);
			if (shader->push_constants.vert.binding == -1 && shader->push_constants.frag.binding == -1) {

```

I'm certain the `type` of `MDCommandBuffer` command is `MDCommandBufferStateType::Render`, as this is blitting the splash screen, therefore the first pointer access in this function is:

```
if (shader->push_constants.vert.binding == -1 
```

The offset of the `push_constants` field is, not coincidentally, `0x24`. So we appear to be looking at a null pointer access of `p_shader`. We know this call stack is drawing the boot image, which means the `blit.glsl` shader is being used, and this error suggests it did not compile. The Metal driver returned `ShaderID(0)` and likely logged an error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
>
> Obtaining logs is going to necessary in understanding what is going on.
author: stuartcarnie

body: Also note that because Metal returned `ShaderID(0)`, the rendering device should attempt to recompile the `blit.glsl` shader from source. It is unclear why would that fail. It looks like `blit.glsl` has changed recently, but there doesn't appear to be anything significant in those changes.

body: Hmm I don't think this is quite it. The line where the center is added should be removed altogether - the first vertex doesn't have to be added as it already is present in the vertex array. Then below, where triangles are constructed with indices, one index of every triangle should point to the first vertex (the one that originally pointed to the center). Also, the function should not create the first and last vertices in the arc and instead share the corner vertices from where the line starts to avoid issues similar to https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/100020 (might even be necessary to fix #110030 properly). That would also need to be fixed for the joints. Edit: That's a probably bit more involved fix than what you singed in for - sorry for making it sound so simple in the issue. You can obviously continue if you have the time to spare but also do not feel pressured to do so.
author: Lielay9

body: There is already a function for creating arcs based on preexisting vertices in the file, used for round joints. I think we can repurpose it here. It's just a bit harder for the begin cap because it is drawn before any vertices of the line exist, but we can call `strip_begin` first in this case and not call it later. I tested more thoroughly this time and I didn't see any difference in behavior from main (minus the gaps not longer being present) but please let me know if you find anything wrong.

vkCreateFramebuffer(): pCreateInfo->pAttachments[0] has mip levelCount of 8 but only a single mip level (levelCount ==  1) is allowed when creating a Framebuffer.
The Vulkan spec states: If flags does not include VK_FRAMEBUFFER_CREATE_IMAGELESS_BIT, each element of pAttachments must only specify a single mip level (https://docs.vulkan.org/spec/latest/chapters/renderpass.html#VUID-VkFramebufferCreateInfo-pAttachments-00883)

Any loads or stores with the variable will produce undefined values to the whole image (not just the texel being accessed).

body:
I tested on Linux Vulkan 1.4.318 - Forward+, integrated AMD Radeon Graphics (RADV RENOIR)
___
The first error means that you directly used the texture with mipmaps as the framebuffer color attachment somewhere. Instead, you should use the mipmap level 0 slice as the color attachment.

>Everything seems to work on Vulkan for me. I'm not all that familiar with `CopyEffect::make_mipmap` and `copy.glsl` but the shader seems to have output options ofr32f, rgba8, and rgba16f. And looking at a frame in RenderDoc it looks correct.

Although `copy.glsl` has rgba8 variant(`DST_IMAGE_8BIT`), the `make_mipmap` does not have a parameter to enable it.
(This is not an issue with Mobile renderer (`make_mipmap_raster`) because `blur_raster.glsl` supports any unorm/sfloat texture formats)
author: beicause

body: I figured out why I was not seeing the errors. I was launching the project manager with `--gpu-validation` and opening the project. I needed to launch the project directly with `--gpu-validation`.

body:
> `tonemap.glsl` and `tonemap_mobile.glsl` seem to have many duplicate code. Should there be a `tonemap_inc.glsl`? which can make it easier for other PRs (like #106940) to rebase.

No, I intentionally duplicated the code since I expect these shaders to diverge even more as time goes on. Trying to share code between them is what led us to leaving a huge amount of performance on the table. This is a case where having optimized code for each platform is worthwhile and saves us literally milliseconds per frame.
author: clayjohn
allenwp

body:
> > > `tonemap.glsl` and `tonemap_mobile.glsl` seem to have many duplicate code. Should there be a `tonemap_inc.glsl`? which can make it easier for other PRs (like #106940) to rebase.
> > 
> > 
> > No, I intentionally duplicated the code since I expect these shaders to diverge even more as time goes on. Trying to share code between them is what led us to leaving a huge amount of performance on the table. This is a case where having optimized code for each platform is worthwhile and saves us literally milliseconds per frame.
> 
> Why aren't specialization constants an option for this case?

Whoops, I hadn't looked into this PR thoroughly enough: I see now that specialization constants were added **AND** `tonemap.glsl` was split into two.

I presume there's a substantial reason that specialization constants weren't an option for optimizations that prompted splitting `tonemap.glsl` into two files?

author: allenwp

body:
I'm struggling to review the code changes of this PR because I'm unfamiliar with both the existing and new glow gathering code. This is a "me" problem and not a problem with this PR.

...But I mention it, because it leads me to some thoughts that I figure are worth mentioning just in case it's helpful:

This PR seems to be a combination of:

- Swapping out the Mobile glow gather implementation and changing how the rendering passes work in relation to this
- Separating `tonemap.glsl` into two files
- Adding specialization constants

This is pie-in-the-sky thinking, but an alternative approach may be to add specialization constants to the tonemap process in a separate PR. This PR could explore specialization constants for both Forward+ and Mobile on different mobile and desktop hardware.

There is also a part of the Godot PR process that I struggle with in general: although a single commit is best for merging, it makes things harder to review for a person not very familiar with the code when a PR combines multiple changes, like this one.

Anyway, not sure if my thoughts are helpful. I understand people only have so much time to work on these things and there is some efficiency to be gained in the development process by bulking multiple changes into one commit. I'm not sure if the extra complexity during the review process balances that or if there is risk introduced by bugs that couldn't be caught during review due to the extra complexity of the single commit during the review process. (This is likely a discussion that is beyond this PR?)
author: allenwp

body:
Oh! I've been so focused on finding any outstanding issues that I failed to voice my positive thoughts about this PR:

- Changes the way that glow is gathered and passes are handled on Mobile to minimize texture reads and greatly improve performance on devices that can't read these textures quickly: 👍👍😄😄
- Adds specialization constants to resolve issues with insane amounts of branching that saturates ALUs: 👍👍😄😄

These changes in this PR are extremely valuable and I'm really looking forward to having these merged in! It will be a game changer in performance on mobile!

author: allenwp

body:
>This test does highlight that level 1 is the only regression in this PR...

I don't intend to make any improvements to glow level 1. I searched Godot projects on Github and couldn't find any that relied on level 1. In practice it is kind of useless because the kernel is so narrow that it doesn't look like anything. Ironically level 1 is the most expensive to compute because it is reading from the highest res source, and writing out the highest number of pixels. 

What this PR does to avoid that cost is simply downsample instead of blur level 1. So what you are comparing is a raw downsampled image to one that has already had an expensive layer of blur applied. They are not intended to match at all
author: clayjohn

body:
> What this PR does to avoid that cost is simply downsample instead of blur level 1. So what you are comparing is a raw downsampled image to one that has already had an expensive layer of blur applied. They are not intended to match at all

This makes sense and matches my understanding.

This is why I suggested we maybe remove level 1 entirely, since it adds jaggies and doesn't work as expected. To do this, I guess we could hide level 1 in the editor and force its intensity to always `0.0`, even if a script tries to set it to something else?

My thought is that if level 1 is left in this state, it will both appear as a bug and also introduce risk of games being made to look worse if upgraded from a previous version that uses level 1 in the blur effect.

There is also a possibility that in some scenes it looks acceptable, but other scenes show the jaggies... So glow is made more difficult to use because jaggies might appear in scenes other than the one the glow was initially configured for. An example is when you have rectangular, axis-aligned objects in your setup scene that only rotate off axis in other scenes.

(Said differently, I'm concerned that this level 1 behaviour produces a bad user experience.)

I have no problem doing that!

>

Let's go with that approach then! One other wrinkle is how the "normalize" function works: level 1 should not contribute to the calculation of normalized levels, in case it's been set through script or by a resource that was saved with an earlier version of Godot. I think that should handle all the edge cases to make this a good user experience...
author: allenwp

body:
I'm Strongly against the opinion of removing level 1 glow as it does Have uses (at least in Forward+)

Some very tiny very bright objects for example get a glow only using level 1 glow and that can be very much desired (again F+ only, I don't know about other renderers)

If anything I'd just document the fact that it's the most expensive level and that it should be avoided if you're working on/for weaker hardware.
author: mrjustaguy

body:
> I'm Strongly against the opinion of removing level 1 glow as it does Have uses (at least in Forward+)
> 
> Some very tiny very bright objects for example get a glow only using level 1 glow and that can be very much desired (again F+ only, I don't know about other renderers)
> 
> If anything I'd just document the fact that it's the most expensive level and that it should be avoided if you're working on/for weaker hardware.

I think you are misunderstanding what Allen is suggesting. Level 1 will still be calculated. Allen is suggesting removing the slider so level one can't be added to the final visual output, but it is still used in the _intermediate_ blur. So very tiny bright objects will still be picked up by glow, but you won't be able to add the jaggie infested single pixel width layer on top of the rest of the glow. 

Take a look at his screenshots above to see what he is talking about. 

Also this PR is for the mobile renderer, so your concern about the Forward+ renderer is totally off topic

@allenwp Rebased and pushed changes to implement the changes you made recently to the tonemapper. 

I ended up catching a bug in the sampling I used that was responsible for making the jaggies extra bad at level 1. They are now a lot less bad (still noticeable on single pixel features and thin diagonal lines), but probably tolerable for a lot of games. So I opted to change the default amount for level 1 to 0, but I did not remove it altogether. I think we should leave it in now and see if people complain.

author: clayjohn

body: In the latest update, not only were the glow level defaults changed, but `glow_hdr_bleed_threshold` was also changed from `1.0` to `0.0`. This changes glow from being something that is is designed to only take effect when values are becoming brighter than the display can reproduce to being something that is more like a bloom effect. HDR Scale does have an impact on this, but this is a substantial default change and I'm curious to know why this change was introduced. Maybe the HDR threshold default change would be better for a different PR?
author: allenwp

body:
> In the latest update, not only were the glow level defaults changed, but `glow_hdr_bleed_threshold` was also changed from `1.0` to `0.0`. This changes glow from being something that is is designed to only take effect when values are becoming brighter than the display can reproduce to being something that is more like a bloom effect. HDR Scale does have an impact on this, but this is a substantial default change and I'm curious to know why this change was introduced. Maybe the HDR threshold default change would be better for a different PR?

2 reasons:
1. Its more "correct", the method of thresholding before blurring and accumulating blur is really dated and never had any physical basis. Setting the threshold to 0.0 looks better in almost every situation. So this is a nice change to go along with our shift to accumulating in linear space and defaulting to screen mode instead of softlight. 
2. The threshold was creating a lot of unnecessary aliasing

I'd prefer to make the change now while we are changing other defaults.
author: clayjohn

body:
Pushed another update after a discussion with Allen on Rocketchat. I brought back 10 bit debanding in the mobile tonemap shader when SMAA is in use. In that case the output of the tonemap shader goes into an intermediate RGB10A2 buffer, so debanding needs to be 10 bit. 

I also introduced debanding to the upscale/gather pass for glow. An unfortunate side effect of this approach is that accumulating glow in multiple 10 bit passes is prone to banding. Previously we accumulated glow in the tonemap shader, so the accumulation was done with full 32 bit floating point values, so banding wasn't as prevalent. Any banding that was created was dealt with by the normal debanding pass in the tonemap shader. The impact of debanding can be noticeable when the intensity of glow is very high since glow intensity is a straight multiplier to the final result of the glow. IMO this is an acceptable tradeoff for people who need glow and also need to use RGB10A2 buffers. The only other alternative is to switch to full RGBA16 for glow.
author: clayjohn

body: > I ended up catching a bug in the sampling I used that was responsible for making the jaggies extra bad at level 1. They are now a lot less bad (still noticeable on single pixel features and thin diagonal lines), but probably tolerable for a lot of games. I'm having a hard time seeing how they're better. This is what I see with HDR 2D disabled, HDR Threshold `1.0` for stable comparison with Oct 10 screenshot. | Mobile (this PR Oct 10) | Mobile (this PR Oct 24) | Mobile (master) | --- | --- | --- | ![mobile](https://github.com/allenwp/godot-glow-comparison/raw/refs/heads/main/glow_comparison/level_1//update_mobile.webp) | | I've tripple checked and I still get the same result from the windows build artifact from the checks page of this PR. If it's not possible to further improve, it's still probably best to hide and disable level one for Mobile for the reasons previously discussed. (Also, I found that the screenshots weren't saving correctly for my automated tests above, but I think it's specific to HDR 2D. Maybe there's something wrong with the image saver(???) Regardless, I don't have time to fix that, so I've saved these two new comparisons manually with good 'ol print-screen instead.) And I've got to head off for vacation and won't be around to do any testing for a few weeks. Good luck! **Edit:** Oh, one last thing: Feel free to include #111333 in this PR if you'd like. Might be better than making a change later that is nothing more than a variable rename. 👋
author: allenwp

body:
> I'm having a hard time seeing how they're better. This is what I see with HDR 2D disabled, HDR Threshold 1.0 for stable comparison with Oct 10 screenshot.

If you swap between your images you can see a huge difference! But more importantly, if you look at it overlaid on actual game content with multiple layers the difference is much more apparent. The jaggies line up better with the underlying geometry and blend into the other layers better. On larger geometry its not even noticeable at reasonable settings. 

Now, if you stretch things to their limits only use level one, then ya, it looks bad. But I'm not really worried about people doing that. The important thing is that it is no longer something that is guaranteed to look bad no matter what. With the earlier draft of this PR it was always worse to use level 1 than to not use it. That isn't true anymore, so I think it is fine to keep. 

If you can't tell, I want to avoid breaking compatibility more than I already have. That change is a compat breakage bigger than everything else in this PR combined, so it should only be done with extreme caution. Since it is no longer absolutely necessary, we shouldn't do it. If users complain a lot during the beta phase we can re-evaluate removing it.

body: I think it would make more sense if the undo/redo action ended up being `Duplicate Node(s)` instead of the transform, since in terms of importance of what is actually is happening within the single action is node(s) are being duplicated first and foremost and how it's being transformed is secondary and not worth mentioning.
author: ryevdokimov

body: good point, adjusted the name of the undo/redo action :+1:
author: winston-yallow

body:
I thought about that and tried it at first, but the code became a bit convoluted. The issue is that `SceneTreeDock` mixes node duplication logic with undo/redo logic, and separating this would mean splitting it into three parts: duplication, tracking the duplication changes, then creating the undo/redo based on the changes. It is possible, but at least to me it seemed to introduce a lot of unnecessary complexity into `SceneTreeDock`.

Reference to duplication code in `SceneTreeDock`: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/825ef2387f87de1c350696886e6c50b039204cef/editor/docks/scene_tree_dock.cpp#L879
author: winston-yallow

body:
I think your flipping of the undo/redo mechanism to focus on duplication instead of transform will solve that problem though. You would be letting the `SceneTreeDock` do all the work for undo/redo (which is just duplication). Undo/Redo for duplication stores a reference of the node including its transform, meaning that the transform of the node is "saved" regardless of when the undo/redo happened, it will still be correct. They shouldn't go back to the transform of the node(s) they were duplicated from because that information is actually NOT stored anywhere, which is the reverse of what one might expect.

Duplicate (ref created) and added to Undo/Redo -> Transform (ref updated), skip creating undo/redo action for transform -> Undo (ref'ed node removed) -> Redo (ref'ed node restored with its correct transform i.e  wherever it was last located).
author: ryevdokimov

body:
I am not sure if I understand what you mean completely. Do you suggest to create the undo/redo already when the transform is started? My issue is that I don't want to create two history actions for what feels like one action from the user, so the duplication must happen on transform start, while the history commit must happen on transform end (when the new transform is known). That way there is only a single history entry. Letting the scene tree dock handle the undo/redo would mean to either have the history entry at transform start when the nodes are duplicated, or changing the scene tree dock logic quite a bit to split duplication and undo/redo logic.

In any case I'll take another look and try to make the duplication logic in `SceneTreeDock` reusable in some way. I will convert this PR to a draft for now as I won't have time to work on this before the next weekend, plus this is unlikely to go into 4.5 so there is a lot of time to work on this anyway.
author: winston-yallow

body:
I took a look a little bit more, and yeah, I can see it getting tricky specifically with cancelling. The undo/redo itself works fine when using `SceneTreeDock`, but as you mentioned we don't want to add the duplication to the undo history when the shift + transform begins. 

I just wonder what would happen is this functionality was desired in 2D as well. It becomes a tradeoff between complexity in `SceneTreeDock` and duplication in a few classes.
author: ryevdokimov

body:
I think `SPACE` might be a better choice of key if possible.
1. `SHIFT` is used to toggle snapping (unless this is being changed). You might want to toggle snapping _and_ duplicate at the same time.
2. This being a high frequency action, it might be ergonomically nicer to use a strong finger instead of the pinky.
author: alazifk

body:
I would prefer to keep it as Shift:
1. The default toggle for snapping is control in the default "Godot" navigation scheme, so it does not interfere by default. If you do use an alternate navigation scheme, then snapping is still possible: Copy is determined while initiating the transform, while snapping is determined while placing. So you can still copy an object by holding shift down while starting the drag. And you can still snap the object by holding shift down while moving the object around. These work independently.
2. I personally find holding space down more annoying, but probably also because I am not used to it and don't have muscle memory for it. Shift on the other hand is more widely used as an action modifier in general but also specifically for copying (for example the Source 2 level editor Hammer uses Shift for copy too)
3. The 2D viewport uses space for panning. If we want to implement this feature for 2D too then it would be nice to have a consistent modifier for this, which Shift can be.
4. Implementing this for space is a bit more involved. It's not impossible, but using modifier keys is a lot nicer to work with compared to multi-key presses to keep the code clean and maintainable.

On another note, I think I'll have some time around the turn of the year. Hopefully I can finally improve the undo/redo system by making at least some code from the SceneTreeDock reusable (and rebase the PR in the process)
author: winston-yallow

body: Now this would definitely be an amazing feature to have! Glad to see there is work being done on this. **EDIT:** In 2D, holding Alt has no currently assigned effect, whereas Shift and Ctrl both activate snapping modes. Perhaps Alt could still be utilized?

author: knn217

body:
@knn217 In your first screenshot there are three diferent keyboard ids. Is this because you really plugged 3 different keyboards to test, or perhaps windows simply did changed the keyboard id in between keystrokes?

Also, does the windows api expose some way of knowing the device ordering? to know what is the first, the second, the third keyboard and os on? the device ids for joypads that godot returns are always ordered starting from zero, so that if you have 4 joypads they will have device ids 0, 1, 2 and 3. Is it possible to at least emulate this with keyboards? even if the OSs api don't tell you the keyboard ordering, couldn't we store a dictionary mapping the raw keyboard ids returned by the OS into a zero based ordered id? even if we just make up this ordering ourselves? for example, if windows returns ids 687, -94601 and 12687 whe order them ascendingly and give them the ids 0 (for -94601), 1 (for 687) and 2 (for 12687).
author: Gnumaru

body:
@Gnumaru 
> @knn217 In your first screenshot there are three diferent keyboard ids. Is this because you really plugged 3 different keyboards to test, or perhaps windows simply did changed the keyboard id in between keystrokes?

Windows can change the keyboard ID (handle), but only when user unplug -> plug keyboard back in. The ID stays the same while plugged in though.
I have updated the test to show this, by unpluging the keyboard, I was able to change the keyboard ID 10 times

> Also, does the windows api expose some way of knowing the device ordering? to know what is the first, the second, the third keyboard and os on? the device ids for joypads that godot returns are always ordered starting from zero, so that if you have 4 joypads they will have device ids 0, 1, 2 and 3

I have not found any API like that.

> Is it possible to at least emulate this with keyboards? even if the OSs api don't tell you the keyboard ordering, couldn't we store a dictionary mapping the raw keyboard ids returned by the OS into a zero based ordered id?
Like I have shown before, after unplugged, Windows assigns a new ID to the keyboard when plugged back in, making the previous ID deprecated.

I think it's possible to emulate this behavior, by using this API: 
https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/inputdev/wm-input-device-change
I have not used this myself but this seems to provide notifications for whenever a device is plugged/unplugged

Here's how I understand the flowchart should be implemented from your description:
- device plugged in -> Add to map { "raw kb id": "lowest available slot" } ->update "lowest available slot"
- device unplugged -> Remove from map at key "raw kb id" ->update "lowest available slot"
- If a key is pressed, contains a raw ID not existed in map -> insert it the same as "device plugged in"

The last step is to solve the issue with laptop's keyboards being always plugged in, and also any keyboards that are already plugged in.
author: knn217

body:
I have added another commit to support:
- Return incremental ID for raw keyboards, starting from 0
- Register ID by plugging in the keyboard or pressing a key
- Unregister by unplugging the keyboard

Test:
- I have updated the test cases to match this. I also tested unplug->plugin again, the keyboard was able to regain its device ID before unplug, so in the test the ID never get above 2 (I have 2 keyboards plugged in, and the laptop's keyboard). 
- The only issue with this approach that I can think of is that the device IDs are reset each time the app closes (since they are managed in a map), so they need to be registered again. 
- I have no joypads, so I'm not sure if this is the same as how joypads work. Does godot have a way to memorize the joypads' registered order in previous process?

*NOTE: 
- I didn't find any Win32 API that returns the device ordering, so it needs to be handled by Godot. 
- I tried `GetRawInputDeviceList`, but the ordering of the device list can be changed by unplugging a keyboard. So unplugging keyboard A will affect the index of keyboard B in the list. The order is also unclear to me, it does not follow the order of plugging in. 
- Here's the test branch: [https://github.com/knn217/godot/tree/raw-keyboard-id-test-branch](url)

author: knn217

body:
> Currently, Wayland doesn't support distinguishing key-presses based on device. Maybe in the future they will consider it

Hi, Wayland should support this behavior already. Compositors can expose multiple `wl_seat` globals, each with a different keyboard, mouse, and touchscreen. Note though that some compositors (e.g. sway) merge multiple devices under a single seat unless the user says otherwise. This is not mandatory according to the spec, it's just compositor-specific policy.
author: deralmas

body:
> Hi, Wayland should support this behavior already. Compositors can expose multiple `wl_seat` globals, each with a different keyboard, mouse, and touchscreen. Note though that some compositors (e.g. sway) merge multiple devices under a single seat unless the user says otherwise. This is not mandatory according to the spec, it's just compositor-specific policy.

I see, but is there a way to make the compositor expose seats from Godot? Compositors are outside of application's control as I understand.
author: knn217

body:
> I see, but is there a way to make the compositor expose seats from Godot? Compositors are outside of application's control as I understand.

Hi, I'm not really sure what you mean. Godot can't play with input settings as they're non-standard and outside of the scope of the Wayland protocol. I also don't know how other compositors outside of sway handle it, they could as well make a new seat every time, it's up to them.

If the compositor is configured (by default or by the user) to expose multiple input devices, godot will be able to detect and use all of them. In fact, we already do that, we already have logic for handling multiple input devices as we're required to do that.

body:
> edit - also, if merged, please fix the typo in the commit message, I don't want to trigger a build just for that... (Stablize -> Stabilize)

We don't use GitHub's squash and merge feature on the main Godot repository, since it loses the merge commit in the process. We prefer keeping the merge commit to make the Git history more self-documented (even if that makes it longer when not using the `--no-merges` option).

Since merge commits keep the original commit message, you have to fix the commit message in your fork.

author: TokageItLab

body:
Tried using this for procedural animation. From my testing it seems to work really well, though I will share a few thoughts/suggestions:

- It would be useful to have a button on the top bar that would reset the IK, since currently to do that you have to toggle the modifier active on and off.
- IterateIK3D derived modifiers can be made deterministic by resetting the IK chain every time before iterating it, which could be made into a toggle. Using it would would require a higher iteration count which would mean it'd be more performance intensive though, and It might lead to the IK snapping under extreme positions, but it still might be useful for more predictable movement.
(I know TwoBoneIK already provides determinism but obviously it's limited to two bones and requires a pole target)
(I'm also pretty sure this is how IK in Blender operates, I think it uses the Jacobian algorithm, and always resets the IK before iterating it)
- There should be a way to lock a specific axis from rotation. Currently it's only possible to constraint rotation to only one axis but it would be nice if it were possible to also constraint it to _all but one_ axes. It would come in handy in cases like this one, where I want the IK to work on all axes but Y/Roll.
<details>
<summary>Video Example</summary>

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0f29c3d3-7e6e-4973-a538-76c6f2c5a869

</details>

- On a related note, it might also be useful to have a "None" rotation axis mode, which would completely disable the rotation of that specific bone. I think this can already be done by using JointLimitationCone3D with 0 radius, but this might be more convenient.
- Blender gives you an option to influence an IK chain by the target's rotation, which gives you an extra degree of control like such:
If this is deemed useful it would definitely be material for a follow up PR though.

<details>
<summary>Video Example</summary>

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/37d1d5be-e998-4991-84f9-fa039ce02e7f

</details>

- Another one related to rotation, it would also be very useful to have an option to set the end bone rotation to the rotation of the target, for example so you can keep a foot aligned to the ground.
This could also work as it's own modifier, and during my testing I made a simple one for that purpose, which I will share in case anyone finds it useful:
<details>
<summary>CopyTargetRotationModifier3D</summary>
A skeleton modifier that aligns the bone based on the target node in world space, while completely cancelling out any external transformation.

```py
@tool
class_name CopyTargetRotationModifier3D
extends SkeletonModifier3D

@export var target: Node3D
@export var bone_index: int

func _process_modification_with_delta(_delta: float) -> void:
	get_skeleton().set_bone_global_pose(bone_index, Transform3D(target.global_basis * get_skeleton().global_basis.inverse(), get_skeleton().get_bone_global_pose(bone_index).origin))
```

</details>
author: ettiSurreal

body: @ettiSurreal Hi, thank you for the feedback! > It would be useful to have a button on the top bar that would reset the IK, since currently to do that you have to toggle the modifier active on and off. I'm not sure whether we should escalate to add a reset function to SkeletonModifier3D's BaseClass (since an implementation that simply toggles active might suffice), but I think we can consider it in a later PR. However, I believe there are two scenarios: resetting a single modifier versus resetting multiple modifiers. When a modifier is selected, it should reset that single modifier; when a skeleton is selected, it should reset all modifiers. --- > There should be a way to lock a specific axis from rotation. Currently it's only possible to constraint rotation to only one axis but it would be nice if it were possible to also constraint it to all but one axes. It would come in handy in cases like this one, where I want the IK to work on all axes but Y/Roll. I don't understand this well. Regarding IterateIK, since it only handles Swing, the Twist axis (parent-to-child between chains) is always locked. The RotationAxis option selects and locks the second axis projected onto the plane normal to the Twist axis. Custom mode allows you to specify any second axis. If you're saying you want to perform Twist with IterateIK, that might require a different algorithm than FABR/CCD/Jacobian. --- > CopyTargetRotationModifier3D > A skeleton modifier that aligns the bone based on the target node in world space, while completely cancelling out any external transformation. This has been sent in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110336 already. Regarding ground matching, it would likely involve combining with ray tracing or similar techniques. This would require a setup where the target moves outside the IK system, which should probably be provided as an add-on and falls outside the scope of this PR. Options for constructing specific scenes, like 2D/3D platformer templates or creating PhysicalBones, could be implemented in additional PRs.

body:
> I don't understand this well.

Sorry, I struggle to explain things well sometimes. I figured out I can give a better explanation with Blender though, since it implements exactly what I was suggesting. 
In Blender you have three Lock IK toggles, each of them disallows movement on that axis. But what you can do there that you currently cannot here is lock only one specific axis.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/27337f99-0209-4f3c-9742-1a81d61cfa6d

I understand if something like this is not possible to implement with how the IK was implemented though.
If it is possible however, this could probably be integrated into the current UI by adding three extra options for the Rotation Axis dropdown (`Exclude X`, `Exclude Y`, `Exclude Z`, could not come up with better names), since there are only three combinations you can make.
<sub>_(Fun fact: Blender also gives you "stiffness" sliders for each axis, which effectively act as a factor of each axis instead of a binary lock, implementing something like this does sound a bit overkill for a game engine though)_</sub>
author: ettiSurreal

body:
@yosimba2000 I think that question isn't so much related to IK itself. It's probably related to https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110987 or https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/106846. This PR already included the fix of https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110987.

---

@ettiSurreal I haven't read Blender's source code in detail, so this is just a guess, but it seems those twists could actually be decomposed and calculated separately from IK. In other words, as I wrote in the description, placing the twists from the IK Root's Aim Constraint before the IK processing might achieve this to some extent.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c3c97c64-bd9c-472a-ade5-90c4ae6d4e42

To ensure stability in this case, the deterministic iteration you suggested might be useful. However, since Godot prioritizes performance more than DCCs like Blender, increasing iterations by hundreds per frame might not be the best approach.

Alternatively, there might be a way to apply some correction after the IK instead of before. BTW, I noticed that LookAt/Aim forces the rest position, so I sent a PR https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/111367 adding a relative option. It might be useful for something.

After considering these behaviors, if IterateIK were to provide twist, properties like AllowTwist or TwistLimitation might be implemented instead of axis locking. However, this is currently outside the focus of this PR.
author: TokageItLab

body:
For all the IterateIK nodes, there is a minimum distance setting that stops further IK calculations when this threshold is met. The default value is 0.1, but I noticed that when playing an animation with one of the IterateIK nodes applied, it causes the bones to move in stutters (I'm assuming because it stops further IK updates until the 0.1 threshold is exceeded again).

Setting the minimum distance to 0.0 gives smooth IK animation, but I'm not sure if this is recommended. Is it possible to allow smaller increments in the minimum distance field? It looks like it can only adjust by 0.1 increments.
author: yosimba2000

body: @yosimba2000 Alright, the default value and minimum increment have been set to 0.001. This makes sense since it uses the same unit as physical nodes and EndBoneLength.

body:
ManyBoneIK3D has been renamed to ModifierIK3D now. Since the opinion is raised by other maintainers that the name ManyBoneIK3D is somewhat unclear.

Since the name SkeletonIK3D is unavailable until Godot 5, the following are the candidates proposed among the maintainers:

- IKGroup3D
- NewSkeletonIK3D
- SkeletonInverseKinematics3D
- InverseKinematics3D
- ModifierIK3D (current)

IKGroup3D sounds to have a role beyond that of a superclass (it sounds like a parent class that has an IK as a children in the scene tree) for me.

InverseKinematics3D might be acceptable as a superclass name, but considering Godot as a whole, there was an argument that it was too generic.

SkeletonInverseKinematics3D feels quite verbose, and considering the name FABRIK3D should not be SkeletonFABRIK3D, SkeletonFABRInverseKinematics3D or SkeletonForwardAndBackwardReachingInverseKinematics3D, I believe it should be avoided.

Considering that the words “Skeleton” and “Bone” are used as an identifier to avoid generality, the third identifier could be a "Modifier". Also, considering naming consistency with subclasses like "ChainIK3D", I tentatively prefer "ModifierIK3D".

Please suggest other name candidates if you have any.
author: TokageItLab

body: What about `IKModifier3D` since that seems to be a more consistent naming convention for skeleton modifiers that have the word "modifier" in their name. The only exception to that is `ModifierBoneTarget3D` which is the only one that has it at the start.

> So I gave this a test, but the results seem far less stable than the existing SkeletonIK3D. I think part of the problem might be that there's no way to to make the IK target follow the rotation of the target, like the "Override Tip Basis" setting on SkeletonIK3D.

@jitspoe As I mentioned in the description, we consider this to be the role of BoneConstraint rather than IK, so it is supplemented by https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110336.

Another possible cause is when the change of the bone pose position or scale from the bone rest is included in the chain, but that needs to be supplemented by https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/111815.

FYI, in FABRIK, setting the `Angular Delta Limit` to `180 degrees` (means no limitation) should make the rotation more similar to old SkeletonIK3D.

Try this:

[test-different-ik.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/23408294/test-different-ik.zip)

I made a simplified example.  SkeletonIK3D goes straight to the target, but the FABRIK3D meanders and twists around.

![fabrik_ik](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ea8c6e52-8720-4e8b-bd7c-bc4b9eaae94f)
author: jitspoe

body: @jitspoe As mentioned above, meanders twisting is related to the angular delta limit. The old SkeletonIK has no rotation limit per iteration, making it equivalent to setting an 180-degree angular delta limit. And another difference between old SkeletonIK vs IKModifier3D's FABRIK is whether it is deterministic or not (IKModifier3D's FABRIK is non-deterministic). As mentioned in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110120#issuecomment-3377094276, resetting rotation every frame to the rest makes deterministic results. But this increases the number of calculations required to reach the result, depending on the angular delta limit, impacting performance. In other words, after resetting the rotation in the beggining of the each frame, if the angular delta limit is set too small with less iteration, the end bone will never reach the target, no matter how many frames are progressed. However, in cases without the limitations mentioned above, such as setting the angular delta limit to 180 degrees, performance should not be significantly affected (since a large number of iterations is not required). Therefore, I believe deterministic behavior can be added as an option (I already discussed this with @lyuma during the review meeting).
author: TokageItLab

body: @jitspoe @ettiSurreal @lyuma I sent a PR https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/112524 to add deterministic option, so you can test it.

body:
Hi

I'm currently using Godot 4.6 dev 4, and I'm doing some tests for the new IKs system. While I'm pretty happy with the results when it comes to bones positioning, I don't see anything related to rotation (For example, rotating the hand of a player model in a way the entire arm will react to the changes of the hand) . I wanted to know if I'm missing something or if it's not in the scope of this PR in the first place.

Thanks ahead!
author: YahelBaram

body: @YahelBaram That is considered more the role of BoneConstraint than IK, and we agreed it upon during the animation meeting. For example, when the twist axis is +Y, the propagation of hand rotation can be fulfilled by transferring the +Y rotation of the hand to the +Y axis rotation of the LowerArm/UpperArm using BoneConstraint(Copy/ConvertTransformModifier3D). See also https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/100984, https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110336 and https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/111367. When propagating a child's rotation to its parent using constraints, a final constraint for canceling by self-references is required (as above image, processing the sequence that UpperLeg 0.25 of Foot -> LowerLeg 0.5 of Foot -> Foot -0.75 of Foot), but it should work as intended as long as the rotation does not exceed 180 degrees from rest.

body:
> If you have multiple scenes open, the signal will be emitted multiple times for one scene when the editor is started.

I've done some testing and here are the results.

## Before this PR
| Scenes open on start | Scene focused on start | `scene_change` emits |
| :------- | :------: | -------: |
| A | A | No signal |
| A, B | A | A, A |
| A, B | B | B |
| A, B, C | A | A, A, A |
| A, B, C | B | B, B, B |
| A, B, C | C | C, C |

## After this PR
| Scenes open on start | Scene focused on start | `scene_change` emits |
| :------- | :------: | -------: |
| A | A | A |
| A, B | A | A, A, A |
| A, B | B | B, B |
| A, B, C | A | A, A, A, A |
| A, B, C | B | B, B, B, B |
| A, B, C | C | C, C, C |

This PR only adds 1 signal emit which fires predictably and correctly no matter how many scenes are open or which is focused when the editor boots. The bug you're seeing with N emits is coming from the existing functionality and is separate from this PR.

It's your call but I'd say having the signal emit an extra time when the editor opens with multiple tabs (until that bug gets fixed) is less bad than not having it fire when we need it to in the case of the editor opening with just one tab.

author: TokageItLab

body:
This change looked scary to me, so I spent a couple hours going through manual tests and code inspection. At this point, I'm pretty happy with it aside from a single bug that occurs in deprecated code.

The problem is with using the deprecated setup where `PhysicalBone3D` is a child of a `Skeleton3D` instead of a child of `PhysicalBone3DSimulator`. In this case, it expects that the skeleton has already called `setup_simulator` in `ENTER_TREE` and tries to access the `simulator` that would've been configured previously but no longer is. This results in the simulation never getting applied to the bones.

As far as I can tell, it seems correct that the simulator setup happened in `ENTER_TREE` and not `POST_ENTER_TREE`. It seems fine to leave that one call in `ENTER_TREE` and move everything else to `POST_ENTER_TREE`, as the simulator setup doesn't actually interact with bones at all.
author: precup

body:
@precup Nice catch for the finding `PhysicalBone3DSimulator` problem! I have confirmed that the PhysicalBone3DSimulator does not function with the old configuration.

For now, I left the two functions not directly related to the modifiers `_process_changed()` and `setup_simulator()` as is in `ENTER_TREE` and set the dirty flag using `POST_ENTER_TREE`. The issue with PhysicalBone3DSimulator should now be resolved.

author: bruvzg

body:
As it was mentioned in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/96158, Metal renderer was designed for unified memory. There are no plans to change it specifically for Intel macs. Most of the engine developers have no Intel macs anymore, and Intel mac GPU capabilities vary a lot., so it won't be maintainable.

We plan to keep supporting it in some capacity while x86-64 macOS is receiving security updates, but platform is deprecated and unlikely to receive any new major features.
author: bruvzg

body:
> platform is deprecated and unlikely to receive any new major features.

I see, so 4.5 will be the last version to officially support Intel Macs? For something that is supposed to be designed exclusively for unified memory, the Metal renderer (aside from the issue mentioned) seems to work just fine on a dedicated GPU's VRAM.

I will close the PR for now; maybe make a fork to investigate the possibility later.
author: ComradeNapCali

body:
> I see, so 4.5 will be the last version to officially support Intel Macs?

No, probably not, most likely Intel macs will be supposed while Apple supports it, macOS 26, which is the last version with Intel support, will be released in a month, and Apple usually keeps releasing security updates for about 3 years after release. But adding huge platform specific features, like new renderer are highly unlikely.

body:
Based on the PR description, most of the changes look good. Some personal feedback from my side:

>Reverted Linear mode renaming back to RAW mode for consistent short names.
>Modes are now [RGB, HSV, RAW, HSL], displayed horizontally without an extra popup for OKHSL.

I disagree with renaming "Linear" and "OKHSL" to "RAW" and "HSL". They are not the same - what is "RAW"? and "OKHSL" is different from traditional "HSL". It's not good to sacrifice accuracy for the sake of shorter names, which may confuse users.

>Sorted intensity slider to appear after the alpha slider for consistency with other slider styles.

In my opinion, placing the intensity slider before the alpha slider is easier to use. It's inconvenient if the unchanged alpha slider is in the middle.

>Fixed regressions from previous intensity slider changes.

What other specific regressions are also fixed?
author: beicause

body: > I disagree with renaming "Linear" and "OKHSL" to "RAW" and "HSL". They are not the same - what is "RAW"? and "OKHSL" is different from traditional "HSL". It's not good to sacrifice accuracy for the sake of shorter names, which may confuse users. We’ve actually been using the name RAW for years, and I haven’t seen any confusion about it, it’s already documented, and even a quick Google search brings up the right meaning. From that perspective, I wasn’t sure if it was necessary to break compatibility for something that hasn’t caused issues. For HSL, what I did was make the naming consistent across all modes so they align horizontally and are quicker to switch between. It’s mainly about UI usability just like Aseprite, which also uses short labels like RGB / HSL. Example from Aseprite: > In my opinion, placing the intensity slider before the alpha slider is easier to use. It's inconvenient if the unchanged alpha slider is in the middle. The intensity slider uses a different style from the colored sliders. Putting it in the middle (before alpha) breaks the visual flow and makes the layout feel inconsistent. Since intensity is also a less frequently used control, I felt it was better placed after alpha, so the main color sliders stay grouped together. > What other specific regressions are also fixed? A couple of regressions were addressed along the way: - Disabling the intensity slider in `EditorSettings` couldn’t be reverted, since project metadata wasn’t updated correctly. - Updating colors in HSV shapes didn’t sync properly with HSL shapes, and vice-versa, Shown in the video in description.
author: WhalesState

body: * Moved the bug fixes to another PR: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110160
author: WhalesState

body:
>We’ve actually been using the name RAW for years.

It is changed after the intensity PR. It used to be the same as RGB sliders, but now the sliders are linear color, so RAW is incorrect.

>Example from Aseprite:

Dose aseprite use OKHSL or standard HSL? Please note they are different and can't exchange.
author: beicause

body: > It is changed after the intensity PR. It used to be the same as RGB sliders, but now the sliders are linear color, so RAW is incorrect. After the intensity PR, disabling the intensity sliders reverts the behavior back to what was previously called RAW, where values greater than 1.0 can still be set. For that reason, calling it Linear wouldn’t really be accurate either. I decided to keep the existing RAW name, since it has been documented and consistently used for years without causing confusion. Changing the label now would break compatibility unnecessarily, especially since the feature can be disabled if not needed. There’s also a translation concern: we’d end up with three modes that stay in English (RGB, HSV, OKHSL) and only one that gets translated (Linear -> e.g. خطي), which looks inconsistent. Keeping RAW keeps all four modes aligned. > Dose aseprite use OKHSL or standard HSL? Please note they are different and can't exchange. Aseprite itself uses the short label HSL for the Hue/Saturation/Lightness sliders, regardless of the exact internal model. The goal here isn’t to expose the technical color model implementation, but to present familiar and consistent labels for users. Since what people are actually doing is adjusting hue, saturation, and lightness visually, the concise HSL name makes sense and aligns with what most artists are used to.
author: WhalesState

body:
>After the intensity PR, disabling the intensity sliders reverts the behavior back to what was previously called RAW, where values greater than 1.0 can still be set. For that reason, calling it Linear wouldn’t really be accurate either. I decided to keep the existing RAW name, since it has been documented and consistently used for years without causing confusion.

It's not about whether the intensity slider is enabled or not, it's about color space. If you set the linear color sliders to 0.5, the actual color is 0.735, it's not raw.
- See https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/103583#issuecomment-2703990932

>and only one that gets translated (Linear -> e.g. خطي)

I think it's better to disable the translation of "Linear" here.

>Aseprite itself uses the short label HSL for the Hue/Saturation/Lightness sliders,

If you want to copy the values of other HSL color to OKHSL, you will find the colors are not the same, so it's better to specify the color model clearly.

author: Repiteo

body:
> The intensity slider uses a different style from the colored sliders. Putting it in the middle (before alpha) breaks the visual flow and makes the layout feel inconsistent. Since intensity is also a less frequently used control, I felt it was better placed after alpha, so the main color sliders stay grouped together.

I understand what you're describing here: the layout is more visually pleasing by grouping the alpha slider with the other colour sliders.

I also disagree that the change should be made to the position of the intensity slider because this negatively affects usability: The intensity slider directly affects the colour defined by RGB, HSV, and HSL and has nothing to do with the alpha value. Placing the alpha slider after all of the colour-related sliders is an intentional GUI design choice that makes the alpha transparency distinct from the colour. Conversely, the intensity slider is placed beside the RGB, HSV, and HSL sliders because it directly changes the RGB, HSV, and HSL values. The original ordering was purposefully designed to favour usability and clarity of function, which I believe is more valuable than visual flow in this case.

**Edit:** one option for improving the visual flow without affecting function and usability would be to change the intensity slider to be coloured, the same as the other four sliders, without moving its position.

The new Hex icon is a nice improvement. I'm on the fence about always showing OKHSL because it's already a visually complex tool for new users...

author: raulsntos

body:
I gave it a bit testing and managed to create a working class, although it wasn't easy. I encountered a few problems. They are probably related to the C++ plugin itself and out of scope, but some of them might be limitation of source plugins.

- The user gets no feedback after the class file(s) are created. They are not visible in the FileSystem.
- The plugin is a bit awkward to use with the C++ project template. I cloned it and opened the demo project. Extension classes are created inside demo folder, which requires some additional scons setup to have them properly compiled.
- The class files are created the same regardless if script template is enabled or not. It does respect selected template though.
- GDREGISTER_CLASS is added in the wrong place and does not add necessary includes.
- The default cpp file uses `res://` path in the include.
- The Open Source Code button in Scene dock only allows opening cpp file, not h.

I don't know what plans are here exactly, but if EditorExtensionSourceCodePlugin is supposed to provide scripting-like experience to extensions, it's missing a few things:
- The source files should be visible in FileSystem. Though probably not as Resources, just as allowed file extensions, like text files.
- Creating extension class should open the new file(s) immediately.
- There needs to be some way to either setup the project to run extensions, or at least detect that it's not prepared and provide instructions.

Also I'm not sure about showing two script buttons. IMO the option to edit extension from Scene dock should only show in the context menu. It would also allow opening both cpp and h files in C++ (by providing 2 options).

I'd be interested to test C# source plugin, as I imagine it will be more friendly to use than C++.
author: KoBeWi

body:
> Also I'm not sure about showing two script buttons. IMO the option to edit extension from Scene dock should only show in the context menu. It would also allow opening both cpp and h files in C++ (by providing 2 options).

I disagree, IMO the workflow for extensions should be the same as scripts. The dialogs and options should be the same (e.g. it should be `New script/extension class` instead of two options). I even think you should be able to create an extension to an existing (though I admit this requires more plumbing for languages that need compilation, so I don't think this is a blocker for a first version).

As for opening headers, we can think of options. I think the easiest way is for the user to open the source and then use the editor to open the header. Of course it needs better source editor integration, but that would be required anyway if you plan to used the integrated editor. I also think C++ is an exception here, pretty much all languages will have 1 class = 1 file.
author: vnen

body:
> The user gets no feedback after the class file(s) are created. They are not visible in the FileSystem.

Since the FileSystem doesn't show non-Resource files, I don't know if there's much we can do here. Also, the C++ source files, at least when following the godot-cpp template directory structure, would be outside of the game project anyway.

For C# we can add the `.cs` extension to the list of allowed file extensions. But I guess this will only change it for users that didn't change the default?

> The plugin is a bit awkward to use with the C++ project template. I cloned it and opened the demo project. Extension classes are created inside demo folder, which requires some additional scons setup to have them properly compiled.

Yeah, it wasn't my intention to provide a fully functional plugin for C++ projects, so I think this is out of scope.

> The class files are created the same regardless if script template is enabled or not. It does respect selected template though.

It should fall back to the default template, but that's up to the plugin implementation. I believe the current implementation for creating scripts falls back to the "Empty" template so that's what I'm planning to do for C#.

> `GDREGISTER_CLASS` is added in the wrong place and does not add necessary includes.

I don't know how to implement this properly, and it would probably be a lot of work. I think it's out of scope.

> The default cpp file uses res:// path in the include.

Oops. I guess we can use `ProjectSettings->globalize_path` but it probably doesn't matter for a demo anyway. Although, maybe the parameter should always be the globalized path? Or should plugins be expected to globalize it themselves?

> The Open Source Code button in Scene dock only allows opening cpp file, not h.

Yeah, unfortunately for languages that allow defining classes in multiple files. I had to choose one and decided opening the `.cpp` would be more useful. I was thinking we could show a dialog to select between the available files if there's more than one.

In C# this is less of a problem, but it can still happen if you use `partial` declarations. So we'll want to solve it, but I've often seen IDEs just open the first file they find in this case so I thought it'd be good enough for the initial implementation.

> Creating extension class should open the new file(s) immediately.

This should now be fixed.

> There needs to be some way to either setup the project to run extensions, or at least detect that it's not prepared and provide instructions.

In C# that's handled by our editor plugin, there's no additional setup. I think it's up to the language providers to add editor plugins as needed to handle this.

> Also I'm not sure about showing two script buttons. IMO the option to edit extension from Scene dock should only show in the context menu. It would also allow opening both cpp and h files in C++ (by providing 2 options).

C# users want to see at a glance whether the node is one of their C# classes, for which the button provides a clear indication. It would also be a bit more inconvenient to open C# classes since it would now take 2 clicks instead of 1.

In practice, I don't know how often users would attach scripts to extension classes. So there may not be many cases where we're showing 2 buttons for the same Node.

> I also think C++ is an exception here, pretty much all languages will have 1 class = 1 file.

@vnen Although it may not be very common, you can have N class = M files in C#. You can define multiple classes in a single file. You can also define a single class across multiple files using the `partial` keyword.
author: raulsntos

body:
> Since the FileSystem doesn't show non-Resource files, I don't know if there's much we can do here. For C# we can add the .cs extension to the list of allowed file extensions. But I guess this will only change it for users that didn't change the default?

EditorExtensionSourceCodePlugin could provide a list of extensions it manages and FileSystem dock would use them to show the files, similar to how `textfile_extensions` works.

@KoBeWi was correct, it wasn't working right in all cases, and the problem was due to the relative tolerance as he pointed out.

I haven't necessarily disabled `restore_scenes_on_load` for performance reasons, I just prefer to start without previous scenes loaded - the disabled behavior defaulting to opening the main scene has been a happy accident. On toy-projects with single or couple scenes, the relevant scene opens automatically, and on bigger projects I can mentally reset after every session and start a day from scratch. On some older projects it's also helpful at locating the main scene to start the process of retracing the project scene hierarchy. Finally the setting has eased editing tool scripts in-editor after _some_ idiot from a minute ago bricked the scene by writing an infinite loop.

Could we keep the toggle or better yet make a new property with 3 options: previous, main, none?

body:
Note that the cull mode the given mesh is rendered with is not being accounted for anyhow (it's the same before/after this PR though).

So while this PR fixes the behavior for the `CULL_BACK/back` case from the tables below, it breaks `CULL_FRONT/back` and `CULL_DISABLED/back` cases.
If `CULL_BACK/back` case is more common/annoying than `CULL_FRONT/back` and `CULL_DISABLED/back` cases together, then this PR could be considered as an improvement even in it's current state. Whether this is actually the case I have no idea.

Without this PR (v4.5.stable.official [876b29033]):

Cull mode | mesh faces looked at from | visible | selectable | correct
-|-|-|-|-
`CULL_FRONT` | front | ✗ | ✓ | ❌
`CULL_FRONT` | back | ✓ | ✓ | ✅
`CULL_BACK` | front | ✓ | ✓ | ✅
`CULL_BACK` | back | ✗ | ✓ | ❌
`CULL_DISABLED` | front | ✓ | ✓ | ✅
`CULL_DISABLED` | back | ✓ | ✓ | ✅

With this PR (v4.5.beta.`gh-110185` [4d10a6550]):

Cull mode | mesh faces looked at from | visible | selectable | correct
-|-|-|-|-
`CULL_FRONT` | front | ✗ | ✓ | ❌
`CULL_FRONT` | back | ✓ | ✗ | ❌
`CULL_BACK` | front | ✓ | ✓ | ✅
`CULL_BACK` | back | ✗ | ✗ | ✅
`CULL_DISABLED` | front | ✓ | ✓ | ✅
`CULL_DISABLED` | back | ✓ | ✗ | ❌

E.g. selection for double sided Sprite3D (by default `double_sided = true`; it uses `CULL_DISABLED` mode internally) gets broken with this PR as it makes the sprite selectable only from the front side.

Before:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/3bc3fb80-86df-4496-bec0-087d4be179cb

After:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/877f1788-381a-49c8-a59d-98342e4a7cc6
author: kleonc

body: In our case the PR would be an improvement, we have levels full of 3D models (each wall, floor, ceiling is a mesh on its own) and none of them have front face culling. I think that will be the case for the majority of users, at least I can't imagine otherwise. We use Sprite3Ds mostly as billboards, and I guess the PR does not account for that either (it's already a problem in the current way of selecting).
author: ratkingsminion

body:
Addressing @kleonc's concerns, I've added an extra commit on which I'd appreciate some feedback.
Assuming the precedence used in MeshInstance3D's `get_active_material()`, for each case, from highest to lowest priority in how the cull mode is inferred:

| MeshInstance3D | Sprite3D | CSGPrimitive3D |
| ------------- | ------------- | ------------- |
| GeometryInstance3D material override | Geometry3DInstance material_override | Geometry3DInstance material_override |
| MeshInstance3D surface (0) override | SpriteBase3D double_sided | CSGShape's specific get_material |
| MeshInstance3D mesh material | | |

This means that the new parameter accepted by `intersect_ray` is now an enum, one of `CULL_BACK`, `CULL_FRONT` and `CULL_DISABLED`.

All of this can be tested in the scene here: [backface_test.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22987974/backface_test.zip)

**Here's the problem:**
It seriously hurts editor performance, adding a noticeable delay when selecting nodes. If anyone here is aware of a more performant way of doing this, please let me know.

Alternatively, I can revert this commit, retaining the bandaid fix for Sprite3D, and then branch off the remaining functionality into another draft PR, where an optimised way of doing this can be discussed, and the fix for Sprite3D can be replaced with something that can represent all mesh planes.

body:
Thanks for opening a pull request :slightly_smiling_face: 

Feature pull requests should be associated to a *feature proposal* to justify the need for implementing the feature in Godot core. Please open a proposal on the [Godot proposals](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals) repository (and link to this pull request in the proposal's body).

Also, is this tonemapper friendly to [HDR output](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/94496)? To me, if we're going to add yet another tonemapper in Godot, it ought to be HDR-friendly from the ground up. cc @allenwp
author: Calinou

body:
That's a really great talk that Timothy Lottes presented at GDC 2016 -- I learned a lot from it! Unfortunately, although this tone curve was advertised as working well with "variable dynamic range", it is actually quite unstable. So I spent a fair bit of time iterating on this approach that Timothy presented and [developed a tonemapping curve that is better suited](https://allenwp.com/blog/2025/05/29/allenwp-tonemapping-curve/) for the variable "Extended Dynamic Range" (EDR) that [Godot will be adopting for HDR output](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/94496).

I made a work in progress prototype that implements this curve as the "Adjustable" tonemapping curve in this draft PR here: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/106696 -- The goal of this is to add an adjustable replacement to the "Filmic" tonemapper that works well with EDR/HDR, because "Filmic" simply cannot work with HDR because of its "white" parameter implementation. I want to spend more time ensuring that the "black" parameter works well with it before making a final PR, though...

Additionally, I have made an update to the AgX tonemapper that uses this new EDR-stable tonemapping curve, slightly modified to better suit the intent of AgX: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/106940
author: allenwp

body:
Any chance I could argue for also removing the `ERR_FAIL_V_MSG` at the bottom of `OS_AppleEmbedded::get_processor_name`? Maybe we can replace it with a `print_verbose` instead or something?

Part of the motivation behind reporting #110168 was to hopefully avoid emitting this particular error, as it's proven to be prevalent and annoying for one of our clients, due to them sending all their errors off to an error reporting service.

Since there's no `OS.try_get_processor_name` or anything, there isn't a whole lot you can do to avoid this error, which basically leaves you having to explicitly filter it out before sending them off to the error reporting service, and since this solution relies on continuously adding new models to this list, it would seem guaranteed that you'll run into this error again eventually.

For what it's worth, Android relies on the default `OS::get_processor_name`, which just returns an empty string without any error, so I don't think it's too out-of-place to just remove this `Couldn't get the CPU model name` error altogether and have the fallback be an empty string.

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/6339f31a0217038ce6ed5e16776e34654895edc6/core/os/os.cpp#L439-L441

Can't we just make `ImageTexture::set_image()` accepts null values to clear the image instead of throwing Errors ?

Anyways, this mimics the old behavior without those annoying errors when duplicating themes which contains empty `ImageTexture` resources.
author: WhalesState

body:
> Can't we just make `ImageTexture::set_image()` accepts null values to clear the image instead of throwing Errors ?

That's the route I'd favor.

In my opinion, the classic behavior had an asymmetry we'd better get rid of as part of the tidying of duplicate semantics. If a given getter can return a null reference, the corresponding setter should be fine with null as well. Therefore, I agree `ImageTexture::set_image()` should work as described.

Moreover, if we reinstated the old behavior of not assigning a null resource, we could have the following situation:
- Resource class `CoolRes` has a resource property for which it creates a resource as the default value.
- An instance of `CoolRes` has that property set to null.
- Such instance is duplicated, but you get the default value instead of null.

I think that would challenge expectations.

@WhalesState, do you think you could implement the lenient `set_image()` approach?
author: RandomShaper

body:
> @WhalesState, do you think you could implement the lenient `set_image()` approach?

I can allow `set_image()` to clear the image when a null or empty image is passed but this will break compatibility since the old behavior protects the image once set, another way which i may prefer is to return silently, also we have `PROPERTY_USAGE_RESOURCE_NOT_PERSISTENT` added here https://github.com/fire/godot/commit/0960f46b0bac8b721cd5863b1b7430f7a2e767e4 which is only used for `ImageTexture::image`, `ImageTextureLayered::_images` and `ImageTexture3D::_images` and we can use to continue while duplicating if the value is null. I'd prefer the last solution to avoid breaking compatibility.
