body:
Is there really a problem with `0.5`; won't both branches simplify to `color` as `(2.0f * glow.r - 1.0f)` becomes `0`?

Otherwise the function looks _correct_ but the form is a bit odd. Personally I'd move to use the [more widely known version](https://www.w3.org/TR/compositing-1/#blendingsoftlight) (unless there's a precision benefit or smth that I'm unaware of) for the left side:
```hlsl
(color.r + (2.0f * glow.r - 1.0f) * (((16.0f * color.r - 12.0f) * color.r + 4.0f) * color.r - color.r))
```
If you want to be space efficient combine the common parts and place the condition in the middle:
```hlsl
(color.r + (2.0f * glow.r - 1.0f) * ((color.r <= 0.25f ? ((16.0f * color.r - 12.0f) * color.r + 4.0f) * color.r : sqrt(color.r)) - color.r))
```

EDIT: Concentrated on the functions at first but if you want to simplify further:
```hlsl
glow = glow * vec3(0.5f) + vec3(0.5f);
(2.0f * glow.r - 1.0f);
```
these lines are inverse of one another, so the combined function (the short version for example) could become:
```hlsl
(color.r + glow.r * ((color.r <= 0.25f ? ((16.0f * color.r - 12.0f) * color.r + 4.0f) * color.r : sqrt(color.r)) - color.r))
```
author: Lielay9

body:
> Is there really a problem with `0.5`; won't both branches simplify to `color` as `(2.0f * glow.r - 1.0f)` becomes `0`?

Ah, I think you're right; I had just seen (0,undefined) in the graphing calculator and blindly trusted it instead of checking the math myself.
author: allenwp

body:
Let's look at the case where glow.r is < 0.5.

Assume `glow.r = 0.4` and `color.r = 0.8`

**Before:**
`(color.r - (1.0f - 2.0f * glow.r) * color.r * (1.0f - color.r))`
`result = (0.8 - (1.0f - 2.0f * 0.4) * 0.8 * (1.0f - 0.8))`
`result = (0.8 - (0.2) * 0.8 * (0.2))`
`result = 0.768`

**After**
`result = 0.8`
author: clayjohn

body:
> Let's look at the case where glow.r is < 0.5.
> 
> Assume `glow.r = 0.4` and `color.r = 0.8`
> 
> **Before:** `(color.r - (1.0f - 2.0f * glow.r) * color.r * (1.0f - color.r))` `result = (0.8 - (1.0f - 2.0f * 0.4) * 0.8 * (1.0f - 0.8))` `result = (0.8 - (0.2) * 0.8 * (0.2))` `result = 0.768`
> 
> **After** `result = 0.8`

Glow can never be below 0.5 as it is first clamped to 0 to 1 range and then 0.5 is added. The path for below 0.5 is completely unnecessary as we only want to use the part of soft-light blending that lightens (i.e > 0.5 range).

Edit: For further clarity: even in your example it doesn't make sense that glow 0.4 + color 0.8 would result in darker color (0.768) i.e. negative glow.
author: Lielay9

body: Aaaaaaah right. I should have read the surrounding code

> these lines are inverse of one another, so the combined function (the short version for example) could become:

> (color.r + glow.r * ((color.r <= 0.25f ? ((16.0f * color.r - 12.0f) * color.r + 4.0f) * color.r : sqrt(color.r)) - color.r))
> ```

Wow, I didn't intend to nerd-snipe like this, but this [new function](https://www.desmos.com/calculator/z625dvwxoq) looks great to me! I've pushed the changes to this PR with you as co-author.

body:
Hey @TCROC, great start on this PR, however, it still suffers from the same problem I previously introduced where the compiled .metallib version would mismatch the serialised version in the baked shader header. I've opened #110264 to use the `-std=metalX.X` argument to guarantee the compiled version of the `.metallib` matches what we serialise, so that when we perform the version check at runtime, we can return an error and the Godot renderer will recompile the shader.

I think the direction you've headed is good, as user's should only have to think about their minimum target OS. However, we'll need to create a table that maps OS version to Metal version, so we can still ensure the compiled `.metallib` language version is known to the serializer / baker to be able to perform the runtime checks and return an error if the target OS has a lower Metal language requirement.
author: stuartcarnie

body:
I really should've responded over here. Sorry for the confusion. Copying in. I'll edit out the other comment:

@stuartcarnie Sorry I left out some details as to why I went the route I did with my PR. I originally added "-std=metal3.1" as you suggested, but then it threw an error that it needed a 2.x version. Which really threw me off because the previous error said 3.1. After doing a bunch of digging, it turns out metal3.1 IS NOT the minimum version of metal for 17.6.1. It is the MAXIMUM version. So first it does a "max" check for the OS. Then it does a "max" check for the hardware, this specific device requires version 2.x (I forget exactly which version) when running 17.6.1. But then it works with 3.2 when updated to iOS 18. Super confusing and not straightforward. Passing the min required OS version ensures that these compiled metallibs will run across all OS's and hardware. At least as far as I can tell.

BUT

You are correct in that we need to find a way to get which metallib xcrun is using for caching purposes and runtime detection if the shader needs recompiling. All this to say, we need a combination of both our PRs in order to fix the issue I have open.

Is there a way to detect the error when "main0" is called and then recompile from there? This would be the most reliable solution as Apple's internal logic will tell us if it is correct or not.
author: TCROC

body: > but then it threw an error that it needed a 2.x version. Is the device different from the logs you shared in [this comment](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/109846#issuecomment-3249672929), as that device is an A15 (Apple8 GPU) which is definitely a Metal 3 (and 4) device according to Apple's feature set tables: Are you also testing on another device that has lower hardware specs? It may be that the issue is determining the device's maximum Metal version. We use `MTLCompileOptions.languageVersion` to determine the maximum language version of the device. It is possible that this only reports the maximum of the OS rather than the hardware. > Is there a way to detect the error when "main0" is called and then recompile from there? This would be the most reliable solution as Apple's internal logic will tell us if it is correct or not. We load the library in the function that parses the shader container, but that clearly does not generate an error. It only fails when it attempts to compile the pipeline, which is currently too late in the Godot render path. Could you try specifying `-std=metal3.1` _and_ the `--ios-version-min` and see if that works? I ask as this log you shared suggests it might still be using `metal3.1` along with the default OS target of your compiler, which is a newer iOS: ``` Function main0 has a deployment target (0x00020007) which is incompatible with this OS (0x00020006). ``` This would ensure we know the language version to be `3.1` and also correctly targeting an earlier OS version.
author: stuartcarnie

body:
> Is the device different from the logs you shared in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/109846#issuecomment-3249672929, as that device is an A15 (Apple8 GPU) which is definitely a Metal 3 (and 4) device according to Apple's feature set tables:

So this is where it gets confusing.  The device is the same.  It **IS** a Metal 3 / 4 device but **IF AND ONLY IF** it is running iOS 18.  On iOS 17 it is metal 2.  At least that is what my tests are showing on this device.  (Also, not yelling.  Just trying to emphasize the conditions that result in certain metal versions being required as it is very confusing and unclear imo).

> Are you also testing on another device that has lower hardware specs?

Nope.  The same device.

> It may be that the issue is determining the device's maximum Metal version. We use MTLCompileOptions.languageVersion to determine the maximum language version of the device. It is possible that this only reports the maximum of the OS rather than the hardware.

This definitely could be the case.  Especially since the issue appears to be a hardware + software combination that determines the actual max metal version.

> We load the library in the function that parses the shader container, but that clearly does not generate an error. It only fails when it attempts to compile the pipeline, which is currently too late in the Godot render path.

Ah I see.  I'm personally much more familiar with writing in Rust.  In Rust there is a "sender / receiver" pattern built in where I want send messages across threads.  But its also useful for just sending information across different stages of a program if a back track is needed in order to handle an error.  How challenging would it be to communicate something back up the render path that "some really bad stuff is going on. lets try this again without the shader baker."?

> Could you try specifying -std=metal3.1 and the --ios-version-min and see if that works? I ask as this log you shared suggests it might still be using metal3.1 along with the default OS target of your compiler, which is a newer iOS. This would ensure we know the language version to be 3.1 and also correctly targeting an earlier OS version.

Yep!  I'll have access to the device today and can run these tests!

Thanks for the feedback!  I look forward to taking a look at these!  I'll report back with what I find :)
author: TCROC

body:
> Ah I see. I'm personally much more familiar with writing in Rust. In Rust there is a "sender / receiver" pattern built in where I want send messages across threads. But its also useful for just sending information across different stages of a program if a back track is needed in order to handle an error. How challenging would it be to communicate something back up the render path that "some really bad stuff is going on. lets try this again without the shader baker."?

I write Rust, and have written a lot of Go, and both have used channels extensively for communicating sequential processes. C++ can do that too, but it's only relevant if the software architecture is orchestrated in such a way to leverage these primitives. The renderer has several phases that take place:

1. Perform background compilation of all the shader variants, which includes pulling them from the baked cache, runtime cache and / or recompiling from original GLSL source if there is an issue or missing.
2. Compile specific pipelines when needed to render a given scene

The issue is that phase 1 happens and Godot may never use the shaders in a compiled pipeline, so nothing more happens with them. Channels wouldn't help here, because it wasn't the renderer (in our situation) asking for a shader to begin with. We would have to change all the Godot code that compiles pipelines, which would break compatibility.

The issue is that for Metal, we _load_ the `MTLLibrary` binary object, which appears to succeed regardless of whether the shader can be used by a Metal pipeline for the current OS. Only when the pipeline is compiled, when the shader is about to be used, does it fail. This happens well after initialisation of Godot, and so there is no back channel, etc. The code requesting it compile the shader may not even have the original source it requested, as it has a reference to a shader object, like [`RDShaderSPIRV`](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/classes/class_rdshaderspirv.html)[^1]

> So this is where it gets confusing. The device is the same. It IS a Metal 3 / 4 device but IF AND ONLY IF it is running iOS 18. On iOS 17 it is metal 2. At least that is what my tests are showing on this device. (Also, not yelling. Just trying to emphasize the conditions that result in certain metal versions being required as it is very confusing and unclear imo).

Apple8 GPU _should_ be a Metal 3.x capable device on iOS 17 – that is a very new GPU. Metal 3.0 was introduced in iOS 16, so all Metal 3 capable GPUs should be running / supporting Metal 3 on iOS 16+[^2]:

```c++
    MTLLanguageVersion3_0 API_AVAILABLE(macos(13.0), ios(16.0)) = (3 << 16) + 0,
    MTLLanguageVersion3_1 API_AVAILABLE(macos(14.0), ios(17.0)) =  (3 << 16) + 1,
    MTLLanguageVersion3_2 API_AVAILABLE(macos(15.0), ios(18.0)) = (3 << 16) + 2,
```

It's an easy test if you try with a little bit of Swift on the device (and this query was also introduced in iOS 16.0):

```swift
guard let device = MTLCreateSystemDefaultDevice() else {
    println("No Metal available!")
    return
}
if device.supportsFamily(.metal3) {
    println("Metal 3 capable device")
    return
}
println("Metal 2.x capable device")
```

[^1]: Internal to the engine it is not quite this, as it has RIDs, but essentially the same thing.
[^2]: Apple's own feature tables indicate that Apple7+ GPUs are Metal 3 capable, so that means from iOS 16+ they would return `true` for the `supportsFamily(.metal3)` query.
author: stuartcarnie

author: stuartcarnie

body:
@stuartcarnie so passing both the min OS and the min metal version seems to fix it as well. I expect it is the min OS arg that is doing the heavy lifting to ensure the metallib is compiling correctly. Even when both are passed.

This leaves me perplexed as to how I feel regarding the appropriate solution...

Does xcrun happen to be open source? If so, we can dig into the internals and see how it is determining the metal version. Otherwise, I feel that the only way we can reliably compile the metallibs is by passing the min OS version we want to target. Simply because Apple's walled garden approach prevents us from knowing for sure how it is handling things under the hood. And if we get too specific ourselves, we will inevitably break things. If not now, in a future apple OS or hardware update.

Maybe there is a happy medium approach? Maybe we can expose a present config for both "min_os" and "min_metal" version? Both of which are optional. And both of which are serialized in the shader headers (forgive me if that is the wrong terminology). If unset, they simply aren't passed to the xcrun compiler.

This thought literally came to my mind as I'm typing this. I'm starting to feel like this might be the magic bullet that answers all of the problems we are running into. It gives users the flexibility to determine if they want to do the heavy lifting on versioning themselves, or if they want to reply on xcrun. And we can serialize these settings and compare them at runtime.

What are your thoughts? It would be a combination of both our PRs. I think it *should* solve all the problems we've encountered here. We'll default the preset to something reasonably old that should cover most users. But users will be free to update to any versions.

body:
I was working on a similar thing. 

This would not make it possible to show the focus stylebox on grab focus with mouse anymore? 
If yes this could be bad for accessibility and customizing how you want the behavior to work in your game/app.

W3 has this [note](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/focus-visible.html):

> There may be situations where mouse/pointer users could also benefit from having a visible focus indicator, even though they did not set focus to an element using the keyboard. As a best practice, consider still providing an explicit focus indicator for these cases.

And I did see a couple of comments when looking into this that says that don't think that a users that need a focus indicator only use keyboard all the time, they might use a mouse in some cases and still need the help of a focus indicator. Say you want an accessibility setting in your game that always draw focus, would this be possible with this PR?


And how does this handle cases like LineEdit focus? In a browser they get a focus ring even if clicked with a mouse.

Does this also include touch input as "mouse"?

body:
Did a quick test, mostly works as expected

Here are some things that still draw focus on mouse input:

- Filesystem tree when you right click on a file
- Inspector scroll container when you click on a float property (in both inspector dock and project settings)
- Scene tree when you add a new node

There are some things that will need to be decided case-by-case, for example popup windows drawing focus after being called is generally good, especially when you're expected to type some text in, or to press a button in a simple yes/no confirmation popup. But some popups however draw focus around the main big area (i.e file selection popup) which most apps don't do because it'd be too visually intrusive. Blender is one example of that, it highlights the buttons in yes/no popups but doesn't highlight the main area of the file selection window even though that's what reacts to keyboard shortcuts

> I've added `gui/common/always_show_focus_state` to the project settings

It currently also affects the editor and not just the game, which will be undesirable if more than one person is working on the project with different preferences on that

body:
> i have tested with editor submenus in master and the issue also exists there by default and the editor is not embedding subwindows.

The PR fixed the issue with non-embedded windows and the Editor, though I found a couple of bugs there that I'm taking care of. What I've got working now for embedded widows is to emit the `popup_hide` signal when a submenu closes, which then creates an `InputEventMouseMotion` event that forces the parent menu to process a mouse movement over the currently hovered item. This seems to offer a solution for the time being. I will set the default submenu wait time to `0.1` and we can test it out to see if it's satisfactory. I think the ergonomics are better with the faster response.

What you demonstrated regarded no window receiving input, might certainly be related to the closed submenu blocking input to the parent menu, which is the source of the blocked submenu issue. This is a deeper problem with `Window` - fixing that might also take care of the "hung" activation of submenus that requires that callable loop to fix.
author: Koyper

body:
After a lot of frustration, I finally had to get rid of the `minimum_lifetime_timer` as it was the source of many of the issues. I replaced it with `close_suspended_timer`, which is only set in the parent menu, whereas `minimum_lifetime_timer` was being set in each submenu. The code now enforces submenus only being activated after any open submenus are closed. This eliminated the need for the hack double-calling `_activate_submenu()`, which I believe was being caused by a race condition where a prior submenu was in a closing state while the next was being activated.

The other major problem was the fixes for issues with embedded vs. non-embedded subwindows were completely different. The only way to get embedded subwindows to work was connecting `popup_hide` to spoof a mouse input on the hovered menu item that's triggered by the submenu closing.

When the mouse moves out of a submenu item, it's last relative motion state inside its menu item is preserved, and if it's moving into the lower right quadrant (or lower left if right-to-left layout), it will start the `close_suspended_timer` which runs for `0.5` seconds to allow the mouse to sweep into the open submenu across other menu items without triggering them to open or close the open submenu. This timing is modeled after the behavior of MacOS system menus, which seems reasonable.

Finally, I reduced the default submenu delay time from `0.3` to `0.1` seconds, as that feels more natural and responsive, and the fixes above make it unnecessary to prolong the opening of the submenu.

This push fixes the flickering issue and ensures proper function with `per_pixel_transparency` and `content_scale_factor` settings. Lots of work.

I also fixed a tricky bug that intermittently caused a submenu to close unexpectedly while moving the mouse toward it. This was caused by an unpredictable delay between when a timer is started and when `is_stopped` will return a valid result. This can unexpectedly be longer than `30` msecs. The result makes the mouse direction detection really reliable and the menu performance feels solid.

Everything seems to be functioning properly for both embedded and non-embedded windows, so give it a whirl.

body:
I would expect it to look like this instead:

```
Can't add Autoload: invalid name.
GlobalTetrahedron: Must not collide with an existing global script class name.
```

Notice the lowercase "i" for "invalid name", and the fact it's on the same line as the "header" of the error dialog.
author: Calinou

body:
> I would expect it to look like this instead:
> 
> ```
> Can't add Autoload: invalid name.
> GlobalTetrahedron: Must not collide with an existing global script class name.
> ```
> 
> Notice the lowercase "i" for "invalid name", and the fact it's on the same line as the "header" of the error dialog.

The "header" part only really exists for invalid name and not other options

```cpp
if (!_autoload_name_is_valid(name, &error)) {
  EditorNode::get_singleton()->show_warning(TTR("Can't add Autoload:") + "\n" + error);
  return false;
}

if (!FileAccess::exists(p_path)) {
  EditorNode::get_singleton()->show_warning(TTR("Can't add Autoload:") + "\n" + vformat(TTR("%s is an invalid path. File does not exist."), p_path));
  return false;
}

if (!p_path.begins_with("res://")) {
  EditorNode::get_singleton()->show_warning(TTR("Can't add Autoload:") + "\n" + vformat(TTR("%s is an invalid path. Not in resource path (res://)."), p_path));
  return false;
}
```

So this would have us
```
Can't add Autoload: invalid name.
%s: Must not collide with an existing global script class name.
---
Can't add Autoload:
%s is an invalid path. File does not exist.
---
Can't add Autoload:
%s is an invalid path. Not in resource path (res://).
```

How about
```
Can't add Autoload:
%s is an invalid name. Must not collide with an existing global script class name.
---
Can't add Autoload:
%s is an invalid path. File does not exist.
---
Can't add Autoload:
%s is an invalid path. Not in resource path (res://).
```

This has message formats the same for each case and only requires `'%s is an invalid name.'` translation.

body:
Note that for testing the fix, you only need to update the export template. 
If you know how to build from source, and already have it set up, just running `scons platform=android module_mono_enabled=yes generate_android_binaries=yes` should be enough to generate a new `android_monoDebug.apk` debug template.

Otherwise, you can use the template generated by the CI. They don't generate a `dotnet` specific export template but since we're testing whether the apk installs or not, using the available [android-template](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/actions/runs/17473963320?pr=110260) should be sufficient (make sure to pick a template that matches your target ABI.

After you have the template, enable `Advanced settings` in the export options, and update `custom_template/debug` to point to  it.
author: m4gr3d

body:
> Do we need this for all exports? If it’s only a problem with C#, maybe we should enable legacy packing just for C# exports.

I want to play it safe as I'm concerned that if we're seeing the issue with native dotnet shared libraries, then the issue may also be reproducible on a non-dotnet build but with unaligned native shared libraries from a 3p extension.. Especially as there's no drawbacks to being cautious:
- Gradle builds still default to `useLegacyPackaging` being turned off
- Non-gradle builds will by default have `useLegacyPackaging` turned on (i.e: native shared libraries compressed), but we've already confirmed that this is not a blocker for 16kb page support (when all the included native shared libraries support it). And we avoid having to explain to end-users why their export templates and exported non-gradle builds grew up in size from 4.4 to 4.5

I ran a quick test with non-uniform and unnormalized scaling. The LookAt modification seems to work in both cases, while the TwoBoneIK seems to work with unnormalized scaling but not non-uniform scaling. [Here's the MRP I was testing with.](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22374725/test_skeleton.zip)

I'll take a look this week and see if there's a way the code can be rewritten without introducing performance overhead while taking into account non-uniform scaling for TwoBoneIK. Failing that, I think it would be best to merge the fix for mirrored scales as it seems to be a more common use case (anecdotally, I initially ran into this bug when trying to flip a character facing left/right, but I cannot think of a use for non-uniform scaling).

I tested a few of the other 2D skeleton modification types: FABRIK breaks for any of mirrored, unnormalized, or nonuniform scaling; CCDIK already works for each of mirrored, unnormalized, and nonuniform scaling.
author: 0xcafeb33f

body:
I investigated the non-uniform magnitudes scaling case, and I don't think it is worth the tradeoff to implement. The primary problem with non-uniform magnitude scaling is not that the bone lengths are cached (there was actually already a scaling adjustment to the cached length prior to this PR), but instead that the scaling is applied *after the skeleton modifications*. In short: with non-uniform magnitude scaling, bone length changes *as a function of angle*. This relationship breaks many of the fundamental assumptions of the Skeleton2D node, making it unreasonable to try to implement a fix.

The CCDIK modifier tracks the target node accurately in non-uniform magnitude scales by fixing the bone lengths, which is unclear if it is the desired behavior. For completeness, the issue of FABRIK not working correctly has been previously reported in #75224.

author: fire

body:
The main thing of note about this commit is that vulkan validation once again reports no issues until we reach `vkCmdDecodeVideoKHR` at which point we segfault somewhere in the driver where my debugger doesn't reach.

VideoStreamH264 takes more responsibility now, it creates the video profile in its entirety, rather than simply adding the little codec specific bit
RenderingDevice now actually has an owner for `VideoProfileState`s and can use it to manage them.
RenderingDeviceCommons has several new enums and structs, which have been mirrored from the Vulkan headers (for now only for VideoProfiles)
And finally by sending VideoProfiles all the way from VideoStreamH264 to RenderingDriverVulkan, it has become far more generic than before, though creating Buffers and Textures still use the hardcoded Profiles rather than being dynamic in any way.

Next step is probably setting up the PDB and active reference pictures. I can't tell why the driver segfaults but I do know that without the PDB and reference pictures we aren't going to decode the stream anyway so might as well.
author: ArchercatNEO

body:
I have reached the point where we can call `vkCmdDecodeVideoKHR` without either getting a segfault or raising validation warnings.

I have also attempted to *sample* the resulting texture but reached 2 problems
1. `TextureStorage` doesn't recognise the image format `RD::DATA_FORMAT_G8_B8R8_2PLANE_420_UNORM`
2. `vkCmdCopyImageToBuffer` doesn't like using `VK_IMAGE_ASPECT_COLOR_BIT` to query `VK_FORMAT_G8_B8R8_2PLANE_420_UNORM` images.

They are similar problems with there seemingly being no support for `VK_FORMAT_G8_B8R8_2PLANE_420_UNORM` which just means more work is needed.

Hopefully once the image is finally sampled and rendered to screen it will be mostly ok and implementing rendering entire clusters and eventually the entire video becomes simpler.

body:
All current work is based on mkv(/webm)/H.264 only.

Of course, it'd be great to separate the "generic" video coding bits from each other so supporting AV1/VP9 is easy but that's still quite a bit away. Also the vulkan headers we use are too old to include VP9 so it might be worth making a PR to update them if you want to support that eventually.

Audio is out of scope of this PR and MP4 definitely is too. Even mkv support is out of scope for implementing vulkan video support but some container was necessary to demux H.264 frames from so that's why it's here.

If you'd like to implement MP4 support based off of this PR I'd be happy to support you in that but the scope is already quite big.

body: I am still unsure what exactly I've done that fixed anything but I was finally able to decode the first frame of a video, though it only contains the red channel, I didn't test AV1 but in theory it will work too.
author: ArchercatNEO

body: Any idea why the decode has corruption?
author: fire

body: Because when I made the video I added a corruption effect. The only real corruption when decoding is that there’s only the red channel (the background should be yellow) but that’s only because when we create the image to display I only used the y component and treated it as a red channel instead of actually converting ycbcr to rgb
author: ArchercatNEO

body: This time the image really *is* corrupted (vulkan validation mentions I shouldn't be using `texelFetch` but it was the only one which would do something other than return yellow) but it's in sRGB and was the result of sampling the YUV decoded picture. Sometime I'll get around to making the shader actually do what it's meant to but at least we can be sure the *rest* of the compute pipeline actually works.
author: ArchercatNEO

body: Thanks to clay john pointing out that when I was dividing the call index by the texture dimensions using *interger* division instead of float division, the shader can now *finally* ***fully*** decode the very first frame of the video. It's a bit more pixelated than it's meant (I am guessing it is a sampler config issue) to be but otherwise the decoding is correct.
author: ArchercatNEO

body:
And the first *16* frames looped. I'm not going to do a push yet because it's that's all the functionally I added and would waste CI time for no reason. I should probably get a different video for when I get around to making sure the frames are presented in the correct order but there's certainly *movement* going on.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c5da46c3-9275-40f9-9b9c-5772ec463b75

From here there's still a lot to do (getting *all* of the frames decoded would be nice right?) but I think there's still a lot I can do with just these 16 frames. So far I've been leaving garbage around that never gets freed so I think now that we know that if everything is set up right things get decoded it's time to clean up the pipeline.

I'm thinking first of all make `parse frame -> decode frame -> convert frame` into a much smoother timeline which waits on CPU a lot less and making the texture pools actual pools instead of just fixed arrays I keep increasing in size. probably several other things I can't remember right now but yeah *finally* things are happening.
author: ArchercatNEO

body:
Update on everything: I managed to decode a full H.264 video and decode a small amount of AV1 video ~last Saturday.
The problem was that in video decode order and present order is different, I couldn't manage to rearange the H.264 frames to align with present order so I pivotted back into AV1 decoding. AV1 decoding has significantly more complex state than H.264 which makes maintaining the DPB across the entire video more difficult. It doesn't help that with my driver decoding AV1 wrong can lead to driver crashes which is why I added a crash condition to godot to prevent that happening. There's not currently much point in testing that the crash condition is guaranteed to crash at some point but I though it's been long enough that I should give an update.
author: ArchercatNEO

body: If you happen to have an AMD GPU (integrated or dedicated should work) there's a chance you will be able to play H.264 video with frames in the correct order now. If you have an nvidia GPU, chances are nothing will work (check with vulkan validation to confirm). If you have AV1 video, hopefully it plays in order until inevitably the DPB state becomes corrupted and triggers the crash condition (not putting a crash in this situation meant my GPU driver itself crashed so this was preferable for now)

author: fire

body:
Editor builds are available from the CI at my fork of the repository

In terms of video compatibility, it *might* be possible to decode 10-bit and 12-bit H.264 video now. Additionally, matroska lacing should be supported now (though I haven’t tested). If anyone wants to test the PR I’d be very grateful.

In terms of implementation details, significant amounts of responsibility has been moved into rendering device (which also broke AV1 video) and buffer creation was moved into the codec video stream. I also added support for capability queries which is how 10-bit and 12-bit support was “added” (my GPU doesn’t support either of them for H.264 decoding, I’m assuming that other GPUs might).

Validation of everything is still very much a work in progress but enough should be implemented for me to believe that if your GPU/video combination is unsupported, an error should be logged somewhere. Which means, if anyone has time, finding what GPUs/videos trigger errors could be very helpful for guiding what the next thing I should implement/fix should be.

As for apple, the reason those devices will fail CI (even if I disable the module for mac/ios, which is already done) is that I made API additions to rendering device driver which I haven’t stubbed out in the metal driver which means they will automatically fail CI until I do that.

body:
The part that I'm worried about regarding this PR is that there's no clear indication of what we're supposed to avoid to not break this particular device. Whoever decides to refactor or optimize this code in the future again will have no clear reason as to why they should avoid it, and we can't exactly test again unless whoever refactors it has access to it.

Seeing as the reason behind the error is so vague, I'd say the ban list for FP16 on Intel would be the best approach for future maintenance (can be device/driver version based too). If the workaround is pushed as is, we at least need a link back to this PR denoting it is a highly fragile area of code in the specific device this bug was encountered on.

Are we absolutely sure this bug only happens on this device and not elsewhere?
author: DarioSamo

body:
> The part that I'm worried about regarding this PR is that there's no clear indication of what we're supposed to avoid to not break this particular device. Whoever decides to refactor or optimize this code in the future again will have no clear reason as to why they should avoid it, and we can't exactly test again unless whoever refactors it has access to it.
> 
> Seeing as the reason behind the error is so vague, I'd say the ban list for FP16 on Intel would be the best approach for future maintenance (can be device/driver version based too). If the workaround is pushed as is, we at least need a link back to this PR denoting it is a highly fragile area of code in the specific device this bug was encountered on.
> 
> Are we absolutely sure this bug only happens on this device and not elsewhere?

@DarioSamo No, I'm not sure. I'm judging based on the fact that this code has been in place for awhile and no one has complained. I am concerned about the increase in complexity and maintenance cost from a ban list if it turns out this is just an issue for a few device/driver combos.

I am not super concerned about someone undoing this change in the future since these specific lines of code were pretty dubious to begin with. The fact that `spot_rim` needed to be in full precision anyway makes the preceding half calculations a lot less impactfull.

body:
(from rocketchat) I'm not happy about changing from returning a cached StringName name; to calling String get_blend_point_name(int p_point) const; which then must perform itos(i) followed by String -> StringName conversion, at every _process

If the names could be cached, I might feel better about it. Or maybe, for compatibility, we should always store the number as the name, and if the name is numeric, then it always uses an index and ignores the name.
author: lyuma

body:
@lyuma That case `parameters/blendspace2d/ + get_blend_point_name(0)` exists solely for compatibility with niche scenarios where paths depend on dynamically changing points. If you want to cache the path name in cases where it depends on this dynamic path, you would likely need to handle caching on the user side before and after `add_point()` or `remove_point()`. Alternatively, adding a signal like `points_reordered` could be another approach to consider.

However, as I mentioned above, I believe use cases that rely on paths being dynamically changed are niche. So in most normal cases where you're just accessing path `parameters/blendspace2d/0` (`0` is "name", not "index") shouldn't affect performance. The current BlendSpace dynamically transcribes index into name using itos, so my suggestion in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110369#discussion_r2335739050 for this PR simply always prevents name from being changed automatically. Furthermore, I argue that inconsistent path behavior depend on whether it is number or not should be avoided, as it can cause confusion.
author: TokageItLab

body:
Pending further opinions, I do request that this PR not close #66336, because if the important part that indexes play in animation blending isn't exposed and made apparent to the user, a lot of unpredictable things can occur. I know it caused me a great deal of confusion before I understood it.

While blendpoint indexes remain a necessary evil that hold serious influence over animation blending, I don't think they should be swept under the rug.
author: vaner-org

body:
In working to address the concerns of the animation team:

- I have removed the restriction on purely numeric names.
- I now convert index names to String names on first launch post this PR using `get_child_nodes`, to retain compatibility with current code paths, that users can then change into descriptors or clear to get the default creation naming scheme (of "BlendSpace1D", "StateMachine 2" etc.). If there's a better mechanism for upgrading, I'd appreciate some guidance.

As for my own concerns about this PR:
- I have removed the name editing box from the toolbar, after improvements to the inline editor's placement. This not only makes this PR more coherent with StateMachine and AnimationTree, but also helps the toolbar be less cluttered.
- To better address [#66336](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/66336), when the index edit box in the toolbar has focus, all of the labels above the points show their indices rather than their names.
- Additionally, rolling the mousewheel over a blend point increments/decrements the index, while similarly revealing the indices of all points, a timed state that reverts back to names after 1.5 seconds. With this, [opening](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/109839), moving, renaming and reordering the index of a blend point can all be done on the point itself rather than via the toolbar.
author: vaner-org

body:
A few questions: 
- Failing checks against the extension API. The name must be added as a non-optional member, so has to be before index in `add_blend_point()`. How should I proceed?
- Is there an external need for `_get_triangles()` and `_set_triangles()` in BlendSpace2D? Otherwise I can pull those into the `set`, `get()` and `get_property_list()` and remove `_validate_property()` too.
- Where should I document the new functionality of rolling the mousewheel to change index, and double clicking the text to change the name?
author: vaner-org

body:
> Failing checks against the extension API. The name must be added as a non-optional member, so has to be before index in add_blend_point(). How should I proceed?

I think there are two approaches.

1. Make the string optional, disallowing empty name only when it's `#ifdef DISABLE_DEPRECATED`, and fallback to automatic naming with a warning like `In the future, empty names will be deprecated, so explicitly passing a name is recommended.` in `#else` block, also empty name is prevented by editor (means that warning is only shown by user script)
2. Move the string before the index and rename it as new function like `add_blend_point_with_name()` with comment `TODO: In Godot5, it should become add_blend_point()`, and mark the old API as deprecated in xml like `<method name="add_blend_point" deprecated="Will be replaced with add_blend_point_with_name in the future.">`, and make editor to use `add_blend_point_with_name` only. Then no longer needed to add `.compat.inc`
author: TokageItLab

body:
Regarding approach no. 2, isn't it problematic to say that `add_blend_point` will be replaced with `add_blend_point_with_name` in the future, when actually the latter will be taking the `add_blend_point` name when replacement does happen?

For approach no. 1, the current `.compat.inc` and refactors remain, correct? With just an additional `#ifdef DISABLE_DEPRECATED` block inside `add_blend_point`? If so, I'm leaning towards preferring it, since the inferred name can also be conveyed to the user with the warning they are given.
author: vaner-org

body: No 2 depends on a future ProjectConverter (as was done from Godot 3 to 4). I think 1 is acceptable for now.

body: Looks like I'm going to have to add exclusions anyway to pass CI. Is it still worth it to do all of our circumventions to `add_blend_point`?

body:
> We discussed this at the animation meeting, but it was difficult to find consensus.

To clarify, the main concern was likely compatibility issues.

In older projects, for example, when a parameter like `BlendSpace2D/3/blend_position` existed, after deleting a point, its path would be automatically changed to `BlendSpace2D/2/blend_position`. However, there should be agreement that this "automatic path change behavior is inconvenient."

While scripts based on this automatic path change behavior (though I believe this is a niche case) will break compatibility, I believe the migration path is secure as demonstrated above using `get_blend_point_name()`. Except for that niche case, no migration is required as long as `disable_deprecated` is `false`.

Even without this, it might not prevent you from making games, but since it's inconvenient that we can't assign arbitrary names to nested BlendTrees like `BlendSpace2D/NestedBlendSpace2D/blend_position`, I suggest to merge this PR.

body: However, if you look closely at the feet and ground in the animation after keying, you can see a difference between IK animation and keyed animation. This occurs because even if the interpolation between IK animation keys is linear, the actual bone transforms do not become linear. To deal with this, we need to bake the bone transforms per frame. However, since the workflow depends on the structure of individual projects, so I believe this should be an Addon rather than core support. I have already tested that this is doable, so I can publish the Addon to awesome-godot or assetlib later.

body:
> Considering that hashes are fine as long as they don't conflict, and given that existing code is working with this, I assume it should be fine.
> 
> However, since I'm not an expert of hash algorithm, it might be better to have someone with more detailed knowledge review it.
> 
> The literature I found on the internet was as follows: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/20511347/a-good-hash-function-for-a-vector

Any hash algorithm may have collisions. If a value with the same hash already exists in the container, we should handle hash collisions (comparing if the values are actually equal always, placing elements with the same hash into a bucket when inserting).

It seems we are not doing so, using hash directly as a key seems problematic, which may be a separate issue.
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/2dd26a027a99633231184616d4dd287bbdd1c0a3/scene/animation/animation_mixer.h#L307
author: beicause

body: It's true that it occurs with astronomical probability. If we want to add avoidance, I think adding it is nice. The track hash was originally implemented in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/86687 to identify blendable track counts, so as long as it can identify them, it's fine.
author: TokageItLab

body: I think we just need to replace `TypeHash` with a struct as key, and implement its hasher and comparator, so we can completely avoid hash collisions.

body:
> I think we just need to replace `TypeHash` with a struct as key, and implement its hasher and comparator, so we can completely avoid hash collisions.

This purpose of this PR is to make the current hashing implementation more efficient, not to rewrite the hashing backend. If you’d like to take it on, feel free to open a separate PR.
author: Ryan-000

body:
> Benchmarking would be nice, you can use the MRP of #101494 if you don't have an MRP of your own to share

I reviewed the MRP, but it is not suitable to benchmark this PR, as there are not enough animations or tracks to reveal the impact during the initial load (only 3 animations, with 4 tracks).  

If you would like to benchmark this PR, you would need a project with a much more animations/track counts (for reference, my game has 27,723 tracks across 375 skeletal animations).
author: Ryan-000

body:
Yea wasn't sure if it'd be suitable, but I'm not aware of any other more appropriate benchmarks (hence the if you don't have your own MRP remark), and wanted to say that some Benchmarking would be nice.

It's fine though if you're unable to share it, but results would still be welcome.

author: beicause

body:
This PR currently looks to be prioritizing safety over performance by adopting StringName, sacrificing a little performance cost.

> It should probably be done only once after the animation loads and all tracks have finished generating their hashes, rather than during hash generation. Using something like a hash_dirty flag for deferred calls might be a preferable approach.

I'm also interested in how the approach I suggested above would fare. Then I think we'll compare the results and finally adopt whichever performs better.

If you could do it, we'd be happy to have your help. If you're not interested, I'll try it myself after I finish my other priority tasks.
author: TokageItLab

body:
In fact, I also quickly tried using 64bit and 128bit (`FixedVector<uint64_t, 2>`) hash algorithms. The 64bit hash is as fast as the original 32bit (136 fps). 128bit is slightly faster than StringName (128 fps vs 122 fps).

64bit hash would be much safer than 32bit, but it might not be sufficient to completely solve the problem ([The birthday bound of 32bit hash is just 77,000, while 64bit hash is 5.1×10^9, ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_attack)but considering the number of Godot users, this doesn't guarantee it won't happen). For now I prefer StringName, we should not sacrifice security for this kind of optimization.

>I think it would be comforting if we could check for collisions only once during hash generation and then increment the int as avoidance, but we should probably consider the performance cost of the collision check since it will likely require iterating over all hashes within the AnimationLibrary. It should probably be done only once after the animation loads and all tracks have finished generating their hashes, rather than during hash generation. Using something like a hash_dirty flag for deferred calls might be a preferable approach.

This method sounds complicated. I suspect the current approach might not be optimal. But I'm not familiar with the animation code, so I'm not sure and it's difficult for me to try other approaches.

If anyone have better ways, feel free to go ahead.

author: kisg

body:
Hi,

I reviewed this PR, and to me this looks like our (Migeran's) 4.4 version of libgodot, which we did not submit for review, because it was too late in the review cycle and were working on other additions. You can find that version here: https://github.com/migeran/godot/tree/libgodot_44_stable

This PR only: 
- updated that code to compile with current master
- removed a lot of code that @zorbathut did not need
- did some minimal changes, like the init parameters and some naming based on review comments here.
- **Added @zorbathut as the original author and downgraded me to a "Co-Author" + removed my colleague and our company reference + other remarks.**
- Everyone started to refer to this work as @zorbathut 's PR.

We don't agree with how this is being handled, and this is not how contributions were handled in the past in this community.

We were working on an update for after the Godot 4.5 release, the only reason for about a ~1 month delay was personal on our side (we targeted a mid-August release).

To be clear: We are happy to break up our PR into smaller pieces. Even when we first submitted our PR we offered that, but back then we were told it was not necessary.

Of course, we are still happy to do it, and discuss and implement any other changes that the maintainers see necessary. However, we feel that the future direction of this code should be discussed on the original PR: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/90510

I also think that we should set up a time next week on the Core meeting to discuss the strategy of merging LibGodot into Godot master.

Kind regards,
Gergely Kis
Co-Founder / CTO of Migeran

CC @dsnopek @clayjohn
author: kisg

body:
For what it's worth, my main goal of this is just to get something mergeable in. People have been working on this for two and a half years now, but I'm using it in production and I don't want to keep messing around with custom patches, because I am extremely lazy. You're correct that I removed a lot of stuff; the plan is to get it in piecemeal afterwards. Note that this doesn't include code *I* need! I'll need another PR after this, even if my sole goal is "my needs are met", which it isn't.

Git makes it kind of a pain to change the author of a change, but if that's the biggest issue, I'll happily jump through the hoops to make it "your" change; you'll also note that I asked for feedback on authors to include in this PR [as well as in the notification I posted in your PR](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/90510#issuecomment-3278168746). I (mostly :V) do not care about credit here, I care about getting the code in.

All that said, I do want to say that I'm worried that this will end up with another multi-month delay and get bumped to 4.7 or later. For reference, I offered to help out [back in May](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/90510#issuecomment-2899200080) and ended up sending you [a fix PR for various issues and regressions](https://github.com/migeran/godot/pull/3) that was never touched or acknowledged. A month later I provided [the code needed for C# support](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/90510#issuecomment-3053344274) which has also not been included. Instead we've got a 7000-line diff that fails 19/20 tests; meanwhile [I've already gotten a small code change merged specifically to fix a problem I ran into with updating libgodot in May](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110360). **Your code is valuable and I am not trying to diminish your contributions** but there's logistical work needed for practical upstream inclusion, and that's what I'm trying to handle.

I am volunteering to do the unexciting grunt work to merge one reasonable chunk at a time. Please let me! Then you can focus on the parts you care about the most, I can focus on the parts I care about the most, and we can all end up better off.

And if that means you want to provide me with a paragraph or two of credit to be included in the eventual commit message, please provide me with that paragraph and I'll get it in, and will make sure it remains in any eventual squash.
author: zorbathut

body:
Dear Ben,

I saw your message last week, and I also clearly (or at least I hoped) answered then, that we are finishing up the update to our PR as well: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/90510#issuecomment-3283937412

Obviously, such a big change needs to be split up, which we always intended and offered to do even during the earliest discussions of our PR, and I also clearly stated in our latest update: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/90510#issuecomment-3306869647

To be clear, my colleagues and I have been developing our Migeran LibGodot version for about 3 years now (first public mention of it is from November 2022), independently from other attempts.

I believe that you only wanted to help with your PR, but the way it was created was unfortunate.

As an example, how a similar situation is usually handled in Godot, here is our work on the Shader Baker feature, which was included in 4.5: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/102552

Here, we made a significant contribution: implemented the Metal support for the Shader Baker, which then Stuart Carnie refactored, and then we worked together to iron out a few more issues. But we did not open a new PR and downgrade Dario (the original developer) to a Co-Author. At the same time, we were properly attributed together with Stuart on the final commit by Dario as Co-Authors.

We (Migeran) will split up our current PR and open new PRs next week, before Wednesday (the next scheduled Core meeting), not just for the core part, but also for the other, more advanced features that build on the core. We will list you on the core PR as Co-Author for your contribution to the splitting of our Migeran LibGodot PR.

This should provide a good basis for future collaboration, because I know a lot of people would love to see .NET support, and it would be great to get your work included as a follow-up PR on top of our PRs.

I hope that this will settle the issue.

Kind regards,
Gergely

author: KoBeWi

body:
> Lack of up/down arrows means you can no longer easily increment or decrement the value.

The main issue was to allow sliders for exported int variables so it shouldn't affect all the editor properties alongside the fix, also it has a workaround to just change the exported variable type from int to float like this.

```gdscript
# Shows arrows
@export_range(-60, 60, 1, "or_greater", "or_less") var my_int: int = 64

# Shows Slider
@export_range(-60, 60, 1, "or_greater", "or_less") var my_int: float = 64
```

A better fix is to add a new hint string to force sliders for integer variables, we already have "hide_slider" so we can add "int_as_float" to always treat integer properties as floats inside EditorSpinSlider.

> Could the arrows be shown in addition to the slider?

It's possible but will increase the complexity of the input and draw methods.
author: WhalesState

body:
@KoBeWi
> Could the arrows be shown in addition to the slider?

That could work, but as Whales said, it would increase code complexity. And I would argue that it would add too much visual noise as well.

@WhalesState
> A better fix is to add a new hint string to force sliders for integer variables, we already have "hide_slider" so we can add "int_as_float" to always treat integer properties as floats inside EditorSpinSlider.

Leaving it up to the user is indeed an option, but I would rather have something like "force_slider" than "int_as_float", since there are some changes internally when an integer is being used.

I will try the extra option path, let's see how it goes.
author: YeldhamDev

body:
> Leaving it up to the user is indeed an option, but I would rather have something like "force_slider" than "int_as_float"

`use_slider` also may be a better hint, since using `force_slider` and `hide_slider` together is confusing where `force` may mean (should always be used), so we can use `force_slider` internally and `use_slider` as the hint string.

> since there are some changes internally when an integer is being used.

Yes i just realized that we can't disable `editing_integer` so it will need another variable `force_slider` and inside the class we use `editing_integer && !force_slider` instead of `editing_integer && get_max() == get_min()`, and inside `EditorPropertyInteger::setup` we add an extra argument for `use_slider` which sets `EditorSpinSlider::force_slider` to true.
author: WhalesState

body: Reworked, see the OP.
author: YeldhamDev

body:
> Reworked, see the OP.

We could have introduced a new variable like `EditorSpinSlider::force_slider` and avoided breaking compatibility by renaming the hint from `hide_slider` to `hide_control`.

By only adding one more string hint `use_slider` or `force_slider`, we keep the fix minimal while preserving compatibility.

body:
I've been wrestling with the pre commit hooks a bit.

* I wrapped the headers, `GraphEdit::update_theme` and the `set_theme` call in `#ifdef TOOLS_ENABLED` so it will not fail non-editor builds
*I added a null check of the editor singleton inside `set_theme` since it failed on headless Linux builds (maybe due to singleton not being ready yet?)

I'd appreciate someone taking a closer look at my workarounds since maybe there's a simple way to handle this.

Question: since GraphEdit is a usuable node in a shipped game, should the default font be msdf outside the editor as well? 
MSDF will look better by default but we might want to leave theme control up to the user.
author: StamLord

body:
Files in `scene/` should not have dependencies on editor code, so I'm afraid this isn't the correct way to fix the issue. The previous approach was more logical from a technical perspective; it just needed to be more scoped to avoid affecting the *entire* AnimationTree editor (and affect just the GraphEdit that's within).

> Question: since GraphEdit is a usuable node in a shipped game

GraphEdit/GraphNode are also usable in exported projects, e.g. in [Material Maker](https://github.com/RodZill4/material-maker).

body: I tried several things, but was unable to replicate your scenario. What I assume happened is, you had point index 10 pinned in the project the previous time you tested (importing the original zip, it does indeed show up as selected in the array editor, but not visually) and now since this visual point contains 2 physical points, with 1 of them being de-selected, it visually shows it as such. In fact, point 10 was still selected even in the 2nd zip you sent (in the array editor, not visually, since there's a deselected overlapping point): (the point in question, index 10, with 142 as the overlap point, coordinate axis for convenience) In a realistic scenario, users can click the "old points" to deselect them, and click again to select all the overlapping ones. Furthermore, (as long as you can't replicate this from clicking or other such actions) I don't quite see this as a bug. The visual clicking adding all the overlapping points is a QoL improvement, but it shouldn't be forced on the users. If a user wants to select 1 specific physical point manually without all the overlapping ones, they should be able to do so (via array editor or in code). Edit: I think I got your point after writing the entire comment - at this point, my solution no longer matches with the title of the PR. I can re-add the highlight as long as _any_ point in the overlap is selected.

body:
Please open a proposal in the [proposals repo](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals) so contributors can gauge the interest in this feature and get a sense of how many users would like to have this option. 

Also note, the implementation here is incomplete since the shaders still read from the transform. Accordingly, they are reading from uninitialized data. As far as the GPU is concerned, you are using the same amount of memory bandwidth, and this introduces an error that will crash on some devices or provide invalid results on others.

body: This seems well done, but we shouldn't add new formats without user demand. There are a lot of potential formats out there and only a few are actually useful in practice. body:
Heya,

there's a recent, open PR that adds the same functionality. Not sure which one is preferred?
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/109976

A small proposal: instead this being hidden in editor preferences, we can have both rotation types available at the same time. In Blender for example, when you click outside the rotation gizmo, you rotate along the current view plane. When you click inside the rotation gizmo, you get trackball rotation. There's a video example in the relevant feature proposal.
https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/7753#issuecomment-2337710444
author: Lamoot

body:
> Heya,
> 
> there's a recent, open PR that adds the same functionality. Not sure which one is preferred? #109976
> 
> A small proposal: instead this being hidden in editor preferences, we can have both rotation types available at the same time. In Blender for example, when you click outside the rotation gizmo, you rotate along the current view plane. When you click inside the rotation gizmo, you get trackball rotation. There's a video example in the relevant feature proposal. [godotengine/godot-proposals#7753 (comment)](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/7753#issuecomment-2337710444)

I completely missed the other PR, so it looks like we are indeed doing the same work. I think either implementation would work. If nothing else, this is the first time I'm doing something in the Godot engine code so it's a good learning opportunity for me. 

I updated the implementation so that if arcball rotation is enabled, dragging outside the gizmo rotates the object in the camera's plane and dragging on the gizmo uses arcball. 

I do still like having an option to turn it off completely, but the default value is set to arcball. A user could set it to traditional if they don't like arcball rotation.

As expected, that was not the issue.  Strange that it is specific to this integration test and all the others work.  I even stepped through the others in the debugger and they are working as expected.

And even more strange that this test does work when manually testing it.  It is just the integration test that fails.

My guess is things are behaving properly, but that this integration test relies on the bug being present. body:
Fixed ``resource_format_binary.cpp`` and also introduced the parameter ``p_parent_path`` to ``ResourceLoader::_load``.  I'll add the reasoning to the issue description as well as this comment.

``ResourceLoader::_load`` is also running into race conditions here:

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/3c9d03b8755968ae305b8cfebe886c56402cb044/core/io/resource_loader.cpp#L294-L300

These lines work great for a synchronous situation.  However, in an async context, it is not gauranteed that the next task on the stack requires the current task to complete before it can run. It becomes especially problematic if multiple async load requests are made for packed scenes with the same dependencies.  A situation such as this (my particular case) can arise.

Say we have a scene setup such as this:

```
sunglasses.tscn
|-- sunglasses.glb
```

and then I have multiple inherited scenes such as:

```
sunglasses_red.tscn (inherits sunglasses.tscn)
|-- sunglasses.glb
```

```
sunglasses_blue.tscn (inherits sunglasses.tscn)
|-- sunglasses.glb
```

```
sunglasses_green.tscn (inherits sunglasses.tscn)
|-- sunglasses.glb
```

And then I load all 3 asyncronously:

```cs
 ResourceLoader.LoadThreadedRequest("res://sunglasses_red.tscn");
 ResourceLoader.LoadThreadedRequest("res://sunglasses_blue.tscn");
 ResourceLoader.LoadThreadedRequest("res://sunglasses_green.tscn");
 ```

And this is where it gets interesting. A rare (but possible as in my case) situation can arise that results in a stack overflow crash.  It goes like this:

```
"res://sunglasses_red.tscn" gets added to loading tasks
"res://sunglasses.tscn" gets added to loading tasks. Registers with "res://sunglasses_red.tscn" as subtask 
"res://sunglasses.glb" gets added to loading tasks. Registers with "res://sunglasses.tscn" as subtask
loader checks for any q'd load requests
"res://sunglasses_blue.tscn" gets added to loading tasks. Registers with "res://sunglasses.glb" as subtask
"res://sunglasses_blue.tscn" and "res://sunglasses.glb" call each other's load methods recursively until stack overflow
```

The solution:

Pass ``p_parent_path`` to ``ResourceLoader::_load`` so we know for sure who to register subtasks with rather than trying to infer based on the current stack.

author: WesleyClements

body:
> To resolve this, I used the fact that the offset in the `child_items` array between any given CanvasItem and it's next sibling is `ysort_children_count + 1` to skip all child CanvasItems if their parent is culled due to `visibility_layer`.

Such offsets are valid only while counting/gathering the items to be y-sorted. After all the gathered items are actually y-sorted these previously calculated offsets are no longer relevant. Currently in this PR the skipping is applied after the sorting, aka the solution is incorrect.

This PR in action:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c0c8b618-58b5-4ead-9ccc-0d7eefeda233




---

I think what should be sufficient is extending these to include visibility layer check:
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/adb2ec043ed61ea8451f2593171d7163303b7ba5/servers/rendering/renderer_canvas_cull.cpp#L114
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/adb2ec043ed61ea8451f2593171d7163303b7ba5/servers/rendering/renderer_canvas_cull.cpp#L164

and marking y-sorting as dirty on changing given item's visibility layer, just like it's done on changing the visibility:
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/adb2ec043ed61ea8451f2593171d7163303b7ba5/servers/rendering/renderer_canvas_cull.cpp#L645-L650
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/adb2ec043ed61ea8451f2593171d7163303b7ba5/servers/rendering/renderer_canvas_cull.cpp#L613-L620

This should make the relevant items (and their children/subtrees) be not even included in `child_items`/y-sorting.
author: kleonc
WesleyClements
kleonc
WesleyClements
WesleyClements

body:
@WesleyClements Actually after rethinking my previous suggestion was incorrect in the first place, specifically combining the layers and-wise. E.g. when we have y-sorted:
```
- Item (layers: 1)
  - Child (layers: 2)
```
Then such child would never be rendered, as and-wise combined layer for the child would be 0. But it should be rendered if the cull mask includes both layer 1 and layer 2 (that's how it works without y-sorting).

> I've thought about this a bit more and figured a way to solve the bug that doesn't add more properties to Item, doesn't add more allocation overhead, and prevents sorting items that won't be rendered.

... and it doesn't have the layer checking issue I've just mentioned above. :)

> Effectively when collecting the children, if an Item doesn't pass the `p_canvas_cull_mask` check, we skip adding it to the array and subtract the size of that branch from the total length of the array. I think the main benefit of this approach is that the skipped Items won't be sorted.

All makes perfect sense to me, good job!

The optimization in `_count_ysort_children` also looks good to me, as when `ysort_children_count != 1` it should be always up to date. Probably we could also add `&& ysort_owner->ysort_children_count != -1` to the loop condition here:
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/149a4b4ca12e2baf700020fc10f00d5b0a5e9f0d/servers/rendering/renderer_canvas_cull.cpp#L174-L179

author: hhyyrylainen

body: > As far as I can tell, there's no error callstack associated with the print, so I cannot easily tell where it is coming from. However, after a bit more searching I discovered this: #111792 > > And in our game we do have quite extensive key handling and those error prints begin after another print related to our custom key rebinding system doing its thing. So it may be triggered by trying to convert keycodes and apply input settings. Though we don't have an absolute ton of startup logging, so it could also be triggered later. > > Edit: I should have looked a tiny bit further, as going to our keybinding menu sure enough I see where the character replacements happened: > > > Edit 2: for reference here is how it looks with Godot 4.5: > > > (those ö, and å letters do show up in 4.5.1 but I cropped the screenshot differently as I was doing it quickly) Yea, that would be the kind of bug introduced by these changes I made to string parsing. I'm very sorry about that! In your custom key handling code, do you happen to call `String::chr` with keycodes? Keycodes being converted through `String::chr` has been the primary cause of this error message being printed in Godot's own codebase (hopefully, all fixed in 4.5.1?). `String::chr` used to (in 4.4) silently return an empty string. In 4.5, it returned a string with the `\0` character in it, which was unintended, because it can cause all kinds of string handling bugs. In 4.5.1, it errors and returns a string with the replacement character instead.
author: Ivorforce

body:
I did a bit of debugging and I do not think our code calls keycode to character conversion (like I initially thought). However, I found out from where the incorrect text was coming from.

It looks like `DisplayServer.KeyboardGetLabelFromPhysical` does not work correctly in 4.5.1

In 4.5 if I run the following code:

```c#
DisplayServer.KeyboardGetLabelFromPhysical(Key.Left)
```

The result is `Key.Left` as expected.

But in 4.5.1 I get the result from the same line of code that it is: `65533` which is not a valid value in the Key enum. So somehow that call to the engine is scrambling up the key codes.

I put this code in my game's startup and it makes it quit in 4.5.1 whereas 4.5 works normally:

```c#
        if (DisplayServer.KeyboardGetLabelFromPhysical(Key.Left) != Key.Left)
        {
            GD.PrintErr("Key code changed!");
            GetTree().Quit();
            return;
        }
```

That code in 4.5.1 also triggers the print I saw:
```
Unicode parsing error, some characters were replaced with � (U+FFFD): Unexpected NUL character
```

So I think that is the thing our game does that triggers the error print from the engine as well.

Should I open a new issue for this?

Edit: I checked the documentation just in case but it doesn't seem like the return value of the function should have changed: https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/classes/class_displayserver.html#class-displayserver-method-keyboard-get-label-from-physical

author: lumiscosity

body:
Even more can be saved (though usually it's not much, in the order of bytes) by combining multiple PNG optimizers like [ECT](https://github.com/fhanau/Efficient-Compression-Tool), [pngwolf](https://github.com/jibsen/pngwolf-zopfli) [Deflopt](https://encode.su/threads/3257-Deflopt), [defluff](https://encode.su/threads/1214-defluff-a-deflate-huffman-optimizer), [deft4j](https://github.com/NeRdTheNed/deft4j), and probably even more that I forgot (yeah, it's a lot). ECT should provide the biggest gains.
I was able to optimize main/splash.png from 14705 bytes to 14568, for example.
All of this should be lossless and safe to do, though I haven't actually checked that.
Not sure how worth it this is (considering it can take a while to optimize, and how small the savings are), but it should be even more free savings, so I thought it was worth to mention.
author: Oondanomala

body: I'll admit this is my first time hearing about some of those extra tools. It's possible some of the remaining byte savings from those are from stripping some chunks that this oxipng config avoids, such as the colorspace information, removing which is often undesirable and can lead to visible changes in the image. I'll have to manually verify these on my own time.
author: lumiscosity

body:
The original main/splash.png does not have any additional chunks, so that is definitely not what happened on that image.
Also, as far as i know (if configured correctly) none of the programs should remove extra chunks, as they are all generic deflate optimizers. (and some of the byte savings are straight up smaller than a chunk can be, which is 12 bytes IIRC)
The output is also pixel by pixel identical according to ImageMagick.
Still, each should probably be checked manually using [pngcheck](https://github.com/pnggroup/pngcheck) for extra chunks and something like ImageMagick to make sure the actual image is the same.

body:
> I'm guessing the bottleneck is the repeated `get_child` call, both because of the thread guard, but also because it does a lot of unnecessary checks.

The bottleneck was that `_update_children_cache()` was called when `include_internal==true` which results in two loops over all children instead of just one loop with the current implementation, you can see the difference when `include_internal==false` it's only faster by 2ms which was the cost of calling `get_child`.
author: WhalesState

body:
> The bottleneck was that _update_children_cache() was called when include_internal==true which results in two loops over all children instead of just one loop with the current implementation, you can see the difference when include_internal==false it's only faster by 2ms which was the cost of calling get_child.

Yes, but we expect children to update rarely. Updating the children cache is _somewhat_ costly once, but iterating it afterwards is nearly free compared to iterating the `HashMap`.The reason being that the children cache has all elements in a contiguous array, while iterating the children `HashMap` can results in a lot of cache misses. It is important for the performance of `Node` to use the children cache wherever possible.
author: Ivorforce
WhalesState

body:
The implementation of `get_child_count` can exploit the fact that we do not need to iterate the children at all when `p_include_internal` is `true`. This makes for an obvious shortcut without trade-off.

However, `get_children` does need to iterate the children, and therefore it needs to use the children cache. If it weren't using the children cache, we'd be losing most of the performance to cache misses in cold access situations.
author: Ivorforce
WhalesState

body:
> a better implementation may be to check if data.children_cache_dirty so we loop over the HashMap instead of resulting in two loops over all children

Yes, this would be faster on first access (with the caveat that it would work for the scenario where `p_include_internal` is `false`). 
However, we shouldn't be optimizing for the 'first access' case. 

Like I mentioned above, in general we expect the children to be relatively constant over multiple calls. Even if they change once every few frames, there might be dozens of calls to `get_children` afterwards where the children cache can be exploited.
While you have benchmarked the improvement of performance on hot, initial access, your benchmark does not represent the case we expect to occur most often, which is cold, repeated access. For this case, the children cache is the best solution, even if it does incur a performance cost on the first call where the children cache needs to be created.
author: Ivorforce
WhalesState

body: So we can update the cache if it's dirty inside the method when `get_children(true)` is called and the cache is dirty instead of looping twice over all children, it will remain fast and yet will update the cache if it's dirty.
author: WhalesState

body:
> So we can update the cache if it's dirty inside the method when get_children(true) is called and the cache is dirty instead of looping twice over all children, it will remain fast and yet will update the cache if it's dirty.

Feel free to benchmark it against my proposed solution! I suspect the second loop isn't a bottleneck, compared to the thread guards and extraneous checks that both my proposed solution and your proposed solution mitigate. After this optimization, I'm guessing the allocation and data copying to be the new bottleneck (and not a secondary loop).
author: Ivorforce
WhalesState

body: Thank you for helping in optimizing it, it appeared that we can't relay on `get_children(true)` without validating cache or the order may be incorrect, I have only insured that `get_child` is never called which have added smaller optimization, also updated the code that i have used for testing and insured that i always validate the cache before the benchmarks which was why the first call was much slower than the others.

The usual pattern we use is to defer the update to the end of the frame using `call_deferred()`. Is there any reason that won't work here? 

Exposing a redundant feature for a small theoretical optimization isn't something we like to do
author: clayjohn

body: For example, If you have 100 icons for your game and you want to update their colors, scale and saturation when the theme changes it will emit changed signal 3 times for each texture instead of just once. All the setters do call `DPITexture::_update_texture()` which emits changed which calls `TextureRect::_texture_changed()` or `Button::_texture_changed()` and both of them calls `queue_redraw()` and `update_minimum_size()`. And to avoid that you will have to create 100 new textures instead of just being able to update them with minimal cost.
