body:
> I also added `canvas_item_set_self_modulate` in an effort to add support for `draw_circle`, but it didn't work. I left it there anyway just in case. AFAIK, highlight on `draw_circle` does not seem to work in 4.5.

Custom drawing is affected by highlighting in 4.5 just fine (with the same nesting problem). In the current version of the PR it works as expected as far as I've tested.

[nested-tilemap-layer-minimal-example_v2.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22493237/nested-tilemap-layer-minimal-example_v2.zip) (`main.tscn`)

- `v4.5.stable.official` [876b29033]

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8fe71168-460c-4d2b-9f8b-8cba87787792

- This PR (`v4.6.dev.gh-110768` [89a9be4e8])

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/09b1d0a4-749f-4b62-bb20-d5bd1912352b

> Here my failing test on my branch:

So not sure why it didn't work for you.


---

> added support for scene tiles, in a separate commit for easier diff reading.
> 
> As you can see, scene tiles highlight work

Applying highlighting to scene tiles by modifying their roots' modulates like it's being done is not really reliable though, as user might modify scene root's modulate anyhow on their own, and such user-made changes won't trigger the internal update like `TileMapLayer::_scenes_update` where the highlighting would be reapplied. Meaning highlighting might be missing (depending on what changes user makes) between:
- (after) user changing the modulate,
- (before) subsequent internal update of the given cell containing scene tile.

Example of the highlighting breaking:
[nested-tilemap-layer-minimal-example_v2.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22493237/nested-tilemap-layer-minimal-example_v2.zip) (`main_scenes.tscn`)

Scene tile is a plain Sprite2D with such script (changing modulate on a timer):
```gdscript
@tool
extends Sprite2D

const T := 2.0
var t := T
func _process(delta: float) -> void:
	t -= delta
	if t <= 0.0:
		t = T
		modulate = Color(
			randf_range(0.5, 1.0),
			randf_range(0.5, 1.0),
			randf_range(0.5, 1.0),
			1.0
		)
```
In action (`v4.6.dev.gh-110768` [89a9be4e8]):

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/98abfb4e-51cd-4ed6-89a4-64e5e718b081

Of course it's a made up example specifically to show it breaking, but the user tweening the modulate etc. is rather not an unexpected use case. Maybe it being done in the editor / `@tool` script is less likely, but still. All I'm saying is that if we ship it like that, I expect it to be reported as a bug, sooner or later.

For the highlighting being applied to the scene roots' modulate to be reliable, we would need to somehow intercept calls / hook to `CanvasItem::set_modulate`. E.g. for TileMapLayer we intercept `CanvasItem::set_self_modulate` to handle the change properly by overriding `CanvasItem::set_self_modulate` (AFAIK it was made `virtual` specifically for that). But not sure how we could intercept `CanvasItem::set_modulate` for scene tile instances. Adding a signal like `modulate_changed` would be a trivial solution, but I suspect it would be not desired performance-wise. So, as already mentioned, I don't know how to tackle this properly.

---

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/3d91a48298f30c34747b9ad9a350be0bd66a87d4/scene/2d/tile_map_layer.cpp#L344

Note this should also use the highlighted `layer_modulate` instead of `get_self_modulate()`. AFAICT a quadrant might be (re)created without any of the flags trigerring the render update being dirty (I think e.g. after placing a new tile).

@Ir1ana And of course thanks for all the efforts! 🙂

author: Repiteo

body:
Thank you for your efforts, but please note that this only affects the static analyzer and does not provide runtime guarantees for untyped code. In the current implementation, Godot/GDScript provide runtime guarantees for all types except enumerations. (Plus, type hints for signal parameters are declarative.)

Adding appropriate checks to the core methods `connect()`, `emit_signal()`, and so on will incur some runtime penalty. Optimization will require multiple versions of these methods: a safe but slow one (for the general untyped case) and a fast but unsafe one (for arguments validated during static analysis). So we need to weigh the pros and cons.

- touch controller
- hand interaction

We're leaving out the simple controller as it doesn't cover our use cases and we have runtimes that will preference it over more applicable interaction profiles.
author: BastiaanOlij

body:
> After that we'll do a separate PR to clean up our default action map so it only contains:

To follow-up on something I said at the XR team meeting yesterday: I just tested on the Pico 4 Ultra, and it looks like I was wrong - it's _not_ able to map to the "Touch controller" profile.

So, we could either continue with our original planned 3 interaction profiles, but just add the "Pico 4 controller" profile to it (which would be my preference, because I'd love to see the default action map get much smaller). Or, be more conservative, and only remove the "Simple controller" profile.

It's a question of if we're worried that there's more runtimes that can't map to the "Touch controller" profile. We can test on all the headsets we have available, but beyond that, we can't really know without going forward with removing most interaction profiles and seeing if users with other headsets find any issues.
author: dsnopek

body:
> So, we could either continue with our original planned 3 interaction profiles, but just add the "Pico 4 controller" profile to it (which would be my preference, because I'd love to see the default action map get much smaller). Or, be more conservative, and only remove the "Simple controller" profile.

I'm in favor of the option of just adding the Pico 4 controller, bearing in mind that new users (who may have a harder time troubleshooting why their controls are not working) will in the majority be using one of Meta, Pico or Google headsets. Whereas advanced users targeting a wider range of devices will know to update the action map.
author: m4gr3d

body:
yeah I'm in favour of culling everything but just keeping the PICO 4 mapping. Just because we have an increasing number of PICO users.

Hopefully once we get confirmation the generic controllers gets support on Meta and Pico headsets we can remove those.

People can **always** add profiles back in that they need for their projects.

This also brings things in line with OpenXRs position that you should only include interaction profiles that you've actually tested.

author: KoBeWi

body:
The code looks good overall. This basically adds `uid` field to script class cache.

However the usefulness is limited right now; the editor filesystem is able to update the cache even if a file was moved externally. Is there any benefit in caching the script UID?
author: KoBeWi

body:
> However the usefulness is limited right now; 

Indeed.

> the editor filesystem is able to update the cache even if a file was moved externally. Is there any benefit in caching the script UID?

Currently, global classes are tracked by class name (no path is needed). This may work when only the file is moved.

If multiple scripts with the same class name exist, when the editor is restarted, the paths in the editor will be sorted in alphabetical order, and the scripts at the end will be registered with the ScriptServer.

`EditorFileSystem` records the class name of each script, although `ScriptServer` does not support alternatives (multiple scripts with the same name).

If perform some complex file operations before starting the editor, the uid and path may not be reliable when the editor starts. Using uid and path makes it possible to track changes to files. Especially when there are multiple scripts with the same name. 

For example, before opening the editor, you copy a previously written script for reuse, but its class name happens to be the same as some scripts in the current project.

The uid is added here to use the uid and path to determine the script activated by the current class name.

body:
Here's a video with a script i made to test the margins issue with different scales. We also can use floor for margins to match the border width, ie. using (1.5 * EDCSALE) for content margins and border width will give the same result since border width is integer.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ce7d1611-3045-4902-b930-4b62f7e56300


<details>
<summary>Script used in the video (Click to Expand)</summary>

```gdscript
@tool
class_name CodeEditTest
extends CodeEdit

var update_requested: bool = false

@export_enum("default", "floor", "round", "ceil") var operation = 0:
	set(value):
		operation = value
		request_update()

@export_range(0, 16, 1.0, "prefer_slider") var normal_content_margin: int = 4:
	set(value):
		if normal_content_margin == value:
			return
		normal_content_margin = value
		request_update()

@export_range(0.5, 2, 0.01) var editor_scale: float = 1.0:
	set(value):
		if editor_scale == value:
			return
		editor_scale = value
		request_update()

@export var normal_style: StyleBoxFlat = StyleBoxFlat.new():
	set(value):
		if normal_style == value:
			return
		normal_style = value
		if normal_style:
			add_theme_stylebox_override("normal", normal_style)
		else:
			remove_theme_stylebox_override("normal")
		text = "(\"Long Long Long Long Long Long Long Long Long Long Long Long Line\")"
		request_update()


func request_update():
	if update_requested:
		return
	update_requested = false
	_update.call_deferred()


func _update():
	if normal_style:
		normal_style.bg_color = Color.BLACK
		normal_style.draw_center = false
		var margin: float = normal_content_margin * editor_scale
		match operation:
			1:
				margin = floor(margin)
			2:
				margin = round(margin)
			3:
				margin = ceil(margin)
			_:
				pass
		normal_style.set_content_margin_all(margin)
		normal_style.set_border_width_all(normal_content_margin * editor_scale)
		prints("left_margin:", normal_style.get_margin(SIDE_LEFT), "right_margin:", normal_style.get_margin(SIDE_RIGHT))
		prints("style minimum size:", normal_style.get_minimum_size())
		prints("Control minimum size:", get_minimum_size())
		print()
	set_deferred("scroll_horizontal", 10000)
	


func _init() -> void:
	line_folding = true
	gutters_draw_breakpoints_gutter = true
	gutters_draw_bookmarks = true
	gutters_draw_executing_lines = true
	gutters_draw_fold_gutter = true
	gutters_draw_line_numbers = true
	minimap_draw = true
	resized.connect(request_update)

```

body:
The border outline issue looks like it is an issue with Labels with transparent borders and antialiasing. 
The actual border you added looks too similar to the Label bg, the border properties can just be removed and it should be fixed.

It should to be much less transparent, it is still too hard to read when there is text behind it.

The Shift modifier only works with Ctrl, so it should say Ctrl+Shift instead.

You should be able to use `TTRN` instead of `TTR` to handle the plurals (I think).

For the UID/Path can this change automatically based on the setting? It would be useful to know which is UID and which is path.

We should probably be as concise as we can here, so it is easy to use as a reference without needing to read too much. 
Most of the message is already implied, the user knows its drag and drop since it only shows on drag over, they are already hovering over 'the script', and "hold %s when dropping" is the only real action.

Like:
```
Drop node path.
Ctrl: @onready var
Alt: @export var
```

```
Drop file path.
Ctrl: const preload file path
Ctrl+Shift: const preload UID path
Alt: @export var
```

body:
> @m4gr3d I just noticed that there's errors in Logcat while running the instrumented tests, and it seems the tests aren’t running.
> 
> Error1: App doesn't auto-launch; I have to launch it manually from launcher. 

I checked out the branch on a new machine, and I'm able to run and replicate the test results, so it may be an issue with how you ran the tests. I've updated the description with some instructions for how to run from Android Studio. Can you give it a try and see if that works.


>Error2:
> 
> ```
> ERROR: Couldn't load file 'res://project.binary', error code 19.
>     at: _load_settings_text_or_binary (core/config/project_settings.cpp:920)
> ```

That's expected. A Godot project can either be loaded from `project.godot` or `project.binary` (binary version of the human readable version), but the engine always tries the binary version first, and shows this error when it doesn't find it before falling back to the human readable version. 
The instrumented tests use the human readable verison (`project.godot`, with the project under `platform/android/java/app/src/instrumented/assets`) so you'll see this error every time they run.

> 
> Error3:
> 
> ```
> ERROR: Variant is too deep! Bailing.
>     at: _variant_to_jvalue (platform/android/jni_utils.cpp:93)
>     GDScript backtrace (most recent call first):
>            [0] test_variant_conversion_safe_from_stack_overflow (res://test/javaclasswrapper/java_class_wrapper_tests.gd:136)
>            [1] __exec_test (res://test/base_test.gd:12)
>            [2] run_tests (res://test/javaclasswrapper/java_class_wrapper_tests.gd:17)
>            [3] _launch_tests (res://main.gd:22)
> ```

Also expected. The failing condition is the test crashing with a stack overflow error as described [here](https://github.com/m4gr3d/godot/blob/a68d36fbac32f7eb5b0405efc3dd7289248a02ea/platform/android/java/app/src/instrumented/assets/test/javaclasswrapper/java_class_wrapper_tests.gd#L135). 
The passing condition is the test not crashing and this error being logged. 
This test validates the fix added in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110452.
> 
> I'm running with `./gradlew connectedInstrumentedDebugAndroidTest -Pperform_signing=true` Also tried with Android Studio but same errors.

body:
For the record, one use case for `is_class` is to workaround tight coupling between classes and avoid having to include a header to resolve the class name. This makes it possible e.g. in `core`  to do `obj->is_class("Node")` without having to include `scene/main/node.h` (which would break `core` encapsulation).

None of the replacements here seem to be that case though so it's fine. We'd usually favor `Object::cast_to<T>()` over `is_class()` for these, but if we have now `derives_from<T>` that's probably better semantics.
author: akien-mga

body:
> This makes it possible e.g. in core to do obj->is_class("Node") without having to include scene/main/node.h (which would break core encapsulation).

I want to raise that Core classes checking for non-Core classes still breaks encapsulation. It just doesn't break it on an include level. It's still worth avoiding this situation, even with `is_class`.

> I obviously don't know the details, but I can imagine that addressing filesystem scan failing is a better solution to your issue than this workaround.

Of course, but I believe this  is mostly a compositor issue, not necessarily on Godot's end. I'm not well versed enough in Wayland or Hyprland's source code to identify the issue let alone fix it just yet.

To specify, I think the issue *might* have to do with a failure to notify godot of the window being focused/unfocused properly, so although I can interact with the engine just fine, processes that rely on these notifications like the filesystem scan doesn't end up running
author: cruglet

body:
You can workaround it with an EditorContextMenuPlugin that calls EditorFileSystem's `scan_changes()`.

Also fun fact: FileSystem dock used to have a reload button, but it was unused for a long time: #103762
It shouldn't be necessary anymore; if editor doesn't automatically find changes, it's a bug.
author: KoBeWi

body:
> You can workaround it with an EditorContextMenuPlugin that calls EditorFileSystem's scan_changes().

Yeah I ran into this while researching before this PR, I just feel as though using a plugin here feels like overkill for something this trivial. 

> Also fun fact: FileSystem dock used to have a reload button, but it was unused for a long time: #103762
> It shouldn't be necessary anymore, if editor doesn't automatically find changes, it's a bug

In some display managers changes can be made externally when the window still believes it's in focus, and I don't think there's necessarily a guarantee those issues will completely disappear (especially when the bug is on the DM's side), so it's nice to have an option to be able to trigger this scan when the alternatives are to rely on plugins or reloading completely. 

But yeah I get where you're coming from. This just provides users with a quick recovery as opposed to either using plugins or waiting for each individual bug causing this issue to be identified and fixed.
author: cruglet

body:
Seems like there is a proposal: https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/12940
But based on the activity, you are the second person to run into this problem. Looks like a niche corner case, which is exactly what addons are for.
author: KoBeWi

body:
> Seems like there is a proposal: https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/12940
> 
> But based on the activity, you are the second person to run into this problem. Looks like a niche corner case, which is exactly what addons are for.

Yeah I get that it might look niche based on activity, but I think it's likely more about underreporting than rarity. It’s probable most users wouldn’t file a bug report or write a plugin for something like this, they’ll just reload the project or live with the inconvenience (I’ve done so until now, and the proposal mentioned seems to have done the same).

In addition, the proposal also notes that addons are more effort than the simple recovery option users expect. With using addons,it is also therefore expected to have the same addon functionality be placed throughout all user projects just to remedy this one issue, which again seems unnecessary.

Having this builtin is an easy way to remove that friction some people experience, and it's not a heavy implementation either. At the very least, I think it’s clear that a nontrivial number of users *do* or at the very least *can* encounter this, and labeling it as niche doesn’t seem to really justify leaving the onus up to the user to work around this, especially when it's a bug.

But once again, I do get where you're coming from. It's just a matter of providing that quick and easy option to the user as opposed to making them deal with it on their own, if encountered.

author: Calinou

body:
> I don't think the `==` and `!=` operators should even attempt to compare the bound arguments and should automatically judge the Callables unequal if they aren't the same instance.

Note that this is likely due to the `disconnect()` case. When disconnecting a callable from a signal, you don't need to repeat all the `bind()` and `unbind()` operations, the base callable is sufficient. This question has already been raised here, see:

* https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/81118
* https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/8341
* https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/10035
author: dalexeev

body:
> > I don't think the `==` and `!=` operators should even attempt to compare the bound arguments and should automatically judge the Callables unequal if they aren't the same instance.
> 
> Note that this is likely due to the `disconnect()` case. When disconnecting a callable from a signal, you don't need to repeat all the `bind()` and `unbind()` operations, the base callable is sufficient. This question has already been raised here, see:
> 
>     * [Inconsistent `Signal.is_connected` behaviour for callables that combine `bind` and `unbind` #81118](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/81118)
> 
>     * [Allow multiple connections from a signal with different bound parameters to the same callable in inspector godot-proposals#8341](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/8341)
> 
>     * [Allow differently-bound callables from the same callable on the same signal godot-proposals#10035](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/10035)

I see this has been a recurring discussion indeed, while it could be considered convenient, I really don't think `disconnect()` disconnecting all custom Callables if they're the same base Callable is enviable behavior. Is it expecting too much to ask users to keep a reference to the custom Callable if they want to disconnect it?
Godot already provides a way to list all Callables currently connected to a signal, so it shouldn't be a problem even if you do lose the reference to the custom Callable
author: PwaPois

body:
This is very bare bones of what I've been working on (and sadly haven't had time to continue on), I think as a starting point instead of doing the whole lot, this may be a good way forward.

I do think that in the long run, we should deprecate all camera setters in the rendering engine and move that logic into `Camera3D` itself. Towards users this will be completely transparent but it opens up the door for much wider use.
Though this is one part of what I was working on.

The main thing that is missing here, and why these types of PRs haven't been merged in the past, is that you can specify any type of projection including many that break rendering code. Most of these are due to shortcuts we've taken in assuming symmetrical projection matrices and we already have solutions there but they are triggered by our multiview setting (as this almost always results in asymmetrical projections).

Just like we have an `is_orthogonal` check in our `Projection` class, we should add an `is_symmetrical` check, and change all the logic in the rendering engine where we use unprojection shortcuts. Likely we can do this with a new define.
We also then need to check all the multiview code and check the impact of this change there.

>

It will be needed by: ray casting, fog, scene shader, LOD, HLOD, SSR, SSAO, SSIL, SSS, FSR, TAA (and likely more that I can't think of off the top of my head)
author: clayjohn

body:
> It will be needed by: ray casting, fog, scene shader, LOD, HLOD, SSR, SSAO, SSIL, SSS, FSR, TAA (and likely more that I can't think of off the top of my head)

With Ray casting do you mean ray casting as in physics, or ray casting as in any effect that does any form of ray marching or other shaders side ray tracing?
As for ray casting in physics after obtaining the ray origin and ray normal from the `Camera3D` object won't work seeing `Camera3D` is blissfully unaware of our overridden projection matrix.

As for the others, I think most will already work, at least most I've checked when stereo rendering.  Still we should indeed check each and everyone to make sure they behave as expected.

> So once the interface is implemented and this PR gets merged, will it be able to handle the oblique camera projection (changing the angle of the near plane) that is necessary for seamless portals

Yes it will. Oblique near/far planes are supported by the interface described in https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/11436 (either the user sets the planes values directly, or they pick a target object to extract plane orientation from)

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/551ce8d47feda9c81c870314745366b24957624b/servers/rendering/renderer_rd/shaders/environment/volumetric_fog.glsl#L135-L138

	vec4 view_pos4 = scene_params.inv_projection * vec4(view_pos, 1.0);
	view_pos = view_pos4.xyz / view_pos4.w;
```
I've spent a good number of days trying every way I can think of to fix the z clip. I'm out of ideas for now.

body:
@Glorax It's great that you are excited about this feature, but I'd like to ask you to refrain from commenting so much on PRs if it's not to do a technical review or report about you testing the PR.

When developing Godot we try not to rush implementing features, especially until we have consensus on the implementation and have validated that it does everything it should. For now concerns remain on both PRs implementing custom projection matrices for rendering, so neither has been merged yet. That's not to say that they're not useful, and if either PR works for your use case, you can cherry-pick it in your own fork and use it for your project.

But nagging contributors and maintainers regularly like you've done the past few months adds stress to already overburdened maintainers, and possibly stress or frustration to contributors.

We know that there is a lot of demand for this feature, and we expect that Godot will have support for this eventually, but for now neither implementation has been evaluated as sufficient by rendering maintainers, and we ask that users respect that and let us do our work without nagging.

author: zorbathut

body:
To @fire's point in this PR alongside #110441 - I see a lot of issues with the name `libgodot`, reason being that the `lib` prefix is a very strong convention in Android. When loading native libraries, Android will automatically bundle `lib`- prefixed libraries (in the folder for the target ABI), thus some binary otherwise named `godot` would in this instance be `libgodot.so`.

What about something like `GDEmbedded` or `GodotEmbedded`? I don't completely hate `embedded`, but that feels too generic.
author: EricApostal

body:
We should discuss if we want to call this "libgodot", however:

> What about something like `GDEmbedded` or `GodotEmbedded`? I don't completely hate `embedded`, but that feels too generic.

I'm not crazy about "embedded". That name is generic enough that it could conflict with some completely unrelated thing we want to call "embedded" in the future.

What I like about "libgodot" is that it is very specific, and sounds kind of like a product/feature name, so it seems unlikely to conflict with something else in the future. It may be the best option we've come up with so far.
author: dsnopek

body:
> What I like about "libgodot" is that it is very specific, and sounds kind of like a product/feature name, so it seems unlikely to conflict with something else in the future. It may be the best option we've come up with so far.

Yeah that makes sense, so long as there's no naming conflicts which it sounds like there aren't.

> Note that using `ProjectSettings.add_global_shader_parameter()` will call `RenderingServer.global_shader_parameter_get()`, which means this error is always printed:
> 
> ```
> ERROR: This function should never be used outside the editor, it can severely damage performance.
>    at: global_shader_parameter_get (./servers/rendering/renderer_rd/storage_rd/material_storage.cpp:1730)
>    GDScript backtrace (most recent call first):
>        [0] _enter_tree (res://addons/global_shader_parameter_test_plugin/global_shader_parameter_manager.gd:9)
> ```
> 
> We should probably have a way to silence this error while in the editor, considering this method is generally only called in an editor context. (Note that it _is_ possible to call during gameplay too, but in this case, I wouldn't silence the error.)

That is odd... In my tests (see my test project) that error is only being printed at runtime. 
I am getting this output in the editor when adding/removing that node from the scene tree:
```
[]
[&"global_test_script_var"]
```

from this code here:
```gdscript
@tool
extends Node
class_name GlobalShaderParameterManager

func _enter_tree() -> void:
	if Engine.is_editor_hint():
		var list: Array[StringName] = RenderingServer.global_shader_parameter_get_list()
		print(list)
	ProjectSettings.add_shader_global_parameter("global_test_script_var", RenderingServer.GLOBAL_VAR_TYPE_BOOL, true)
	if Engine.is_editor_hint():
		var list: Array[StringName] = RenderingServer.global_shader_parameter_get_list()
		print(list)
	ProjectSettings.set_shader_global_parameter("global_test_script_var", false)

func _exit_tree() -> void:
	ProjectSettings.remove_shader_global_parameter("global_test_script_var")
	if Engine.is_editor_hint():
		var list: Array[StringName] = RenderingServer.global_shader_parameter_get_list()
		print(list)
```

body:
This patch introduces the `touchscreen_only` and puts some juicy code behind that flag.

On touch devices, it would be great if buttons responded to screen taps, without having to rely on "emulate mouse from touch", as it is not always possible to set that flag without interfering with other things.

So I think that the touchscreen_only makes sense as a feature, but the code that processes events should still process it for cases where touchscreen_only is not set, for folks that just added a button and wanted the button to work without flipping a switch that might conflict.

> References to `VirtualJoystick` can be useful.

Why is a reference to the VirtualJoystick useful?
author: Kazox61

body:
> TouchScreenButton
Button for touch screen devices for gameplay use.
TouchScreenButton allows you to create on-screen buttons for touch devices. It's intended for gameplay use, such as a unit you have to touch to move.

Issue reports / MRP with `TouchScreenButton` are mostly related to virtual input control; now there's a pre-built solution for that. (`VirtualJoystick`) If we cannot completely remove the `TouchScreenButton` yet, the documentation should be adjusted accordingly.
author: Alex2782

body:
I just want to offer a rebuttal that there are touch inputs that support hovering.

For example, I plan to use multitouch support for XR finger input, and in XR you have full 6 degree of freedom information about the finger locations at all times when using hand tracking.

Also, tablets with a pen and stylus often show the hover location of the stylus. it's not multi-touch, but it is a form of touch input.

I'm not here to block this on hover support for touch input, just trying to make a case that there are applications (such as my VR framework) that may have a use for hover.

body:
I think some sort of setting like this is a very important addition from a UI/UX perspective after the changes in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110250 . As I understand it that PR changed things to make it easier to avoid showing unwanted focus rings around buttons when you're not using keyboard/controller navigation. But this misses the other big use of focus highlights of controls to show the user where keyboard entry or other secondary editing is currently directed (as in what text field you're currently editing, what color well is active when picking a color or other similar custom input controls). For these cases I think it's expected and needed for the control to get and show focus when clicked with a mouse, and unfortunate if this current functionality would break in 4.6.

If I understand it correctly however, this PR would only add a possible exception for TextEdit and LineEdit explicitly, so it won't be possible to use for custom Controls where you want or may be relying on the old behavior?

Perhaps this whole showing focus behavior could be handled better via something like the [control focus modes](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/classes/class_control.html#enum-control-focusmode) instead, allowing to set the behavior per control with a default rather than project wide with project settings like this?
author: lostminds

body:
> If I understand it correctly however, this PR would only add a possible exception for TextEdit and LineEdit explicitly, so it won't be possible to use for custom Controls where you want or may be relying on the old behavior?

Whether a custom `Control` shows focus or not depends entirely on you. Hidden focus is only ignored if you use `has_focus()` with `true`, so if you want your `Control` to always show it, you still can.
author: YeldhamDev

body:
> Whether a custom `Control` shows focus or not depends entirely on you. Hidden focus is only ignored if you use `has_focus()` with `true`, so if you want your `Control` to always show it, you still can.

I'm not sure I follow, I assume the engine would call `has_focus()` with `true` in this case to determine if it should show the focus ring? And as it's a built in class method `has_focus` can't be overriden to change what it returns in my custom control class. Say I have a custom control inheriting Button that brings up some selection interface (like a color picker). Currently if I set this to `focus_mode` ALL it will get a focus ring when it is clicked, which is what I want. This will also disappear as expected when changing focus to some other control. But as I understand it this will no longer happen after https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110250 (with default project settings) as the control received focus with from a mouse click, which will not be shown?
author: lostminds

body:
> Say I have a custom control inheriting Button that brings up some selection interface (like a color picker).

You can still force an existing `Control` that handles its own focus to show it by using `grab_focus(false)`.

2.1 also builds and runs on macOS 26 with the following small patch, so AGL code is unused.

<details>
  <summary>Patch</summary>

```diff
diff --git a/platform/osx/detect.py b/platform/osx/detect.py
index 20d313809e..321241b7f2 100644
--- a/platform/osx/detect.py
+++ b/platform/osx/detect.py
@@ -81,7 +81,7 @@ def configure(env):
     env.Append(LIBS=['pthread'])
     #env.Append(CPPFLAGS=['-F/Developer/SDKs/MacOSX10.4u.sdk/System/Library/Frameworks', '-isysroot', '/Developer/SDKs/MacOSX10.4u.sdk', '-mmacosx-version-min=10.4'])

-    env.Append(LINKFLAGS=['-framework', 'Cocoa', '-framework', 'Carbon', '-framework', 'OpenGL', '-framework', 'AGL', '-framework', 'AudioUnit', '-framework', 'CoreAudio', '-lz', '-framework', 'IOKit', '-framework', 'ForceFeedback'])
+    env.Append(LINKFLAGS=['-framework', 'Cocoa', '-framework', 'Carbon', '-framework', 'OpenGL', '-framework', 'AudioUnit', '-framework', 'CoreAudio', '-lz', '-framework', 'IOKit', '-framework', 'ForceFeedback'])
     env.Append(LINKFLAGS=["-mmacosx-version-min=10.9"])

diff --git a/thirdparty/libpng/pngpriv.h b/thirdparty/libpng/pngpriv.h
index 57e1bf989b..d8f5e6d277 100644

body:
> For one, some callers currently call Image::get_data() to gather each image's data into some list. If we simply used get_data().span(), the callee would be allowed to copy their data before return, the data would be deallocated after the call, and the Span created from it would be invalid. While this is not currently the case, the current API allows for it. To avoid this situation, I introduce get_data_span() to Image, to force it to return a Span to its own owned data. The new API could be used in other situations where a quick view into the data is needed.

I don't understand this. Doesn't `texture_create` always copy the contents of the `p_data` buffer before returning? Why are we worried about a span no longer being available after the call has returned?

body:
After deeper investigation using RenderDoc, I found that GPUs pad per-instance attributes to 8 float32 for cache/alignment purposes, even when only 4 float32 are actually used. 

Therefore I think a better solution would be to expose the transform matrix directly to the shaders. Since the 2D MultiMesh transform already occupies 8 float32, allowing shaders to reinterpret the matrix or apply custom math gives users the same flexibility intended by the initial proposal.

body:
All `Color` in Godot [is assumed](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/104666) to be encoded using the nonlinear sRGB transfer function. Any cases where this is not the case must be clearly documented. (As in, any time a `Color` is expected to use linear encoding, it should be clearly documented.)

Would you mind going through the chain of functions and clearly documenting (with comments or otherwise) when functions accept linear-encoded values instead of the expected nonlinear sRGB-encoded values?

When writing this, you can simply say that “color must be linear-encoded” or “color must use linear encoding”.

As a side note, I see some multiplications against an energy multiplier that are happening on the colour channels: indeed, this multiplication must occur on linear-encoded values otherwise it will produce incorrect results.
author: allenwp

body:
@allenwp I updated the pr with a better description of what is happening. Where I removed the ```srgb_to_linear``` call, ```clear_color``` is in fact encoded using the nonlinear sRGB transfer function. The issue is that converting this to linear now is incorrect because it will be converted a second time later when it is put into the uniform buffer for the sky shader.

Not sure if the comments should still be added given that the value is not being passed in linear encoding.
author: Kaleb-Reid

body:
> @allenwp I updated the pr with a better description of what is happening. Where I removed the ```srgb_to_linear``` call, ```clear_color``` is in fact encoded using the nonlinear sRGB transfer function. The issue is that converting this to linear now is incorrect because it will be converted a second time later when it is put into the uniform buffer for the sky shader.
> 
> 
> 
> Not sure if the comments should still be added given that the value is not being passed in linear encoding.

Ah, so there is another problem that colour/lighting maths are being performed on these nonlinear sRGB-encoded values (when they are multiplied by the energy multiplier). This is likely incorrect and should be addressed.

To put it simply, no maths of any sort should be performed on nonlinear sRGB-encoded values. The only exception is if you are using the math as a layer of "encoding" that will be directly reversed before doing any further operations, like with a "luminance multiplier".
author: allenwp

body: @allenwp I've updated the pr to correct this. If fog is enabled, ```bg_energy_multiplier``` is already applied to linear colours in the shader via ```sky_energy_multiplier```, so it doesn't need to be multiplied into the colour before the shader. For the cases where the sky is not drawn (clear colour / background colour with no fog), the colour is converted to linear encoding, multiplied, and converted back.
author: Kaleb-Reid

body: I've added a similar change to compatibility, which applies the brightness multiplier to linear-encoded colours.
author: Kaleb-Reid

body:
Thanks! It will take me a while to review this, and I'm not familiar with this part of the code base, but at a glance, I believe the intent of this updated PR is correct.

A couple of things that I can mention before doing a thorough review: dithering must always be applied as the final step before quantization. The dithering in the sky shaders is implemented in quite a weird way, and probably can be improved, but for now it should be left as-is, as the final step in the shader.

Another thing to mention is that conversions to and from nonlinear sRGB encoding are expensive and should be minimized. So if there's a way to keep things in linear (and document it with comments) the whole way through to limit conversions back and forth, that is preferred for performance reasons.

Finally, the Compatibility renderer intentionally performs some colour maths incorrectly for performance reasons, so avoiding decoding and re-encoding in this renderer may actually be the intended design, even though it produces incorrect results. You can see [this comment](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/90259#issuecomment-2704992993) for more details about this. Conversely, this sort of optimization to perform colour maths on nonlinear sRGB-encoded values should never happen in the Mobile/Forward+ renderers.

Anyway, at some point I'll take a deeper look into this PR to verify it.

And thanks for prompting me to write all of this out. Our discussion here is a good template for some colour documentation for contributors...
author: allenwp
Kaleb-Reid

Great; this sounds entirely reasonable and does not go against the spirit of the Compatibility renderer!

That's true, but I personally wouldn't feel comfortable risking regressions over this without any tangible benefit (i.e. profiling or benchmarking performance improvements).
author: Ivorforce

body:
@Ivorforce Understandable, but then I am left stuck between a rock and a hard place:

1. I want to allow new code to be explicit when duplication is required, and gradually migrate to this, so I proposed adding the `duplicate` function.
2. You stated that we can't do that unless we migrate all existing copy constructors to `duplicate` for consistency.
3. Migrating all existing copy constructor uses to either explicit or `duplicate` implies that everywhere we are currently using a copy constructor will change, even when the code author did not intend that.
4. I am hesitant to change the visible intention of existing code, especially to something I am confident is incorrect (using copy constructors where none was intended).

This really just leads me back to option 1 again, adding the duplicate function without deleting the copy constructors, which is risk-free (unlike this PR) and does not destroy the intent of existing code (unlike blanket changing everything). I can update PR #107377 to add this to the rest of the container types if this is desired.

Alternatively, I would be happy with whatever course of action you propose, if you would like to make a PR yourself.
author: aaronfranke

body:
I get your struggle. Sorry it's turning out so complicated.

Modifying Core APIs is often a delicate balance. The approach I usually go for is the one that is the safest and will lead to the least amounts of problems in the future.

In this case, I deem this to be changing existing callers to use the copy constructor explicitly instead of implicitly. I share your concerns about this changing the intention of the code. But compared to the code executing correctly, and compared to changing the constructor to be `explicit`, it doesn't take priority. Personally, when I read code and see inefficiencies or weird logic, I usually assume it's unintentional anyway, unless there is a comment explaining why the code looks suboptimal. By exposing copy constructors as being copies, I think overall the situation is improved, because people will see the copy being happening if it is performance relevant, and can fix it if they examine the surrounding code carefully and deem it safe for reference semantics.

> This really just leads me back to option 1 again, adding the duplicate function without deleting the copy constructors, which is risk-free (unlike this PR) and does not destroy the intent of existing code (unlike blanket changing everything). I can update PR #107377 to add this to the rest of the container types if this is desired.

I have to re-iterate that adding `.duplicate` is not a goal right now. It's an interesting proposition, but it's a change of paradigm, and it would need a proposal and discussion by the core team. Adding just this one `duplicate` function temporarily would be an inconsistency. Instead of doing this, I much prefer changing existing code to expose what it's doing, even if that alters the perceived intent of the code.

> Alternatively, I would be happy with whatever course of action you propose, if you would like to make a PR yourself.

I'm happy to take action, but it may take a while to get through everything. I'm slowly chipping away at implicit conversions and constructors anyway, but there are many, and each requires a lot of code changes, so I haven't gotten around to e.g. `HashSet` yet.
author: Ivorforce

body: @Ivorforce Understood. However, I'm still uneasy about opening up a single PR that performs massive changes throughout the codebase, especially if it may be controversial. Call me stubborn if you'd like, but I insist on making incremental enhancements towards this instead of doing everything in one go. To that end, I opened PRs #110948, #110949, #110951, #110977, and #110952. Each of the first 4 can be reviewed by area maintainers plus a core maintainer, but the 5th is a general PR that just needs core approval. I can open up more later, but I'll wait until these 5 are reviewed and merged.

body:

I have no real preference to which approach we take, though I will note the PRs already caught several erroneous cases of a missing `p_`/`r_` prefix, passing by value, and/or missing `const` qualifiers. This is similar to other stylistic changes in the repo, which are usually subdivided into chunks, so it's arguably the "correct" approach

body:
I've resolved all the review comments. I'm not sure what to do about relative paths — I'm not even sure they're worth handling. Especially since the main focus of this is for `uid://` paths, which can't be relative.

Maybe I could have a separate RegEx just for `(pre)?load`? Like
```regex
\b(?:pre)?load\s*\((?P<quote>"|"""|'|''')(?P<inner>.*?)(?P=quote)\)
```

Then it can display the load button in addition to the full path, maybe. I'd be replicating a lot of work already done elsewhere without a refactor though, I think. And then it'd need another setting for whether relative paths should be replaced with absolute paths, or whether they should remain relative, or whether they should be converted to UIDs, etc.

author: sockeye-d

body:
While the color preview also acts as a picker, I think it adds value to represent what color is set instead of just a bunch of values. For the UIDs, you can already see their path and extra options if you hover on it, so I feel like this is just adding more noise to the editor:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/dc029768-a023-470e-8fb2-0d24db9c5a7e

You can see this kind of _noise_ if you use any live-preview editor for markdown, where it becomes super complicated to know if the text you are editing is actually text on your file, or a representation of it made by the editor. In this case you are adding whole paths to a string (including a white space) where it doesn't exists one. 

This change would fit better in a visual scripting system, not on a plain text editor. I don't think it is necessarily a bad thing to add this, but it would open the gates to add something like this everywhere, and it would lead to editor clutter. ie: are we adding a quickload folder to each node to select the type of node? etc... 

So, I would make this an addon and, if not possible as an addon, I would add the necessary changes to make addons like this possible, but I wouldn't include it in the default experience for users.

body:
> I don't see the benefits of using a TouchscreenButton instead of a Control.

Right now, TouchScreenButton supports multitouch whereas BaseButton doesn't. But as I mentioned in the proposal I linked, the long-term plan is to make BaseButton support native touch events (and therefore multitouch) and deprecate TouchScreenButton.

TouchScreenButton was added very early in Godot development (it was already present in 2014 when Godot was open sourced), back when Godot didn't have dedicated event types for touch inputs.

I implemented the Joystick based on [this proposal](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/11192).

Proposal says:

Proposal says:

author: Kazox61

body:
> what changes should i make to the deadzone?

I compared your implementation with the one in VirtualJoystick addon. The only significant difference is that you use raw input vector for pressing/releasing actions, completely ignoring the joystick's deadzone.

Seems like if you apply the deadzone correctly, the action's deadzone is still applied on top of that. This is actually convenient. You can make `deadzone_ratio` default to 0, so the joystick only uses the action's deadzone. And if user wants to set the deadzone via the joystick, they can set action's deadzone to 0.

Just change `_handle_input_actions()` to use `input_vector` instead of `raw_input_vector` and set default `deadzone_ratio` to 0 and it should be fine (though this behavior also needs to be properly documented).

author: syntaxerror247

body:
> set_process to disbaled when there is no TouchScreenDevice with VISIBILITY_TOUCHSCREEN_ONLY?

The only way to disable `gui_input()` calls is to either:
- Make the Control invisible
- Set `process_mode` to disabled

Both involve changing properties of the node, which is not ideal. You can however override `has_point()` to always return false when the joystick is disabled.

> Otherwise this enum should be removed, because I don't see the need for it then.

tbh I'm also not sure how useful is that. Like, it saves you a couple lines of code if your game targets both mobile and desktop. 
Removing the TOUCHSCREEN_ONLY allows to add it later, while leaving it makes it not possible to remove it later, so removing it now is _technically_ safer. However the `has_point()` workaround is feasible, so IMO it's fine either way.

TouchScreenButton did not have this problem, because it's not a Control and does not automatically block input.

body:
I don’t know if this is the right place to post this, but I would have a proposal for an additional mode for the joystick:

“extend”

For each initiating touch that lands inside the ring of the joystick it would act exactly like the static fixed mode. The difference would be when you initiate a touch interaction outside of the ring: the joystick moves its position towards  it so that its outer ring lands right on the initial position of your finger. It remains in this position until you lift your finger again, similarly to the dynamic mode, just with a different jump target in relation to the initiating touch position (having it in the centre vs having it on the ring).

This kind of configuration could be good to minimise friction on the finger of the user, since you don’t have to swipe into the direction where you want to move, having the option to just tap it instead. The static fixed mode already offers similar functionality, with the major caveat being that it is not as tolerant when you want to tap the joystick area near its boundary.

Before I open the PR for review, @Jesusemora could you please let me know what your issues with the feature are? Hopefully those can be resolved.

body:
> Before I open the PR for review, @Jesusemora could you please let me know what your issues with the feature are? Hopefully those can be resolved. I also see some confusion from @scgm0, could you please let me know what is confusing you, so that I may resolve that confusion?

I feel like gdscript has a risk of becoming bloated, I still haven't had time to test all of the new features from 4.5, and we should be careful with adding new features that don't do a lot.

I just don't see a point to a `@read_only` annotation. What does it acomplish? Why is it needed?
author: Jesusemora

body:
>  I still haven't had time to test all of the new features from 4.5

Is that supposed to be a problem? Not all projects need all the capacities of the language, that shouldn't be a barrier to adding language features.

> I just don't see a point to a `@read_only` annotation. What does it acomplish? Why is it needed?

The best way to approach this is to compare it to immutables. The use of `@read_only` is that it:
1. Guarantees the value has an initial value, which also cannot be changed (like immutables)
2. The initial value can be defined inline (like immutables) or in the constructor (where it can be a construction parameter, or have conditions-based values, unlike immutables) 
3. It supports a non-trivial getter (unlike immutables)

This set of behaviors exists in other programming languages, but is not currently possible in GDScript. The primary use case, as I see it, is to define unchanging class variables that are construction parameters, which is very useful with abstract and child classes. 

Here's an example:
```GDScript
@abstract class_name Entity:
  @read_only var position_component: PositionComponent

  func _init(p_comp: PositionComponent) -> void:
     position_component= p_comp
```
```GDScript
class_name StaticEntity extends Entity
  func _init() -> void:
    super._init(StaticPositionComponent.new())
```
```GDScript
class_name MovingEntity extends Entity
  func _init() -> void:
    super._init(MovingPositionComponent.new())
```
```GDScript
@abstract class_name PositionComponent:
  var position:= Vector2.ZERO

  func _process(delta: float) -> void:
    update_position(delta)

  @abstract func update_position(delta: float) -> void
```
```GDScript
class_name StaticPositionComponent extends PositionComponent:
  func update_position(_delta: float) -> void:
    pass
```
```GDScript
class_name MovingPositionComponent extends PositionComponent:
  var velocity:= Vector2.ZERO
  func update_position(delta: float) -> void:
    # Velocity maths
    position += velocity * delta
```
This is not achievable safely in GDScript at the moment, as the `position_component` needs to be settable by the child class, which would make it settable by anything, i.e. it could be nullified or replaced, even though it should be the same object throughout the entity's lifetime (due to having state which shouldn't be reset). 

Please let me know if this answer is unsatisfactory.
author: StarryWorm

body:
> I also see some confusion from @scgm0, could you please let me know what is confusing you, so that I may resolve that confusion?

I believe this is not a necessary feature for GDScript. In fact, I think the merging of `@abstract` (https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/67777 & https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/107717) has set a bad precedent. Currently, there are several pull requests proposing to add annotations for GDScript, and I feel these features do not align with Godot's best practices guidelines (https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/guidelines/best_practices.html).
However, you don't need to ask for my or any other user's opinion; you just need to get the approval from the GDS reviewers. That's how the Godot source code operates.

author: StarryWorm

body:
> > I still haven't had time to test all of the new features from 4.5
> 
> Is that supposed to be a problem? Not all projects need all the capacities of the language, that shouldn't be a barrier to adding language features.

It is a problem when working with other people, and is a problem if it makes code less clear. The more tools you give to people, the more likely they are to use them wrong, and this not only applies to using them in a way that breaks things, but also using them in a situation that doesn't help the problem and just makes the code harder to read/understand.

> 
> > I just don't see a point to a `@read_only` annotation. What does it acomplish? Why is it needed?
> 
> The best way to approach this is to compare it to immutables. The use of `@read_only` is that it:
> 
>     1. Guarantees the value has an initial value, which also cannot be changed (like immutables)
> 
>     2. The initial value can be defined inline (like immutables) or in the constructor (where it can be a construction parameter, or have conditions-based values, unlike immutables)
> 
>     3. It supports a non-trivial getter (unlike immutables)
> 
> 
> This set of behaviors exists in other programming languages, but is not currently possible in GDScript. The primary use case, as I see it, is to define unchanging class variables that are construction parameters, which is very useful with abstract and child classes.
> 
> Here's an example:
> 
> ```gdscript
> @abstract class_name Entity:
>   @read_only var position_component: PositionComponent
> 
>   func _init(p_comp: PositionComponent) -> void:
>      position_component= p_comp
> ```
> 
> ```gdscript
> class_name StaticEntity extends Entity
>   func _init() -> void:
>     super._init(StaticPositionComponent.new())
> ```
> 
> ```gdscript
> class_name MovingEntity extends Entity
>   func _init() -> void:
>     super._init(MovingPositionComponent.new())
> ```
> 
> ```gdscript
> @abstract class_name PositionComponent:
>   var position:= Vector2.ZERO
> 
>   func _process(delta: float) -> void:
>     update_position(delta)
> 
>   @abstract func update_position(delta: float) -> void
> ```
> 
> ```gdscript
> class_name StaticPositionComponent extends PositionComponent:
>   func update_position(_delta: float) -> void:
>     pass
> ```
> 
> ```gdscript
> class_name MovingPositionComponent extends PositionComponent:
>   var velocity:= Vector2.ZERO
>   func update_position(delta: float) -> void:
>     # Velocity maths
>     position += velocity * delta
> ```
> 
> This is not achievable safely in GDScript at the moment, as the `position_component` needs to be settable by the child class, which would make it settable by anything, i.e. it could be nullified or replaced, even though it should be the same object throughout the entity's lifetime (due to having state which shouldn't be reset).
> 
> Please let me know if this answer is unsatisfactory.

This seems to be a very specific thing.
1 - If they can only be set from a constructor, there won't be more than, say, 3, as you are not supposed to pass a dozen constructor parameters.
2 - This should be handled using proper encapsulation. It is more a problem with godot's intellisense recommending private properties (variables starting with underscore `_`), which should be its own proposal and would be far more useful.

there are two ways I can think of for doing this:

1 - using a method
```gdscript
var _foo : Resource

func get_read_only() -> Resource:
    return _foo

func _init(param : Resource) -> void:
    _foo = param
```

2 - using getters
```gdscript
var _foo : Resource
var read_only:
    get:
        return _foo

func _init(param : Resource) -> void:
    _foo = Resource
```

in both scenarios there's a private property `_foo`, which no one is supposed to touch outside of those getters or the constructor.
author: Jesusemora

body:
I don't see how 1 is any better than my suggestion, since it now has an extra method of boilerplate instead of an annotation. 2 is the same but now it's an extra variable on which the getter is applied, even more boilerplate. 

Also, those are still not safe, even if we assume proper private variables. The base class itself could modify them after initialization. Now I know there's a level of developer responsibility, but that same responsibility argument can be applied to private variables themselves. On the other hand, single-initialization properties are forced to be constant. They are also forced to be initialized before being read. No null exceptions possible. 
It is a safety feature first and foremost, and a boilerplate reduction feature.
author: StarryWorm

body:
> It is a problem when working with other people, and is a problem if it makes code less clear. The more tools you give to people, the more likely they are to use them wrong, and this not only applies to using them in a way that breaks things, but also using them in a situation that doesn't help the problem and just makes the code harder to read/understand.

What about those of us that do use them correctly? Should we all be punished because some people not only lack the correct knowledge but refuse to fill that gap by learning? This doesn't sound like a very good argument to me.
author: produno

body:
> What about those of us that do use them correctly? Should we all be punished because some people not only lack the correct knowledge but refuse to fill that gap by learning? This doesn't sound like a very good argument to me.

However, we shouldn't add a feature just because some people can use it correctly. For instance, I might want `eval`, the prototype chain I'm familiar with in JavaScript, WebAssembly (WASM), and so on. People always have more desires for features, and there will always be those who can use them appropriately.

But I believe it's more important to keep GDScript simple and deepen its role in rapid game prototyping. After all, Godot isn't limited to GDScript; it also supports C# and GDExtension, each serving its own purpose.

Also, please refer to https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/guidelines/best_practices.html
author: scgm0

body:
GDScript has never been specifically for rapid prototyping. At least in the 8 years I've been using Godot, I have never seen GDScript touted as such. It has however been mentioned as a language specific for game dev, tied directly to Godot to make development of games easier and quicker (more efficient). The latter part being mentioned in the link you provided.

With that in mind, there are specific language features that certainly help making games easier, and to allow for a much more robust codebase. Imo this is one of them.
author: produno

body:
> With that in mind, there are specific language features that certainly help making games easier, and to allow for a much more robust codebase. Imo this is one of them.

This still isn't a good reason. As I mentioned, there are numerous similar language features that are beneficial for game development—as long as someone can use them correctly, they are useful.

Considering the multitude of existing programming languages and their current user bases, there are undoubtedly many features that could make game development easier.

Should we then add all of them to GDScript?
author: scgm0

body:
> Considering the multitude of existing programming languages and their current user bases, there are undoubtedly many features that could make game development easier.
> 
> Should we then add all of them to GDScript?

I am not sure if you are being purposely obtuse but no one here has said that.

Currently in my project, I use JSON as data which gets imported to RefCounted resources. I overwrite the variables within these on first load and pull data from these variables throughout the classes they are assigned to. These should be set only once (When importing the data) and then should be read only. It would be much easier for me to just put `read_only` in front of all of them which clearly states their function instead of the current workarounds. Not only does it make my code more robust but its also self documenting for anyone else that works within my codebase, whether it be a modder or a colleague. I am assuming this pull request will fix that issue for me.

body:
It looks like the version check in `RegisterTypesInternal` is not playing nice with Godot's CI build cache, as the Jolt source files in `thirdparty/jolt_physics` are being recompiled with the new `JPH_VERSION_ID` but `modules/jolt_physics/jolt_globals.cpp` is still using the old one, leading to `std::abort()` being called.

None of the feature bits are causing problems, so I'm assuming it's because `JPH_VERSION_MINOR` is mixed into `JPH_VERSION_ID`. The mismatch mask is `0b11100000000`, which I guess makes sense if the minor version went from 3 to 4.

There's probably some way of wiping the CI cache, but this will cause issues for anyone compiling ~~with caching enabled~~ downstream as well, so that surely can't be the fix.

EDIT: It turns out you don't need SCons caching enabled. The same thing happens when doing normal incremental builds as well.

I don't really understand how this can happen in the first place, short of SCons doing some sloppy caching, like not running source files through the preprocessor first. There's no way `modules/jolt_physics/jolt_globals.cpp` should not have been invalidated and recompiled, since it has to include `Jolt/Core/Core.h`, which has the new version number in it.

I would've thought this would invalidate all the `modules/jolt_physics` source files either way:

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/9283328fe70d27f148c403943a283d93a9785beb/modules/jolt_physics/SCsub#L178-L179
author: mihe

body:
> the Jolt source files in `thirdparty/jolt_physics` are being recompiled with the new `JPH_VERSION_ID` but `modules/jolt_physics/jolt_globals.cpp` is still using the old one

It seems it's actually the other way around, because `Jolt/RegisterTypes.cpp` isn't being recompiled for some reason. That's frankly just as weird though.
author: mihe

body:
The SCons output with `--tree=prune` seems to suggest that unlike the files in `modules/jolt_physics`, the files in `thirdparty/jolt_physics` don't have any dependencies on any header files, or any files at all for that matter, which I guess explains why `Jolt/RegisterTypes.cpp` isn't being rebuilt.

This is unlike (for example) the integration of the Manifold library in `modules/csg`. Its `SCSub` file is pretty much identical with the Jolt one though, so that doesn't make much sense.

Someone more familiar with SCons probably needs to take a look at this.
author: mihe

env_jolt.Prepend(CPPEXTPATH=[thirdparty_dir])

author: jrouwe

body:
I think I figured it out, I now added both lines:

```
env_jolt.Prepend(CPPEXTPATH=[thirdparty_dir])
env_jolt.Prepend(CPPPATH=[thirdparty_dir])
```

The first disables warnings in external headers, the 2nd makes scons search the correct path for dependencies.

The reason why it still isn't working is that I was passing `dev_build=yes` which has a special check here:

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/b4472f4670a0b7fd34137d18f1e080e3319e3819/SConstruct#L536-L538

that enables the option `implicit_cache`. According to [this](https://scons.org/doc/4.8.1/HTML/scons-user/ch06s03.html) means that any changes to `CPPPATH` are ignored. 

I commented those 3 lines out locally and then it works. This should at least fix all the non `dev_build`'s. I'm not sure what the build cache actually caches. If it only caches output files then we may get lucky, if it also caches scons state then it may still ignore the `CPPPATH` changes.
author: jrouwe

body:
`env_jolt.Prepend(CPPPATH=[thirdparty_dir])` was apparently changed to be `env_jolt.Prepend(CPPEXTPATH=[thirdparty_dir])` in #104893.

Maybe @Repiteo or someone else in @godotengine/buildsystem can help us figure out why that seems to mess with SCons tracking of header dependencies in `thirdparty/jolt_physics`. Perhaps it's related to the nixpkgs issue mentioned in that PR?
author: mihe

body:
Just to unblock this I've changed the Jolt `SCsub` file to use `CPPPATH` again, instead of `CPPEXTPATH`, which seems to still be a thing with a few other third-party integrations anyway (pending #108611). I've also added a single whitespace to `‎thirdparty/jolt_physics/Jolt/RegisterTypes.cpp` in order to force that file to be compiled, which resolves the `std::abort` happening in Jolt's version check.

However, this of course does nothing for whatever other unchanged Jolt source files that might possibly need recompiling due to some header dependency having changed. It only addresses the `std::abort` crash that's preventing the CI tests from passing. Either way, the dangers of `fast_unsafe` won't be resolved in this PR.

Also, even with this hack, I believe this can still cause problems if the following happens:

1. Somebody updates Jolt to a newer version by overwriting all the files in `thirdparty/jolt_physics`, effectively reverting this single whitespace in `Jolt/RegisterTypes.cpp`, assuming it hasn't changed in some other way by that point.
2. Somebody then tries to bisect some bug, while having `dev_build=yes` enabled (and thus `fast_unsafe=yes`), and ends up jumping between two commits where this whitespace change never happened.

In such a case you will most likely `std::abort` in Jolt's version check and scratch your head for a while wondering why.

body: Is there any proposal on this change? I'm personally not convinced that it is a better solution than providing some way to mark abstract methods as co-routine.
author: HolonProduction

body:
> Is there any proposal on this change? 

Great question.

I found a couple of candidates, but neither are both exactly aligned and popular:

- godotengine/godot-proposals#5893
    - proposes an optional `@async` annotation
    - but when `@async` is not applied, the gdscript analyzer will still have no way to determine if an `@abstract` method is awaitable
- godotengine/godot-proposals#8363
    - proposes an `.is_async()` method on `Callable`
    - likely can't address this use case on its own, since it's a helper and doesn't provide additional information on whether an `@abstract` method is awaitable

> I'm personally not convinced that is a better solution than providing some way to mark abstract methods as co-routine.

There is a tradeoff here, no matter how we would mark methods - abstract or otherwise - as a coroutine.

Either this "marking" is mandatory, and gdscript analyzer will always have that info, making this PR redundant in purpose.

Or this "marking" is optional, and in its absence, this PR will remain needed to ensure gdscript analyzer does not raise this warning wrongly.
author: wheatear-dev

body: I don't really understand the two cases you describe. Suppose the logic for marking was `annotated_with("async") or body_has_await()`, what would be the issue here?
author: HolonProduction

body:
> I don't really understand the two cases you describe.

The problem this PR tries to solve is surfacing enough information to gdscript analyzer that such false positives are eliminated.

> Suppose the logic for marking was `annotated_with("async")`

This is closer, but is what I meant by optional. If this becomes the signal that an abstract method is a coroutine, then when/if it is absent, the error will still fire.

There would still not be enough information in that case.

Making such an annotation mandatory would be a breaking change to the syntax of gdscript, and that proposal doesn't seem very popular.


> or `body_has_await()`

This won't work for methods decorated with `@abstract`, since they don't need to declare a body.
author: wheatear-dev

body: The or was meant as in a boolean expression.
author: HolonProduction

body:
> The or was meant as in a boolean expression.

I addressed the second condition separately as it is never true in this case.

Abstract methods should not specify bodies.

author: HydrogenC

body:
> Great work! Can you be specific about which areas were copied from Unreal Engine?
> 
> For clarity, we cannot merge any code that was copied from Unreal since their licence is restrictive and does not allow it.
> 
> Anything in this PR that was copied from Unreal needs to be removed before we can consider merging this

Well, none of the code is actually copied, the relationship to Unreal is only on the logic level and somehow indirect. Direct references are the [flower renderer](https://github.com/qiutang98/flower/blob/main/source/shader/gtao/gtao_evaluate.glsl) (MIT licensed) and [O3DE](https://github.com/o3de/sig-graphics-audio/blob/main/rfcs/GTAOIntegration/Proposed%20RFC%20Feature%20GTAO%20(Ground-Truth%20based%20Ambient%20Occlusion).md) (Apache 2 Licensed), both of which are logic transcripts of the Unreal implementation. Thus, since there's no direct copying, I believe that these changes won't encounter license issues (do they?).
author: HydrogenC

body:
> Well, none of the code is actually copied, the relationship to Unreal is only on the logic level and somehow indirect. Direct references are the [flower renderer](https://github.com/qiutang98/flower/blob/main/source/shader/gtao/gtao_evaluate.glsl) (MIT licensed) and [O3DE](https://github.com/o3de/sig-graphics-audio/blob/main/rfcs/GTAOIntegration/Proposed%20RFC%20Feature%20GTAO%20(Ground-Truth%20based%20Ambient%20Occlusion).md) (Apache 2 Licensed), both of which are logic transcripts of the Unreal implementation. Thus, since there's no direct copying, I believe that these changes won't encounter license issues (do they?).

I am very confused by this respond. In the same paragraph you say that you didn't use any of the code, but then say it is a "logic transcripts of the Unreal implementation" which sounds like the code is copied. Which is it?

For clarity, you cannot copy any code from Unreal Engine, nor can you look at the code in Unreal Engine and then write code that is functionally the same, but uses different variable names / coding style. The implementation needs to be totally fresh without any reliance on the Unreal Engine source code. 

The fact that there exist open source implementations that use the same algorithm as Unreal doesn't change that. Unreal's licence doesn't cover the GTAO technique, it covers the code in their engine. Any copying of their code violates the licence. Similarly, if Flower copied Unreal's code and then we copy them, it would still be a copyright violation.
author: clayjohn

body:
> 
> For clarity, you cannot copy any code from Unreal Engine, nor can you look at the code in Unreal Engine and then write code that is functionally the same, but uses different variable names / coding style. The implementation needs to be totally fresh without any reliance on the Unreal Engine source code.
> 
> The fact that there exist open source implementations that use the same algorithm as Unreal doesn't change that. Unreal's licence doesn't cover the GTAO technique, it covers the code in their engine. Any copying of their code violates the licence. Similarly, if Flower copied Unreal's code and then we copy them, it would still be a copyright violation.

Thanks for your explanation. I didn't "look at the code in Unreal Engine and then write code that is functionally the same, but uses different variable names / coding style". So my version isn't a line-by-line logic transcription of the UE version, but according to your definition, I used code snippets from O3DE and flower that almost somehow similar to the corresponding part of source code from UE and they might have been taken from UE (and maybe not, but I don't know, so there might be a potential violation here).  
To be safer, I will check the original paper and other independent open-source implementation and rewrite snippets of code that look similar to the implementation of UE.

The latest commit rebased the logic on MIT-licensed XeGTAO instead of Unreal. Also, added paper references for some of the magic-looking logic to make it more traceable. The changes might slightly affect the visual appearances, also the performance might be worse in a very slight degree (due to lack of some Unreal magic in the inner integration part), but it's now free of potential copyright violations.

author: jcostello

body:
> Would you may benchmark the performance?

On my NVIDIA RTX4070 Laptop (unit: ms): 
|   | Very Low | Medium | High | Ultra |
| -------- | -------- | -------- | -------- | -------- |
| ASSAO | 0.07 | 0.13 | 0.18 | 0.18 |
| GTAO | 0.05 | 0.14 | 0.20 | 0.26 |

The Ultra version of GTAO aims to create an ultimate AO quality, so it uses far more samples than Ultra ASSAO, which makes sense that it takes considerably longer. In other quality levels, GTAO is no slower than ASSAO.  
Besides, to keep the logic align with ASSAO, the current version of GTAO gather pass is also run 4 times. If anyone can explain why does it run 4 times and claim that this isn't necessary for GTAO, I can make GTAO gather only run once to make it even faster.  
Also, The removal of Unreal optimizations doesn't cause a slowdown more than 0.01ms.
author: HydrogenC

author: mrjustaguy

author: clayjohn

body:
> You can try to run it once and see what kind of differences you see.
> 
> I wouldn't be entirely surprised that it's a mistake, it wouldn't be the first, won't be the last.

Visually there's none, but theoretically there will be some. For example, the fallout is calculated differently (this makes the attenuation looks different but can be compensated by adjusting the radius parameter), and that when calculating step length Unreal uses `NUM_TAP + 1` as denominator and XeGTAO uses just `NUM_TAP`, and that Unreal starts from step 1 while tracing largest angle whereas XeGTAO starts from step 0. But however maybe they are too little to cause a conspicuous visual difference so that I cannot spot any obvious visual nuances. 

> Also I'm curious if fast acos is actually faster then just using normal acos

Well, the [original slide from SIGGRAPH](https://blog.selfshadow.com/publications/s2016-shading-course/) indicates using fast acos and fast invsqrt, so that I followed it.

author: arkology

body:
What I don't understand is why not opt to do the complete implementation of XeGTAO, as was mentioned at the GodotCon? I know this requires temporal filters, but in the end, it will be an alternative to ASSAO and not the default.

Clarification: At GodotCon 2024, as I understood it, it was said that there is a desire to improve the quality of ASSAO (in addition to other ss effects) with a temporal filter, I assume to resemble XeGTAO, but in 2023 it was implied that XeGTAO was intended to be implemented, I assume as an alternative to ASSAO. Am I wrong?
author: LiveTrower

body:
> What I don't understand is why not opt to do the complete implementation of XeGTAO, as was mentioned at the GodotCon? I know this requires temporal filters, but in the end, it will be an alternative to ASSAO and not the default.
> 
A temporal filter within GTAO is what UE does, not XeGTAO. AFAIK, XeGTAO just uses a spatial edge-aware smart blur within the GTAO pass and relies on a subsequent TAA effect to filter temporally. In godot we can do the same (enable GTAO+TAA to bring temporal filtering). The reason why Unreal does temporal filtering within GTAO is, I guess, perhaps to make GTAO look better without TAA, with a cost of performance. So, in terms of XeGTAO, the current implementation is already "full".
author: HydrogenC

body: That explanation is unlikely, considering the fact that these days UE pushes for TAA really, REALLY hard, I mean to an Absolutely Absurd Extent, to the point that if You aren't using TAA with UE5, Why use UE5, as it's needed for like 90% of their Rendering to even be usable.
author: mrjustaguy

body:
> > Looks really good. I found an I think when you are under and close to geometry.
> > ASSAO
> > Screencast.From.2025-09-28.11-22-23.webm
> > GTAO
> > Screencast.From.2025-09-28.11-22-42.webm
> 
> Still having similar problem while running GTAO half size

This might be because that the maximum tracing radius is defined in screen pixel units. When the object is very near, then the screen tracing distance will exceed the maximum value and be clamped down. Half resolution would mean twice the actual tracing distance (in view space), bringing unwanted artifacts. I'll push an attempt of a fix later.  
A large radius would bring more potential artifacts. XeGTAO uses a default radius of 0.58, whereas Godot uses 1.0. I instead added a multiplier in the shader to scale down the radius a little bit to make 1.0 look similar to XeGTAO 0.58.
