I succeeded to reproduce a similar problem on my side. I checked the code and I failed to find any logical error that could possibly cause this artifact. I noticed that it is more severe under half resolution than full resolution (max radius is the only factor differing between full and half resolution), and that the size of radius has a strong correlation with the severity of the artifact, so I guess it would because of tracing too far. I decreased the default radius to fix this problem, and with a smaller radius the artifact indeed become less visible.

Thank you. The effect may not be as good as XeGTAO, but please also take the current SSAO as a baseline and see if it does looks better than the current SSAO. 

> Moreover, since the pass has been decreased to 2 (from 4), would increasing the spp or quality be possible while not eating that much performance( say to match xegtao samples on it quality modes.

The SPP would already be enough, I suppose. The sample count of a single gather pass is comparable to other engines which typically run only one gather pass. XeGTAO runs a single pass on a full-res depth texture. If we also pass in a full-res depth texture, we could also cut it to one pass. But however to keep the input aligned with ASSAO the current implementation would be better.

author: Saul2022

body:
> this is basically XeGTAO.
> 

> But I'm not sure what the goal is here. The visual results seem to be very similar to the current SSAO implementation
>

Yes, this is adpated from XeGTAO. There are notable visual improvements. As for the scenes we've tested, ASSAO creates large regions of dilute AO, whereas GTAO creates darker AO that spreads across a smaller range, looing sharper and more realistic.  

As for SSAOVB, I've tested it locally, but I found that it underperforms the original GTAO in some circumstances. This is because that SSAOVB doesn't have falloff. This means far and near occluders within the radius contribute the same, causing some visual degenerations compared with orthodox GTAO. Now within mainstream game engine (Unreal, Unity) GTAO still remains the popular choice.
author: HydrogenC

body:
> There is, GTAO looks sharper and less blurry than ASSAO, the only reasson it might look worse than xegtao is because of the blur logic

My implementation is mostly XeGTAO. The blur part might be among one of the reasons. Another possible reasons is thta godot uses four downsampled depth texture for all screen space effects (possibly for bandwidth reduction) whereas XeGTAO uses full resolution depth texture.

body:
> For context, we have discussed improving SSAO for a while in the rendering meetings, but what we had in mind was to switch to new technique completely. The idea was to replace ASSAO with something like XeGTAO or SSAOVB to significantly improve quality.

@clayjohn All right, I understand what you would like to have. From now on I'll split the GTAO implementation from ASSAO and extend it to a full XeGTAO implementation (port the blur part of XeGTAO and stop reusing the blur passes of ASSAO). That will make this a complete XeGTAO that could is parallel to ASSAO and could serve as a drop-in replacement for it. I'll notify when I finishes.
author: HydrogenC

body:
>  extend it to a full XeGTAO implementation (port the blur part of XeGTAO and stop reusing the blur passes of ASSAO). 

Also take into account xegtao has also   bent normal computation for better ao. Which  helps a lot in test scenes like bistro.
author: Saul2022

body:
> 
> Also take into account xegtao has also bent normal computation for better ao. Which helps a lot in test scenes like bistro.

Be aware that bent normals doesn't actually affect the AO term itself. Bent normals are encoded during AO and used in the subsequent lighting pass for anisotropic and specular lighting. So bent normal shouldn't make a difference in SSAO debug view. I suspect whether the real improvement with and without bent normals is large.  
See this page on [UE docs](https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/en-us/unreal-engine/bent-normal-maps-in-unreal-engine) for comparison and it is indeed very slight. 
If I do have time I will investigate it, but I cannot guarantee that I will implement bent normals. XeGTAO works fine without bent normals.
author: HydrogenC

body:
I don't understand where this is going if XeGTAO is going to replace ASSAO. That would lead to the forced use of TAA since XeGTAO is very noisy despite having a spatial denoiser. And yes, a spatio-temporal denoiser can be used to avoid forcing the use of TAA, but let's be honest, it produces the same results as having TAA active, such as ghosting and the noise is still visible when there's movement.

> > Also take into account xegtao has also bent normal computation for better ao. Which helps a lot in test scenes like bistro.
> 
> Be aware that bent normals doesn't actually affect the AO term itself. Bent normals are encoded during AO and used in the subsequent lighting pass for anisotropic and specular lighting. So bent normal shouldn't make a difference in SSAO debug view. I suspect whether the real improvement with and without bent normals is large. See this page on [UE docs](https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/en-us/unreal-engine/bent-normal-maps-in-unreal-engine) for comparison and it is indeed very slight. If I do have time I will investigate it, but I cannot guarantee that I will implement bent normals. XeGTAO works fine without bent normals.

Godot already supports [bent normals](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/tutorials/3d/standard_material_3d.html#bent-normal-map), the only detail is that the user has to generate the bent normal maps. I can understand that GTAO would generate them automatically, but I think this would be redundant.

author: Saul2022

body:
> That would lead to the forced use of TAA since XeGTAO is very noisy despite having a spatial denoiser. 

It's possible to implement a temporal reprojection pass separate from TAA, so TAA is not a strict requirement for it. We do the same for volumetric fog right now (it's enabled by default). Volumetric fog is low-frequency data, so it works well for this use case. SSAO is proportionally higher-frequency data, but still rather low-frequency when you consider the blur passes and half-resolution rendering (by default).

That said, games that feature high-end visuals (and are likely going to use SSAO in general) almost always use TAA or some upscaler by default nowadays, so you might as well make use of it to improve performance *and* visuals.

I think the problem is that `update_tree()` does not take effect for some reason (or it's updated wrong). The fix might be simply forcing a refresh. It should be possible without reload, and without lots of changes.

For a hacky solution, calling `scene_tree->set_valid_types({})` will force full tree update, but I think a dedicated method would be better.
author: KoBeWi

body:
> I think the problem is that `update_tree()` does not take effect for some reason (or it's updated wrong). The fix might be simply forcing a refresh. It should be possible without reload, and without lots of changes.

In my investigation, the "Clear Inheritance" operation is not complete. It only clean `inheritated_state` and emit an event, without clear all of it's children state. So after `update_tree`, we still get the old children's state.

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/e825169957e4ec67bc0baa6168af02b337399f6f/scene/main/node.cpp#L2809-L2813

And before "Clear Inheritance", there is only one node with a reference for an "ext_resource".

```
[ext_resource type="PackedScene" uid="uid://bowuloxmdkrly" path="res://prefab.tscn" id="1_by10c"]
[node name="PrefabChildToTest" instance=ExtResource("1_by10c")]
```

After "Clear Inheritance", we need create the real tree structure for the new scene.

```
[ext_resource type="Script" uid="uid://cdao4baykx3v" path="res://prefab.gd" id="1_3o2mv"]
[node name="PrefabChildToTest" type="Node2D"]
script = ExtResource("1_3o2mv")
[node name="Node2D" type="Node2D" parent="."]
[connection signal="draw" from="Node2D" to="." method="_on_node_2d_draw"]
```

I agree the current PR is not so good. I can do more digging to find a better solution.

---

**Edit:**

`scene_tree->set_valid_types({})` can resolve the left menu tree warning, but signal warning on Inspector is still.
I will keep digging.

body:
Thanks for opening a pull request!

Feature pull requests should be associated to a feature proposal to justify the need for implementing the feature in Godot core. Please open a proposal on the [Godot proposals](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals) repository (and link to this pull request in the proposal's body).

Be aware that there's not much support for AI integration, and several proposals for this has already been rejected due to lacking support
author: AThousandShips

body:
I don't think there's a proposal for this so I'll type my review comments here.

1. Godot engine doesn't have a typescript engine so adding typescript support won't work with Godot Engine core.
2. Gemini is not the only large language model, so from the perspective of Godot Engine contribution it wouldn't make sense to support only Gemini. I think cline and visual studio copilot support 5-20 large language model providers. The typical implementations of this use MCP and openrouter.
3. Since godot engine doesn't have a database built-in (see my sqlite module) I am not sure where you would store memory locally. Probably a .godot folder
4. Technical github action checks are failing

There's a one sided debate on should we add ai features.. So I'll leave that debate to other people but these are my technical review comments.

body:
I'll convert this PR to a draft for now. After a little bit of digging, I found out that the NDK (27) specifically states that:

> A RISC-V sysroot (AKA riscv64, or rv64) has been added. **It is not supported.** It is present to aid bringup for OS vendors, **but it's not yet a supported Android ABI**. It will not be built by default.

(emphasis mine)

So in other words, the platform's own tooling itself isn't actually ready yet 😅 (even if it is included secretly and compiles correctly). I jumped on the bandwagon way, way too early and that's on me.

I'll monitor the [android-riscv64](https://github.com/google/android-riscv64) repo, official NDK releases and RISC-V news. And when RISC-V becomes an officially supported ABI, I can revive this PR accordingly.
author: MBCX

body:
> So in other words, the platform's own tooling itself isn't actually ready yet 😅 (even if it is included secretly and compiles correctly). I jumped on the bandwagon way, way too early and that's on me.

Is there any hardware available for testing? OpenGL ES 3 or Vulkan 1.0.

We're already experiencing various issues with GPU drivers on ARM32/ARM64 devices. It's hard to imagine we'll find enough new volunteer users and developers to test it and implement workarounds.

> Qualcomm to Bring RISC-V Based Wearable Platform to Wear OS by Google

https://www.qualcomm.com/news/releases/2023/10/qualcomm-to-bring-risc-v-based-wearable-platform-to--wear-os-by-

3 weeks ago I tested a Godot 2D project on Firebase test lab on a smartwatch ("Wear OS" but arm64, I think). I saw the Godot logo for 3 seconds, after 10 seconds the screen was black and the test aborted.

The proposal can link back to this (then closed) PR for reference on the implementation, so we could later restart from this code.

The original git blame points to https://github.com/godotengine/godot/commit/e086bccd63e64b8d3bd2b6b5ce000ef8abd71584

From my understanding the property contained the path to the node relative to the scene root, but based on the time when the scene was imported (from a 3d file). This was then used to merge nodes on reimport. This is not needed in the current import system as imported scenes are read only so they can be completely reimported without any merging logic to maintain changes by the user.

```

The local push changes are technically working as expected, but reflect a potential inherent limitation where only the most recent commit is compared against (`.github/workflows/static_checks.yml` excluded):

author: clayjohn

body:
While I haven't gotten to measuring it, this PR is likely to have caused a performance regression due to the rearrangement of the values.

The reason behind material_id and shader_id being the MSB of the bitfield is that it allows the renderer to sort and group based on being able to avoid rebinding pipelines and materials as much as possible. By inverting their order on the keys, other less significant elements have now been prioritized and the possibilities of batching together compatible draw calls has been reduced.

I'd make a PR myself to fix it, but I'd like to know OP's thoughts first into addressing this. The reason behind the original ordering is that:
- Shaders can be often reused because BaseMaterial3D will assign the same shaders to materials if their configuration is similar, which means only textures must be rebound if groups of draw calls with the same.
- Grouping materials avoids the need to rebind textures.
- Grouping geometry avoids the need to rebind vertex and index buffers.

The other miscellaneous parameters having priority over these IDs are likely to cause inefficient ordering over the criteria I laid out above. If you'd prefer, I can address this ordering on a PR myself while keeping 'priority' as the MSB of the key.
author: DarioSamo

body: @DarioSamo I hadn't really thought of that. I was looking at the pr which changed the order and broke "priority" and assumed that the arrangement was just changed in order to make the bitmask simpler. I guess the fix would be reverting the bitmask changes from this pr + #111125, and then rearranging the "*_id" fields to be the msbs after "priority". If you want to make a pr to fix it, I say go ahead.

body:
Tested briefly on Fedora 43 (using PipeWire as audio server with default config) and it seems to work fine. I haven't reviewed the code yet.

How to validate that it's indeed using PipeWire directly and not via the pipewire-pulse plugin?

What kind of benefits are expected from this new driver, aside from sidestepping pipewire-pulse on systems defaulting to PipeWire?

*Edit:* I just read the proposal in https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/10723. It mentions lacking audio input in ALSA, and too much latency in PulseAudio. Does this PR solve those two shortcomings?

---

Regarding the vendored headers in `thirdparty/linuxbsd_headers/pipewire/`, there's quite a lot of them. If possible, we like to trim down which thirdparty files we vendor to the minimum subset needed by Godot. body: I check if a program is using pipewire directly by doing `pw-dump > ~/pw-dump`, searching for the program's binary name in the file and checking its `"client.api"`, if there is no `"client.api"` for the program - it's using pipewire directly.

I'm currently failing to get this PR working with a multichannel setup. I have an 8-channel audio sink in PipeWire, and I'm selecting that sink with `AudioServer.set_output_device("8_channel_sink")`. Godot is reporting 3 (7.1 surround) for `AudioServer.get_speaker_mode()`.

However in `qpwgraph` it seems that Godot is only creating a 2-channel source for the game. Anything sent to Surround or Center is just getting lost as there's no output from Godot to connect for those speakers.

`spa_pod_builder_add_object()` is getting `8` for the number of channels in the pipewire object to construct, but doesn't seem to be actually creating an 8-channel object, only a 2-channel one.
author: majenkotech

body: Ok, don't worry. It is, as you can imagine, a PEBCAK. The sink's ports have to be named specifically by the surround sound standard, even if they are not, strictly speaking, surround sound sinks.
author: majenkotech

body:
Ok, this one I feel sure is a bug. Probably some order-of-execution thing.

If you route your audio manually to a multi-channel device with `AudioServer.set_output_device()` all is groovy. If you set the multi-channel device as the default audio and don't manually route the audio all is groovy. However if the default is not the multi-channel, and you don't manually route it in your code, but instead use KDE's "Applications" feature in the volume control to tie your game to a specific multi-channel device, it's not so groovy. 8 channels are created. 8 channels are connected. But only 2 channels are actually used.

Supposition: it's first connecting to the default, setting the core to generate audio as 2 channel (down-mixing surround into stereo), then the OS is reconfiguring the audio interfaces to 8 channel. But the core is still working in 2-channel down-mixed mode. Or something.

Interestingly, starting the game connected to a 2 channel device, then after a delay redirecting it to the multi-channel in code doesn't show this problem, so it's something to do with the external signal to say "It's got to use this device". Not knowing how any of that works, I can't really say any more...
author: majenkotech

body:
So some more digging.  PipeWire sends a `param_changed` event that needs to be handled. That sends an updated POD (I think) inside of which is such things as the new channel count and arrangement, and other things related to the audio format (bit depth, sample rate, etc) all of which really need to be handled by the driver to reconfigure the core audio system as needed.

There is also an acknowledge signal that needs to be sent back (from what I have read) by calling `pw_stream_update_params()` - though I have only seen that in passing and can't comment on its validity.

POD has a pod/parser.h with `spa_pod_parse_object()` in it which is supposed to be used to get the updated settings out of the incoming POD, but that needs extra things with it to get it working and I am not familiar enough with scons and the PipeWire driver & linuxbsd file structure to even attempt getting that going.

Needless to say, though, I would label this PR as incomplete until the `param_changed` signal is properly handled by the driver, since that is a pretty fundamental part of the PipeWire protocol.

author: j20001970

body:
Did some more testing on **how to detect output device change of the stream when specified externally**, so it's time for report. Tested on PipeWire 1.6.4, WirePlumber 0.5.14, KDE Plasma 6.6.4

- In the case of `param_changed` stream event, **when connecting the stream from the driver**, the event would usually report with id **2**(`SPA_PARAM_Props`), **4**(`SPA_PARAM_Format`), **15**(`SPA_PARAM_Latency`) and **17**(`SPA_PARAM_Tag`).
Among these param types, `SPA_PARAM_Format` can be useful for determining channel count. But **when the output device of the stream is changed from volume control panel**, ~~only `SPA_PARAM_Latency` is reported, which doesn't reveal any info about newly selected output device.~~
Changing the default sink does not count, as `AudioDriverPipeWire::on_metadata_property` will reconnect the stream if current device is `Default`.
**EDIT**: After tinkering a bit more, `SPA_PARAM_Format` will also report but not as reliably as I thought, I guess this need more investigation.
- Other stream events are also attempted, `control_info`, `command` events do not emit when changing output device from volume control panel, while `io_changed` event will emit with id **7**(`SPA_IO_Position`), which relevant struct `spa_io_position` is about raw clock times.
- When changing the stream output device externally, **stream properties does not reflect newly selected target object**. So checking for stream `PW_KEY_TARGET_OBJECT` property in every `process` event won't work.
- `AudioDriverPipeWire::on_metadata_property` metadata event seems to be able to detect change in stream output target for **all** streams (this includes other non-Godot processes/apps) with key-value pair
{`target.node`: output sink id}, but I haven't found a way to associate the subject id with the driver.

Concluding what I've found currently, I think ~~the `AudioDriverPipeWire::on_metadata_property` approach is the most promising way to achieve detecting externally changed output device of the stream if such functionality is desired~~. And `param_changed` event might be a more ideal way to update stream channel count as part of format negotiation when connecting the stream.
author: j20001970

body:
After figuring out how to get stream channel count reliably, cb22c4c7fcc2b43245ac1749589ef9efb242b6e8 has made quite some change to improve multi-channel handling:

- Stream channel count are now updated from `param_changed` event instead of using node port count, and `SPA_FORMAT_AUDIO_channels` is removed from stream param to get actual stream channel count in `param_changed` event.
This change means that `AudioServer.get_speaker_mode()` will based on channels actually connected to the sink node, instead of total ports of the connected node.
- Since channel count is now updated from `param_changed` stream event, `info` event for sink/source nodes are also removed.
- Tracking for current default sink/source node in order to get channel count in advance is no longer needed, so metadata `property` event is removed as well.
- Added `state_changed` event for both output and input stream, could be useful for future debugging.
- Improved uneven channels handling for both output stream (e.g. playing 4-channel samples to 3-channel sink, or connecting to sink node with more than 8 channels) and input stream (in the case that the source node has more than 2 channels).

body: I was testing the PW CPU usage with the Godot profiler and was getting anomalous results. PulseAudio PipeWire That PW was taking 0ms seemed very suspicious so I looked a bit more at `start_counting_ticks`. The PA driver counts ticks when copying from the Audio server to local buffers (which the PW driver also does) but also measures time spent iterating the main loop (which the PW driver doesn't do). https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/3badc0ee23caf1e5269fe90f8ee17e09d4d2b682/drivers/pulseaudio/audio_driver_pulseaudio.cpp#L447-L453 Shouldn't we be using plain loops (https://docs.pipewire.org/group__pw__loop.html) that we iterate ourselves to measure the CPU usage of the PW loop like we do for the PA loop?
author: ArchercatNEO

body: > I was testing the PW CPU usage with the Godot profiler and was getting anomalous results. > > PulseAudio > > PipeWire > > That PW was taking 0ms seemed very suspicious so I looked a bit more at `start_counting_ticks`. > > The PA driver counts ticks when copying from the Audio server to local buffers (which the PW driver also does) but also measures time spent iterating the main loop (which the PW driver doesn't do). > > https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/3badc0ee23caf1e5269fe90f8ee17e09d4d2b682/drivers/pulseaudio/audio_driver_pulseaudio.cpp#L447-L453 > > Shouldn't we be using plain loops (https://docs.pipewire.org/group__pw__loop.html) that we iterate ourselves to measure the CPU usage of the PW loop like we do for the PA loop? I think the audio driver time in the debugger `Monitors` tab comes from `AudioDriver::get_latency`, which is not yet implemented in PipeWire driver since I was not sure on how to measure that. I'll look into it again to see how to implement this method.
author: j20001970

body:
The PR has been updated to d2b7d50046283e32c310a021ecbdb57083184d62 with the following changes:

- `AudioDriverPipeWire::get_latency` is now implemented. Latency calculation is based on PipeWire documentation [pw_time](https://docs.pipewire.org/structpw__time.html#details):

> For an audio playback stream, if you were to queue a buffer, the total delay in milliseconds for the first sample in the newly queued buffer to be played by the hardware can be calculated as:
> ```c
> (pw_time.buffered * 1000 / stream.samplerate) +
>   (pw_time.queued * 1000 / app.rate) +
>    ((pw_time.delay - elapsed) * 1000 * pw_time.rate.num / pw_time.rate.denom)
> ```

- `AudioDriverPipeWire::input_stop` error is silenced when input stream is destroyed before the call.
- The driver now use `PW_KEY_NODE_LATENCY` stream property to configure output stream buffer size from `audio/driver/output_latency`. The previous `SPA_PARAM_BUFFERS_size` param approach doesn't seem to have effect on `AudioDriverPipeWire::get_latency`.
author: j20001970

body: Tested again, and now we do get some numbers: This was tested with the `audio-effects` sample project and me spamming as many buttons as I could, and indeed we got latency improvements. The minimum pulse latency was higher than the maximum pipewire latency, but the main point is whether this is relevant for most people. I remember two of the reasons listed for this PR are lower latency (which we can put numbers on now) and that ALSA didn't support input. From the dev chat, there's a sentiment that fixing ALSA input would be simpler than implementing a pipewire driver. Other than these reasons, and that pipewire is useful for screen capture anyway (which is a separate PR), are there more benefits to having this pipewire audio driver?
author: ArchercatNEO

body:
> I remember two of the reasons listed for this PR are lower latency (which we can put numbers on now) and that ALSA didn't support input. From the dev chat, there's a sentiment that fixing ALSA input would be simpler than implementing a pipewire driver. Other than these reasons, and that pipewire is useful for screen capture anyway (which is a separate PR), are there more benefits to having this pipewire audio driver?

I suppose achieving lower latency is the primary and immediate benefit of including a native PipeWire driver, along with theoretically lower CPU usage as the result of skipping `pipewire-pulse`.

In long-term, adopting PipeWire API in audio driver enables the flexibility of what PipeWire provides (which is already the default audio server on several Linux distributions) as Godot audio subsystem continues to evolve. PulseAudio remains the default driver for maximum compatibility, but including PipeWire as a option gives developers and users a opportunity to test the driver for performance and stability before PulseAudio is eventually phased out.

Note that ALSA driver currently only support stereo output, and it seems that only "Default" device is listed in `device_changer` demo project (this might be system-specific though). So more work might be needed for ALSA driver in addition to fixing input to have the same features as PulseAudio/PipeWire driver.

body:
Related to https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/86172

As stated over there, I agree with the approach of handling `GET_NODE` in `_guess_expression_type`. However, when we do this, we have to remove the `_get_subscript_type` codepath at the same time, to prevent code duplication, to keep the number of codepaths low and to ensure, that the new code handles all edge cases correctly. (There are quite a lot of test cases for get node completion and they would all take the old codepath at the moment. Ideally the new codepath should pass all those tests as well.)

Oh? This has already been discussed! I will take a look at the prior discussion.

Saying that though, I am a fan of incremental work. There is a lot to get right with a "complete" solution, however this knocks one more edge-case out where autocomplete fails. There's even a test!

It's funny, originally I tried to unify the `_get_subscript_type` codepath myself too! ...but it actually contains a lot of mixed type / parameter spaghetti if you do that. You're welcome to try and untangle it. Once the unified method started creeping down the page, I just deleted it. I would rather fix this today; more complex PRs never get a serious shake.

If you want a more comprehensive refactor at some point in the future, consider using this as a stepping stone and a validation point.

body:
I partially agree with Mickeon. Having `Color8()` deprecated and simultaneously _offering_ it as a choice in the color picker seems a bit inconsistent to me. However, it makes sense to _support_ this option (wrapped in `#ifndef DISABLE_DEPRECATED`) for compatibility with existing code, even if we keep `Color8()` deprecated (currently, the inline color picker doesn't appear in the script editor for `Color8()`).

May I ask, what's the problem with `Color.from_rfba8()`? Is it just that its name is too long? Personally, I usually use `Color("<hexcode>")`, and coincidentally, it's the shortest notation (unless there's a named constant):

```gdscript
Color.from_rgba8(255, 128, 128)
Color8(255, 128, 128)
Color("ff8080")
```
author: dalexeev

body:
I do a lot of 2D and retro-lospec style work where the individual numerical values often matter to me from a usability perspective. I use *exact values* of rgb in shaders, regularly perform arithmetic on individual channels, and have to communicate these values between a few different programs.

Hex is just cumbersome to go back/forth between at every point in the process. I mean I could do it but it's a lot of pointless conversion into base-10 to do work, leave it there for a few days/weeks while I work, and then fold it back into hex so that I can tolerate working with the code? I'd have seemingly arbitrary mixes of hex and rgba *everywhere*.

<sub>(There is also a rounding error I sometimes see doing conversion between all the different forms (usually from 1.0 -> 255), values don't seem to map reversibly to rgba; however this is possibly only on my local fork? I have not confirmed the behaviour. I don't want to deal with it. 😂)</sub>

---

At the end of the day, I just don't want this kind of hassle. 0-255 is very human to use and doesn't obfuscate anything that I'm trying to do. Information density is important to me, and this is more ergonomic to work with.

body:
> This works, but it's a bit awkward:

You could try using something like:

```gdscript
const PATH = "res://test_built_in_script.tscn::GDScript_tpawx"
var resource := load(PATH)
print(ResourceLoader.get_recognized_extensions_for_type(resource.get_class()))
# Prints: ["tres", "res", "gd", "gdc"]
```

But as I mentioned above, unfortunately, this is a design flaw in the `EditorTranslationParserPlugin` API. We probably should have relied on the resource type instead of the file extension.

Besides, an array of extensions does not help much, I'd have to try all of them...
author: KoBeWi

body:
> Besides, an array of extensions does not help much, I'd have to try all of them...

I agree that this isn't good. But it depends on your goals.

* If you're creating a utility specifically for your project, you know exactly its structure and the plugins you use.
* If you're creating a project-agnostic addon, things are more complicated, but you can use roughly the following logic:
  * The `tres` and `res` extensions are universal and should be considered last, if a parser is registered for them.
  * `res` is a binary resource that is not typically used in projects for VCS-friendliness reasons, so `res` should probably be considered later than `tres`.
  * For the remaining extensions, you can find overlap with the set of supported extensions:

```gdscript
print(EditorTranslationParserPlugin.get_all_recognized_extensions())
# ["gd", "tscn", "res", "scn", "dae", "obj", "escn", "fbx", "gltf", "glb", "blend"]
```

You'll likely only get one extension. If not, then yes, you will have to choose one of the extensions and which parser to pass its processing to (although it may turn out that all of these extensions are processed by the same parser). However, this primarily only applies to unknown resource types and extensions. For the `GDScript` resource type, you can essentially hardcode the extension to always be `"gd"`.

> Yeah I'd rather avoid involving ResourceLoader, as it might make parsing considerably slower. I have hundreds of files to process

I haven't worked with very large projects, but I think you're overstating the importance of performance in this case. Translation plugins are executed quite infrequently, it's not a real-time task, so a few seconds or even a few tens of seconds isn't that long.

An alternative is to break compatibility further and introduce a new required virtual method `_get_recognized_types()` instead of `_get_recognized_extensions()`.
author: dalexeev

body:
> An alternative is to break compatibility further and introduce a new required virtual method _get_recognized_types() instead of _get_recognized_extensions().

We could have both methods and require either of them (so e.g. you could return only extensions and empty array for types and they'd be unused). Although that's something for a separate proposal and not that important, if it can be worked around.
author: KoBeWi

body:
> I think this seems to be overly complicated.

I don't quite agree. This PR relies on the fact that the `_parse_file()` callback _already_ exists, and you can add multiple parser plugins simultaneously. While this PR does break compatibility in a minor way (it changes the order in which parsers are processed), it's otherwise quite compatible with the existing approach.

I can't imagine an alternative that would fix the shortcoming of the existing approach (the inability of multiple custom parsers to process the same files in a chain), solve my problem (eliminate the inconvenient workflow with POT and manual interaction with the UI in the Project Settings, and instead use a tool script that would extract all translatable strings by calling standard parsers), and would not break compatibility in a major way.

> We could extract the parsing logic away from POT generator and expose that instead.

Could you briefly describe the API you propose so I can compare it with my approach and KoBeWi's?
author: dalexeev

body:
> Could you briefly describe the API you propose so I can compare it with my approach and KoBeWi's?

Made a proof of concept: #111706
author: timothyqiu

body:
> Made a proof of concept: #111706

So, you prefer to do nothing about the fact that multiple custom parsers can be added for the same file extension (and currently the first matching one is taken into account, not the last one)? I thought it was important to fix this shortcoming and add support for parser chaining.
author: dalexeev

body:
> So, you prefer to do nothing about the fact that multiple custom parsers can be added for the same file extension?

Yes.

If I understand correctly, the main issue addressed by this PR is the difficulty in generating formats other than POT. Parser chaining is one suggested approach to solve the problem, but not the problem itself.

If the CSV generator is implemented as a chained parser, it only works when being the last parser in the chain. Otherwise some strings will be missing. So I don't think it's a proper solution.
author: timothyqiu

body:
Well, your approach solves my problem, although it's less flexible than the one I proposed. We could simply document that multiple custom parsers for the same extension are not supported and/or add a warning when registering the parser.

The only minor issue with the current implementation of your PR is that it requires a full list of files to parse, but doesn't allow retrieving a list of supported extensions (`get_all_recognized_extensions()` in my PR), and passing files with unsupported extensions will result in warning spam. This isn't a problem for a specific project (I know that only `*.tscn` and `*.gd` can contain translatable strings), but it is a problem for a project-agnostic plugin or tool script.

author: lawnjelly

body:
> Regarding mbedtls, I suggested in [#108382 (review)](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/108382#pullrequestreview-2994852130) that for 3.6.x we might want to take a more minimal approach and not add support for TLSv1.3. I'm fine adding it anyway but it's a new feature which is not usually what we want in a patch release. But maybe it's considered a bugfix too if some websites start requiring TLSv1.3? CC @Faless

@akien-mga this is a hard one.

Enabling TLSv1.3 did have consequences in the past, notably breaking some websites due to an improper implementation on the mbedTLS side, and some unannounced breaking changes that caused a race condition in our implementation.

That said, it seems that as of now the situation is stable (we didn't find new issues in a while), and mbedTLS is clearly moving full steam towards a PSA-only world with mbedTLS 4 on the way, which will require PSA, so that's their focus now (i.e. they are more likely to fix PSA issues in 3.6 that "strange" non-PSA issues, at least that's my feeling).

So, I'm really leaning towards keeping the PSA-enabled config, as this will also make maintaining it easier on our side.
What we could maybe do, to avoid enabling a new feature by default in a point release, is swap the `enable_tlsv1.3` flag to false in `3.6`.

Again, this is a hard choice, and I also fear doing a "partial" update is riskier, so I would try to keep implementations more or less in sync whenever we can, and we can also more promptly release security updates if needed.

body:
I added one-way collisions to 3D physics (the concept makes sense there too), so now those are working e.g. in the [2D Physics Platformer Demo](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-demo-projects/tree/master/2d/physics_platformer) (with `RigidBody2D` player) and in the [2D Platformer Demo](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-demo-projects/tree/master/2d/platformer) (with `CharacterBody2D` player).

So far I only implemented one-way collisions in GodotPhysics3D (and not yet in Jolt Physics) since it could be done by adapting the GodotPhysics2D code.

I didn't expose a `one_way_collision` property on `CollisionShape3D` here since it isn't necessary to expose it for this feature (but it could be exposed).

author: LeaoLuciano

body:
Thanks for that @LeaoLuciano!

I updated the PR. I also added `JoltBody::has_one_way_collision_shapes()` to optimize contact filtering, and I added support for custom one-way collision direction, which allowed to make this PR work without introducing a strange default value in 3D (namely `Vector2.DOWN` is `(0, 1)`, and our embedding is mapping `(x, y)` to `(x, y, 0)`, but `Vector3.DOWN` is `(0, -1, 0)`, so without custom one-way collision direction we would have had to make the default 3D one-way collision direction `Vector3.UP`).

The main thing left to do is one-way collisions for character bodies (as in the 2D platformer demo) in Jolt, probably around `JoltPhysicsDirectSpaceState3D::_body_motion_recover`, `JoltPhysicsDirectSpaceState3D::_cast_motion_impl`, and `JoltPhysicsDirectSpaceState3D::_body_motion_collide`.

author: podiki

body:
@podiki what do you mean when using vulkan-loader 1.4.321.0? All of the Vulkan headers are supplied by Godot in the third-party/Vulkan folder. In what way are you using a Vulkan loader at compile time? 

Are you pointing to custom Vulkan header files instead of using the matching ones provided?
author: clayjohn

body:
@clayjohn I think I might know what is happening here. There is a `dlopen` call for `libvulkan` (in `thirdparty/volk`) which won't work in Guix as there is no global library loading (no `/lib` for instance, everything is specified with full paths in `/gnu/store`). So, we patch that call to find the system-supplied library from the vulkan-loader package. We do keep `thirdparty/vulkan` but my guess is that since vulkan-loader is in the build environment, its headers are found first.

Anyway, I guess that is to say that in the Guix case, it is on our end to be worked out in packaging, though this is a new problem with 4.5 (something must have changed we need to adapt to on the packaging side). That said, we would love if we could further unbundle thirdparty stuff for packaging in general; the current `builtin_` options in SConstruct don't cover everything.

Sorry for hijacking, though maybe this will help with the original issue. Something may have changed from 4.4 where now system vulkan headers are picked up before the Godot ones? Because at least with the packaging in Guix, 4.4 builds fine but 4.5 hits this issue, changing nothing else.

Edit: See also https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/104893#issuecomment-3299773478 probably pretty similar (Nix/Guix very similar here), so I think the below guess about `CPPEXTPATH` is indeed related.
author: podiki

body: Perhaps f25fc3443957945f42d5db4fef6d494a654da672 which uses `CPPEXTPATH` for thirdparty directories? (I'm not very familiar with the details of the Godot SCons build system, just a guess)
author: podiki

body:
@podiki That's a really good insight. This could definitely be an issue caused by https://github.com/godotengine/godot/commit/f25fc3443957945f42d5db4fef6d494a654da672. It sounds like a mismatching version of the Vulkan headers. 

So I guess a better solution would be to figure out why https://github.com/godotengine/godot/commit/f25fc3443957945f42d5db4fef6d494a654da672 caused a regression here and figure out how to avoid polluting compilation units 

Any ideas @Repiteo @akien-mga?

We now have three places that have regressed in basically the same way:
1. Nix building
2. Guix building
3. Building for whatever platform panreyes is building for
author: clayjohn

body:
I think we might need to revert https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/104893 and its follow-up(s).

The change was promising but it's proving to be unreliable, and I don't have a deep enough understanding of include precedence and shenanigans to fully make sure it's bulletproof.
author: akien-mga

body:
So, in the end, I conclude this:
- There are no compatibility issues from using the Vulkan headers from godot/thirdparty instead of the ones from the OS.
- Something broke from 4.4 to 4.5 because of this line from that commit:
```
env["EXTINCPREFIX"] = "-isystem "
```

It might be even more quirky than adding my macros, but it might be a good idea to either:
- Prioritize thirdparty/vulkan headers over the OS headers (`env["EXTINCPREFIX"] = "-ithirdparty/vulkan -isystem "` ?)
- Add ifdefs for every symbol 😅
author: panreyes

body: Reverting the change (it worked fine before) is definitely better than adding more hacks with potential pitfalls.
author: akien-mga

body:
Thanks for the quick responses all! I just wanted to follow up with one thing from the Guix side, as it is actually a bit of an accident we hit this.

So far we had disabled volk (via `use_volk=no`) yet still had vulkan-loader so we could patch the `dlopen` calls in `thirdparty/volk.c` That doesn't quite make sense (I assume it would never hit that code if volk is disabled) and since the header includes has vulkan only when not using volk, we end up finding those system libraries due to the change mentioned above. If I enable volk, it builds fine (and I think works, have to check).

[Edit: Actually, I forgot there needs to be libvulkan found via pkg-config, so vulkan-loader is always in the build environment]

Anyway, I think it is good to find this out soon after the commits and in a way that was pretty easy to figure out. Having proper precedence for thirdparty/system libraries would be good. Probably this hasn't been seen more widely because the default has volk enabled. And in Guix we try to unbundle as much as we can. But I would think building on any system where vulkan-loader/headers are a different version and volk is disabled could hit this. Well, as long as the build is properly isolated and does not include the thirdparty stuff from system, it would be fine.

I'll work around this in the Guix package for 4.5 one way or another, but feel free to ping to test any changes in building.

author: Ivorforce

body: This text was recently added by @RolandMarchand in PR #109925, maybe he would like to give feedback on this.
author: aaronfranke

body:
> The way values are calculated should not be discouraged.

Errors should be discouraged, and multiplying a velocity vector by `delta` twice is a significant physics calculation error. This happens when the user multiplies `velocity` by `delta` and then uses `move_and_slide()` which internally also multiplies the velocity by `delta`. Users should never do that, so it should be discouraged. We can argue about the specific language used, but I have not heard a single complaint about it.
author: RolandMarchand

body:
@RolandMarchand This PR is saying the same thing as you're describing.

The original sentence discourages any form of `x * delta`. This PR changes it to noting that `px/s * delta` is a common mistake.

The key point is, `x * delta` is valid as long as `x` is in `px/s²`.

The root cause of the error lies in users not caring about the units of values. The original wording not only has false positives, but also encouraged the root error rather than resolving it.
author: timothyqiu
timothyqiu

body: Thanks!
author: Repiteo

body:
I think the focus of his problem is not on the unit, but on clearly stating in the description of the move_and_slide method that the delta parameter has already been used for calculation. Similar to what the direction_to method explicitly states as returning a normalized vector. At present, this page(https://docs.godotengine.org/en/latest/tutorials/physics/physics_introduction.html#move -and-slide) indicates this point, but the built-in document method description has not been updated synchronously.
This 'delta-related' thing only clearly stated in the description of the move_ond_comlide method like "Moves the body along the vector motion. In order to be frame rate independent in Node._physics_process() or Node._process(), motion should be computed using delta".

author: coobenguy

body:
> I feel like a small amount of blue should be warranted to keep it Godot-y? Im not suggesting the same blue I use to be clear, just an example

For context: most grays that can be seen in UIs are actually not true neutral grays. Try using a color picker on dark GitHub's background; you'll see it's slightly tinted blue. These tinted grays tend to look more pleasing[^1], but they can skew the white balance a bit. Blender has true neutral grays in comparison (R/G/B are always identical).

I lean in favor of making the Godot UI very slightly tinted by default, but there's a good argument to using true neutral grays for design apps too.

[^1]: They're popular enough that the [Tailwind color palette](https://tailwindcolor.com/) has no less than 5 gray palettes 🙂

> 
> If it isn't much to suggest, would be good to have `ss02` (disambiguation, enables uppercase i with serif, lowercase L with tail and slashed zero) defined as it fixes the biggest complain [certain FOSS projects have]

I'd also suggest using the OpenType features Blender uses (which I listed on my original discussion on switching to Inter, as I think they really improve on readability.
```
cv02 - Open four
cv03 - Open six
cv04 - Open nine (maybe leave nine closed or leave six closed?)
cv05 - Lowercase L with tail
cv08 - Uppercase I with serif
cv09 - flat top three
tnum - Evenly spaced numbers, makes the interface less "jumpy". (example gif on the original discussion)
```

body:
> As I already mentioned in our internal discussion, the theme makes various elements lose distinction by blending their background with surroundings. Some examples:

I know that theming is subjective, but to me all of the "new" images you supply are a vast improvement over the default theme. The default theme is far too busy, pointing out categories blending with properties in the inspector (among other things) does not resonate with me as a bad thing, it's in fact perfectly readable without the eyesore of having multiple slightly different shades of gray as text backgrounds when the bold white text and dropdown arrow prefix are arguably just the right amount of visual distinction when contrasted with the dimmer regular font of the properties. 

Mind you I'm not saying the minimal theme is perfect or anything, but the main features it provides are both better use of available screen space, and much less visual clutter which to me improves readability greatly.

body: > For context: most grays that can be seen in UIs are actually not true neutral grays. Try using a color picker on dark GitHub's background; you'll see it's slightly tinted blue. These tinted grays tend to look more pleasing[1](#user-content-fn-1-69bdd0b0fe582a21b3f5f92fb8c20621), but they can skew the white balance a bit. Blender has true neutral grays in comparison (R/G/B are always identical). Just a reference with popular themes (how many times Green and Blue are higher than Red): | Theme | G | B | |--------------------------|------|------| | One Dark Pro/One Monokai | 1.1 | 1.3 | | Dracula | 1.05 | 1.35 | | Nord | 1.13 | 1.39 | | GitHub | 1.14 | 1.38 | | Tomorrow | 1.07 | 1.11 | | Xcode Dark | 1.17 | 1.41 | Godot's own offset is the same as GitHub's for reference. Using the One Dark Pro offset over the proposed color, the result would be around `#24282f`, which looks neutral enough while also not being as blueish as the original: Considering there is consensus that having a blue tint is an issue, here is using Tomorrow's offset (`#242728`): **Edit:** after a bit of research, it seems every other popular game engine uses true neutral colors, so this is a reasonable thing to do.
author: DeeJayLSP

body:
> For context: most grays that can be seen in UIs are actually not true neutral grays. Try using a color picker on dark GitHub's background; you'll see it's slightly tinted blue. These tinted grays tend to look more pleasing, but they can skew the white balance a bit. Blender has true neutral grays in comparison (R/G/B are always identical).

Godot is closer to Blender here as it is used for color sensitive work, unlike GitHub and IDEs. It would be good to at least have a toggleable preset for neutral gray, in case it ends up not being the default.

body: I gave this PR a test run again, it's in a pretty good state now. In terms of design/UX, these are my only remaining concerns before merging: - The height of each Tree item (21 pixels per item at 100% editor scale) still feels too low by default. It should be increased to match PopupMenu's item height at least (24 pixels per item): This is also noticeable in the Project/Editor Settings, where the list of sections appears much more busy than the settings themselves: The current height is well-suited for the Compact preset, but even then, it might need one more pixel to be vertically centered. - The inspector lacks visible hover feedback for foldable sections. - The background for selected inspector properties is barely visible: I regularly rely on this background color to visually "bookmark" the property I last edited, so it makes usability worse for me. The rest can be addressed in follow-ups.

author: shakesoda

body:
Without getting caught up in my own personal nitpicks with this chosen theme (of which I have many), I would hope that function and usability remains the primary factor by which any default theme is selected.

Functionally, there appears to be a loss in effective screen real estate for many elements, as they now take up more space than before. And as pointed out a moment ago, the new Input Map is simply a downgrade. I can't see a good argument for this as it currently exists.

As a positive, I like the new scene and file browser list density, as they both display more items than before in the same space. (Would still prefer a return of a darker background element to help distinguish the item list, though.)

Subjectively, I find this new theme quite difficult to look at. I find this shade of color to be very disagreeable, and I can't recall a time in any software I've ever used where a dark mode theme was similar to this tone. Again, this is a subjective complaint, but I also would argue that a theme based around a highlighter yellow base would not be a great default choice either.  Personally, I think a color selection closer to the existing default theme would help a lot.

body:
Do note for anyone reading this, we are keeping track of all issues opened about the new theme. So, although it's being repeated constantly, it would be beneficial to the community as a whole to open unique, **individual** [issues](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues) to provide specific feedback about the new theme and discuss potential solutions.
We'll always be aware that, because this is such a fundamental change, the new theme will always be quite upsetting to some. Still, try to be constructive in your criticism.
The existing Godot theme isn't lost and is available to choose in the editor settings, as a preset.

Side note, this PR has the potential to uncover [certain omissions](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/112141) with "custom themes" as a whole, which personally has me confident it's going to make the general UX more consistent going forward!

Closing in favor of #111162
As mentioned above, side menu is not compatible with native file dialogs (i.e. dialogs managed by the operating system). On some platforms native dialogs are the only available file dialogs, so this feature effectively causes platform disparity.

For advanced file options, you should either use the options provided by FileDialog (compatible with native dialogs), or show a config dialog after selecting the file (like some apps do). Blender is an exception here; I actually wonder how it works in sandboxed environments.

author: Repiteo

body:
> I do feel like we should eventually add a mechanism to do the free on a background thread. In a perfect world the free function would always return quickly without stalling.

The main problem here is that there's no guarantee to be able to do that because a shader could be freed while the pipeline that needs the SPIR-V is being built. There'd need to be some deeper mechanism built into RenderingDevice probably to be able to defer this in that way and decouple the SPIR-V's allocation from the Shader to allow the pipeline to finish compiling.

It is unlikely a driver doesn't use a copy of the data, but it's a race condition none the less.

Drivers also are allowed to pull other bits of the shader using the RID during pipeline creation so it definitely needs to be kept alive, and if RenderingDevice isn't aware of the compilations currently in progress in the background, the safest way now is to indeed just free the pipeline before the shader, forcing the wait.
author: DarioSamo

body:
On the whole this looks great.

I do suspect (with the RAM regressions you mention) that we may need to go over the codebase and identify some areas that leak memory (maybe loading scenes in the editor or something) that would be appropriate to call `reset()` on, which previously relied on the self-shrinking properties.

We should probably also consider exposing `reset()` for users via binding, otherwise they will have no way to control memory use in their own projects?

I can't remember the details in the Core meeting, but if this was the consensus, we should go for it. In the past such decisions were normally made by @reduz, who may want to chime in.

Auto-shrinking is one of those "damned if you do, damned if you don't" type things I suspect. But if there are major problems with this once merged, we can always have auto-shrinking _combined_ with the ability to `reset()` and free memory.

### Testing
In terms of testing, it would be nice to have some really rough testing prior to proceeding imo, perhaps just running the editor and loading some huge scenes, closing etc and repeated opening scenes and seeing the total memory used by Godot, just to check beforehand for any major regressions.
author: lawnjelly

body:
> I do suspect (with the RAM regressions you mention) that we may need to go over the codebase and identify some areas that leak memory (maybe loading scenes in the editor or something) that would be appropriate to call reset() on, which previously relied on the self-shrinking properties.

We might be able to identify some in code that we're familiar with, or simply by profiling. But I suspect that we'll miss something until more people actually use the code, which is why it would be important we merge this early in a dev cycle.

> We should probably also consider exposing reset() for users via binding, otherwise they will have no way to control memory use in their own projects?

This is addressed in the OP:

> To mitigate this, users can construct a new Array, Packed*Array, Vector, or String, and use append_array (or similar) on it, to transfer the elements without transferring the capacity.
Still, it is likely that we want to add a dedicated method for capacity shrinking in the future.

> I can't remember the details in the Core meeting, but if this was the consensus, we should go for it. In the past such decisions were normally made by @reduz, who may want to chime in.

Yes, we had a consensus to go ahead with it. Though it would be nice to get @reduz's opinion as well!

> In terms of testing, it would be nice to have some really rough testing prior to proceeding imo, perhaps just running the editor and loading some huge scenes, closing etc and repeated opening scenes and seeing the total memory used by Godot, just to check beforehand for any major regressions.

Totally agreed. I haven't tested the PR in this way yet, but I also think it should be used for a good 1-2 hours in the editor while profiling, to ensure no ugly leaks, before we merge it.

Edit: Lawnjelly and I discussed some potential risks of this PR, so we'll be bringing it back to the core meeting.
author: Ivorforce

body:
Just for reference, we discussed this in private chat (sorry, my bad should have been in public channel), but here is the info here where we discussed some caveats of auto-shrink versus hysteresis:

<details>

lawnjelly
9:49 AM
On this point : https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/111131#discussion_r2425554769
Link Preview

[Remove automatic capacity shrinking from `CowData` (`String`, `Vector`, and `Array`) by Ivorforce · Pull Request #111131 · godotengine/godot](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/111131#discussion_r2425554769)
Currently, CowData based types (Array, Packed*Array, and String) will automatically reduce their own capacity if element counts fall below some threshold. With this change, backing buffers will kee...
[GitHub](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/111131)
If ref count is not 1, then surely we are creating memory for scratch either way?
Say if the buffer is 1024, and 2 people are using it, and we want to make a new one with size 1023, is there anything to gain by not changing it (aside from staying at magic capacities e.g. power of 2)?
I'm not sure I understand exactly your comment about opaque for shrinking or not, when by definition we are having to create new memory?
Particularly when resizing.
If we e.g. have 2 users of a vector that is 5 megs, and we want to resize one of them to e.g. 1Kb, then it's more expensive to allocate a new 5 meg memory than to allocate 1Kb.
The costs will always be different between there being 1 user and being 2 users of COW, because 1 user can do a NOOP, and the second user will always have to allocate when performing COW?

ivorius
10:46 AM
I did initially consider auto-shrinking the capacity here as well, but ultimately landed on the 'opaque behavior' instead
I can construct a simple example that would break expectation otherwise:
```
Vector vector;
vector.reserve(10000);
Vector copy = vector;
for (int i = 0; i < 10000; i++) {
    vector.push_back(i);
}
```
Since vector is the one splitting off, on the first push_back, it's copy that inherits the capacity
And vector which would 'throw it away' while splitting
So the reserve would have been useless

lawnjelly
10:48 AM
That's weird, shouldn't logically the copy be the one creating new memory?
Is that just the way it's currently setup, can we change this?

ivorius
10:48 AM
No, because copy just references the old array. After the assignment, both have equal ownership
And the first push_back is what forces vector to split off into its own copy

lawnjelly
10:49 AM
Ah, I see there's no COW until you push back to vector...
👍
1
That's kind of weird but also I can see it being problematic the case I said, that if you want to resize to a small size, it doesn't reduce the memory.

ivorius
10:50 AM
Well, resize never reduces memory
After the PR anyway
That's the point of the PR 😛

lawnjelly
10:50 AM
Yeah, but that's the thing, if it's COWing, maybe it should.
I get it when there's only 1 user referencing the data...
But when you are creating a new allocation anyway, then it would seem optimal to reduce the memory.
And that might be a more important case than the reserve case you mention above.

ivorius
10:52 AM
I think both situations are pretty niché
But either way, if you "auto-shrink" when you're splitting off, you're effectively giving power over your capacity to other owners of the array when you hand it off
When you say "I'm giving you a copy", you're also saying "I'm OK with my capacity shrinking again automatically right now"
Which I don't think is expected

lawnjelly
10:54 AM
No, this should only occur when COW has occurred, after splitting off.

ivorius
10:54 AM
Sure, but the decision happens when you hand off the copy
No copy, no shrinking

lawnjelly
10:55 AM
The new user is responsible for their own capacity, it shouldn't affect the first user I think

ivorius
10:55 AM
The only way I can think of to mitigate this would be if CowData were to locally store its own capacity (instead of in the backing array)
Then the previous owner could keep its capacity when splitting off, and for the new user it resets
But that also kind of messes with expectations since which of the two is the 'new owner' and which is the old changes depending on context
So nevermind, that's actually a bad idea lol

lawnjelly
10:57 AM
Maybe I'm missing something as it's a while since I looked at cowdata.
Situation example would be e.g.:
```
Vector a;
a.resize(10000);
Vector b = a;
b.resize(10);
```
When b.resize(10) is called, it does the copy on write.
And creates a new allocation, so I don't see why it should need to allocate 10000 and not just 10..

ivorius
10:59 AM
Yea, I can see how that could be problematic
At the same time:
```
Vector a;
a.resize(10000);
Vector b = a;
a.resize(10001);
b.resize(10);
```

Now, with either behavior, b will be bloated
Because a splits off first, and b keeps the large capacity
I mean, this could be an argument against removing auto-shrink altogether

lawnjelly
11:02 AM
Well, I guess we have to think of common cases versus super convoluted cases.
🤔

ivorius
11:02 AM
It's a little harder to manage sane capacities when you don't know whether you keep the capacity yourself, or will hand it off to copies

lawnjelly
11:03 AM
You are right though with your example too. But personally I would err on the side of reducing capacity where possible, where it is essentially free I think.

ivorius
11:04 AM
We could also just keep going with the current solution

lawnjelly
11:04 AM
But it's just a guess, it depends on the actual use, you can contrive examples to be better in either case.
👍
1

ivorius
11:04 AM
Since we have capacity stored, CowData auto-shrink uses hysteresis
Instead of the 'hard boundaries' in the 4.5 and earlier implementations
So the situation is already a lot better than before
Maybe worth discussing again in the next core meeting?

lawnjelly
11:05 AM
To be honest I'd be tempted to just try it as is and see what the situation is in the wild. It's easy to follow up and re-add hysteresis.
But might be worth discussing again in core meeting yes.
This is partly why I wondered if Juan would have a view, he tends to have some good idea of overall allocation patterns, but he's not been very active lately.
👍
1
But on the other hand he's sometimes a bit "my way or the highway", and then later turns out to be wrong.
😄
1
😀
I suspect the clincher will be either in the rendering server boundaries, or in level reload behaviour or something like that.

ivorius
11:09 AM
I'd hope that internal APIs aren't too affected

lawnjelly
11:09 AM
There's a lot of churn with data going over the multithread server queues (which uses COW I think).

ivorius
11:09 AM
Internal APIs should (mostly) use LocalVector anyway

lawnjelly
11:09 AM
And if something went wrong there, it could go horribly wrong.
👀
1
Yeah but for servers, you can't use LocalVector, because it would have to copy the data every time.

ivorius
11:10 AM
Ah

lawnjelly
11:10 AM
That's why COW Vector is handy.
Because the client and server might be out of sync by e.g. 3 frames or something.

</details>

body:
> * Add overload method for `GodotPlugin#emitSignal(...)` that takes in a `SignalInfo` argument.
>   It's common for `GodotPlugin` implementations to define a set of `SignalInfo` objects to use for registration, then reuse those objects when a signal is emitted. This typically looks like:
> 
> ```
> private val EVENT_SIGNAL = SignalInfo("event_signal")
> ...
> override fun getPluginSignals() = setOf(EVENT_SIGNAL)
> ...
> fun dispatchEvent() {
>     emitSignal(EVENT_SIGNAL.name)
> }
> ```
> 
> With this update, the call to emit the signal can be changed to:
> 
> ```
> fun dispatchEvent() {
>     emitSignal(EVENT_SIGNAL)
> }
> ```

Hm.. I don't see any use for this overloaded method because you can create string constants for signal names. Eg.
```
private val EVENT_SIGNAL = "event_signal"
override fun getPluginSignals() = setOf(SignalInfo(EVENT_SIGNAL))
fun dispatchEvent() {
    emitSignal(EVENT_SIGNAL)
}
```

body: Doesn't the `Transform2D.looking_at()` method make more sense in this context?
author: ydeltastar

body:
> Doesn't the `Transform2D.looking_at()` method make more sense in this context?

I guess it really depends what one wants to do/achieve. Since `Node2D.look_at` expects `some_global_position` as its parameter, you'd need to do something like `node2d.global_transform.looking_at(some_global_position)` as the equivalent (or `node2d.transform.looking_at(some_parent_space_position)`). And then do something with the result. Seems a little more complicated to me (or rather easier to use incorrectly) than using `Node2D.get_angle_to` which allows to obtain a value directly applicable to `Node2D.rotation`.

Nevertheless, I would say it shouldn't hurt to add a note like `See also: [Transform2D.looking_at]`. However, looking at `Transform2D.looking_at` implementation it doesn't actually do the same thing as `Node2D.look_at`, seems there are some scaling issues with it: #57998, #74966. So, at least for now, I conclude it's better to not add such link.

> One thing bothering me, however, is how hinting messes up alignment with uppercase i, making it look like it's out of place...

> This doesn't happen if hinting is disabled, or the font size is 13 or 15. Maybe we should disable disambiguation for it and keep for lowercase L? They would end up different anyway.

In case we want those other variations, #111461 is mandatory.

**EDIT**: Apparently not, in case we use font feature freeze.

On the other hand, doing so implies modifying the font, which requires copyright info modification by the terms of OFL (at least Adwaita Sans, which *is* a font feature freeze by GNOME that only enables a character variant, does that). 

With that said, I think it's better to wait for HarfBuzz auto-hinting.
author: DeeJayLSP

body:
> There's also Adwaita Mono (a customized Iosevka) which could replace JetBrains Mono, as it's designed to pair well with Adwaita Sans.

Offtopic but, if we truly want a customized Iosevka, the best variant would probably be Iosevka SS14 Extended, which is customized to look like JetBrains Mono.

On the other hand, JetBrains Mono's WOFF2 file is 90.2KiB, while the average pre-compiled Iosevka WOFF2 won't be lower than 300 KiB. And comparing, I personally don't see any advantages.
author: DeeJayLSP

body:
With the issue with variant hinting gone, I have re-enabled serif uppercase i and slashed 0. Added more screenshots to the main comment.

The [proposal](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/discussions/9012) suggests `ss01` (all alternate digits), `ss04` (I and l disambiguation without slashed 0) and `tnum` (tabular numbers). But for now, I'll just let the default disambiguation in unless someone really wants something else.

If anyone disagrees with slashed 0, we can just replace `ss02` with `ss04`.
author: DeeJayLSP

body: Decided to showcase variant candidates separately. `ss01` (alternate digits): `ss02` (serifed I, tailed l and slashed zero): `ss04` (same as `ss02` but without slashed zero): `tnum` (tabular numbers): `ss01` + `ss02`: `ss01` + `tnum`: `ss02` + `tnum`: `ss04` + `tnum` (***current candidate***): `ss01` + `ss02` + `tnum`: My personal opinion: - Uppercase i being serifed makes it closer to Noto Sans' which is serifed by default. - Either serifed uppercase i, or tailed lowercase L, or both, are desired, as the defaults are literally the same character. - Slashed zero only serves to further differentiate it from uppercase o. Fonts like JetBrains Mono do this by default, although with a dot in the middle instead of a slash. Could be desired too. - Tabular numbers might make number fields more consistent. Float sliders benefit from this. - Alternate digits is pretty much a stylistic choice, but could be better for reading due to looking unique compared to every other character. If anyone else has a preference, I'm interested in hearing it.
author: DeeJayLSP

body: I would go for `ss02` + `tnum` personally. Tabular (= fixed-width) numbers will make many places in the editor look more consistent when values change, such as the **View Information** and **View Frame Time** panels in the 3D editor.
author: Calinou

body: I personally don't like the slashed zero! I feel like there's very few places in the editor where the distinction between `0` and `O` matters, because the editor itself (outside of code) generally avoids this ambiguity. For example, "text" and "numbers" tend to be distinct from one another, and very few properties have a `0` in their names (and it hardly can be confused for a lone letter). With little benefit, the slash only makes the zero noisier.

body:
I was thinking about such an early return, but I had some suspicion of it breaking something when the method is used in some `parented` callback, where the parent/child data is already set, but the tree isn't yet.

If such a state can occur `tree` might be `nullptr` for the node that is currently in the process of being added, but `tree` would be the actual pointer for the other node.

To avoid regressions in such edge cases I opted to just use the old behaviour in such cases.
A save early return might be `data.tree != nullptr && p_node->data.tree != nullptr && data.tree != p_node->data.tree`. But I don't even know if a scenario with two scene trees is something we fully support, and its probably so rare that it didn't feel worth it.

body: @Calinou This PR should only have an impact on devices that support UMA. Your RTX 5090 doesn't support UMA, so we don't expect it to make a difference. The changes on LLVMpipe are within a margin of error too. So at most we can conclude that this doesn't cause regressions on that device
author: clayjohn

body:
Actually Calinou's performance on RTX 5090 are within the expected outcome (though the difference is so small it could be noise).

Even though the RTX 5090 isn't UMA, this PR takes advantage of CPU-visible, device-local VRAM (which is plentiful in GPUs with ReBAR enabled). In other words, the RTX 5090 will pretend it is UMA and we will use that.

This means:

1. CPU may execute slower, because CPU "pushes" data through the slower uncached PCIe bus (instead of having the GPU "pull" that data later through the bus). This could be theoretically improved if the copy itself is offloaded to a worker thread when possible (not always possible).
2. GPU might execute faster.
3. Overall may or may not execute faster, because some of the code now pushes data directly to GPU memory instead of doing two copies (first to staging area, then to GPU).
4. Low 1% / 0.1% should improve because there's fewer GPU copy commands being scheduled. This is most likely the most noticeable difference and it was the only metric that consistently always improved for Framerate and GPU time, and consistently deteriorated for CPU time. This is exactly expected.
5. On a true UMA architecture, the difference is that there is no slow PCIe bus. The CPU writes directly to GPU memory without downsides(\*), making the PR (theoretically) more impactful on such Hardware.

llvmpipe is an outlier in too many ways (it's not an actual GPU, it's so slow that noise is huge, and rendering threads compete with Godot's threads for execution time).

(\*) On some SoCs like the PS4 and PS5, the CPU bandwidth to RAM is artificially throttled though (to avoid CPU from starving the GPU's bandwidth) so the analysis is more nuanced in such cases as it will resemble the RTX 5090 case.
author: darksylinc

body:
@Calinou thanks for testing. As discussed with @clayjohn in [this thread on RocketChat](https://chat.godotengine.org/channel/rendering?msg=8w4kFqTM6HiR8TdFG), we're removing the push constant changes, which significantly reduces the blast radius of this PR. Note that no `.glsl` shaders are modified after that change.

UMA are only used for instance buffers now, and I have incorporated #104566 into this PR, as it was quite small and showed a measurable improvement in the 2D batching demo I tested (10% on my M1 Pro Max; I'll retest on my M4 Pro Max)

body: > device-local VRAM (which is plentiful in GPUs with ReBAR enabled). In other words, the RTX 5090 will pretend it is UMA and we will use that. Note that the NVIDIA Windows driver only enables ReBAR by default in specific games that are known to benefit from it (according to the per-game profile), but on Linux, it *seems* to be enabled for everything by default as long as it's enabled in the UEFI. I can confirm it's enabled on my end.

author: blueskythlikesclouds

body:
> This PR seems to have caused a big performance regression in D3D12. In the commit right before this PR, I get about 145~ FPS in the editor, but with this PR, it drops to 60~ FPS. I'll investigate what caused it.

What GPU and driver? What's the log info? (all info you'll be asked when creating a ticket). And of course the MRP.

I'm specifically interested in knowing which one you get of these msgs:

1. `D3D12: Device supports GPU UPLOAD heap.` (only printed in **verbose** logging mode).
2. `D3D12: Device does NOT support GPU UPLOAD heap. ReBAR must be enabled for this feature. Regular UPLOAD heaps will be used as fallback.` (printed as a warning).

Godot will try to use `D3D12_HEAP_TYPE_GPU_UPLOAD` when supported, else it will use `D3D12_HEAP_TYPE_UPLOAD`. These two can have different performance profiles. If your system is using `D3D12_HEAP_TYPE_GPU_UPLOAD`, try forcing it off by modifying the source code (look for `dynamic_persistent_upload_heap = D3D12_HEAP_TYPE_GPU_UPLOAD;` in the code).

In both cases, the way we upload data to GPU changed compared to how it worked before the PR, though `D3D12_HEAP_TYPE_UPLOAD` resembles a little more to how it was before.

body:
> We discussed this yesterday in the Godot proposal meeting. We acknowledge it's a desired feature and could be useful, but at the same time, we're wary about uses of the "global add-ons" feature for add-ons that should clearly be stored within the project. In other words, your project might break if it relies on global add-ons but other people in your team forget to install them. We don't want to require people to scour the Internet manually to find add-ons before they can start working on a project.

This pull request does not address the problem of the project breaking if it relies on global add-ons but other people in your team forget to install them.

https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/831#issuecomment-702602267
author: fire

body:
> This pull request does not address the problem of the project breaking if it relies on global add-ons but other people in your team forget to install them.

This pull request only implements the global file system, not the user interface and user experience things, so there's no place to tell the user not to install project specific add-ons into it. That's for another pull request when the editor UI stuff for this is added.

But I guess the only thing we can do here is to change the name from “global resource” to “editor resource” and the path prefix to `editor://`. This way, it will be more obvious that it's only for the editor.
