body:
I tested with my [quick scenes](https://github.com/KoBeWi/Godot-Quick-Scenes) addon. As expected, the addon did not work out-of-the-box, but there is no effective way to fix the errors. Clicking the `editor://` paths in the Output does not open the file. When you open the script in Script Editor and try to save, it shows a file dialog, as if trying to save a new file. If you put `project.godot` into the global directory and open it, you get warning spam about duplicate UIDs (because the files are loaded twice). There needs to be some way to actually develop the global addons, right now editing and testing them is cumbersome.

And another thing is that the global addons appear in the project settings dialog. When you develop the project in a team, everyone will need the same set of global addons, otherwise they will get errors at startup and the addon will get disabled, resulting in `project.godot` conflicts. The global addons should appear in editor settings instead (probably in a new global addons tab).
author: KoBeWi

body:
> I tested with my [quick scenes](https://github.com/KoBeWi/Godot-Quick-Scenes) addon. As expected, the addon did not work out-of-the-box, but there is no effective way to fix the errors. Clicking the editor:// paths in the Output does not open the file. When you open the script in Script Editor and try to save, it shows a file dialog, as if trying to save a new file. If you put project.godot into the global directory and open it, you get warning spam about duplicate UIDs (because the files are loaded twice). There needs to be some way to actually develop the global addons, right now editing and testing them is cumbersome.

I thought so too. I think the best way is to have an automatic project-plugin to global-plugin converter, where it changed all the reference of `res://addons/` to `editor://`. Maybe for future PR?

Also, in the future, there should be a way to show the global addons in the editor file system and allow resources there to be edited, so that it is easier to develop global addons.

> And another thing is that the global addons appear in the project settings dialog. When you develop the project in a team, everyone will need the same set of global addons, otherwise they will get errors at startup and the addon will get disabled, resulting in project.godot conflicts. The global addons should appear in editor settings instead (probably in a new global addons tab).

Yup, I already considered it here: https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/831#issuecomment-3703682084
author: PiCode9560

body:
Tested with a couple of personal favorites on Linux:
- [Edit Resources as Table 2](https://godotengine.org/asset-library/asset/1479)
- [DualEditor](https://godotengine.org/asset-library/asset/3563)
- [Ridiculous Coding](https://godotengine.org/asset-library/asset/1559)

All of these worked pretty well, with only some minor modifications (mostly renaming all references of `res://addons/` -> `editor://`). I'm really surprised how effortless it was to get them to work :partying_face: 

I second @KoBeWi's point that the editor's UI/UX really needs to be improved to allow addon developers to "convert" addons to editor addons, and help with troubleshooting eventual issues in `editor://` files. But I see this MR as a great starting point. It will require further work around the editor and the plugin ecosystem to really be a stable experience, but I personally don't see much of an issue for this feature to be incrementally implemented through multiple MRs, and only be "announced" to the community as a feature once it reaches a mature state. I don't know what's Godot's strategy when dealing with such cases, but these are my quick _2 cents_ on the topic.

body:
I came across this PR after I reached a bottleneck in my multiple projects due to not having editor wide plugins. I think that this PR is really solid, but two concerns. Maybe you have addressed it, and I just missed out but here goes:
- Where would new editor plugins be expected to store things like configurations? Most editor-only plugins I see store it in `ProjectSettings` or have their own custom configuration location. Maybe this is outside the scope of this PR.
- How would these proposed editor plugins deal with context-based things? For example, I wrote a [dead simple plugin](https://godotengine.org/asset-library/asset/4118) that simply runs a set of commands defined in the assets folder. Sure, if there was a real system for Editor Plugins, i would've done it differently, but right now it just `OS.execute()`s the commands. Should such executions be treated as taking part in `editor://` or `res://`? And so on, but again, maybe I misunderstand the scope of this PR.

Overall, I like this PR, and I tried it out in it's current state. It's a bit of a weird workflow, but hoping that future PRs will build on this system to create a really good editor plugin ecosystem (something I wish already existed :')  )
author: WinnerWind

body:
> Where would new editor plugins be expected to store things like configurations? Most editor-only plugins I see store it in ProjectSettings or have their own custom configuration location. Maybe this is outside the scope of this PR.

Adding project settings from global plugins is fine, so it's the same as with regular plugins.

> Should such executions be treated as taking part in editor:// or res://?

There is only one working directory for `OS.execute()`, so `editor://` stuff will execute in the context of the current project.

body:
> However, I just wanted to comment that I think `editor://` is a better prefix for the path. `edres://` is confusing as an abbreviation ("ed" vs "editor") and it makes a connection to the `res://` prefix that I don't think is particularly helpful for users

My thought is that `editor://` **doesn't** really sounds like a resource path, especially when its not consistent with the project resource path prefix, which is `res://` instead of something like `project://`.

`editor://` is going to be more confusing **if** in the future, we are going to add a path prefix to the _editor data/settings folder_ for example. The prefix may look like this: `editordata://` (becasuse `editor://` was taken). Now comparing `editordata://` and `editor://` side by side, `editor://` sort of looks like it supposed to be a parent folder of `editordata://`.

So for that reasons and since `edres://` is kind of confusing, how about `editorres://`? (Its more consistent with `res://`). However, I understand that `editorres://` might be too long and still confussing. So feel free to disagree with me. Maybe `editor://` is enough.

body:
This does not produce valid GDScript, as your examples show. The indentation parameters are for adding new lines, not for formatting existing lines, there isn't enough information to handle all cases that will be wanted for good formatting. Actual code formatting is much more complex and needs more language info. 
So this is only maybe useful in some simple languages, users will still have to add their own formatting system in almost all cases.

What is the use case of this? The proposal says it is to "improve the CodeEdit node", but [there needs to be an actual issue first](https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/guidelines/best_practices.html#the-problem-always-comes-first).

My first idea was to add a bunch of tools for the `CodeEdit` node but it was pointed out to me that some of my ideas were either too useless for the node or not well thought out, this function being the only thing I finished before I was told that, I pushed it in case you are interested and to have feedback on how to improve it. Fantastic work! 
One thing that completely breaks the upstream SSR is fog - is fog/volumetric fog handled correctly in this PR?
author: RPicster

body:
> - The default depth tolerance seems low. In most scenes I tested, 0.2 caused too many artifacts. Since it's also in view space, checking it in the SSR loop currently requires linearizing two depth values. Maybe the parameter could also be defined in hyperbolic Z space?

See https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/3919, where I proposed increasing the default to `0.5`. Note that a depth tolerance that's too high can also be distracting, particularly in third-person action games where the camera is behind a fast-moving character. That said, I think it's safe to increase the default to `0.3` at least.

> - Now that the roughness filtering is as high quality as it can get, what do we about the option?

The project setting can be removed entirely, while the RenderingServer methods should remain (as deprecated methods) to preserve compatibility with existing projects. You can modify the class reference XML to add a `deprecated="Reason here..."` attribute.

> - Using the previous frame causes the reflections to appear black for a singular frame when the framebuffers get cleared. You can reproduce this by just resizing the viewport. Not sure how this behavior should be accounted for.

SSIL has the same issue: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/56723

We should apply the same fix in the long run, i.e. force low-processor mode to redraw at least 2 frames on every visual change when either SSR or SSIL is enabled. See https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/93966 which implements this for volumetric fog.

Also, resizing the viewport should try to reuse previous information (but scaled) to minimize flicker. There's a PR doing this for volumetric fog too:

- https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/93979

> - Investigate how TAA could be utilized when it's enabled.

See my experiments in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/97516.

> One thing that completely breaks the upstream SSR is fog - is fog/volumetric fog handled correctly in this PR?

I've just tested this PR and can confirm the issue is resolved :slightly_smiling_face: 

### `master`

[master.webm](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d82e0b84-3c82-4e78-bb11-c7a6fae20a35)

### This PR

[pr.webm](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/89e6453d-f460-499a-985f-c55aeadd967a)
author: Calinou

body:
> # Questions
> * The default depth tolerance seems low. In most scenes I tested, 0.2 caused too many artifacts. Since it's also in view space, checking it in the SSR loop currently requires linearizing two depth values. Maybe the parameter could also be defined in hyperbolic Z space?

Let's increase it to 0.5 as suggested by Calinou. I don't think we should define it in hyperbolic space since then the actual distance will change based on the distance from the camera. 

For reference AMD's SSSR does the depth tolerance check in linear view space https://github.com/GPUOpen-LibrariesAndSDKs/FidelityFX-SDK/blob/c16b1d286b5b438b75da159ab51ff426bacea3d1/sdk/include/FidelityFX/gpu/sssr/ffx_sssr_intersect.h#L249-L260

> * Checking depth tolerance inside the loop allows the rays to pass behind objects, but due to the nature of Hi-Z tracing, gaps from low sample counts can look splotchy. Should users be simply expected to increase the max steps for this, or could we provide a toggle for allowing rays to go behind objects?

Is this not the problem that depth_tolerance solves? AFAIK setting a depth_tolerance of 0 is equivalent to not allowing the ray to trace behind objects.

> * Now that the roughness filtering is as high quality as it can get, what do we about the option?

As Calinou mentioned, just deprecate the existing method and change the docs to say that it is no longer needed

> * Using the previous frame causes the reflections to appear black for a singular frame when the framebuffers get cleared. You can reproduce this by just resizing the viewport. Not sure how this behavior should be accounted for.

We need to stretch the previous frame to cover the new resized texture. I.e. instead of throwing away the old texture and replacing it with the new texture, we copy the contents from the old texture to the new one. This will require a bit of refactoring in how we manage our scene buffer, so it does not need to be part of this PR and can be done in a follow up

> # To Do
> * SSR is currently applied after everything in the forward shader. We can sample SSR first, and skip everything afterwards that contributes to the indirect specular color. I looked into doing this, however indirect diffuse and specular sampling code seemed tightly coupled in a lot of places, so it will likely require careful separation of the logic.

Leave this for a follow up. I would like to refactor our indirect lighting even more and ideally move most of it out of the scene shader and into the GI accumulate pass.

> * Investigate whether Hi-Z and gaussian-blurred mip chain reductions can be parallelized with shared memory to reduce the dispatch count. At very low mip resolutions, spawning the dispatch takes longer than the actual workload. Similarly, the resolve shader can also write to shared memory to pick samples from neighbors.

Also a good follow up. The mip chain reductions should absolutely be moved to a single pass (see how it is done for SSAO/SSIL or AMD's Single Pass Downsampler for reference). Ideally we would also use a Hi-Z buffer for SSAO/SSIL that we share for all effects. 

What you have is fine for this PR

> * Share the previous frame texture between SSIL and SSR if both are enabled.

Would be good to add this before finalizing the PR

> * The main SSR trace pass shows a large gap at the end where the SM usage suddenly drops. This may be caused by long rays keeping the finished threads in the group idle. Tiling the rays and dispatching an indirect compute workload could potentially solve it.

Leave this for a follow up

> * Investigate how TAA could be utilized when it's enabled.

Leave this for a follow up

## Other Todos

- [x] Halos should be fixed. I noticed in your earlier screenshots that there is also a bad seam where the wall connects with the floor. This might just require a small bias in the normal direction and/or towards the camera (https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/111210#issuecomment-3365805791)
- [x] NaNs with roughness of 0.0 (https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/111210#pullrequestreview-3299787478)
- [x] Increase the lower bound of max steps (https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/111210#pullrequestreview-3299787478)
- [x] Nans with "fade out" of 0.0 (https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/111210#pullrequestreview-3299787478)

author: blueskythlikesclouds

body:
> On materials that have a roughness of exactly 0.0, NaN artifacts will appear if the material's specular mode is Shlick-GGX (the default).

I found where this is happening: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/60b7b8b16e07b344117fc33525f31d65c499cb22/servers/rendering/renderer_rd/shaders/scene_forward_lights_inc.glsl#L22

In D_GGX function, a division by zero can happen here. And since SSR using last frame information causes iterative infinite bounces, the NaN propagates really really quickly.

This only happens with screen space roughness limiter. When the roughness is 0, the limiter transformation makes it very slightly above 0, which makes the roughness > 0 check in the forward shader succeed. But then, the roughness^4 in the division collapses down to 0 due to the precision not being enough, which causes the NaN.

Should this issue be fixed in this PR, or is it out of scope?

EDIT: I've added an epsilon check in the screen space roughness limiter code, it solves the issue.

body:
> You'll be able to observe how the Red Sphere in the Metalic Torus reflections will change as you change where you're moving (or if you're moving

Ah yeah, this is due to not utilizing motion vectors. The SSR previous frame reprojection only accounts for camera movement, not the movement of objects themselves. 

Using motion vectors could probably fix this, but it introduces extra overhead, so it's definitely not something that SSR should force on by default, since in most scenes I tried it didn't really create issues.

From what I see, SSIL doesn't utilize them either, in fact it doesn't even care about camera moving at all, so it might be an area of improvement for both effects.

body:
This looks amazing and performs a lot better than the previous implementation! This even performs better at full resolution compared to the previous method at half resolution (both at 64 steps).

Some things I've noticed: 
1. SSR does not work for objects with emission if the environment reflection is completely black. (So no SSR if the sky is completely black, making the reflective puddles in 'cyberpunk' environments impossible, which is (of course) the main use-case for ssr) (This is not an problem in the pre-4.5 ssr.)
2. The SSR also flickers a lot with TAA or FSR2.2. 

Both of these issues are shown in this video: 

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d2c9fcb5-eeb8-4a7f-9585-249dc37ba770

One question: would it be possible (and logical) to add an even cheaper 1/4 res mode? Some games (like racing games where ssr is only used for reflections on the road, and the roads are almost always blurred in motion) can look good with even lower resolution ssr.
author: WrobotGames

body:
> SSR does not work for objects with emission if the environment reflection is completely black. (So no SSR if the sky is completely black, making the reflective puddles in 'cyberpunk' environments impossible, which is (of course) the main use-case for ssr) (This is not an problem in the pre-4.5 ssr.)

I wonder if this is a consequence of https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/63587.

Can you reproduce this when specular occlusion is disabled in the Project Settings? (Note that the [specular occlusion](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/89988) being disabled is not the same as https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/63587, but I thought this could be related too.)

> One question: would it be possible (and logical) to add an even cheaper 1/4 res mode? 

It's possible, but I would be wary about the compounding effect of FSR2 upscaling and quarter-res (per-axis) SSR rendering. For example, if you use FSR2 Performance with a 4K output, the game internally renders in 1080p. With a quarter-res SSR option, the screen-space reflections would render at 270p, which is very little data to work with. The reflection will likely look unstable in motion.

A 1/3rd resolution option could be workable (it would be 360p in the above example), but I don't know if it's technically feasible.

Either way, this should be discussed in its own proposal.

author: mrjustaguy

body:
> This introduces an issue with non static elements however

The new SSR uses the previous frame's data (like the current SSIL implementation), so it's going to lag behind a bit. I'm not sure if much can be done about this, especially when the camera position/rotation suddenly changes (see below).

One concern where the issue may look signifcant is in camera cuts during cutscenes, which is why some games intentionally insert a 1-frame stutter (keeping the last frame before the cut visible for one more frame) to allow SSR to initialize in the new scene without a visible transition.[^1] This stutter is generally not too noticeable if the cutscene runs at 60 FPS or more.

[^1]: This is not the only reason; commonly cited reasons are TAA convergence, as well as GPU-based occlusion culling that relies on the depth buffer.

author: Saul2022

body:
> there seems to be a problem when normal map is using mipmaps (default), reflection disappears at oblique angle. This is unfortunately very noticeable because it happens where the reflection is at its sharpest:

So this is a self intersection issue, which is something I could handle more gracefully in the SSR shader, but the mipmaps of the texture seem weird too. It's a completely flat normal map, so all the pixels are set to 0.5. However, it keeps getting darker in lower mipmap levels, ending up at 0.45 in the lowest mipmap level. This ends up bending the normal downwards and cause this self intersection issue. Is this a bug with Godot's mipmap generator?

EDIT: I made the bias a bit more aggressive, which fixes your particular example, though I also made a PR for the mipmap problem.

---

> It breaks at 2x MSAA but works at 4x MSAA

2x MSAA seems to resolve the depth texture in some weird way:

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/master/servers/rendering/renderer_rd/shaders/effects/resolve.glsl#L115

This makes the geometric normal computation in the SSR shader think the surface is spiky, so the ray bias goes all over the place. Could likely be fixed if that logic was changed to simply pick the furthest or closest sample instead.

---

> Worth mentioning that if I do the simplest lens distortion in Post Transparent by writing distorted image back to screen it doesn't work with ssr and there are no render hooks to do it later:

Seems to be because the last frame texture ends up having distortion applied to it as well, so we should do the copying a bit earlier.

EDIT: I moved the copying to before compositor effects and it seems to fix it.

---

> SSR does not work for objects with emission if the environment reflection is completely black. (So no SSR if the sky is completely black, making the reflective puddles in 'cyberpunk' environments impossible, which is (of course) the main use-case for ssr) (This is not an problem in the pre-4.5 ssr.)

This seems to be due to a specular occlusion term that gets applied in the forward clustered shader. It looks like reflections fade if the ambient color is dark enough? I'm not sure if I should be touching this logic.

---

> The SSR also flickers a lot with TAA or FSR2.2.

IIRC, all the post effects suffer from this and I'm not sure how to fix it yet. It may need to be handled in a future PR that also fixes it for other post effects.

---

> This introduces an issue with non static elements however

Like I said in an earlier comment, this likely can be fixed with motion vectors, but we'll have to discuss if it should be handled in this or a future PR.

---

> Are these distortions normal?

No, absolutely not. I'll see what's wrong with those.

EDIT: Fixed.

> 
> Also to be clear, its not a problem with all dynamic objects, its a problem with having highly reflective objects parented under the camera that don't move relative to the camera. The solution will be to apply motion vectors more fully. Which we intend to do already in a follow up PR when we add support for temporal supersampling with TAA.

Two things

1) SSIL to SSR comparison is horrible, SSIL is both a significantly weaker effect and one that's significantly more.. nebulous...
It's much harder to pinpoint an issue with something that isn't so clearly defined like Reflections or Shadows are.
2) This reprojection issue does NOT require the camera to be moving in the world, all that is required is that the World is doing stuff, which is guess what, the thing that Games have happening in them.
The reason I pinpointed that scenario is because it is the precise scenario that happened in a Real World Game Test in the first two Seconds of gameplay testing of this PR.
For a moment I was like "Oof, am I running into a major Driver issue or sth in a running project?" until I quickly realized exactly what was going on.

Given there is a clear plan to resolve this issue and I don't see any problems with said plan I'm not against this merging, it's just that this type of thing seems like a bad idea to me.
Also the TAA changes have certainly peaked my curiosity now.

author: KoBeWi

body: `NOTIFICATION_VISIBILITY_CHANGED` is called after a popup is shown, so it won't work in this case. `_popup_base()` should intercept it before it's shown (and show native dialog instead).
author: bruvzg

body:
Currently, `Window::popup_*` do the following:
    - `about_to_popup` signal is emitted
    - `_pre_popup` is called to override window size (used only by `PopupMenu`)
    - `_popup_base` is called, this shows the window and emit `NOTIFICATION_VISIBILITY_CHANGED` + `visibility_changed` signal.
    - `_post_popup` is called and `NOTIFICATION_POST_POPUP` is emitted.

So overriding `_popup_base` should allow it to prevent window from being shown, and show native dialog. `NOTIFICATION_VISIBILITY_CHANGED` is too late, and overriding `_pre_popup` do nothing, since the rest of the called will happen in any case.

body:
Well, this led me down a rabbit hole. 

I don't think we should go for this quick fix. The code needs to be rewritten. Even with this change we will run into issues when we need mipmaps on the color buffer, but `check_backbuffer()` was called before `_create_render_target_backbuffer()`. 

We shouldn't have two completely different implementations of `check_backbuffer()` that do almost the same thing. And then two different paths for creating the `rt->backbuffer_fbo` and the `rt->backbuffer`. 

The core problem is that the needs of 2D and 3D are slightly different. For some reason the code currently tries to share the backbuffer as much as possible between 2D and 3D. I think the best approach would be to just remove `TextureStorage::check_backbuffer()`entirely and use `RenderSceneBuffersGLES3::check_backbuffer()` for 3D and `_create_render_target_backbuffer()` for 2D
author: clayjohn

body: Alternative, and probably simpler, implementation is having a `custom_tooltip_callback` Callable, called by viewport when making the default tooltip.
author: KoBeWi

body:
> Alternative, and probably simpler, implementation is having a `custom_tooltip_callback` Callable, called by viewport when making the default tooltip.

I believe this will generally fall into some of the same traps with the current method. While that would solve the problem I mention for a bulk of the existing UI elements (excluding if you use subviewports anywhere, because you'd have to remember to use the same callback for any of those), it does not solve the issue for any element that has a PopUp involved, which will have it's own hidden viewport (OptionButton, MenuButton, etc). It would mean for any of those types, you'd have to create a custom script to get your tooltip working again. My goal with this proposal was to generally simplify the process for having your custom tooltips across the entire project, so that you don't need to create a script/custom type _just_ to give it a custom tooltip. 

The point of the default setting is to ensure any viewports/windows that get created from those elements will also have the tooltip scene you want, and I actually only added it after making the viewport function as I noticed the issue with PopUps at that time. (It also ended up simplifying the user experience as now you just change a project setting and you have custom tooltips, instead of needing to use a script)

[Crossposting from the Godot chat site](https://chat.godotengine.org/channel/input?msg=Dpa2oFT9WqzefnfkY)

I'm not quite sure how hats should be handled in "raw input" mode. Currently, they are mapped to the D-Pad if no mapping already exists, but at least with my joystick (Thrustmaster T.16000M) doing this for raw mode conflicts with existing button indices. Additionally, some joysticks (such as the VKB Gladiator NXT EVO, which I am upgrading to soon) have multiple hats and so those may not work at all.

In SDL, they are an entity completely separate from axes and buttons, but I'm not sure introducing this distinction into Godot is useful, or even relevant at this point (though it would not necessarily be a breaking change, so it may be worthwhile). I'm not sure whether to map them in raw mode to axes or to buttons, or both?

What are everyone's thoughts on this? I've marked the PR as a draft until this blocker is resolved.

body:
Just noticed that the following actually is relying implicitly on the `paged_allocator.h` declaration included by `variant.h` to fill the last argument.
```cpp
typedef PagedAllocator<Page, true> Allocator;
```

Fixed it with the following, which also happens to match the default argument, so there's no change in behaviour.
```cpp
typedef PagedAllocator<Page, true, DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE> Allocator;
```

author: passivestar

body: > Agreed, it's most useful for multi-column trees. Maybe editor shortcuts need stripes too I tried that before, but it ended up being somewhat confusing for foldable trees. > Any chance they could be styleboxes? It would be handy to be able to draw them with rounded corners, including in editor Right now this can be enabled with a single line of code `tree->add_theme_constant_override("draw_color_guides", 1)` If we switch to using styleboxes, managing this becomes more complicated. Most styleboxes are disabled by default using `EmptyStyleBox`, so to keep the current default behavior we would need the default stylebox to remain empty. Enabling it in the editor would then have to be handled by the editor theme, which would likely enable it for all Tree instances by default. The alternative would be creating a type variation specifically for this feature so it can still be enabled easily. Otherwise, if we keep it disabled by default using the theme constant `draw_color_guides` instead of empty style, users might set the stylebox and see no visible change, which could be confusing. > This is probably a matter of preference but I think having them enabled is a better default and they slightly improve readability with multiple columns In my opinion it doesn’t look very good for trees with many foldable children, and it can also look awkward for trees that only have a single column. Because of that, it’s probably better to evaluate the visual impact across these different cases before deciding on changing the default behavior.
author: WhalesState

body:
> Right now this can be enabled with a single line of code
tree->add_theme_constant_override("draw_color_guides", 1)
If we switch to using styleboxes, managing this becomes more complicated. Most styleboxes are disabled by default using EmptyStyleBox, so to keep the current default behavior we would need the default stylebox to remain empty.

Right now the guides can be customized with a single color. This could be changed to a StyleBox, separately from `draw_color_guides`, which would still toggle it.

I also wonder if `draw_color_guides` should even be a theme property if it's not something you'd want to have consistently enabled/disabled for every tree. Could be a regular property.

I've tried this PR and while I do see the option for enabling alternating lines for Tress I make in the engine, I can't find anyway to actually enable this for these editor views in particular, which is what I most was looking forward to. I understand that the usability of this mostly comes from when it's a multi-column type of Tree, but I do think there's a certain level of added "niceness" in having stripes for even simple views. Blender for instance isn't much bigger than how I have mine setup in Godot, and while the effect is much more subtle than the proposed look here, not to be greedy or anything, but I would be appreciative of a toggle that allowed for it, and if not that's okay too. Unless of course there already is, in which case I'm surely blind because I cannot seem to find anything alluding to it in either the code or the editor settings :sweat: As for the naming of the property... while I don't believe I'm any source of authority, I'm partial towards `alternate_row_style_visible`, I don't think prefixing with `use_` makes sense, given the context of the other property just above (`column_titles_visible`), which is functionally similar to the alternate row property, and doesn't have `use_`. body: > Hello, I have added an option in EditorSettings `docks/scene_tree/show_row_stripes` to allow enabling this new feature for scene tree dock. > > For `EditorFileSystem`, it can be added later, since it doesn't go well with folder background colors. I've been using this PR for a couple weeks now and this has very much helped with readability with the scene tree, thank you. However, I have ran into an issue regarding the color of it, as with certain background colors the alternate row becomes completely invisible, and with the background color I normally use (#1e1e2e) it is quite hard to see. I'm not entirely sure how this should be handled as I'm not sure if a new color setting in the editor theme settings is something desirable. With a fully black background, or otherwise referred to as the "OLED" theme, the stripe has no actual appearance. Additionally, this may seem a bit nit picky, but for both the editor and the actual theme usage of this, would it be possible to have an option or way of extending the alternate row through out the entirety of the panel? As seen in Blender, I find this to be a more visually pleasing approach as it gives the look that each alternating row is a "slot" so to speak for something to go inside of it, I'm not sure how many people actually care about this sort of thing so I understand if it's not something you want to do. (And perhaps it could be something I can look into otherwise) I once again thank you for your work, and I hope to see this new style option become used elsewhere in the editor in the future, especially with the FileSystem dock. --- I went ahead and modified this to allow an alternate_row_color within interface/theme It does feel slightly overkill having a new color option here, but given it's mostly a readability/accessibility feature perhaps it's okay? As an aside, I think the alternate rows, specifically within the tree view, are visually better if the rounded corners are removed. Here's what a catppuccin-like theme would look like before and after colored alternate rows and no rounded corners. A minor difference, but when the tree view is at the corner of a screen where the color is slightly shifted, it actually does help quite a bit. If this is something desirable I can add presets for each theme (currently all just empty) and give you a .patch

body:
> The error will still print once but that's kinda expected. If two separate Godot instances try to start the language server on the same port there exists a conflict.

The error is pretty generic so I think we should still aim to address that. Users will think it's a bug.

Instead of a networking error, we should either say nothing (most users don't use the LSP so don't care if it's not available on a second instance) or print a warning that's explicit about the issue.
author: akien-mga

body:
> The error is pretty generic so I think we should still aim to address that. Users will think it's a bug.

This PR is needed regardless of that.

It might be something worth looking into. However, if some component below it all of a sudden fails with error message instead of failing silently that's nothing the language server can influence.

In general the codebase lacks proper rules for usage of `ERR_FAIL` macros in methods that return `Error`. There is no way to force a method to fail silently (except maybe relying on `ERR_PRINT_OFF` but that's really not a pattern I would want to introduce to the language server).
author: HolonProduction

body:
> > The error is pretty generic so I think we should still aim to address that. Users will think it's a bug.
> 
> This PR is needed regardless of that.
> 
> It might be something worth looking into. However, if some component below it all of a sudden fails with error message instead of failing silently that's nothing the language server can influence.

This is addressed by #111300.

> In general the codebase lacks proper rules for usage of `ERR_FAIL` macros in methods that return `Error`. There is no way to force a method to fail silently (except maybe relying on `ERR_PRINT_OFF` but that's really not a pattern I would want to introduce to the language server).

Yeah that's a problem in general, we need to rely less on `ERR_FAIL` and do a better job at bubbling up and handling error codes in the original call site.

body:
Oh! Now THIS is an exciting revelation. The CI for this PR initially failed on all non-msvc compilers, citing some variation of:
```
ERROR: In file included from ./core/config/project_settings.h:33,
                 from platform\windows\os_windows.h:36,
                 from platform\windows\godot_windows.cpp:31:
./core/object/object.h:497:24: error: 'ClassDB' in namespace '::' does not name a type
  497 |         friend class ::ClassDB;                                                                                                                 \
      |                        ^~~~~~~
./core/config/project_settings.h:39:9: note: in expansion of macro 'GDCLASS'
   39 |         GDCLASS(ProjectSettings, Object);
      |         ^~~~~~~
./core/object/object.h:497:9: error: friend declaration does not name a class or function
  497 |         friend class ::ClassDB;                                                                                                                 \
      |         ^~~~~~
./core/config/project_settings.h:39:9: note: in expansion of macro 'GDCLASS'
   39 |         GDCLASS(ProjectSettings, Object);
      |         ^~~~~~~
```
Meaning that, for the first time that I can recall, `ClassDB` has been decoupled to such an extent that there's incidental header paths which *don't include it* anymore! This bug was resolved by simply declaring `class ClassDB;` in the `core/object/object.h`, but I'm popping off that I encounted it in the first place

body: Having the uid as a separate file, but at the same time the metadata as an annotation inside the script looks _really confusing_.
author: arkology

body: In the attempt to solve https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/1316 , I don't think it ever crossed most contributor's minds to utilize a single `@meta` tag as a custom tag. It does sound quite clever, but it's far too limiting in many occasions where implementing supplementary code is necessary.
author: Mickeon

body: Btw in your commits you ping user with `@meta` name, be careful.
author: arkology

body:
> Having the uid as a separate file, but at the same time the metadata as an annotation inside the script looks _really confusing_.

Unlike UIDs, the keys/values here are user-defined at the class and class-member level, so I think the text of the script is an appropriate place for them.

That said, I understand that calling this "metadata" could be confusing given that other kinds of script metadata (like UIDs) already exist and live elsewhere. The name was inspired by `Object.get_meta` but I have no objection to renaming this new annotation to `@tag` or similar if people prefer.
author: khellste

body:
> In the attempt to solve [godotengine/godot-proposals#1316](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/1316) , I don't think it ever crossed most contributor's minds to utilize a single `@meta` tag as a custom tag. It does sound quite clever, but it's far too limiting in many occasions where implementing supplementary code is necessary.

What application of custom annotations from the original proposal did you have in mind? I think this PR unblocks the original `@Serialize` case from that proposal. My intuition is that a good chunk of the more complex use cases ("I want to run some code when a certain annotation is present") can be accomplished by running the desired code in whatever script does the `@meta` introspecting.
author: khellste

body: \+@TokageItLab , since you took some interest in PR https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/102516.
author: khellste

body:
> > In the attempt to solve [godotengine/godot-proposals#1316](https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/1316) , I don't think it ever crossed most contributor's minds to utilize a single `@meta` tag as a custom tag. It does sound quite clever, but it's far too limiting in many occasions where implementing supplementary code is necessary.
> 
> What application of custom annotations from the original proposal did you have in mind? I think this PR unblocks the original `@Serialize` case from that proposal. My intuition is that a good chunk of the more complex use cases ("I want to run some code when a certain annotation is present") can be accomplished by running the desired code in whatever script does the `@meta` introspecting.

@Mickeon - to be more concrete, here's how `@Serialize` from the original proposal might be implemented using the `@meta` tag:

**my_data_class.gd**
```gdscript
class_name MyDataClass
extends RefCounted

@meta("serialize", { name = "json_foo" })
var foo: int

@meta("serialize")
var bar: String

var x: bool
```

**main.gd**
```gdscript
static func to_dict(data_object: RefCounted) -> Dictionary[StringName, Variant]:
    var result: Dictionary[StringName, Variant]
    var script = data_object.get_script() as Script
    if script == null: return result

    # Introspect @meta("serialize") annotations.
    for meta in script.get_script_meta("serialize"):
        if meta.target_type != Script.MetaTargetType.META_TARGET_VARIABLE:
            push_warning("ignoring @meta(\"serialize\") on non-property")
            continue
        var key: StringName =\
            meta.target_name if typeof(meta.value) == TYPE_BOOL and meta.value \
            else meta.value[&"name"]
        result[key] = data_object.get(meta.target_name)

    return result


func _init() -> void:
    var data_object := MyDataClass.new()
    data_object.foo = 0
    data_object.bar = "test"

    var dict := to_dict(data_object)
    print(dict) # { &"json_foo": 0, &"bar": "test" }
```

It's even powerful enough to implement something resembling the builtin `@rpc` annotation:

**annotated_node.ts**
```gdscript
extends Node

@meta("rpc", {
    rpc_mode = MultiplayerAPI.RPCMode.RPC_MODE_AUTHORITY,
    transfer_mode = MultiplayerPeer.TransferMode.TRANSFER_MODE_UNRELIABLE,
    call_local = false,
})
func foo() -> void: pass
```

**main.ts (root node)**
```gdscript
extends Node

static func apply_rpc_settings(node: Node) -> void:
    var script = node.get_script() as Script
    if script == null: return
    for meta in script.get_script_meta("rpc"):
        if meta.target_type != Script.MetaTargetType.META_TARGET_FUNCTION:
            push_warning("ignoring @meta(\"rpc\", ...) on non-method")
            continue
        node.rpc_config(meta.target_name, meta.value)

func _init() -> void:
    child_entered_tree.connect(apply_rpc_settings)
```
author: khellste

body:
I agree the implementation as pure metadata, but I assume that this PR way seems quite indirect. So I feel that this way doesn't differ much from having the script have a reserved word dictionary.

As I mentioned in a previous PR https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/102516, my opinion is that this kind of implementation should be a little more invasive to the core and made readily available in C++/GDExtension and similar contexts.

In other words, I think implementing a function like `ClassDB::bind_meta()` in ClassDB would allow the class itself to hold the meta, rather than relying on scripts. Then `@meta` will be implemented as just syntactic sugar. And I think, the approach for implementing custom analyzers will likely resemble the registration process for EditorPlugins.
author: TokageItLab

body:
I considered `ClassDB`, but the [docs](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/classes/class_classdb.html) state pretty plainly that script-defined classes are outside the purview of `ClassDB`:
> Note: Script-defined classes with class_name are not part of ClassDB, so they will not return reflection data such as a method or property list. However, GDExtension-defined classes are part of ClassDB, so they will return reflection data.

Whether or not I agree with that design choice for `ClassDB`, I think it means it's not the right place to interface with metadata on script-defined classes. And in my opinion, this metadata feature will not be very useful if it can't be used on script-defined classes.

That said, I absolutely support something like `bind_meta(...)` so that metadata is settable outside of GDScript.
author: khellste

body:
I don't consider this PR a solution to the linked proposal.

This syntax is utterly unreadable and I don't even understand why, given that the challenge with custom annotations certainly isn't the parsing part.

One of the big technical challenges for custom annotations is, that we don't have a way to resolve conflicts because we don't have namespacing. So if two plugins use the same annotation name we have a problem.

This PR doesn't solve this issue. It just pushes the responsibility onto plugin devs.

> Hey plugin dev: here is an arbitrary dictionary that might or might not contain what you are looking for. Did the user make a mistake? Is there a conflicting plugin? Idk but I'm sure you'll figure it out! 

We can't expect plugin devs to figure all this verification out themselves, otherwise implementing annoatations will be a huge mess.

It's also basically impossible to design good UX around free form metadata:
- autocompletion? yeah no
- Want errors ahead of runtime? nope, plugin dev has to verify at runtime, not our responsibility
- want to read the docs? good luck figuring it out, there is no specific language construct involved, which could own the documentation
- made a typo for the parameter? How should the engine know? The plug-in might just behave differently then you expected


All of those concerns are totally unrelated to the question of "key value metadata pairs" vs "python decorators". JVM annotations are also only key value pairs. But they are readable, have well defined parameters and can provide good UX.
author: HolonProduction

body:
Fails at readability. That is enough to make it not work.

Only way I can see it work if you allow some form of metaprogramming with it but that is also outside scope.

Metaprogramming here means something like junit5 annotations where you can put annotations on annotation classes to combine them. Which means custom annotations in the end.
author: Frontrider

body:
I don't consider a single `@meta` tag a suitable solution to the issue of custom annotations. While it is able to drive the purpose that "annotations are metadata" it fails to create an extensible first-class annotation system, and hides the lack of extensibility in the single `@meta` annot.

Basically, having `@meta` forces what should look something like `@annot1(params)` into `@meta(annot1, { params })` which is a mess, comparatively.
author: Shadows-of-Fire

body:
Okay, I'm definitely sensing that this is not the thing people want and this needs to be fleshed out more.

That discussion is probably better to have back in the original proposal, https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/1316.

> Would this cause color clipping in reflection probes? Or was that never ever a concern?

Ya, this will cause clipping since probes use RGB10A2 format. We probably need to be more clever here and use a reversible tonemapper for both reflection probes and sky, or just accept that clipping is going to happen

Ok, looks like I hit a snag with this.

Because of the way we're creating our depth buffer in the OpenXR module, we can't write into it with the resolve shader. It's the wrong format, and has the wrong attachment type.

I'm looking into verifying those findings but it leaves us with a problem because we have no knowledge or access to the viewport with which we're about to render our XR content, and on which we can detect that MSAA is applicable and we need to change the format.
